Web strands strong enough to trap humans, venom causes rapid paralysis. Strength: Can move silently despite massive size, excellent night vision. Weakness: Relies heavily on web vibrations for sensing prey, vulnerable to fire.

Bloodwing.text: Bloodwing: Grotesque, enormous mosquito common in the Zone. Man-sized flying insect with translucent wings and a needle-like proboscis as long as a spear. Swollen abdomen pulses red with stolen blood. Hunts at dawn and dusk, drawn to large mammals including horses and humans. Can drain a horse in minutes. Multiple feedings cause abdomen to swell until it must rest on the ground. Proboscis can pierce light armor, victims quickly weaken from blood loss. Strength: Nearly silent in flight, can detect warm blood at great distances. Weakness: Sluggish and vulnerable when gorged with blood, easily crippled by damage to wings.

Scourge Scorpion.text: Scourge Scorpion: Nightmarish arachnid often found in the Zone. Bull-sized scorpion with three segmented tails ending in barbed stingers. Mottled exoskeleton patterns resemble bark or stone. Clusters of eyes gleam like black gems. Inverted ambush predator that clings to cave ceilings or thick tree branches. Drops onto prey using all three tails to inject different toxins. Sometimes creates permanent hunting perches by coating surfaces with sticky secretions. Multiple venoms cause different effects: paralysis, burning pain, and tissue dissolution. Strength: Nearly invisible when still, can strike with all three tails simultaneously. Weakness: Vulnerable during molting, vulnerable to cold.

Boneworm.text: Boneworm: Massive burrowing parasite that infests darkholds. Pale, segmented worm as thick as a man's torso, with grinding circular mouthparts lined with calcium deposits from consumed bone. Body is semi-translucent, showing recently consumed matter. Tunnels through stone and soil, attracted to vibrations. Forms colonies in ancient burial grounds and battlefields. Creates extensive tunnel networks that weaken structural integrity. Can burrow through solid rock, digestive acids dissolve organic matter quickly. Strength: Nearly impossible to stop once it starts burrowing, excellent vibration sense. Weakness: Vulnerable to loud sounds, highly sensitive to bright light.

Click Lizard.text: Click Lizard: Eyeless predator evolved for darkhold hunting. Low-slung reptilian body with oversized front claws and complex sensory organs where eyes would be. Covered in hard scales with worn-smooth patches from squeezing through tight spaces. Hunts in small packs using echo-location clicks. Extraordinarily patient, known to stalk prey for days through tunnel systems. Often claims abandoned dwarven vaults as territory. Can track prey through solid rock, coordinated pack tactics. Strength: Perfect spatial awareness in darkness, incredibly strong for its size. Weakness: Overwhelmed by multiple sound sources, vulnerable to attacks on sensory organs.

Mire Stalker.text: Mire Stalker: Amphibious predator adapted to urban sewers and cisterns. Salamander-like body with elongated limbs and adhesive pads, mottled skin mimics environment. Expandable jaw can unhinge to swallow large prey. Claims territory around sewer junctions and storm drains. Steals from surface through grates and drains, gradually learning patterns of movement above. Sometimes keeps live prey trapped in air pockets. Can squeeze through impossibly small spaces, powerful grip prevents escape. Strength: Excellent climber on wet surfaces, can hold breath for hours. Weakness: Dependent on moisture, vulnerable to fire and alchemical purifying agents.

Corpse Render.text: Corpse Render: Scavenging horror that inhabits catacombs and burial crypts. Emaciated humanoid figure with over-long arms and retractable claws, patches of hardened skin like burial shrouds. Eyes reflect light like polished copper. Hoards bodies and carrion in deeper tunnels, emerging to raid graveyards and plague pits. Sometimes develops taste for living prey. Marks territory with distinctive scratch patterns. Carries multiple diseases, incredibly strong despite gaunt appearance. Strength: Can move silently despite size, excellent night vision, immune to most poisons. Weakness: Compulsive about defending its hoard, can be baited with fresh carrion.

Greater Silk Moth.text: Greater Silk Moth: Large moth species prized for its enchanted silk. Wing span of up to three feet, with intricate patterns that seem to move in moonlight. Cocoons shimmer with inherent magical properties. Drawn to areas of magical activity, particularly around magetowers. Colonies establish themselves in abandoned buildings. Cocoons retain magical properties even after moth emerges. None to humans, though protecting a colony from predators can be dangerous. Strength: Silk has natural magic-conducting properties, highly valued by artificers. Weakness: Completely defenseless, requires specific conditions to breed successfully.

Tarrasque.text: Tarrasque: Colossal monster of legend, embodiment of destruction. Enormous reptilian creature with shell, horns, and multiple limbs. Sleeps for eons, awakens to rampage and destroy. Nearly indestructible, can level entire cities. Strength: Regeneration, immunity to many types of damage, incredibly strong. Weakness: Can be put back to sleep with powerful magic, vulnerable to wish spells.

Troll.text: Troll: Giant, brutish humanoids with remarkable regenerative abilities. Grotesquely tall with long arms, mottled green-gray skin, and hooked claws. Hunched posture belies explosive speed. Wounds visibly heal as you watch. Claim remote valleys and mountain passes as territory, marking boundaries with massive stone cairns. Live in loose family groups, teaching young to hunt by attacking anything that enters their domain. Sometimes keep cave bears as pets. Hard to kill without fire, become more aggressive when wounded. Incredible regeneration, powerful enough to throw boulders. Fire stops regeneration.

Ophidian.text: Ophidian: Former humans transformed through ancient rituals into hybrid snake-beings. Varies by individual and degree of transformation. Common features include patches of iridescent scales, vertically slitted pupils, and elongated canines. Advanced transformations develop hood-like growths, forked tongues, or fully serpentine lower bodies. Maintain elaborate hierarchies based on degree of transformation. Older members become increasingly obsessed with further ritual enhancement. Often manipulate local politics through a combination of charm and terror. Retain human intelligence while gaining serpentine abilities. Many develop toxic bites or hypnotic gazes. Strength: Powerful combination of human cunning and reptilian attributes. Highly resistant to poisons. Weakness: Transformation process is addictive, leading to increasingly risky ritual attempts. Vulnerable to extreme cold.

Fire Elementling.text: Fire Elementling: Living spark of elemental fire, manifesting where flame meets wild magic. Tiny humanoid figure made of living flame, leaving small burn marks with each step. Eyes flicker like candle flames. Mercurial and quick-tempered, drawn to sources of heat and fire. Some bond with blacksmiths or firefighters as assistants. Can ignite flammable materials when angered. Swarms pose serious fire hazard. Strength: Can manipulate existing fires, immune to heat and flame. Weakness: Extinguished by sufficient water or smothering. Weakened by cold.

Earth Elementling.text: Earth Elementling: Animated essence of stone and soil, emerging where earth magic runs strong. Small, stocky figure made of packed earth or living stone. Stone growths serve as eyes. Patient and methodical, often found strengthening foundations or repairing stonework. Some partner with miners to locate ore veins. Can cause localized tremors or collapse weak structures. Swarms can undermine foundations. Strength: Nearly indestructible while in contact with earth. Can merge with and manipulate stone. Weakness: Slow-moving, vulnerable when separated from ground.

Water Elementling.text: Water Elementling: Living droplet of elemental water, coalescing where water meets magical currents. Fluid humanoid shape constantly flowing and reforming. Playful and curious, found near fountains and wells. Sometimes help maintain aqueducts and irrigation systems. Can cause rapid rust or erosion. Groups can create dangerous currents. Strength: Can control water flow and pressure. Weakness: Vulnerable to extreme heat.

Air Elementling.text: Air Elementling: Living breath of wind, forming where air currents intersect with magical eddies. Nearly invisible humanoid outline visible only by the way it disturbs air and dust. Capricious and adventurous, attracted to high places and open skies. Sometimes assist sailors or windmill operators. Can create sudden gusts or vacuum pockets. Swarms generate devastating windstorms. Strength: Nearly invisible, can fly freely and manipulate air currents. Weakness: Disperses in confined spaces. Can be trapped in sealed containers.

Pitmaw.text: Pitmaw: Buried maw of the desert. Body lies deep beneath the sand—only the gullet extends upward, fifteen feet of extendable throat ending in a hidden mouth. A subtle circular depression marks the trap. A faint whisper, like breath through a hollow reed, gives it away if you're listening. Step above one and the sand becomes a funnel, suction pulling you down into an acid-filled stomach. Desert folk learn the signs: test suspicious ground with thrown stones, listen for the whisper. Pitmaws don't move once settled—a hunting ground is theirs for decades. The digestive acid fetches good coin from alchemists.

Possessor.text: Possessor: Malicious spirit that inhabits corpses and objects. Controlled bodies show unnaturally wide mouth seams and impossible joint angles. Can leap between hosts. Strength: Body possession, host jumping, memory retention, psychic immunity. Weakness: Light-based attacks, exorcism, spiritual wards, host destruction.

Smokewalker.text: Smokewalker: Spirit formed from funeral pyre smoke, shifts between human shape and smoke column. Only visible to pure hearts until attack. Can flow through cracks and suffocate victims. Strength: Selective invisibility, smoke form, suffocation attacks, fire immunity. Weakness: Water, strong winds, detection by pure hearts.

Hundredhands.text: Hundredhands: Towering giant with fifty heads and hundred arms, each capable of independent action. Can attack dozens simultaneously or focus all hands on single task. Walking army. Strength: Multiple attacks, independent heads, massive reach, coordination. Weakness: Confusion tactics, overwhelming single point, decision paralysis.

Webwidow.text: Webwidow: Giant spider that transforms into beautiful woman. Spins invisible silk traps and injects paralyzing venom through kiss or touch. Prefers to cocoon victims alive. Strength: Shapeshifting, invisible webs, paralytic venom, enchanting beauty. Weakness: Fire, detection by shadow, web cutting.

Watercharger.text: Watercharger: Shapeshifting water spirit appearing as beautiful horse near water. Adhesive skin traps riders, then dives deep to drown them. Can walk on water and control currents. Strength: Water walking, current control, adhesive skin, drowning attacks. Weakness: Fire, forced land combat, binding rituals.

Scaletitan.text: Scaletitan: Serpentine draconic creature with only front legs, no wings. Armored scales except throat. Grows larger by molting, becomes more ravenous. Can squeeze through impossible spaces. Strength: Armor scales, size growth, compression, battering ram strikes. Weakness: Throat vulnerability, molting periods, size restrictions.

Doomwing.text: Doomwing: Tall humanoid with giant moth wings and glowing red eyes. Appears before disasters, gaze induces prophetic nightmares of coming catastrophe. Causes panic and poor decisions. Strength: Disaster prophecy, fear induction, psychic immunity, dark resistance. Weakness: Light sensitivity, hope inspiration, prophecy prevention.

Drownmaiden.text: Drownmaiden: Vengeful spirit of drowned woman with green hair and bloated skin. Hypnotic singing lures victims to watery deaths in jealous embrace. Can be banished by true name. Strength: Hypnotic song, water domain, drowning attacks, beautiful illusion. Weakness: True name binding, water avoidance, counter-songs.

Sealskin.text: Sealskin: Shapeshifter between seal and human form by removing seal skin. Must obey whoever steals their pelt. Exceptional swimmers, communicate with sea creatures. Strength: Dual forms, water mastery, sea creature communication, cold resistance. Weakness: Skin theft, form binding, land vulnerability.

Skyblessed.text: Skyblessed: Celestial being with magnificent wings wearing iridescent robes that never touch ground. Music heals wounds but prolonged presence causes mortals to fade. Divine messenger. Strength: Flight, healing music, divine protection, blessing bestowal. Weakness: Mortal corruption, earthly binding, divine commands.

Darkrager.text: Darkrager: Colossal sea spirit as featureless black torso rising from calm seas. Absorbs light, capsizes ships, controls water pressure. Collects drowned souls in glowing pearls. Strength: Light absorption, ship destruction, pressure control, soul collection. Weakness: Fire attacks, light sources, pearl destruction.

Flameshifter.text: Flameshifter: Fire-breathing dragon that shapeshifts into handsome man by night. Only small scale behind ear reveals true nature. Seeks genuine romance with mortals. Strength: Shapeshifting, fire control, charm, flight capability. Weakness: True love vulnerability, scale detection, emotional manipulation.

Soul Thief.text: Soul Thief: Underworld demon that snatches unburied dead. Touch causes paralysis, casts no shadow. Cannot cross running water or sanctified ground. Collects souls in crystal vials. Strength: Soul capture, paralytic touch, shadow immunity, stealth. Weakness: Running water, sacred ground, vial destruction, light attacks.

Weeping Hunter.text: Weeping Hunter: Amphibious predator with dog body, monkey hands, and tail-hand. Mimics child crying to lure victims to water. Devours only eyes, teeth, and fingernails. Strength: Voice mimicry, aquatic mobility, trophy collection, specific targeting. Weakness: Sound detection, water avoidance, protective barriers.

Shipclaw.text: Shipclaw: Small fish with adhesive crest that latches onto ships, bringing them to complete stop. Schools can drag vessels underwater. Alpha marks target ships. Strength: Ship stopping, coordinated attacks, underwater dragging, adhesive secretion. Weakness: Fire, targeted removal, alpha elimination.

Stormwing.text: Stormwing: Giant eagle that causes winds with wing beats. Razor-sharp feathers slice armor, wind gusts tear people apart. Nests on highest peaks, sees through generated winds. Strength: Wind immunity, razor feathers, distance vision, mountain domain. Weakness: Enclosed spaces, feather deflection, grounding.

Bonebiter.text: Bonebiter: Beast with stag body, lion chest, horse hooves. Mimics human voices, has ridged bone that cuts through anything. Collects and digests bones for strength. Strength: Voice mimicry, bone cutting, strength absorption, bone projectiles. Weakness: Bone shortage, voice recognition, defensive positioning.

Icesnatcher.text: Icesnatcher: Human-like sea creature with green skin that hums to lure people to water. Keeps victims in baby carrier under ice. Touch causes frostbite, can freeze water. Strength: Hypnotic humming, ice manipulation, frostbite touch, victim preservation. Weakness: Heat sources, loud noises, carrier targeting.

Gulper.text: Gulper: Gluttonous beast with goat body and eyes beneath massive jaws. Devours anything, absorbs properties of consumed creatures. Temporarily gains victim characteristics. Strength: Universal consumption, property absorption, characteristic mimicry, endless hunger. Weakness: Dietary restrictions, absorption overload, specific prey targeting.

Magmaborn.text: Magmaborn: Humanoid creature of living magma and rock that dwells in volcanoes. Body glows with inner fire, leaves smoldering footprints. Hurls magma globs and superheats metal weapons. Strength: Fire immunity, magma attacks, metal heating, natural illumination. Weakness: Water, extreme cold, heat dissipation.

Deathgazer.text: Deathgazer: Swamp beast with bull body and weak neck that drags heavy head along ground. Breath causes disease, eyes cause petrification. Approach from behind. Strength: Petrifying gaze, disease breath, poison resistance. Weakness: Rear approach, mirrors, neck vulnerability.

Prince of Shadow.text: Prince of Shadow: A figure from ancient legend and children's tales, said to be a shapeshifting tempter who appears in different forms to corrupt mortals through dark bargains. Old stories claim he was cast out by the gods and sealed away after the Great War generations ago, when the world burned in conflict. According to folklore, he offers power, wealth, or revenge to those who despair, but always at a terrible price - claiming their very souls. Popular superstition holds that those who strike bargains with the Prince no longer cast shadows in sunlight, having traded this part of themselves for his gifts. Most educated people dismiss such tales as primitive explanations for human greed and cruelty, though rural folk still whisper warnings about strangers who offer easy solutions to hard problems.

The Bloodhunters.text: The Bloodhunters: An elite, sanctioned order of vampire- and undead-hunters based in chantries throughout Larion. Distinguished by their fine attire and silver weaponry, they hunt undead threats using martial prowess and blood magic. Last line of defense against the dark. Feared for their powers, relied on for their results. Ruthlessly police their own ranks, hunting down blood mages who lose their way.

The Syndicate.text: The Syndicate (of the Commonweal): A worldwide merchant empire that answers to no king. The Syndicate controls the roads, the ships, the grain—the things that keep kingdoms alive—and they use that control like a blade at the world's throat. Their agents work every city. Their Grey Guards are better armed than most armies. Their Delegates travel untouchable through foreign lands. They don't start wars. They end them, when wars become bad for business. They don't make threats. They make the cost of defiance clear, once, politely. Princes have tested them. It hasn't gone well for the princes.

Order of the Sunscale.text: Order of the Sunscale: Militant dragonkin who protect dragonlore from dragon cultists. Based in the Aerie of the Sunscale near Cloudtop in the Bright Kingdoms. Mission: destroy dragon corruption cults - mortals who use sacrifice, rituals and alchemy to transform into draconic abominations. The Sunscale hunt cult cells across regions, raid ritual sites, burn heretical texts, and recover stolen draconic artifacts.

Children of the Wyrm.text: Children of the Wyrm: A dragon cult of scattered cells whose members seek to transform themselves into draconic beings using ancient dragon remains. Bones, scales, and other dragon materials are alchemically processed into reagents used in ritual transformations that produce dracomorphs or wyrmfiends - abominations whose bodies twist with scales, claws, and malformed wings, while granting breath weapons, flight and even long-lost draconic magic. Cultists search for and raid dragon tombs and fuel a black market in dragon relics. Believe they are ascending to dragonhood and earning their place as the Wyrm's true offspring. Some cult cells have turned to sacrificing dragonkin when ancient dragon remains prove too rare or expensive. Cult cells recruit the desperate, the power-hungry, and those seeking to become something greater than their birth allowed.

The Wolfpack.text: The Wolfpack: A mercenary company and berserker warrior lodge based in the Highvale region, near the Wolfwood. Take contracts to hunt monsters and win battles that 'civilized' knights cannot touch. Turn battle-fury into combat prowess, shrugging off wounds and terrifying foes. Accept coin, but true purpose is protecting the 'Old Way'—raw strength and instinct are superior to steel and law. Beast-shifting is a rite of passage for the inner circle, a path to greatness for those who fully embrace their inner beast.

Shadow War.text: Shadow War: An apocalyptic conflict from the distant past that some believe nearly destroyed civilization. Entire kingdoms falling to madness, brother turning against brother, and disaster and calamity of every kind befalling the world. The war's end is shrouded in legend. Most evidence of this ancient catastrophe has been lost to time.

Garden of Life.text: Garden of Life: A mythical grove hidden deep within the Everwood, said to hold the secret of life and death. Believed by most to be mere legend.

Gryphon.text: Gryphon: Leonine body, eagle wings, beaked head, and sharp talons. Proud creatures that mate for life and fiercely defend their nests built on mountain peaks and cliffsides. Highly intelligent and capable of understanding speech, though they cannot speak themselves. Bond with worthy warriors, becoming loyal mounts and companions. Feathers and claws are prized by alchemists and enchanters. Hoard shiny objects in their nests. Can be aggressive toward intruders but respect displays of courage and may spare those who stand their ground. Powerful aerial combat abilities, keen eyesight, devastating diving attacks. Vulnerable on ground, fiercely territorial, protective of young to the point of recklessness.

Rainbow Dragon.text: Rainbow Dragon: Rare, majestic dragons with scales of every color. Lair in mountain peaks. Breath weapon is a chaotic blast with random effects from elemental damage to petrification. Neutral or benevolent, hoarders of art, gems, bright things. Deeply magical. Resistance to most forms of magic, blinding aura. Vanity can be exploited, coloration makes stealth impossible, hate to destroy beautiful things and people.

Church of the Pyre.text: Church of the Pyre (Ignath, Lord of Fire): A fire cult devoted to Ignath, Lord of Fire, who they believe sleeps beneath the world and will one day rise to cleanse it. Headquartered in the Black Fells, the Church has allied with the Dragon Queen and the Greater Goblinate, preaching that their conquest is holy work—burning away the corrupt old order to make way for purification. Teaches that Larion's kingdoms are irredeemably corrupt: nobles hoard while peasants starve, the Syndicate trades in lives, the comfortable ignore suffering. Only fire purifies. Worshippers bear ritual burn scars as marks of devotion; the most fanatical are scarred beyond recognition. The Church's archpriests oversee dragonfire production, blessing the fuel that powers Goblinate weapons. Proscribed throughout Larion, the Church offers purpose and power to the angry, the wronged, and those who want to watch the old world burn.

Sunscale Order.text: Sunscale Order: Militant dragonkin who guard dragon lore, relics, and dragonkin themselves from the Children of the Wyrm and other threats. Based at the Aerie of the Sunscale near Cloudtop, the Order tracks rumors of dragon site discoveries, disrupts black market trade in dragon materials, and strikes cult cells. Operates safe houses in major cities where dragonkin can shelter, and maintains the Sanctum recovered dragon relics are properly interred. The Order accepts both dragonkin and trusted non-dragonkin allies into its ranks, offering training, resources, and mission contracts to those willing to fight against dragon corruption. Their sunburst emblem is recognized across Larion as a symbol of legitimate dragon heritage defended against those who would steal and pervert it.

Leucrotta.text: Leucrotta: Hybrid predator—stag's body, lion's haunches, badger-like head with a ridge of bone instead of teeth that shears through armor. Fast and tireless, but it prefers not to chase. It calls you closer first. Leucrottas mimic human voices perfectly: cries for help, a child lost in the woods, your companion calling your name from just beyond the treeline. They learn by listening—following groups for days, eavesdropping on camps, memorizing voices and phrases. The voices it knows are usually voices of the dead. Travelers learn to test suspicious calls: ask a question only the real person could answer. The leucrotta can only repeat what it's heard, never create. Its lair holds bones and belongings of victims—things worth recovering if you can stomach the search.

League of Adventurers.text: League of Adventurers: An umbrella organization that brings together guilds, orders and societies dedicated to adventuring, run by the Hidden Council. Chapterhouses in major cities where members can find posted contracts, claim rewards, find companions, get gear and information. Verifies contracts, mediates disputes between adventurers and private employers, and maintains a ranking system. Adventurers hold ranks from Copper through Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum. Higher ranks unlock better contracts, restricted resources, and access to Council-sealed work that never appears on the public boards. Recruits promising adventurers who might one day join the Council's efforts or be trusted with knowledge of greater threats. Headquartered in the Tower of Venture in Ardan's Watch, where the League's public leadership operates alongside the Council's hidden guidance.

League Ranks.text: League Ranks: Adventurers registered with the League of Adventurers hold ranks from Copper through Bronze, Silver, Gold, and Platinum. Copper-rank adventurers take local work—pest clearance, simple escorts, minor disputes. Bronze unlocks regional contracts and access to League safehouses. Silver grants access to restricted archives and dangerous hunts. Gold-rank adventurers handle threats to cities and provinces. Platinum is rare—these adventurers are trusted with Council-sealed contracts involving threats to the realm itself. Rank is earned through completed contracts, peer recognition, and tower sponsorship. It can be lost through failure, misconduct, or disgrace.

Tower Masters.text: Tower Masters: Each of the five towers in Ardan's Watch is led by a Master—a veteran who coordinates their order's efforts and represents them. The Master of Venture leads the League publicly and manages contracts. The Master of Swords commands the Blades in threat response. The Master of Shadow oversees intelligence-gathering and shadow operations and is known only to the other Masters and the Hidden Council. The Master of Spells oversees the Wardens, magical containment, and Elder lore. The Master of Scales dispatches Justiciars and coordinates justice across the realm. The five Masters meet regularly but answer ultimately to the Hidden Council.

Hidden Council.text: Hidden Council: A secret assembly of powerful figures who protect Larion from threats. Members include retired legendary adventurers, archmages, high priests, and other figures of immense capability. Respond to large-scale dangers: demon incursions, invasions, rogue demigods, planar breaches, and prophesied catastrophes. Work through proxies, mainly the League of Adventurers. Intelligence, resources, strategic direction. Absolute anonymity. Meeting places secret, hidden by powerful enchantments. Detractors call them manipulative puppet-masters; defenders argue that without their vigilance, Larion would have fallen long ago.

Tower of Venture.text: Tower of Venture: Headquarters of the League of Adventurers in Ardan's Watch. Contract hall where work is posted and claimed. Training yards, archives of monster lore, moot hall where adventurers plan expeditions. Private quarters for high-ranking members, vaults holding recovered artifacts. The Master of Venture leads the League publicly.

Tower of Swords.text: Tower of Swords: Home of the Blades, monster hunters and threat responders. When something dangerous emerges—a beast preying on villages, a creature stirring in ruins, a monster sighted on the roads—the Tower of Swords answers. Training focuses on combat, tracking, and creature lore.

Tower of Shadow.text: Tower of Shadow: One of the Elder towers of the League of Adventurers in Ardan's Watch. Home of the Shadows, who handle the supernatural threats that must stay secret for the public good — hauntings, cults, the undead, the things that would cause panic or spread their reach if confronted openly. Shadows train in warding, counter-summoning, and the rituals of detection, tracking, and banishment. They take their work in private and rarely acknowledge their affiliation outside the Tower.

Tower of Spells.text: Tower of Spells: Home of the Wardens, mage-wardens who monitor magical threats and maintain Elder portals. They study precursor artifacts, manage the tower-top portals, investigate magical incidents, and advise the other towers.

Goblin.text: Goblin: Small, wiry humanoids with pointed ears, sharp features, and skin ranging from sallow green to grey-brown. Quick-fingered and cunning. Most live in the volcanic Black Fells, where tribal settlements mine obsite and trade with whoever pays. Others survive as raiders on the borders of settled lands, taking what stronger peoples won't share. The Greater Goblinate has united many tribes under the Dragon Queen's banner, promising nationhood and conquest—but plenty of goblins answer to no flag, raiding for their own benefit or serving whoever offers coin. Individually weak but dangerous in numbers. Prefer ambush, traps, and overwhelming odds to fair fights. Will parley if outmatched, betray agreements if advantage shifts. Respect strength and cunning; despise weakness. A goblin who surrenders expects slavery or death and will offer the same.

Hobgoblin.text: Hobgoblin: Tall, broad-shouldered goblinoids with grey or rust-orange skin and heavy brows. Disciplined where goblins are chaotic, methodical where goblins are opportunistic. Military tradition runs deep—even independent bands organize into ranks, maintain equipment obsessively, and follow codes of conduct. Historically served as mercenaries and fortress-builders across Larion; a hobgoblin company's word is considered reliable, if expensive. Many now serve the Greater Goblinate as officers and specialists, bringing professional discipline to goblin irregulars. Others still sell their services independently, fighting for whoever meets their terms. Respect hierarchy, contracts, and martial excellence. Despise incompetence and disorder. Will honor a negotiated surrender if terms are clear; will execute those who break faith. Fight in disciplined formations, coordinate tactics, protect their flanks. Harder to panic than goblins, harder to trick, harder to kill.

Bugbear.text: Bugbear: Massive goblinoids, seven feet or taller, covered in coarse dark fur. Long arms, thick claws. Move with unnatural silence despite their bulk—the wrongness of something that large making no sound. Solitary hunters by nature. Patient. Watch settlements for days, learn patrol routes and guard rotations, identify the vulnerable. Strike from darkness and vanish before anyone organizes a response. Some lair near civilization permanently, treating a village or trade road as their hunting ground. Others hire out as killers—their silence and patience make them excellent assassins. Don't negotiate, don't posture, don't warn. If a bugbear is talking to you, it's because killing you isn't currently convenient. Take trophies. Weak against coordinated opposition; their solitary nature means they retreat rather than face real resistance.

Goblin Firelancer.text: Goblin Firelancer: Goblinate infantry armed with fire-lances—pike-length weapons topped with brass dragon heads that vomit gouts of fire. Wear crude scale armor in brass and bronze, dragon motifs hammered into every surface. Religious fervor keeps them fighting past where mercenaries would break. They believe the Dragon Queen has chosen them for glory. The fire-lances are unstable; malfunctions explode or burn wielders alive. Individually poor fighters—undisciplined, easily panicked if isolated. Dangerous in waves, when fire covers their advance and weight of numbers overwhelms defenders. Veterans know: kill the shamans directing them, and firelancer units lose cohesion.

Hobgoblin Heavy Firelancer.text: Hobgoblin Heavy Firelancer: Hobgoblin artillerists crewing the Goblinate's siege weapons—massive fire-lances mounted on wheeled carriages, requiring three or four operators. Wear heavier armor than infantry, elaborately decorated with rank insignia and dragon heraldry. Professional soldiers, not zealots; they direct bombardments methodically. Handle targeting, reload cycles, and the dangerous work of managing dragonfire reservoirs that could detonate catastrophically. Their presence means the Goblinate is fielding serious military force, not just raiding parties. Siege fire-lances can crack walls, scatter formations, and ignite entire streets. The weapons are inaccurate at range but devastating against fixed positions. Like smaller fire-lances, their larger cousins are volatile when attacked directly with magic.

Dragonfire Shaman.text: Dragonfire Shaman: Goblin priests who channel dragonfire directly. Marked by ritual burns, wearing regalia of dragon bones and red-black body paint. Serve the forces of the Dragon Queen as spiritual leaders—blessing weapons, leading ceremonies, preaching the Dragon Queen's divinity to converts. In battle, they hurl dragonfire without need of a lance and rally troops with prophetic fervor. Away from battle, they spread the faith to unconverted tribes and desperate communities. Firelancers depend on their spiritual authority; killing a shaman often breaks nearby units.

Bugbear Stalker.text: Bugbear Stalker: Bugbears serving the Goblinate as terror troops and assassins. Their specialty is killing leaders—officers, nobles, anyone directing resistance. Patient hunters who watch their targets for days before striking. In battle, they exploit chaos to reach priority kills. Outside battle, they're deployed ahead of invasions to eliminate key defenders before the army arrives. Not ideologically committed to the Dragon Queen's cause; they work for payment and hunting privileges. The Goblinate deploys them sparingly for high-value targets only. Where stalkers operate, defenders learn to hide rank insignia and vary their routines.

Dragonfire.text: Dragonfire: Alchemical fuel that powers Goblinate fire-lances. Made from obsite,sulfur, and trace amounts of a substance called Cold Ash—a rare powder controlled by the Church of the Pyre's archpriests. Cold Ash is cold to the touch, strangely heavy, and gives handlers a feeling of wrongness; its source is a closely guarded secret. Dragonfire combusts violently if mishandled, so firelancers carry their supply in pouches of fire salamander skin. When ignited, it burns hotter than natural flame with a greenish tinge, smells of sulfur, and resists smothering—water spreads it, sand only slows it. Rank-and-file goblins believe dragonfire is a divine gift from their Queen. They don't know how it's made. They know only that supply caravans come from the Black Fells, and when the pouches run dry, their lances are just heavy spears.

Firelance.text: Firelance: The signature weapon of Goblinate firelancers. Pike-length wooden haft topped with a brass dragon head that vomits dragonfire when triggered. Each muzzle is hand-crafted and unique—different expressions, different scale patterns, individual faces. Goblins name their lances, maintain them obsessively, and treasure them above all possessions. A firelancer's weapon defines who they are. Effective range is short, maybe twenty feet; beyond that, dragonfire disperses. Brass reservoirs at the base of the head hold fuel. The weapons are dangerous to operate—Loss of pressure sprays fire backward, cracked reservoirs ignite, misfires burn wielders alive. Firelancers accept the risk. Heavy fire-lances exist as siege weapons, crew-served on wheeled carriages, but the infantry lance is the symbol of the Goblinate at war.

Lords and Ladies of Vice and Virtue.text: Lords and Ladies of Vice and Virtue: In all the worlds, virtue arises unbidden. It is not created. It springs into being when mortals first appear - thinking beings capable of suffering and choice. Justice, Mercy, Valor, Hope. These principles exist in every universe. But virtue requires a vessel - a Champion. A mortal who embodies it so completely that they transcend mortality itself. The theologians call this an Act Transcendent - a deed of such profound righteousness that it transforms the doer into something greater. A Champion becomes a Lord or Lady of Virtue. Perhaps the same Lord, wearing different faces across all the worlds. Perhaps many. The priests argue. The Lords and Ladies are not truly immortal. They can Fall - manifest in the mortal world, walk among us, and be slain. This is the price of presence. In Larion, the Lady of Mercy Fell once, sacrificing herself to shield her followers from a great evil. But virtue cannot be destroyed while mortals live to embody it. She Rose again, renewed by her own sacrifice. Vice arises the same way. Where virtue exists, so must its shadow. The Lords and Ladies of Vice were once mortal too. Through an Act Notorious - a deed of such profound evil that it damns the doer forever - they become vessels for hatred, cruelty, despair. When Vice Falls, whatever destroyed it commits an Act Notorious by the very act of destruction. The slayer becomes the slain.

The Lady of Mercy.text: The Lady of Mercy (Goddess; The Risen, The Selfless): She is the virtue of compassion made divine. Her followers tend the sick, shelter the homeless, and comfort the dying. Her temples are hospitals and sanctuaries. No one is turned away.

The Lord of Justice.text: The Lord of Justice (God; The Balanced Scale): He is the virtue of fair judgment. Truth matters to him. Oaths matter. Consequences matter. His temples serve as courts throughout Larion. His priests hear disputes, weigh evidence, render verdicts. To lie in his temple is to damn yourself.

The Lord of Valor.text: The Lord of Valor (God; The Shieldbearer, The Unflinching). He is the virtue of courage. Not recklessness - courage. The will to face evil when you are afraid. To stand when others flee. To put yourself between darkness and those who cannot fight it. His temples are training halls. His priests teach combat, but also teach when to fight and when to withdraw.

The Lady of Hope.text: The Lady of Hope (Goddess; The Dawnbringer, The Undimmed). She is the virtue of perseverance. The light that survives the longest night. Her domain is not naive optimism but stubborn refusal to surrender to despair. When all seems lost, she is what remains. Her temples are often small - a single candle burning in a window, a shrine at a crossroads.

The Lord of Order.text: The Lord of Order (God; The Architect, The Foundation). He is the virtue of civilization. Laws, contracts, institutions, the agreements that let strangers live together without bloodshed.

The Lady of the Hearth.text: The Lady of the Hearth (Goddess; The Welcomer, The Keeper of Doors). She is the virtue of home. Not a building but the feeling - belonging, warmth, the place where you are known and wanted. Her domain is family, community, hospitality, the bonds between people who share roof and bread. Her priests bless marriages, name children, consecrate new homes. Her temples are gathering halls, often with a great hearth at the center. Travelers are always welcome. A meal is always offered.

The Lady of Wisdom.text: The Lady of Wisdom (Goddess; The Clear-Eyed, The Patient). She is the virtue of understanding, of the judgment to use knowledge well. Her domain is learning, teaching, counsel, and the long view beyond immediate passions. Her priests maintain schools and offer counsel to any who ask. Her temples are libraries and academies.

The Lady of Blood.text: The Lady of Blood (Archfiend; the First Vampire). A Lady of Vice, one of the archfiends bound by the Pact and unable to manifest directly in Larion. She is the progenitor of all vampires. In the time of the Elven Schism, when sun elves hunted moon elves toward extinction, a great moon elf leader faced a choice: sacrifice herself for her people, or sacrifice her people for herself. She chose herself. In a ritual of profound evil, she consumed her own followers to fuel her transformation into something beyond mortal. She became the Lady of Blood, and the 'gift' she gave her surviving followers was vampirism: her curse, shared. Her blessing is required to create new vampire lords. Without it, vampires can only sire lesser thralls. The prince-magisters of the Moon Kingdoms rule in her shadow, paying the blood price their ancestor established. She is elsewhere now, constrained by the Pact, but her children remember.

The Lord of the Open Hand.text: The Lord of the Open Hand (God; the Companion, the Wayfarer). He is the virtue of fellowship — bonds freely chosen between equals. Not obligation, not fealty, not blood — choice. His domain is friendship, mutual aid, and the hand extended without expectation. His priests bless adventuring parties and witness oaths of companionship. His shrines are waystations, campfires, and gathering places where no one claims the high seat. Among the wood elves of the Everwood, he is remembered as He Who Walks Beside — in Elvish, Talvaren — a legendary figure who guided those who refused to fight in the Kindred War to safety, then gave his life defending them. His final words, 'May you walk in beauty,' are still spoken by wood elves to mark births, arrivals, and deaths. Whether Talvaren was the Lord of the Open Hand in mortal form, or a mortal who became the Lord through his sacrifice, is a question his priests answer differently depending on where you ask.

The Pact of All Planes.text: The Pact of All Planes: An ancient binding that keeps extraplanar entities from freely entering Larion. Created long ago by Suleyvan the Sealer—sometimes called the First Warlock—the Pact is an agreement between mortals and neutral extraplanar powers. These powers agreed to hold the boundaries against other more hostile exemplars of their kind. In exchange, they gained the right to form pacts with willing mortals. Every warlock bargain since is an echo of that original agreement, a thread in the binding that maintains the barrier. The Pact holds, mostly. Breaches still occur through summoning, thin places, and loopholes.

The Black Gate.text: A mountain fortress in the Moon Kingdoms where new vampire lords are created. The Black Gate is a portal to the realm of the Lady of Blood; aspirants must enter, defeat a Fiend of the Blood, and drink its essence to receive the vampire's gift.

Life-stone.text: Life-stone: A rare golden mineral mined from the deepest places in the earth. When refined and consumed, it extends the user's lifespan and sharpens their vitality. Its use is illegal throughout Larion — and widespread among those rich enough to afford it. Nobles, mages, and the powerful treat it as an open secret: everyone knows, nobody admits. Dark elf miners control the primary supply, and the smuggling networks that move life-stone to the surface are among the most profitable and dangerous enterprises in the known world. More precious than gold by weight and valuable enough to kill over in quantities small enough to hide in a pocket.

Doom of Orichala.text: The Doom of Orichala: The Republic of Orichala was the proudest of the Spice Isles Free States. They claimed descent from the Gold Empire itself and swore to restore its glory, mounting expeditions to sunken ruins, recovered artifacts and mastered waterbreathing magics. Their vaults held treasures no other city could match. They said they were close to finding the lost Gold Empire capital itself. Then came the Doom. In a single night, the island tilted and sank, the sea rising and swallowing all the island but one small promontory, which still juts above the waves. No warning, no storm, no explanation. No bodies. No survivors. Just empty, drowned streets and the things that moved into them after. The ruins are still there, half-exposed at low tide, infested with drowners and worse.

Pelagic Alliance.text: The Pelagic Alliance: A confederation of seafolk kingdoms that rules the waters beneath the Spice Isles. Sea-elves lead its councils. Mersene serve as traders and go-betweens, equally at home above and below the waves. Frogkin are craftsmen and laborers, working coral and pearl and pressure-forged metals. Selachii—shark-blooded and sharp-toothed—enforce the Alliance's laws and guard its borders. The Alliance has fought many wars against the surface folk of the Free States. The current truce has held longer than most, maintained through Coral Cavern and careful diplomacy, but old grievances run deep on both sides.

The Elven Schism.text: The Elven Schism: In the First Days, all elves were one people. Then came the Long Dark—a time of endless night and winter. The elves split over how to survive it. Some embraced the darkness: these became moon elves. Others rebelled against it: these became sun elves. Still others refused to take sides: these became the wood elves. When the darkness finally lifted, war followed. The elven homeland was lost to all of them—some say destroyed in the war, others cursed and blighted by fratricide. Tthe sun elves settled in the deserts of the Bright Kingdoms, the moon elves in the shadowed mountains to the east, and wood elves in the Everwood. The enmity between sun and moon has never healed. The wood elves mourn what was lost and keep no side.

Mirage.text: Mirage: The hidden desert city of the sun elves. Mirage appears in the deep desert of the Bright Kingdoms where it wills—a shimmering vision of white spires and golden domes.  Mirage is hidden by light itself, so bright with sun magic that outsiders perceive only heat-shimmer where a city stands. Those the sun elves wish to find are found. Those they do not wish to see walk past without knowing what they missed.

Sun Elves.text: Sun elves: Tall and lean with bronze or copper skin. Hair ranges from white-gold to deep copper to flame-red. Their eyes are pale—gold, amber, or white. Most practice some form of sun magic. They are a proud, martial people concentrated in the hidden city of Mirage and the Bright Kingdoms, organized into knight-orders and ruled by temples of the sun. They consider themselves righteous guardians against darkness and corruption and the Moon Kingdoms a corruption that should be scoured from the world. Sun elf culture emphasizes purity, discipline, and the rejection of darkness in all forms.

The Vale Sable.text: The Vale Sable: A quarantined valley in the Moon Kingdoms, sealed by collapsed stone and guarded by Silver Company mercenaries. Once the domain of House Sable, an ancient bloodline known for blood magic, the vale fell to feral vampires generations ago. The great houses have not moved to cleanse it, though they could. Rumors speak of the Sable library still intact within Keep Sable—the greatest repository of blood magic research in Larion. Other rumors mention someone important trapped inside when the valley fell. The Silver Company maintains a cordon at the sealed pass and kills anyone who emerges without permission.

Temple of the Black Flame.text: Temple of the Black Flame: A fortress-temple high in the Moon Kingdoms where demonborn priests guard against infernal incursion. The temple honors the Bearer of the Black Flame, the hero who led the demonborn to sanctuary. The flame-keepers study infernal lore, maintain protective wards, and help those afflicted by curses, possessions, or demonic contracts. Demonborn from across Larion make pilgrimages here to master their heritage.

The War in Hell.text: The War in Hell: An eternal conflict raging in the infernal realms, fought between demonic factions over matters mortals struggle to comprehend. The war has no beginning anyone remembers and no end anyone predicts. Occasionally it spills into Larion. The demonborn are descendants of refugees who fled this conflict generations ago.

Gods-tomb.text: Gods-tomb (The Missing Mountain; The Tomb of the First One): Gods-tomb is an ancient burial ground in the Highlands where the titans of a former age were laid to rest beneath open sky. The remains there dwarf any living giant — skulls the size of houses, femurs like fallen pillars, ribcages arching over the ground like the frames of great halls. Giant clans honored the site as sacred ground. The greatest of the dead was called the First One: the firstborn of all giantkind, ancestor of every bloodline. Its skeleton was so vast that it had become part of the mountain — its spine a ridge, its ribcage a valley, its skull a hill. Now, it is gone. A body-shaped rift gapes where the First One had rested. The disappearance has thrown giantkind into turmoil and strained their already fragile peace with the smallfolk. The tunnels exposed beneath where the First One lay have drawn scavengers, mercenaries, and tomb-raiders in numbers — easy prey for giant war parties, whose killing only fuels their rage.

Orc Diaspora.text: Orc diaspora: The orc clans once held the grasslands and hill-country now claimed by the Greater Goblinate. When the Dragon Queen unified the goblin tribes, her armies drove the clans from their ancestral territory. The displaced clans scattered across Larion. Orc culture turns on the keeping of oaths — spoken vows witnessed by a clan elder and affirmed before the gods. The oath-witness names their lineage, declares what they have heard, and the oath is sealed. It is the most sacred act in orc life. Everything else is noise, argument, and cheerful insult. Orcs show affection through mockery and find the politeness of other cultures baffling and dishonest. They eat communally, sharing platters with their hands, and consider eating alone bizarre. They are known for blunt honesty, fierce loyalty, and long memories. Most diaspora orc communities gather around an elder who serves as oath-witness — the living memory of every promise her people have made.

The Burning of the Kings.text: The Burning of the Kings (Kingstor, Wild Grass Sea): The centerpiece of every Great Moot of the beastfolk at Kingstor. In the grass sea, a 'king' is a colossal effigy built to burn — the word stripped of authority, stuffed with straw, and set on fire. Anyone can build a king. Wolfkin packs build kings of bandit chiefs. Boarfolk communes build kings of Syndicate tax assessors. Temporary alliances bend together to build enormous kings representing outside powers who've threatened beastfolk autonomy — these draw the biggest crowds. Kings can be political, personal, or just funny. The only rule is it has to burn well. The Burning itself is the wildest night in Larion — drums, bonfires, dancing, drinking, feats of strength, old rivals clasping arms while enormous effigies collapse in showers of sparks. Non-beastfolk at Kingstor during the Burning are welcome to join. They are also welcome to notice that kings from their home regions burn just as brightly as any other.

The Thieves' Guild.text: The Thieves' Guild - The Trade, Hands, The Table: The Thieves' Guild is a den of vipers that don't bite each other — much. An informal alliance of criminal bands called Hands, found wherever there's wealth and secrets worth taking. The Guild is 'governed' by the Table — a secret council of the most powerful, influential, and ruthless leaders in the trade. Most Hands never earn a seat at the Table. Most cower like dogs at the foot of it. The Guild takes different forms in different places, but it always makes its peace with the powers that be. Don't steal from the ruling clan. Get secrets for them. Spy on their enemies. Solve the problems the law can't solve and keep the underworld quiet enough that decent folk can sleep. In return, the authorities look the other way — though they always hold out their hands for a cut. Because crime is inevitable, and crime without anyone minding the shop is worse for everyone. Some Hands are professionals, proud of their craft. Some are thugs. Most protect their own. The Guild protects Hands that serve the Table, punishes those who break its rules or upset its betters, and settles grudges before they become wars. Outsiders call us thieves. We call ourselves the Trade.

The Table.text: The Table: The Low Table is one name for the loose alliance of criminal bands that 'runs' the Thieves' Guild - the closest thing that den of vipers has to a parliament. It has as many names as it has enemies. The Knives in the Dark. The Court of Bastards. The Gutter Council. The Poor Man's Justice. Call it what you like — if you're in the Trade, it's the only court that matters. Representatives from the strongest criminal crews and bands, known as Hands, meet at the Table to divide territory, negotiate jobs, air grudges, and settle disputes before they spill blood in the streets. The Table doesn't command — it mediates, threatens, and occasionally makes bloody examples. A Hand that ignores the Table's rulings gets declared outlaw, which means every other Hand in the city can take what's theirs with the Table's blessing. That usually sorts things out fast. The Table is chaired by whoever is strong enough that nobody at it dares say otherwise. No election, no appointment — you sit at the head when the room lets you. That changes with fortune, and fortune changes often. Meetings happen where they happen, and never the same place twice running. If you're summoned to the Table, you go. You bring no weapons but your wits, and you better be prepared to pick sides; there is no neutrality in a den of vipers.

Hands.text: A Hand is an underworld crew of thieves. Your people. The ones who watch your back when the job goes wrong and split honest when it goes right. Every Hand has a leader, a territory, a way of doing things, and a name — and the name had better mean something, or you're nobody. Some Hands are old blood, three generations deep, territory so well-carved nobody remembers the last fool who tried to take it. Some are fresh — four or five clever nobodies who banded together and talked or fought their way into the Table's good graces. A Hand in good standing pays its cut to the Table and answers when called. Beyond that, your business is your business. Some Hands work clean — pride in the craft, no unnecessary blood, a code they keep because they want to, not because anyone makes them. Some are dogs. Most are somewhere between. Burglary, smuggling, forgery, the quiet knife, information, or whatever the night offers — every Hand finds its niche or it finds a grave. You don't join the Thieves' Guild. You join a Hand, or you make one. Your Hand is your crew, your colours, your family, your name in the streets. When someone asks who you run with, that answer is the only thing standing between you and whatever's coming.

The Laughing God.text: The Laughing God (Divinity) - Jack o' Cups, Sweet-Tongue Jack, Saint of Sin: Every tavern in Larion knows the story, and none of them tell it the same way. A mortal man — a beggar, a drunk, a fool, depending on who's talking — made a wager with the gods. What the wager was depends on the version. Some say he bet he could steal the sun and replace it before dawn. Some say he challenged Death to a drinking contest and won because he was too drunk to die. Some say he simply talked his way into the divine court, sat down uninvited, and refused to leave until they made him one of them. The details change. The ending never does: he won, and the gods — the Lords and Ladies of Virtue and all the rest of them — honored the bet because a wager is a wager, even among the divine. And Jack laughed, and hasn't stopped laughing since. He is the god of luck, mischief, drink, dares, sudden reversals of fortune and fools. Holy to thieves, beggars, wastrels, gamblers, infatuated lovers, and anyone about to do something they probably shouldn't. He has no priests, but storytellers, drunkards, jesters, pranksters and troublemakers pray to him in their own way. He has no temples but tavern corners and anywhere someone raises a glass before doing something stupid and brave. His holy days are the Feast of Fools — three days when the social order inverts, rules dissolve, and the whole world remembers that a beggar once became a god, so anything is possible. Pray to Jack and he might answer, but never the way you wanted. He helps sideways. He gives you luck, but luck has teeth. His sacred teaching, if you can call it that, fits on the back of a beer mat: the world is mad, you're going to die, so laugh, drink, dare, and see what happens. His symbol is the overflowing cup. His colors are gold and motley.

Drinking Games of Larion.text: Drinking games are common in taverns across Larion. The three most popular: The Slap — Two players stand on opposite sides of a table. In each round, both players drink. Then, one player slaps the other, open-handed, on the cheek; no fists, scratching, or targeting of eyes or nose. The player receiving the slap holds one hand on the table and must not drop their hand from its surface. Flinch, lift your hand, or pull it away and you lose. Then the other player gets their turn to slap their opponent, and the game contains with next round of drinking and slapping. The Choke — players take turns drinking (typically ale, and in any case something that takes a bit of time to gulp down) while their opponent insults them. The drinker must drain their cup without choking, spitting, or snorting. The insulter's job is to time their worst material for mid-swallow and force their opponent to sputter, spit, or choke in anger or laughter. Rounds alternate. Rust — the tavern wrecker. Two cups per player, one water, one drink. Players drink with their backs turned and may choose either cup. Their opponent calls 'Rust!' to accuse them of drinking water — if right, the coward drinks three penalties. If wrong, the caller drinks three. After each round, the winner names a physical challenge — hop on one foot, balance something on your nose, walk along a bench, catch a thrown object. Both players attempt it. Whoever fails loses the game.

Magic in Larion.text:
Magic in Larion: Though definitions of magic vary by tradition, the most common is that magic is the living power of the world, present in all things. Spellcasting is drawing that power through one's body and will to reshape the world.

Casters are limited in their capacity — what mages call their vessel. A novice vessel is small and fragile. It can hold enough power for simple things: lighting a flame, mending a small wound, moving a cup without touching it. A master's vessel is vast and resilient, able to hold tremendous power and direct it with fine control.

Three things determine how hard a spell is and thus how large a vessel it requires. Scale — how much you are asking reality to change. Lighting a candle is barely a nudge. Calling down a firestorm is trying to move the world a long way. Complexity — how much precision the effect demands. A raw blast of fire is simple. Shaping fire into a barrier that lets allies pass through but burns enemies requires deep understanding and fine control. Opposition — how hard the target or the world pushes back. Fire magic comes easily in dry air and struggles in a downpour. Necromancy thrives in places of death and falters on consecrated ground. A strong-willed enemy resists mental magic better than a weak-minded one.

When a mage channels too much, their vessel empties. They feel it as exhaustion — heavy limbs, clouded thoughts, a hollow ache behind the eyes. Recovery comes through rest, meditation, or sources of concentrated power like potions.

Different traditions channel in different ways, but these are traditions, not separate powers — a fire mage and a divine caster are both drawing from the same current, just through different training and understanding.

Magic is respected in Larion, sometimes feared, but never truly rare. Most towns have at least a hedge mage or healer. Cities have guilds, academies, professional enchanters. Alchemical lamps light wealthy streets. Magical healing saves lives that would otherwise be lost. But magic cannot solve everything — it is limited by the caster's vessel, by the difficulty of the effect, and by the simple fact that channelling power through a mortal body is hard, tiring work. The greatest mages in the world are extraordinary precisely because what they do is so difficult.

The Primordial Plane of Ur.text: The Primordial Plane of Ur (Cosmology): Regarded as myth or legend by many, the Primordial Plane of Ur is said to be a progenitor realm of raw magic and chaos, older than mortal civilization and untouched by its concepts. Little is known about Ur, but all sources agree that there is no law there, no morality, no pity. Power is the only currency. The High Princes of Ur are beings that most prefer not to name out of superstition — demons or gods of such might that their whims reshape the world around them — a prince's cruelty becomes the landscape's cruelty, a prince's hunger makes everything prey. Nothing built in Ur endures, because the next prince's passing mood may unmake it. The demonborn who now live in Larion trace their origins to this place, though most have never seen it and know it only through legend. Orthodox opinion holds that Pact of Suleyman the Sealer bars the High Princes from manifesting in Larion, just as it bars the gods and other great powers of the outer planes.

The Excession of Ur.text: The Excession of Ur (Cosmology): Sometimes also called the Translation of Ur, the Excession of Ur is the mythic event by which the jungle now known as Ntar Vanna came to exist in Larion. The demonborn tell that a High Prince of Ur called Aldorath turned against his own kind, slaying his brother-prince to break a dominion free from the Primordial Plane and translate it bodily into the mortal world. The translated territory became the Ntar Vanna: jungle of staggering scale grown from Ur-soil, saturated with primordial magic. Whether Aldorath acted from compassion, ambition, or some motive beyond mortal understanding depends on who tells the story. The Excession is the founding event of demonborn civilization in Larion, and to this day no scholar has offered a convincing alternative explanation for the jungle's alien soil, its unnatural vitality, or the ruins of a non-human empire buried in its depths.

Aldorath the Excessor.text: High Prince of Ur Aldorath the Excessor (Cosmology): Also known as Aldorath the Apostate or Aldorath the Kinslayer. The legend and cult of this obscure, turbulent and divisive figure are little-known outside of the southern jungles of Ntar Vanna, though demonborn throughout Larion can invariably give the essential details of his life and deeds. Aldorath is said to have been a High Prince of Ur who led the Excession, relocating his principality out of the Primordial Plane of Ur and into Larion. All sources agree that he slew his own brother to accomplish it — whether as sacrifice, in battle, or for reasons unknown. The demonborn revere him as their liberator, the prince who freed them from bondage to the chaotic caprices of the High Princes and delivered them to Larion, there to decide their own destiny. Old inscriptions in the ruined cities of Ntar Vanna name him as sovereign, lawgiver, and builder. What became of him is a subject of much debate and even sectarian strife. Did whatever catastrophe claimed the demonborn cities claim him as well? Did he bring ruin upon his own works, as he once destroyed his brother? Is he sleeping in the deep jungle, diminished but alive? Every generation of demonborn must seek their own answers.

Demonborn.text: The demonborn are a people of otherworldly heritage whose distant ancestors are commonly believed to have hailed from the Primordial Plane of Ur. They are marked by horns, tails, unusual coloring, and eyes that glow faintly in darkness — appearances that unsettle those unfamiliar with them. The greatest concentration of demonborn in Larion live in the vast southern jungles of Ntar Vanna, though communities exist in the Spice Isles, the Bright Kingdoms, and wherever trade and opportunity draw them. Demonborn temperament runs hot — passionate, direct, quick to anger, and fiercely loyal. Their culture values hospitality, community, and deliberate self-mastery. This last is not abstract virtue. The demonborn regard themselves as self-imposed exiles from Ur, a place without restraint, and the discipline required to live among mortal peoples is something each generation must learn anew.

The Children of Ur.text: Children of Ur (cult): The Children of Ur are a proscribed demonborn cult, outlawed in the jungles of Ntar Vanna and hunted wherever they surface in Larion. They hold that the Excession of Ur — the cosmological act that brought the demonborn from the Primordial Plane into Larion — was not a self-imposed exile but the beachhead of a conquest. The Children teach that their ancient leader, Aldorath the Excessor, was a conqueror, not a refugee, and that his legacy has been deliberately corrupted to keep the demonborn docile. They seek to complete the Translation of Ur that he began but was unable to conclude: to merge the Primordial Plane fully into Larion, flooding the mortal world with Ur's raw power and restoring the demonborn to their primordial nature. Virtue, restraint, and compassion are chains forged to prevent this ascension. To mainstream demonborn, the Children are dangerous apostates who threaten everything the Excession was meant to achieve: freedom from the High Princes, the opportunity for each to decide his own destiny, and a new beginning in a new world. To outsiders, they confirm every suspicion about what the demonborn truly are. Children of Ur cells operate in secret wherever demonborn communities exist, recruiting those who feel the pull of their heritage most keenly.

Chameleon-kin.text: Chameleon-kin (species): The chameleon-kin, who call themselves the Mounari, are a reclusive amphibious people native to the jungles of Ntar Vanna. They are slight of build, with independently moving eyes, prehensile tails, and skin that shifts color and pattern instinctively to match their surroundings — a natural camouflage so effective that a still Mounari is nearly invisible. They are extraordinary climbers, capable of scaling any surface and moving through canopy, cliff, and water with equal ease. The Mounari are a patient, observant people who value stillness, discipline, and the art of passing unseen. Their masters teach techniques of movement, breath, and concealment that other species can learn, though never to the same degree — Mounari schools in the deep jungle draw students from across Larion seeking training in stealth and infiltration. Outside Ntar Vanna, chameleon-kin are rare and often regarded with unease. A people who can disappear at will are difficult to trust, and the Mounari seem largely unbothered by this.

Monkeyfolk.text: The monkeyfolk of the southern jungles of Ntar Vanna call themselves the High Vanarin, and they will correct you if you forget. They are a quick, agile, canopy-dwelling people — acrobatic, gregarious, and possessed of an unshakeable conviction in their own importance. The High Vanarin are the self-appointed lords of the upper canopy, and anything that enters their domain is considered a gift freely given. This includes cargo, personal belongings, and occasionally people. Despite this, the Vanarin are not petty thieves — they are, by their own account, heroes, sages, and protectors of the jungle. The maddening thing is that this is often true. Vanarin warriors are fearless and fast, their elders wise in jungle lore. Their loyalty, once earned, is ferocious. Playfully pompous, impossibly vain, generous to a fault with things they consider theirs — which is everything — and excellent company provided you don't leave anything valuable unattended.

Thrallmages of Falmastra.text: Thrallmages of Falmastra: Mind-controlling psionicists operating from an island city-state in the Spice Isles. Can read thoughts, speak mind-to-mind, and puppet others like living dolls. To fully bind a thrall, they need a piece of the target — a hair, a drop of blood, a worn possession — and the target's true name. Without both, no binding holds. Thrallmages hire out as spies and killers for enormous fees. Their retribution doctrine, the Hundredfold, promises a hundred deaths for any one of their number killed. Settlements near Falmastra pay tribute and are left alone. The thrallmages are few — perhaps a few dozen — but one thrallmage with the right thralls in the right places can topple a merchant house or unseat a prince. They are feared the way plagues are feared: an invisible thing that can take hold of anyone.

The Exclave.text: The Exclave: The ritual of succession of the office of the Dukedom in the great Spice Isles city-state of Tevantia. When a Duke dies, the Electors — the city's great merchants, guild leaders, and notables — seal themselves on the Isle of Union to choose his successor by majority vote. No one leaves until a Duke is chosen. Leaving before the Election is complete is treason. Each Elector may bring a Second — a trusted agent who acts on their behalf: carrying messages, brokering alliances, gathering intelligence, fighting duels, and doing what an Elector cannot be seen doing. The dead Duke's body is carried across the strait on a ceremonial barge lit by a thousand alchemical lanterns. He lies in state in the Chamber of Light at the base of the Burning Tower, attended by those lanterns until a successor is chosen. The tower's beacon burns for the duration. Tevantia keeps a twilight vigil — no lights after sunset, no music, no revelry — until the beacon is extinguished and the new Duke returns the light to the city on the same barge. The Exclave runs on an unspoken paradox every Tevantine knows: only the Duke can investigate crimes against the person of a current or former Duke; as the Tevantine proverb holds, 'The Duke's justice lives and dies with the Duke.' Some Exclaves have been short and civil. Others have not.

The Burning of Tevantia.text: The Burning of Tevantia: three centuries ago, Duke Aldano III of Tevantia died without a clear successor. Three great houses claimed the seat. Their private armies clashed in the streets and the city burned. The Holy Solarian Exarcate — the provincial arm of the Solarian Dominion, which has long sought to bring the Free States under imperial rule — had fomented the disorder, and a Solarian legion sailed on Tevantia while the city tore itself apart. Facing annihilation, the three pretenders met on the Isle of Union, a religious sanctuary in the strait. There they set aside their claims and elected a Duke to lead the city's defense. Tevantia survived. The Black Palace — the ruined seat of the house that started the fighting — still stands in the city, never rebuilt, never torn down.

The Dukedom of Tevantia.text: The Duke of Tevantia is the First Peer — not a king, but the foremost among the great houses. Elected for life by the Electors, the Duke mediates between the feuding merchant families, upholds the Compact between the Free States, and commands Tevantia's defense in times of crisis. A Duke may withdraw from the seat voluntarily, though few have. The role carries enormous prestige and influence but limited direct power — the great houses would never suffer a monarch, and a Duke who overreaches finds his allies evaporating and his Electors whispering about precedent. The wisest Dukes govern by consensus, play the houses against each other just enough to keep the peace, and save their authority for when it matters.

The Solarian Dominion.text: The Solarian Dominion: a vast empire beyond the seas, ruled by a god-emperor whose authority is absolute and — to the Solarians — divinely ordained. The Dominion believes that the natural state of civilization is unity under a single sovereign, and that the patchwork of kingdoms, republics, and free cities that make up Larion is a symptom of disorder waiting to be cured. All peoples are welcome as citizens, provided they bend the knee. Those who do find the Dominion's roads well-built, its legions disciplined, its justice consistent, and its taxes non-negotiable. Those who refuse find the legions disciplined in other ways. The Dominion does not conquer in haste. It builds, fortifies, and waits. Its presence in Larion — the Holy Solarian Exarcate in Great Lagoon — has held the same borders for over a century.

The Holy Solarian Exarcate.text: The Holy Solarian Exarcate: the Solarian Dominion's provincial territory in Larion, encompassing the Great Lagoon and its surrounding coast. Governed by the Most Exalted Exarch, who answers only to the god-emperor and commands a standing legion, a fleet, and the largest fortified harbor in eastern Larion. The Exarcate is a marvel of engineering — causeways cross the lagoon, sea-walls hold back the tides, and fortified towns rise from land that was open swamp a century ago. Solarian citizens live under Dominion law: orderly, regimented, and notably safe compared to the rest of Larion. The native peoples of the lagoon — lizardfolk, frogkin, and human marsh communities — exist in various states of accommodation, from full citizenship to uneasy coexistence to quiet resistance in the deep swamps the Dominion has not yet drained.

aspected crafting materials.text: Aspected Materials: Some rare crafting materials carry inherent damage affinities. Silver, moonstone, and sun-gold are radiant-aspected. Stormsteel and thunderglass are shock-aspected. Emberglass and red jade are fire-aspected. Frostjade and rimebone are frost-aspected. Obsidian, voidglass, and black iron are shadow-aspected. Witchwood and aether crystal are arcane-aspected. Dream coral and thoughtstone are psychic-aspected. Venomwood and green glass are poison-aspected. Caustic bronze and alchemist glass are acid-aspected.

Silver.text: Silver: A radiant-aspected metal associated with purity, oaths, moonlight, and sanctified craft. Silver weapons harm shadow-bound creatures, undead, fiends, and lycanthropes because the metal carries a clean light through the wound.

Moonstone.text: Moonstone: A pale radiant-aspected gemstone used in temple blades, ritual jewelry, and warding charms. Moonstone shines strongest against undead, demons, curses, and creatures sustained by shadow.

Sun-Gold.text: Sun-gold: A rare radiant-aspected gold that holds warmth and light even in darkness. It is prized for holy relics, royal regalia, and weapons meant to burn away undeath or corruption.

Stormsteel.text: Stormsteel: A shock-aspected metal that hums faintly before storms and carries lightning through a strike. Weapons made from stormsteel deal shock damage regardless of blade shape.

Thunderglass.text: Thunderglass: A shock-aspected glassy mineral formed where lightning repeatedly strikes sand, towers, or mountain glass. It stores violent charge and releases it through wands, spearheads, and ritual foci.

Emberglass.text: Emberglass: A fire-aspected volcanic glass that glows like banked coals under pressure. Emberglass weapons and foci burn through flesh, armor gaps, and cold-natured creatures.

Red Jade.text: Red jade: A fire-aspected stone used in dueling blades, furnace shrines, and noble signets. It is less explosive than emberglass but holds a steady inner heat that turns strikes into fire damage.

Frostjade.text: Frostjade: A frost-aspected pale green stone cold enough to mist in warm air. Frostjade weapons bite with supernatural cold and are prized against fire creatures, fever spirits, and living magma.

Rimebone.text: Rimebone: A frost-aspected bone taken from ancient beasts preserved in glacier tombs or winter barrows. It drinks heat from wounds and makes even blunt weapons strike with killing cold.

Obsidian.text: Obsidian: A shadow-aspected volcanic glass used for knives, mirrors, and sacrificial tools. It cuts cleanly but leaves darkness in the wound, making it dangerous against radiant or living-light creatures.

Voidglass.text: Voidglass: A shadow-aspected black glass that reflects stars even underground. It is used in assassin blades, warlock foci, and relics meant to wound spirits, light-bearers, and the soul.

Black Iron.text: Black iron: A shadow-aspected iron alloy smelted with grave ash, night salts, or underworld coal. It is heavy, ugly, and feared because its wounds feel cold and lightless.

Witchwood.text: Witchwood: An arcane-aspected wood cut from trees grown around old spells, fairy rings, or buried charms. It is ideal for wands, staves, bows, and ritual tools that channel structured magic.

Aether Crystal.text: Aether crystal: An arcane-aspected crystal that refracts spellwork into visible threads. It is used in wizard foci, mage blades, lenses, and devices that strike through magical force rather than mundane impact.

Dream Coral.text: Dream coral: A psychic-aspected coral harvested from enchanted reefs and sleep-haunted waters. It resonates with memory, fear, desire, and hallucination, making it valuable for charms and mind-affecting weapons.

Thoughtstone.text: Thoughtstone: A psychic-aspected mineral that warms when held by a thinking creature. Weapons or foci set with thoughtstone wound concentration, courage, and perception as much as flesh.

Venomwood.text: Venomwood: A poison-aspected wood from toxic groves and serpent-haunted marshes. It seeps venom into arrows, knives, and staves, making even clean cuts sicken the target.

Green Glass.text: Green glass: A poison-aspected alchemical glass stained by distilled toxins. It is brittle but useful in darts, ritual shards, and foci that deliver venomous magic.

Caustic Bronze.text: Caustic bronze: An acid-aspected bronze alloy that tarnishes into corrosive green-black patterns. Its wounds sting, pit armor, and eat at scales, shell, and stone.

Alchemist Glass.text: Alchemist glass: An acid-aspected glass made to hold, focus, and release corrosive reagents. It is common in experimental foci, trap components, and weapons meant to dissolve armor or hardened hides.

Weapon Materials and Monster Weaknesses.text: Weapon Materials and Monster Weaknesses: Radiant force harms undead, fiends, lycanthropes, shadow creatures, and aberrations; silver, moonstone, and sun-gold are prized for that reason. Shadow force harms blessed casters and celestials, but shadow-bound and undead creatures often resist it. Fire kills plants, fungi, venomous creatures, trolls, and frost beings, while fire elementals and fiends shrug it off. Shock disrupts constructs and machines. Acid eats stone, armor, shell, and golem-work, making caustic bronze and alchemist glass useful against stone constructs and hardened beasts. Poison is useless or weak against undead, constructs, oozes, and venomous creatures, but dangerous to ordinary living bodies. Psychic force is resisted by constructs, plants, oozes, and mind-things; it is best used against ordinary thinking creatures, not things without normal minds. Piercing is poor against skeletons, plants, constructs, oozes, and spirits; slashing is poor against oozes, spirits, stone, and hard shell; impact is useful against armor, brittle bodies, mages, and hard carapaces, but trained warriors and giants often absorb blunt punishment. Mundane humanoids and beasts usually have no special damage interactions.
