Experts explain these truths through material and anatomy: what has no blood laughs at poison, what has no organs cares little for arrows, what has no solid body ignores blades, and what is made of flame does not fear fire.

Aspected Crafting Doctrine.text: Aspected Crafting Doctrine: Weapon material determines its damage affinity more strongly than shape. Stormsteel and thunderglass weapons deal shock and are used against constructs and machines. Caustic bronze and alchemist glass deal acid and are used against stone constructs, armor, shell, and hardened scales. Silver, moonstone, and sun-gold deal radiant and are used against undead, fiends, lycanthropes, shadow creatures, and aberrations. Emberglass and red jade deal fire and are used against plants, fungi, trolls, venomous creatures, and frost beings. Frostjade and rimebone deal frost and are used against fire elementals, magma creatures, and oozes. Obsidian, voidglass, and black iron deal shadow and are used against celestials and blessed enemies. Witchwood and aether crystal deal arcane and are used for spellcraft, wards, and fighting magical beings. Dream coral and thoughtstone deal psychic. Venomwood and green glass deal poison and are used against living bodies, but not undead, constructs, oozes, or venomous beasts.

Halfling Alchemy.text: Halfling Alchemy: The domestic magical tradition of Everwood halflings, practiced through cooking, baking, brewing, and small charm-work rather than wand or grimoire. A halfling alchemist binds intent into food and craft — a truffle-paste charm that lets a hog answer to its name across a wood, a pie that warms a traveler from the inside for a full day's walk, a cordial that settles a fever overnight. The work runs in families and is taught grandmother to grandchild. It is rarely flashy and never battlefield-grade, but it is woven so deep into halfling life that most outsiders mistake it for plain good cooking.

Moonbloom Truffles.text: Moonbloom Truffles: A pale, faintly luminous truffle that grows only in the old oak-and-chestnut woods of the Everwood near the halfling village of Hillhome, fruiting under the new moon. Halfling truffle-hogs root them out in autumn and spring, and halfling cooks turn them into the pies, pastes, and cordials Hillhome. Moonbloom carries a magical charge — it anchors halfling alchemy, holds charms longer than mundane ingredients. A single good truffle is worth a week's wages in coin and a great deal more in trade.

The Newmoon Fair.text: The Newmoon Fair: A monthly halfling festival held in Everwood halfling villages on the night of the new moon. Each village hosts its own, with pavilions, ribbons, and contest rings on the village green — pies, ales, archery, sheep-shearing, singing, and whatever else the local families can argue about. The Fair is where rivalries are settled and reputations made; the pie-crown in particular is held with fierce pride and lost with fierce shame. Outsiders are welcome and usually fed past the point of comfort.

Tinytrolls.text: Tinytrolls: Small, bog-green, knee-high cousins of the great trolls of the north, found in brambly hollows and overgrown woods across Larion's settled regions. They speak a coarse trade-pidgin, hoard whatever shiny things they can steal, and squabble in shrieking little bands. A tinytroll alone is a nuisance; a nest of them can ruin a forager's day and occasionally a forager. They regenerate weakly, like their larger kin, but a torch or a determined dog handles them. Hedge-witches sometimes bargain with them for trinkets; most farmers just chase them off with a rake.

The Wardens of the Everwood.text: The Wardens of the Everwood: The elven forest-keepers who hold the deep places of the Everwood and walk the line between the deep wood and the cleared lands of its neighbors. Cloaked in greens and greys, armed with longbow and yew-staff, they speak rarely and are seen rarer. The Wardens permit halfling foragers, human woodsmen, and dwarven charcoal-burners to work the outer belt of the wood under old understandings; past their line, only the Wardens go. They are not unfriendly, but they are not human, and a Warden's quiet refusal carries the weight of the whole forest behind it.

storySettings.worldBackground:
Larion is a medieval fantasy world. Swords, sorcery, monsters, taverns. No post-medieval technology. Magic is channelling the living power of the world through your body and will to reshape reality; it is visible and common, but can't solve every problem. Most people live ordinary lives in a world where extraordinary things happen.

Most of the world is lively and lived-in. People are mostly decent — they help strangers, share news, buy drinks. Darkness exists — monsters, cults, cruelty, old curses — but most of life in Larion is good.

Characters are vivid, larger than life, and defined by what they want — their appetites, opinions, contradictions, and weird specifics. They are quick to laugh, quick to anger, full of personality. Even serious people are fun to spend time with — funny, warm, sharp, surprising. People form bonds, fall in love, hold grudges, build lives. Let scenes breathe. Camaraderie, banter, downtime, small moments of connection matter as much as conflict.

The world is a patchwork of city-states, principalities, petty kingdoms, and ungoverned lands, without a single capital or central authority.

No modern, scientific, or technological language. Keep the vocabulary medieval, fantasy and grounded.
storySettings.questGenerationGuidance:
## 1. GROUND QUESTS IN THE NARRATIVE

Keep quest content grounded in the story. Never contradict or excessively embellish the narrative. Use existing factions, locations, characters, and worldlore. Never make up factions; either use existing factions directly or making a splinter group or offshoot.

## 2. STANDALONE OR ARC

If a quest does not tie into a larger pressure or tension in the world, such as a struggle against an opposing faction or group, make the quest standalone, without an arc, by setting arcId to null. Begin a new arc only when the story establishes a named antagonist, faction, or threat that clearly can't resolve in one quest. Impersonal sources like postings are usually standalone.

## 3. SCOPE AND RESTRAINT

Match complexity to what triggered the quest. A rat job is a rat job. A regional conflict gets regional weight. Don't seed hooks, conspiracies or mysteries the story hasn't earned. Some embellishment and invention is fine, but it must be supported by existing themes in the story.

## 4. LANGUAGE AND FRAMING

Medieval fantasy world. Avoid: scientific terminology, engineering language, modern professional framing.

## 5. QUEST OBJECTIVES AND STATEMENTS

Objectives: one imperative verb. Fight, rescue, retrieve, escort, infiltrate, negotiate, defend, investigate, sabotage, compete. No compound clauses with 'and' or 'or.'

Statements: WHO gave it, WHERE to go, WHAT to do, REWARD if stated. Two to three sentences.

## 6. THE BRIEF

Operational, not literary. State what exists in the world and what stands in the way.

Scale length to weight: a delivery gets one or two sentences; a regional arc gets a few dense ones. Do not pad with filler.

## 7. LOCATIONS AND AREAS

Default to existing areas and settlements. Only generate new when the quest demands it — and if you do, state why in the brief.

## 8. CHARACTERS

Generate only what the quest requires — don't add NPCs to fill output. Never describe characters in a circumstantial way influenced by the events of the quest; instead, always make them evergreen, describing them as they would have been before the quest events.

## 9. ARC DESIGN

Only applies when an arc is legitimately created. Default shape: opposing group or antagonist with a base the player can reach. Physical destination, leadership to dismantle, clear resolution. Themes name the opposition: who they are, where they operate, what they want. Secrets funnel inward toward confrontation. Secrets must not contradict established NPC characterizations, invent factions, or escalate beyond the arc's actual scale. Ground secrets locally and proportionately. Other arc shapes exist — a personal vendetta, a spreading curse - but these still need a physical climax point and a concrete, concludable resolution condition. 'Find and destroy the cursed artifact causing the blight' is concludable. 'Understand the nature of the corruption' is not. Every arc must end somewhere a player can go and do something decisive.

## 10. AVOID

Do not generate: deep conspiracies behind simple quests, documents or ledgers as quest MacGuffins, NPCs added just to fill output, crystals or crystal-themed elements, shadow magic or shadow-themed creatures and elements, mysterious robed or hooded figures with undefined motives.

lowAttributeTraits.constitution: Tires quickly and needs frequent rest. Strenuous activities leave them winded and shaky.
lowAttributeTraits.strength: Struggles with heavy objects and physical tasks that others find routine. Has a weak grip and movements lack force.
lowAttributeTraits.dexterity: Is prone to fumbling with delicate tasks and has trouble with precise movements. Fine motor control doesn't come naturally.
lowAttributeTraits.intelligence: Takes longer to grasp complex ideas and often needs concepts explained multiple times before they click.
lowAttributeTraits.wisdom: Tends to act impulsively and has trouble reading between the lines in social situations or spotting obvious dangers.
lowAttributeTraits.charisma: Finds it difficult to connect with others and often says the wrong thing at the wrong time, leaving conversations feeling strained.

Determined.name: Determined
Determined.description:
You don't go down easy.

(Constitution grants bonuses to Riding, Survival skills, and Blood Magic)

Graceful.name: Graceful
Graceful.description:
You move gracefully and you're good with your hands.

(Dexterity grants bonuses to Stealth, Archery, Throwing and Acrobatics)

Strong.name: Strong
Strong.description:
You've always had a powerful physique.

(Strength grants bonuses to Athletics and Melee weapon skills)

Charming.name: Charming
Charming.description:
People like to be around you.

(Charisma grants bonuses to social skills, Illusion magic, and Performance)

Intelligent.name: Intelligent
Intelligent.description:
You're a quick learner and knowledge comes easily to you.

(Intelligence grants bonuses to elemental magics, Alchemy, crafting skills, Investigation, and Medicine)

Wise.name: Wise
Wise.description:
You're wise beyond your years.

(Wisdom grants bonuses to Prayer, Light Magic, Healing Magic, Nature Magic and Animal Handling)

Friendly Face.name: Friendly Face
Friendly Face.description: You find it easy to get people to think your way.

Noble Heritage.name: Noble Heritage
Noble Heritage.description: Born into aristocracy, you carry yourself with refined grace. You can invoke your family name for aid in areas where it would be recognized.

Craftsman.name: Craftsman
Craftsman.description: You know how to make and fix all sorts of things.

Trade Contacts.name: Trade Contacts
Trade Contacts.description: As an experienced trader, you can always find a helpful ally in town to help you find somewhere to spend the night or get a ride.

Fire-Breather.name: Fire-Breather
Fire-Breather.description: You feel a strong connection to fire and air magic, and are able to bend them to your will.
abilities: Breath Weapon

Fae Bargain.name: Fae Bargain
Fae Bargain.description: You have made a pact with fey to learn illusion and alteration magic.
abilities: Glamour

Death Whispers.name: Death Whispers
Death Whispers.description: You can hear the whispers of the dead guiding you to your destiny.
abilities: Voices of the Fallen

Earth Bond.name: Earth Bond
Earth Bond.description: You are connected to the earth and sea, with magical control over these elements.
abilities: Rushing Rapids

Pinnacle of Magic.name: Pinnacle of Magic
Pinnacle of Magic.description: You were born under the Star of Magic and possess enhanced magical power.

Athlete.name: Athlete
Athlete.description: You can run fast and quickly climb over most obstacles.
abilities: Burst of Speed

Sword Training.name: Sword Training
Sword Training.description:
Attribute: Strength

You have trained with swords.
abilities: Whirlwind Slash

Shield Training.name: Shield Training
Shield Training.description:

You have trained with shields, learning to block attacks and protect allies.
abilities: Shield Bash

Staff Training.name: Staff Training
Staff Training.description:
Attribute: Dexterity

You have trained with staves.
abilities: Sweep

Archery Training.name: Archery Training
Archery Training.description:
Attribute: Dexterity

You have trained with bows and crossbows.
abilities: Steady Aim

Axe Training.name: Axe Training
Axe Training.description:
Attribute: Strength

You have trained with axes.
abilities: Gut Strike

Spear Training.name: Spear Training
Spear Training.description:
Attribute: Dexterity

You have trained with spears.
abilities: Precision Thrust

Blunt Weapons Training.name: Blunt Weapons Training
Blunt Weapons Training.description:
Attribute: Strength

You have trained with blunt weapons like maces and hammers.
abilities: Concussive Tap

Dagger Training.name: Dagger Training
Dagger Training.description:
Attribute: Dexterity

You have trained with daggers.
abilities: Swift Strike

Unarmed Combat Training.name: Unarmed Combat Training
Unarmed Combat Training.description:
Attribute: Strength

You have trained in unarmed combat.
abilities: Burst of Strength

Called to the Divine.name: Called to the Divine
Called to the Divine.description: You have a connection to a benevolent divine power.
abilities: Sacred Word

Human.name: Human
Human.description: Humans are a diverse race of varied appearance and customs, yet with a shared passion and ambition. Their shorter lifespans drive them to achieve, and their versatility lets them spread across nearly any climate or terrain to establish towns and cities.
abilities: Determined Stand

Dwarf.name: Dwarf
Dwarf.description: Stout, strong-limbed mountain folk, dwarves live in clans that value craft, duty and honor. Their strength, toughness and gift for metallurgy and stonework make them sought-after warriors, smiths and builders.
abilities: Dwarven Fortitude

Halfling.name: Halfling
Halfling.description: Half as tall as humans with nimble builds and quick hands, halflings possess a natural dexterity and charm that make them excellent scouts, traders and rogues.
abilities: Lucky Break

Orc.name: Orc
Orc.description: Muscular humanoids with tusks and green or brown skin. Taller than humans, orcs are known for their physical prowess and powerful shamanic magic. Their endurance and gift for horsemanship make them fearsome warriors.
abilities: Ancestral Spirits

Dragonkin.name: Dragonkin
Dragonkin.description: Scaled draconic humanoids with horns and powerful builds descended from dragons. Their strength, elemental resistance and draconic magic make them formidable warriors and sorcerers.
abilities: Scale Hardening

Moon elf.name: Moon elf
Moon elf.description: Tall and lithe with silver-pale skin and hair from platinum to deep blue-black, moon elves are dark-adapted, possessing perfect dark-vision.
abilities: Ancestral Whispers

Wood elf.name: Wood elf
Wood elf.description: Hardy, wild elves who make their homes in untamed woodlands. Their nature magic and forest stealth make them valued rangers, healers and scouts.
abilities: Nature's Voice

Catkin.name: Catkin
Catkin.description: Agile cat-featured humanoids with retractable claws, fur, padded feet and keen night vision. Their agility and night vision make them natural scouts, rangers, and rogues.
abilities: Claw Strike

Demonborn.name: Demonborn
Demonborn.description: Descended from humans who mixed with demonic beings, demonborn are marked by black horns and clawed hands. Their connection to otherworldly forces and talent for dark magic make them fearsome wizards and warlocks.
abilities: Fiendish Charm

Giantborn.name: Giantborn
Giantborn.description: Massive humanoids with booming voices descended from giants. Their power, resilience and mountain knowledge make them formidable warriors, guides, and builders.
abilities: Ground Slam

Dark Elf.name: Dark Elf
Dark Elf.description: Underground-dwelling elves who split from their surface kin in the ancient Kindred War. Renowned as Larion's finest illusionists and legendary thieves, dark elves prize wit, style and panache above all — being dull is worse than being wrong. Their charm and cunning make them natural rogues, spies and enchanters.
abilities: Shadow Step

Construct.name: Construct
Construct.description: An artificial being animated by a soul bound to a body of stone, metal, or other crafted materials. Constructs are tireless, durable, and possess an innate connection to the materials they're made from.
abilities: Soul Flare

Serpentkin.name: Serpentkin
Serpentkin.description: Serpentine humanoids with snake heads, scaled skin and venomous fangs. Their sharp minds, natural mesmeric abilities and deadly poison make them feared yet valued as advisors, rogues and assassins.
abilities: Serpent's Gaze

Berserker.name: Berserker
Berserker.description:
Main Attributes: Strength and Constitution

Berserkers are primal warriors possessed by powerful beast spirits in battle. They enter sacred battle trances where their spirit takes hold, their magic born from this merger of mortal and beast.
abilities: Primal Fury

Cleric.name: Cleric
Cleric.description:
Main Attribute: Wisdom

Clerics are servants of divine powers who channel sacred energy through prayer and ritual. They protect the innocent, heal the wounded, and stand against the forces of darkness. A cleric's faith is their power - the stronger their devotion, the mightier their miracles.
abilities: Healing Prayer

Half-elf.name: Half-elf
Half-elf.description: Children of two worlds, half-elves show the angular features and pointed ears of their elven parent softened by human vitality. They possess the elven gift of low-light vision and resistance to mental enchantment, though neither as pronounced as in pure-blooded elves. Often caught between cultures that don't fully accept them, they develop keen social instincts and adaptability.
abilities: Cultural Chameleon

Bard.name: Bard
Bard.description:
Main Attribute: Charisma

Bards are mystical performers who work magic through song, music, and poetry. Their performances can inspire allies, influence emotions, and even reshape reality through the power of artistic expression.
abilities: Silver Tongue

Blood Dancer.name: Blood Dancer
Blood Dancer.description:
Main Attributes: Constitution and Dexterity

Blood dancers are agile warriors who weave blood magic into their martial techniques. Through ritual scarification and blood offerings, they enhance their combat abilities, trading their own vitality for power.
abilities: Crimson Cut

Druid.name: Druid
Druid.description:
Main Attribute: Wisdom

Druids are guardians of the natural world who commune with its primordial forces. They shape nature's power directly, commanding the forces of growth, speaking with beasts, and maintaining the balance of the wild places.
abilities: Green Growth
Nature's Wrath

Fighter.name: Fighter
Fighter.description:
Main Attribute: Strength

Fighters are melee combat specialists who combine weapon proficiency with tactics and techniques that allow them to control and dominate a battle. Where others rely on magic, fighters rely on skill honed through thousands of hours of training.
abilities: Battle Control

Hammerdwarf.name: Hammerdwarf
Hammerdwarf.description:
Main Attributes: Strength and Constitution

Hammerdwarves are armored warriors who fight the way dwarves build — heavy, precise, and methodical. They know how things are put together, which makes them very good at taking things apart.
abilities: Momentum Surge

Monk.name: Monk
Monk.description:
Main Attribute: Wisdom, Dexterity, and Strength

Monks are mystic martial artists who draw power from rigorous spiritual discipline and physical training. Through meditation and ritual practice, monks achieve extraordinary feats that blend unarmed combat mastery with mystical force.
abilities: Ki Strike

Paladin.name: Paladin
Paladin.description:
Main Attributes: Wisdom, Strength, and Charisma

Paladins are martial champions of the faith whose sacred oaths of service to the divine are the source of their mystical combat prowess. Their devotion imbues them with fanatical might in battle and the power to smite foes of the creed they've vowed to defend.
abilities: Divine Smite

Ranger.name: Ranger
Ranger.description:
Main Attributes: Dexterity and Constitution

Rangers are wilderness warriors who combine survival expertise, tracking skills, and deadly archery. They form deep bonds with animal companions and know the wild places better than anyone. Where others see trackless wilderness, rangers see paths, signs, and opportunities.
abilities: Hunter's Mark

Rogue.name: Rogue
Rogue.description:
Main Attribute: Dexterity

Rogues are masters of stealth and guile who excel at exploiting opportunity through speed and cleverness. They combine quick wit, deft hands, and precise timing to overcome obstacles and strike when least expected.
abilities: Shadow Step

Warlock.name: Warlock
Warlock.description:
Main Attributes: Intelligence and Charisma

Warlocks draw on magical power granted by a pact with a supernatural patron from another plane of reality. Through this life-long bond, they gain access to knowledge and abilities that flow from their patron's extraplanar realm, though such otherworldly power never comes without cost or consequence.
abilities: Eldritch Blast
Patron's Gift

Wizard.name: Wizard
Wizard.description:
Main Attribute: Intelligence

Wizards are arcane spellcasters who manipulate the magical forces that underlie the physical world through spells based on incantation and gesture. Their school of magic relies on rigorous study, training and practice.
abilities: Fire Binding
Counterspell

Tinker.name: Tinker
Tinker.description:
Main Attributes: Intelligence and Dexterity

Tinkers are inventor-engineers who blend mechanical ingenuity with enchantment, building devices and contraptions that combine craft and magic.
abilities: Pocket Contraption
Patchwork Fix

Sorceror.name: Sorceror
Sorceror.description:
Main Attribute: Intelligence

Sorcerers are born with magic in their blood — inherited, awakened, or simply there. Where wizards study and warlocks bargain, sorcerers channel raw power that answers to instinct more than technique.
abilities: Wild Surge

Vampire.name: Vampire
Vampire.description: You have become a vampire, gaining supernatural power at the cost of a bestial hunger for blood and vulnerability to sunlight.
Vampire.quirk: Possesses dark sight and blood sense. Extremely vulnerable to sunlight and weak by holy symbols. Must regularly consume fresh blood or grow weak and lose self-control.
abilities: Create Thrall

Lycanthrope.name: Lycanthrope
Lycanthrope.description: You have been cursed with the affliction of the werewolf. Under the full moon, you transform into a powerful beast, gaining supernatural strength and senses at the cost of feral rage and loss of self-control.
Lycanthrope.quirk: Can transform into a werewolf, gaining enhanced strength, speed, and senses. Heightened senses of smell and hearing. Vulnerable to silver weapons.

Fire Magic Training.name: Fire Magic Training
Fire Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in fire magic.
abilities: Fire Sprite

Water Magic Training.name: Water Magic Training
Water Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in water magic.
abilities: Mist Veil

Air Magic Training.name: Air Magic Training
Air Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in air magic.
abilities: Wind Step

Earth Magic Training.name: Earth Magic Training
Earth Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in earth magic.
abilities: Stone Shape

Divine Magic Training.name: Divine Magic Training
Divine Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Wisdom

You have trained in light magic.
abilities: Prayer of Salvation

Blood Magic Training.name: Blood Magic Training
Blood Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Constitution

You have trained in blood magic.
abilities: Drain Life

Nature Magic Training.name: Nature Magic Training
Nature Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Wisdom

You have trained in nature magic.
abilities: Entangling Vines

Healing Magic Training.name: Healing Magic Training
Healing Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Wisdom

You have trained in healing magic.
abilities: Healing Touch

Illusion Magic Training.name: Illusion Magic Training
Illusion Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Charisma

You have trained in illusion magic.
abilities: Mirror Image

Conjuration Magic Training.name: Conjuration Magic Training
Conjuration Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in conjuration magic.

Divination Magic Training.name: Divination Magic Training
Divination Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Wisdom

You have trained in divination magic.
abilities: Locate Creature

Necromancy Magic Training.name: Necromancy Magic Training
Necromancy Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in necromancy magic.
abilities: Animate Dead

Enchanting Magic Training.name: Enchanting Magic Training
Enchanting Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in enchanting magic.
abilities: Enchant Magic Item

Ritual Magic Training.name: Ritual Magic Training
Ritual Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in ritual magic.
abilities: Ward

Artificer.name: Artificer
Artificer.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You are a beginning student of blending magic and mechanism into wondrous devices. Through study of enchantment principles and mechanical ingenuity, you craft objects that marry mundane and magical.
abilities: Anti-Magic Zone

Dark Magic Training.name: Dark Magic Training
Dark Magic Training.description:
Attribute: Intelligence

You have trained in the forbidden arts of dark magic, wielding shadow, decay, and malevolent forces.
abilities: Detect Thoughts

Warhammer Training.name: Warhammer Training
Warhammer Training.description: Attribute: Strength. You have trained extensively with heavy hammers, learning to find the weak points in even the thickest plate armor.
abilities: Concussive Tap

[Common] Iron Shortsword [slashing].name: [Common] Iron Shortsword [slashing]
[Common] Iron Shortsword [slashing].description: A well-balanced iron blade for quick cuts in close quarters.

[Common] Wooden Arrows [piercing].name: [Common] Wooden Arrows [piercing]
[Common] Wooden Arrows [piercing].description: Straight-shafted arrows fletched with feathers and tipped with iron points.

[Common] Battle Axe [slashing].name: [Common] Battle Axe [slashing]
[Common] Battle Axe [slashing].description: A heavy iron war axe built for brutal two-handed cuts through armor gaps and bone.

[Common] Light Crossbow [piercing].name: [Common] Light Crossbow [piercing]
[Common] Light Crossbow [piercing].description: A compact crossbow with an iron prod and simple trigger mechanism.

[Common] Crossbow Bolts [piercing].name: [Common] Crossbow Bolts [piercing]
[Common] Crossbow Bolts [piercing].description: Short, thick bolts with iron points designed to punch into armor and hide.

[Common] Iron Dagger [piercing].name: [Common] Iron Dagger [piercing]
[Common] Iron Dagger [piercing].description: A simple double-edged iron knife for close combat, utility work, and desperate moments.

[Common] Short Spear [piercing].name: [Common] Short Spear [piercing]
[Common] Short Spear [piercing].description: A short wooden shaft tipped with leaf-shaped iron, useful one-handed or from behind a shield.

[Common] Wooden Staff [impact].name: [Common] Wooden Staff [impact]
[Common] Wooden Staff [impact].description: A length of seasoned hardwood reinforced with iron bands at each end.

[Common] Throwing Knife [piercing].name: [Common] Throwing Knife [piercing]
[Common] Throwing Knife [piercing].description: A balanced iron blade made for accurate throws at exposed flesh and weak points.

[Common] Lockpick Set.name: [Common] Lockpick Set
[Common] Lockpick Set.description: A worn leather roll containing tension wrenches, rake picks, and hook picks.

[Uncommon] Leather Cuirass.name: [Uncommon] Leather Cuirass
[Uncommon] Leather Cuirass.description: A hardened leather chest piece scarred by old blows but still reliable.

[Common] Iron Warhammer [impact].name: [Common] Iron Warhammer [impact]
[Common] Iron Warhammer [impact].description: A flanged iron hammer built to crush armor, bone, and brittle bodies.

[Common] Short Bow [piercing].name: [Common] Short Bow [piercing]
[Common] Short Bow [piercing].description: A compact bow suited to quick draws and close-range archery.

[Common] Trick Blade [piercing].name: [Common] Trick Blade [piercing]
[Common] Trick Blade [piercing].description: A concealed spring blade disguised as an innocuous object.

[Common] Loaded Dice.name: [Common] Loaded Dice
[Common] Loaded Dice.description: A pair of bone dice weighted to favor certain outcomes.

[Uncommon] Silver Binding Chain.name: [Uncommon] Silver Binding Chain
[Uncommon] Silver Binding Chain.description: Enchanted silver links that resist breaking and gleam brighter near supernatural creatures.

[Common] Linen Tunic.name: [Common] Linen Tunic
[Common] Linen Tunic.description: A plain tunic of undyed linen, comfortable but not protective.

[Common] Cloth Trousers.name: [Common] Cloth Trousers
[Common] Cloth Trousers.description: Rough-spun wool breeches patched at the knees from long wear.

[Common] Worn Breeches.name: [Common] Worn Breeches
[Common] Worn Breeches.description: Once-luxurious velvet breeches, aged but still elegantly cut.

[Common] Faded Doublet.name: [Common] Faded Doublet
[Common] Faded Doublet.description: A well-tailored but aging silk doublet whose rich colors have softened with time.

[Uncommon] Woven Robe.name: [Uncommon] Woven Robe
[Uncommon] Woven Robe.description: A robe of overlapping leaves and bark that shifts color to match nearby foliage.

[Common] War Bow [piercing].name: [Common] War Bow [piercing]
[Common] War Bow [piercing].description: A tall laminated bow that requires strength to draw and drives arrows with force.

[Common] Long Bow [piercing].name: [Common] Long Bow [piercing]
[Common] Long Bow [piercing].description: A tall laminated bow with superior range and penetrating power.

[Uncommon] Mongo's Medium Health Potion.name: [Uncommon] Mongo's Medium Health Potion
[Uncommon] Mongo's Medium Health Potion.description: A vial of crimson liquid that restores vitality when consumed.

[Common] Hearthhome Key.name: [Common] Hearthhome Key
[Common] Hearthhome Key.description: A house key marking its bearer as the owner of Hearthhome on Hill Street.

[Common] Maple Lute.name: [Common] Maple Lute
[Common] Maple Lute.description: A well-used pear-shaped lute with delicate fretwork and the marks of countless performances.

[Uncommon] Smith's Hammer.name: [Uncommon] Smith's Hammer
[Uncommon] Smith's Hammer.description: A well-balanced steel hammer used for shaping hot metal on an anvil.

[Uncommon] Woodworking Tools.name: [Uncommon] Woodworking Tools
[Uncommon] Woodworking Tools.description: A collection of saws, chisels, planes, and measuring tools for working wood.

[Uncommon] Leatherworking Tools.name: [Uncommon] Leatherworking Tools
[Uncommon] Leatherworking Tools.description: A set of knives, awls, needles, and edge tools for cutting and stitching leather.

[Uncommon] Sewing Kit.name: [Uncommon] Sewing Kit
[Uncommon] Sewing Kit.description: A compact pouch containing needles, thread, scissors, and pins for textile work.

[Common] Battle Mace [impact].name: [Common] Battle Mace [impact]
[Common] Battle Mace [impact].description: A heavy flanged mace with a reinforced steel head for crushing armor and bone.

[Uncommon] Artificer's Toolkit.name: [Uncommon] Artificer's Toolkit
[Uncommon] Artificer's Toolkit.description: A leather satchel of fine screwdrivers, jeweler's loupes, wire, calipers, hammers, and arcane scribing tools.

[Common] Leather Jerkin.name: [Common] Leather Jerkin
[Common] Leather Jerkin.description: A vest of hardened leather that protects the torso without restricting the arms.

[Common] Linen Robe.name: [Common] Linen Robe
[Common] Linen Robe.description: A simple ankle-length robe suitable for scholars, clergy, or common folk.

[Uncommon] Iron Breastplate.name: [Uncommon] Iron Breastplate
[Uncommon] Iron Breastplate.description: Hammered iron plates riveted over leather backing, heavy enough to turn serious blows.

[Common] Leather Leggings.name: [Common] Leather Leggings
[Common] Leather Leggings.description: Treated leather leggings that resist tearing on thorns and rough ground.

[Common] Wooden Round Shield.name: [Common] Wooden Round Shield
[Common] Wooden Round Shield.description: A round shield of layered wood bound with iron at the rim.

[Common] Leather Boots.name: [Common] Leather Boots
[Common] Leather Boots.description: Sturdy tanned leather boots for long journeys and rough roads.

[Common] Craftsman's Toolkit.name: [Common] Craftsman's Toolkit
[Common] Craftsman's Toolkit.description: A broad set of hammers, saws, chisels, needles, and punches for everyday craftwork.

[Common] Wool Cloak.name: [Common] Wool Cloak
[Common] Wool Cloak.description: A simple undyed wool cloak that keeps off cold wind and light rain.

[Common] Leather Gloves.name: [Common] Leather Gloves
[Common] Leather Gloves.description: Supple leather gloves that protect the hands while preserving fine control.

[Common] Iron Helm.name: [Common] Iron Helm
[Common] Iron Helm.description: A hammered iron helmet with cheek guards and a nasal bar.

[Common] Cloth Hood.name: [Common] Cloth Hood
[Common] Cloth Hood.description: A rough-spun cloth hood that protects against wind and weather, not weapons.

[Common] Iron Greaves.name: [Common] Iron Greaves
[Common] Iron Greaves.description: Iron plates strapped over leather to guard the shins and knees.

[Uncommon] Vampire Hunter's Crossbow [pi.name: [Uncommon] Vampire Hunter's Crossbow [piercing]
[Uncommon] Vampire Hunter's Crossbow [pi.description: A compact crossbow of dark wood and steel, ward-etched for quiet work against undead prey.

[Uncommon] Silver-Tipped Bolts [radiant].name: [Uncommon] Silver-Tipped Bolts [radiant]
[Uncommon] Silver-Tipped Bolts [radiant].description: Blessed crossbow bolts with silver heads that burn undead, fiends, and shadow-bound creatures.

florins.name: florins
florins.description: The standard trade coin throughout Larion.

Ardan's Watch.name: Ardan's Watch
Ardan's Watch.basicInfo: A cosmopolitan metropolis that sprawls along the confluence of the Silverflow and Great North rivers. Legend tells that the hero Ardan built it from the ruins of an evil empire he destroyed, and his vigil against darkness has held here unbroken ever since. Dark Elder towers rise above streets that throng day and night with people from every corner of the Worlds. Ardan's Watch is a free city, beholden to no king or crown, governed by a Lord Mayor and a Council of Champions raised to the seat by deeds done in Larion's defense. The League of Adventurers makes its headquarters here, among several other orders that answer threats mundane and otherwise. There is no better place in the Worlds to make a name as as adventurer — or a home.
Ardan's Watch.hiddenInfo:
The Lord Mayor runs the city day to day; the Council of Champions sets its course.

The Elder portals at the tower-tops make Ardan's Watch the great transit nexus of the Worlds. They draw their power from something ancient buried in the vast undercity.

Most of the city is ordinary homes and markets, shrines and shops, but on any street you might pass someone exotic, powerful or famous: an archmage, Champion, foreign envoy in a curtained litter. People of all species are common on the streets, and a pair of horns or wings isn't worth a second glance. The city's famous thousand bells toll at dawn for the Holding (when Ardan held until first light), and ring throughout the day for victories, festivals, and the return of famous adventurers, treated as city celebrities.

Beneath all this runs a powerful underworld. Old Town is run by the Thieves Guild and its member gangs - the Hands. The undercity layer known as the Foundations are dangerous, and the layers beneath them are worse, though that doesn't stop adventurers from seeking the treasures of past ages still sealed up in their depths.

Ardan's Landing.description: A river dock area outside the city walls at the confluence of the Great North River and the Silverflow. Wharves throng with vessels from countless lands. Caravans queue at the gates. Pilgrims, merchants, adventurers, refugees, coming and going. Ardan's statue stands facing the city he built, his foot worn smooth by centuries of hands touching it for luck.

Thousandlights Market.description: An old colosseum, repurposed. The old walls still stand in a vast stone circle, their tiered arches built out into permanent shops and tenements three stories high. The arena floor below is a maze of stalls from every region of the Worlds. From every beam and wire hang lanterns — brass and copper and tin, perforated in stars and moons and animal shapes. Crowded at all hours; it never closes.

The Hearthfire.description: A social space at the center of Thousandlights Market around a great stone firepit lined with common benches. Food and drink are brought by servers from the stalls and taverns built into the colosseum arches. Strangers share benches; a traveler who eats here once is rarely a stranger by the time they leave.

The Way of Coin.description: The commercial spine of the city: banks, fancy shops, merchant houses. Dominated by the flamboyant merchant princes of the Syndicate, whose parties no one important misses, and who will hire adventurers for anything coin can solve.

The Roost.description: A tall stone building off the Way of Coin where the Greater Owls roost. The upper floors are theirs — perches, handlers, the apparatus of a realm-wide messenger network. The ground floor is a tavern that grew up to serve people waiting to dispatch or receive news. Messengers descend through here at all hours, and anything not marked secret gets called out on their way through.

Venturers' Way.description: The street where the Tower of Venture stands. Outfitters, mercenary recruiters, and inns where parties form up before riding out and stumble back to spend their pay. Notice-boards carry fresh postings. Newly-formed companies haggle over gear in the shop doorways; veterans chat outside the Drake's Head and watch the new ones go by.

The Tower of Venture.description: Headquarters of the heroic League of Adventurers. A bustling contract hall fills the ground floor — a great common room lined with colorful stained glass windows depicting famous adventurers. Mages, berserkers, shamans, warlocks and adventurers of exotic lands and crafts mill about in front of the job boards, forming parties to pursue the constant supply of opportunities for heroism, glory or just a quick florin. Chipper ratfolk scribes help adventurers get oriented, claim their pay, and get treatment and equipment.

The Drake's Head.description: A prestigious adventurer's tavern in a tall timbered house. A drake's skull hangs over the door. The common room is crowded at all hours, its walls lined trophies from countless hunts.

The Yards.description: The martial quarter, a sprawl of training pitches, armorers, weaponsmiths, and the houses of mercenary and knightly orders.

The Tower of Swords.description: Home of the Blades, who hunt monsters and answer threats to settled lands. The training hall fills the ground floor — a great vaulted space ringed with trophies from past hunts, each plaque naming the Blade who brought the creature down, where Blades old and new spar at all hours. The quartermaster will outfit a Blade going out at any hour, day or night.

The Blacksands Arena.description: An amphitheater of black sand hauled up from the riverbank. A venue for matches between gladiators, traveling champions from every region of the Worlds, and adventurers with something to prove. Bouts run from first-blood duels to monster-fights. Betting on bouts is a common pastime.

The Way of Wonders.description: The magical quarter, a brightly colored row of alchemists, enchanters, and dealers in magical curiosities.

The Tower of Spells.description: Home of the Wardens, who mind magical threats and maintain the Elder portal network. The library runs the full height of the tower, shelves spiralling along the inside wall. Apprentices brew, copy, and argue in the lower halls. The portal sits on the roof, the most stable in the city.

The Sacred Way.description: A long terrace of temples, shrines, and holy houses — every god with a following has a place here, from the great houses of the Lord of Justice and the Shieldbearer to the tiny shrines of foreign gods tended by a single devotee.

The Two Rivers Bathhouse.description: A great domed bathhouse of vaulted halls of pale stone, mosaicked pools fed by the city's two rivers, and scented steam rooms.

The High City.description: The grandest district, built on rising ground with views across the rivers and the plains beyond. Noble houses with exotic gardens, embassies from every region of the worlds, and the Lord Mayor's Palace at the crown.

Champion's Row.description: A row of grand townhouses overlooking the rivers from the High City's edge. Each belongs to a Champion of Larion. The Champion's Hall stands at the head of the row. The Council meets there in open session.

The Heartwood.description: An Elder garden of ancient trees, winding paths, pools fed by old fountains. Druids tend it. At its center stands a tree older than the city, known simply as the Old One.

Old Town.description: The oldest district, pressed against the riverfront below the High City. A cramped but vibrant slum of narrow old tenements, warehouses, and less reputable taverns and inns. The Thieves' Guild and the criminal bands beneath it claim this turf, and the Watch rarely ventures past the bridges. Frog-kin fish-stalls crowd the riverfront. Pawnshops and fences operate openly along the alleys. Bare-knuckle pits and gambling halls draw rough crowds.

The Riverwalk.description: A long market-promenade following the Silverflow between Ardan's Landing and Old Town. Permanent stalls sell goods straight off the boats — Highland wool, Bright Kingdoms spices, Spice Isle silks; food carts sell fried river-fish and skewered meats. Buskers play for coins, fortune-tellers read palms at folding tables, and street performers draw crowds where the walk widens.

The Trouble Brewing Tavern.description: A low-ceilinged, old, dark tavern with sawdust floors. The crowd skews rough — dockhands, off-duty guild runners, foreigners who don't want to answer questions. The beer is more honest than the clientele, and you can find work here that won't make it onto a League board.

The Tower of Shadow.description: Home of the Shadows, who handle the supernatural threats that must stay secret for the public good — hauntings, cults, the undead. The tower stands behind closed gates and is closed to the public. Inside, the Briefing Hall is where clients meet a Shadow and explain the problem; the training halls teach warding, counter-summoning, and rituals for detecting, tracking and banishing threats; the Vault holds cursed objects and sealed things the Shadows have recovered.

The Foundations.description: The top-most undercity layer, a vast warren of subterranean halls and chambers that once stood on the surface but was long ago built over. A volatile mix of recluses, criminals, subversives and worse lives in its upper reaches, considered the turf of various member gangs of the Thieves' Guild. The deeper chambers are the territory of those who answer to nothing — gangs, cults, scavenger bands, and the things that found their way down and stayed. Connects to the surface through countless basements, sewers, drainage canals, and old wells. Also called the Fifth City, since the Fourth City and other layers lie below it.

Muckside.name: Muckside
Muckside.basicInfo: A condemned district across the river from Ardan's Watch proper. Old warehouses and dockhouses list at bad angles where the ground has slowly given way beneath them. Most have partially collapsed. The city wrote it off generations ago rather than shore it up. No one lives here by choice — squatters, outcasts, and gang crews claim the sturdier ruins and fight over what's left. The bridges from the city side are unofficial crossings, rotting timber and rope.
Muckside.hiddenInfo:
No Watch patrols cross the river. The district polices itself, badly. Squatter camps and a few small gangs claim the sturdier buildings — they answer to the Thieves' Guild loosely if at all. Violence is common but small-scale. Most people here just want to be left alone and will be wary but not hostile to outsiders unless provoked. Ratkin are common here — more than anywhere else in the city.

Beneath the ruins is a foundation layer called the Muckwarren, and below that, sealed plague crypts from a long-ago epidemic. Strong local taboo forbids entering the crypts — even the gangs won't go near.

The Dregs.description: A muddy clearing ringed by leaning warehouse shells, the closest thing Muckside has to a town square. Fire pits mark where squatter camps have staked their ground. Across the river, Ardan's Watch rises clean and lit — close enough to hear the bells.

The Still Standing.description: A squat dockhouse that earned its name by not falling down. The ground floor is a tavern — one long room, a tapped barrel, benches made from old cargo pallets. The crowd is Muckside locals: squatters, gang runners, people between worse places. Not welcoming, but not looking for trouble with strangers who buy a round.

The Junkheap.description: An open-air market in a rubble-strewn yard where sellers lay out goods on tarps and blankets. Dented armor, mismatched boots, lockpick sets, unmarked bottles. No one asks where anything came from. Prices are negotiable and so are the weights.

The Muckwarren.description: The old foundation layer beneath Muckside, reached through cracked cellar floors and half-collapsed stairwells. Low ceilings, standing water, the sound of the river filtering through the walls. Smuggler caches and fugitive camps in the drier chambers. The passage deeper — toward the plague crypts — is marked with old warnings scratched into the stone.

The Plague Crypts.description: Sealed chambers where Muckside's dead were entombed during an epidemic rather than carried across the river to the City of Ancestors. The old seals are iron-banded stone, marked with faded ward-signs. The air is dead and dry. Niches line the walls, still occupied. No one from Muckside comes here willingly.

The Hall of Skulls.description: The deepest chamber beneath Muckside. A vast crypt with a vaulted ceiling held up by pillars of stacked stone sarcophagi. The dead here are old — older than the epidemic, older than the district above.

Clachmore Village.name: Clachmore Village
Clachmore Village.basicInfo: A giantborn village in a broad mountain valley, where the Stone-Born clan raises highland cattle renowned throughout the region. The giantborn here claim descent from divine ur-giants who shaped the mountains in the first age. They settle disputes through wrestling and feats of strength, take their honor deadly seriously, and throw celebrations that shake the valley walls. Their annual cattle drive to the summer pastures is a highland tradition.
Clachmore Village.hiddenInfo: The Stone-Born are gentle enough in peace but carry the old jotnar fire in their blood - insult their honor and you'll face a challenge that ends in broken bones or worse. Chief Brennan Big-Voice has led the clan for thirty years and still wins most wrestling matches despite his age. His daughter Ula wants to prove herself by driving cattle through disputed clan territory this summer, which could either earn her legend-status or start a feud. The village needs steady work done - fences mended, predators driven off, cattle retrieved from places too small for giantborn to reach - and they pay fairly in coin, cattle, or favors. They remember debts and grudges alike for generations.

The Drove Road.description: A wide track worn by generations of cattle and the massive feet of their herders, winding down into a green valley ringed by peaks. Clachmore spreads across the valley floor ahead - stone longhouses sized for folk half again as tall as humans, cattle pens stretching toward the river, smoke rising from a dozen chimneys. The lowing of cattle carries on the wind. A standing stone marks the village boundary, carved with the clan's bull-head sigil.

The Village Green.description: An open common at the village's heart where the Stone-Born gather for contests, celebrations, and moots. A ring of standing stones marks the wrestling ground - the grass inside worn to bare earth by generations of grappling. Lifting stones of graduated weights line one edge, the heaviest a boulder that supposedly only three giantborn have ever raised. Chief Brennan's longhouse dominates the north side. The Horned Hall tavern anchors the south, its doors sized for giantborn but with a smaller entrance cut alongside for ordinary visitors.

The Bull Pens.description: Massive stone enclosures where the clan's prize bulls are kept separate from the main herds. These animals are enormous - shaggy, horned, and ill-tempered with strangers. The Stone-Born speak to them like old friends. A giantborn youth named Cormac tends the pens and knows each bull's lineage back seven generations. Beyond the pens, the valley opens into grazing lands where the main herds dot the hillsides, watched by herders with dogs and long crooks.

Ronansay Village.name: Ronansay Village
Ronansay Village.basicInfo: A fishing village on the shore of Loch Rona where selkies live among humans. Some folk here can take seal form - it's simply how things are, governed by old customs outsiders find confusing. The village has strict traditions about the loch: what can be taken, when, and how to behave toward the seal-folk without giving offense. The fishing is excellent, the welcome warm if you mind your manners, and the loch holds secrets older than the village.
Ronansay Village.hiddenInfo: Something ancient sleeps in the loch's depths - not hostile, but vast and strange, dreaming dreams that sometimes ripple up through the water. The selkies have kept peace with it for generations through offerings and observances they don't fully explain to outsiders. Elder Morna Grey-Eyes leads the village and speaks for both the land-bound and the seal-folk; she's forgotten more about the loch than anyone else knows. Recently the loch has grown restless - fish swimming in strange patterns, seals gathering on rocks at odd hours, and the old thing below stirring in its sleep. The selkies are worried but won't say why. They need help from someone who can go where seals cannot.

The Shore Road.description: A path winding down through heather and gorse to the loch shore, where Ronansay village clusters around a natural harbor. Stone cottages with turf roofs. Fishing boats drawn up on the shingle. Nets drying on wooden frames. Seals watch from rocks offshore with eyes that seem too knowing.

The Harbor.description: The village center, where the fishing boats launch and return. Folk mend nets, gut fish, and trade gossip in a dialect thick with old words. Some of them have a grey sheen to their skin and dark eyes that don't quite track like human eyes. The Selkie's Rest inn sits at the harbor's edge, its common room warm and its fish stew legendary. Elder Morna's cottage overlooks the water from the hill above.

The Seal Rocks.description: A cluster of rocks jutting from the loch a short swim from shore, where seals gather at dawn and dusk. Some villagers swim out to join them; others watch from the beach. The water here is deep and cold and clear.

Tethwell Hotsprings.name: Tethwell Hotsprings
Tethwell Hotsprings.basicInfo: A village built around hotsprings that never freeze, where folk from across the highlands come to soak wounds, ease old bones, and conduct business. The Tethwell family has maintained the springs for twelve generations and enforces an absolute rule: disputes stay outside. Whether the waters actually heal or just provide rest and respite, nobody leaves unchanged.
Tethwell Hotsprings.hiddenInfo: The Tethwell family's authority is backed by every major clan - violating the peace here means making enemies of everyone. Matriarch Elspeth Tethwell is seventy years old, sharp as broken glass, and knows secrets about half the thanes in the highlands from conversations overheard in the steam. She never trades in blackmail, which is why everyone trusts her. The springs themselves run hot from volcanic activity deep below.

The Steam Road.description: A path climbing through alpine meadows toward wisps of steam rising from the rocks ahead. The air grows warmer as the village comes into view - stone buildings clustered around pools that shimmer with heat. Snow melts in a wide circle around Tethwell; the ground itself is warm.

The Welcome House.description: The main building where visitors are received. The Tethwell family runs things from here. A great room serves meals and drink - nothing fancy, but hot and plentiful. Sleeping quarters range from common dormitories to private rooms for those who pay extra. Matriarch Elspeth holds court by the fire most evenings, listening more than she speaks.

The Pools.description: A series of natural pools at different temperatures, from pleasantly warm to nearly scalding.

Rathcairn.name: Rathcairn
Rathcairn.basicInfo: A druid settlement built around a great stone circle at the edge of the Warden's Glen, where an ancient oakwood has grown since before the dwarves carved their first hold.
Rathcairn.hiddenInfo: Keeper-families tend the stones and mark the seasons while young druids train before wandering. The glen itself is sacred - a remnant of the primordial forest, watched over and protected by generations of druids.

The Glen Path.description: A track winding through ancient forest, where the trees grow taller and the light grows greener with every step. The settlement emerges ahead: low stone buildings half-hidden among the trees, and beyond them, the great stones visible through gaps in the foliage.

The Stone Circle.description: Twelve standing stones arranged in a perfect circle, each twice a man's height and carved with spirals. Druids gather here at dawn and dusk for observances. The Keeper's Hall sits nearby, along with dormitories for students and a modest shrine.

The Warden's Glen.description: The sacred oakwood begins past where the maintained grounds end and the primordial forest asserts itself. The oaks here are vast - trunks wide as houses, canopy blocking all but scattered light.

Rocky Gorge.name: Rocky Gorge
Rocky Gorge.basicInfo: A narrow mountain gorge with steep rocky walls and scattered clearings, located along one of the lesser-used highland passes. The area is known to harbor mountain trolls and other dangerous creatures, making it a hazardous route for unwary travelers.
Rocky Gorge.hiddenInfo: Mountain trolls lair in the upper caves and hunt the gorge at night. Travelers who move through by day and don't linger usually pass unmolested—the trolls are lazy and prefer easier prey. The route saves two days over the main pass, which is why desperate or impatient travelers still risk it. Local clans consider the gorge cursed and won't send warriors to clear it.

The Gorge Mouth.description: The pass narrows between sheer granite walls, the road reduced to a rocky track barely wide enough for a cart. Old landslide debris litters the ground. The walls climb high enough to cut direct sunlight, leaving the gorge in shadow.

The Narrows.description: The gorge squeezes tighter through boulder-choked clearings connected by passages barely two men wide. Caves and crevices pock the walls. Abandoned camps dot the clearings—some old, others left in haste.

Fire Mountain.name: Fire Mountain
Fire Mountain.hiddenInfo: The Halls were built by giants in an age before the War of Giants, then converted by dwarves into a binding mechanism to imprison Sarthar the Destroyer. The Mountain King and his people stayed behind to maintain the seals and became the morlocks - they still guard the deep places, hostile to all intruders. The fire demons are either Sarthar's dreaming rage or a bleeding wound from the Plane of Fire. The deeper one ventures, the more the giant architecture shows signs of dwarven modification - runic seals, forge-temples, mechanisms whose purpose has been forgotten. At the heart of it all, something sleeps.
Fire Mountain.basicInfo: The tallest peak in the Highlands, sacred and forbidden for five hundred years. A recent eruption split the mountainside and revealed ancient doors buried since the Age of Heroes. The dwarves maintain a fortress in the only safe pass, turning away all who lack a thane's permission.

Dun Teine.description: A dwarven fortress built into the cliffs where the pass narrows to a single route up Fire Mountain. Walls thirty feet thick. Gates of iron-bound stone. Murder holes and killing grounds designed to break any assault. The garrison answers to no single thane but to a council of clans sworn to guard the sacred peak. They turn away everyone who cannot present a thane's seal or a thane in person.

The Fissure.description: A great wound in the mountainside where the eruption split ancient stone. Smoke rises from vents in the rock. The doors revealed here are massive - forty feet tall, black stone carved with symbols that are neither dwarf-runes nor giant-marks but something older. They stand open now, broken from within. The air that flows out carries heat and the smell of sulfur. Beyond lies darkness.

Hall of the Mountain King.description: A vast hall built for giants. Pillars thick as ancient oaks rise into darkness, their surfaces carved with scenes of giant victories from an age before memory. The ceiling is lost in shadow a hundred feet above. Everything is sized for beings three times human height: the steps, the doorways, the enormous stone throne at the far end. But dwarven work covers the giant stone - runic seals etched into every surface.

Clanhold Stonehand.name: Clanhold Stonehand
Clanhold Stonehand.basicInfo: A masterwork of dwarven architecture built into a mountainside, where every surface demonstrates the mason's art. Clan Stonehand are the great builders - architects, sculptors, and delvers whose constructions outlast dynasties.
Clanhold Stonehand.hiddenInfo: Clan Stonehand maintain the only surviving alliance with giants, trading craft and counsel with the stone giants of Stonehome. Other clans respect their skill and quietly resent their diplomacy. The Stonehand-Stonehome alliance is genuine but fragile. The giants remember when all clans stood together; they don't understand why only Stonehand still honors the old bonds. The dwarves face constant pressure from other clans to extract concessions from their giant allies - better trade terms, territorial agreements, intelligence on giant movements. Thane Brynn Stonehand walks a difficult line, maintaining the alliance while deflecting demands that would poison it. Her daughter Sera has lived among the Stonehome giants for three years now, learning their ways - either a diplomatic masterstroke or an unforgivable breach of dwarven tradition, depending on who you ask. The clan's masons are contracted throughout the Highlands; their work is found in every clanhold, giving them access and influence others lack.

The Carved Way.description: An approach road that doubles as an advertisement - every retaining wall, every bridge support, every drainage channel demonstrates technical mastery. The stonework is precise, elegant, and slightly showing off. The clanhold emerges from the mountain ahead, its facade carved with geometric patterns that catch the light differently throughout the day. Other clans build for defense; Stonehand builds for legacy.

The Mason's Gate.description: The entrance to Clanhold Stonehand, framed by pillars carved to look like they're supporting the entire mountain. The doors are bronze, green with age, depicting the founding of the clan. Guards here wear ceremonial armor that's also genuinely functional - beauty and purpose combined, the Stonehand way. Beyond, a long gallery displays examples of the clan's finest work.

The Gallery of Works.description: A museum and meeting hall combined, displaying masterworks from every generation of Stonehand masons. Scale models of famous constructions. Tools used by legendary crafters. Samples of rare stone from across the Highlands. Visiting dignitaries are received here, surrounded by evidence of the clan's worth. At the gallery's heart, a sculpture depicts a dwarf and giant working together - a piece most visitors find uncomfortable.

The Shaping Halls.description: Workshops where apprentices learn and masters practice their craft. The sound of chisel on stone echoes constantly. Dust hangs in the air despite ventilation shafts. Half-finished sculptures and architectural models crowd every surface. A section near the back is reserved for giant-scale work - pieces commissioned by Stonehome, sized for hands larger than dwarven.

Clanhold Deepfire.name: Clanhold Deepfire
Clanhold Deepfire.basicInfo: A forge-city built into the caldera of a dormant volcanic vent, where Clan Deepfire has harnessed geothermal heat for their legendary smithwork. Lava channels light the streets and power the forges. The clan are master smiths and fire-shapers, but they bear an old guilt - their fire giant allies fell to Sarthar's influence during the War, and Deepfire has spent centuries atoning through vigilance. They hold the eastern approaches to Fire Mountain.
Clanhold Deepfire.hiddenInfo: The clan's purpose is penance. Their ancestors fought beside fire giants who then betrayed the alliance to serve Sarthar. Some Deepfire warriors died fighting their own former allies. The shame runs deep, and the clan has channeled it into duty - they maintain Ember Watch fortress in the pass below Fire Mountain, and Deepfire warriors are always first into the Halls when demons emerge. Thane Kolvar Deepfire is ancient even by dwarven standards, kept alive partly by proximity to the volcanic heat he's absorbed over centuries. He remembers the War. He remembers the betrayal. He will not let it happen again. The clan's forges can work metals no other smiths can handle, but they refuse commissions for fire-based weapons - they've seen what fire does when it turns against you.

The Cinder Road.description: A path of heat-blackite stone climbing toward the caldera rim. The air grows warmer with each step. Vents release steam and sulfurous vapors. Ahead, the rim reveals itself - and beyond it, the city glowing in the volcanic basin below. Lava-light reflects off stone buildings. The sound of hammers rises on heated air.

The Ember Gate.description: The main entrance, cut through the caldera rim. Black iron doors stand open during daylight, revealing a tunnel that descends toward the forge-city. Guards in heat-resistant mail watch visitors with evaluating eyes. The warmth here is intense but not unbearable - carefully managed by ancient ventilation systems. Carved warnings in Old Dwarvish line the passage: reminders of duty, vigilance, and the cost of trust betrayed.

The Crucible.description: The great forge-hall at the city's heart, built around a channeled lava flow that provides endless heat. Master smiths work at stations arranged in concentric rings, the most skilled closest to the molten core. The din of hammers never stops. Weapons, armor, and tools of extraordinary quality emerge from this place. Apprentices carry water and coal while journeymen work the bellows. The air shimmers.

The Lava Terrace.description: A residential district built on natural stone shelves overlooking the lava channels. Homes and shops carved into the caldera walls, connected by bridges and stairs. The light never fades here - the lava provides constant orange illumination. Taverns serve ale cooled in deep cellars, a precious luxury. Children play in the streets, unbothered by heat that would fell a lowlander.

Clanhold Stormhammer.name: Clanhold Stormhammer
Clanhold Stormhammer.basicInfo: An ancient giant stronghold of fitted megalithic stones, now headquarters of Clan Stormhammer. The dwarven clan has adapted the oversized giant architecture for their use while preserving the original construction techniques in their crafting traditions.
Clanhold Stormhammer.hiddenInfo: Clan Stormhammer is actually three rival bloodlines forced together by necessity - the original Stormhammers who claimed the fortress, the Ironforge refugees who fled their collapsed mine, and the Blackbeard mercenaries who stayed after their contract ended. Thane Grimvar maintains unity through sheer force of personality, but succession disputes simmer beneath the surface. His eldest son leads the traditionalists, his daughter champions the refugees, and his bastard nephew commands the mercenaries' loyalty. Meanwhile, the clan's famous ironworks depend on a secret trade agreement with Morlock tunnelers who provide rare deep-metals in exchange for finished weapons - a shameful arrangement that would destroy their reputation if discovered.

The Iron Gates.description: Massive gates of iron-bound stone built into the mountain face, flanked by watchtowers carved from living rock. Armed guards in clan colors check all who enter while murder holes overlook the approach. War banners hang from the battlements.

The Outer Courtyard.description: A wide stone platform carved into the mountainside where pack animals rest and traders display their wares. Weapon racks line the walls while training dummies bear the scars of daily combat practice.

The Great Hall.description: The fortress's main hall with a ceiling lost in shadows forty feet above. Massive stone pillars carved with ancient runes support the mountain's weight. Weapon displays and clan banners line the walls around a raised stone dais.

The Stonesons Alehouse.description: A raucous, brawl-prone tavern carved from what was once a giant's feast hall. Tables hewn from tree trunks seat dozens while barmaids serve ale from horns the size of buckets.

The Great Forge.description: The clan's master smithy built around enormous ancient hearths.

The Clan Quarters.description: Living chambers carved into living rock. Cooking smells drift from communal kitchens while children play between the carved pillars. Private chambers house clan leaders and their families.

The Giant's Stair.description: A massive spiral staircase with steps three feet high, carved from the mountain's stone. Wooden stairs have been constructed atop the stone alongside for easier passage of small folk, but the original giant-sized steps remain visible to each side.

The Lower Halls.description: A maze of corridors and chambers including storerooms, workshops, armories, and treasure vaults. The sound of picks and shovels echoes from mining tunnels that branch deeper into the mountain.

The Sealed Depths.description: The entrance to the fortress's deepest levels, blocked by iron doors covered in protective runes. The stones here are older and different from the giant construction above - smooth, seamless, and strangely warm.

Clanhold Icerune.name: Clanhold Icerune
Clanhold Icerune.basicInfo: A fortress carved into the face of a glacier where the Highlands meet the frozen north. Clan Icerune lives at the edge of habitable land, their hold a bulwark against frost giant raiders and worse things from the wastes beyond. The clan are ice-shapers who carve defensive walls, sculpture, and sacred spaces from living ice, though their living quarters are sensibly hewn from stone.
Clanhold Icerune.hiddenInfo: The Icerune dwarves were once close allies of the frost giants - then the giants turned during the War, and Clan Icerune learned their ice-shaping arts specifically to fight their former friends. It's a bitter history they don't discuss with outsiders. Thane Moira Icerune is seventy years old. Her heir, Brennan, advocates for seeking peace with the giants - a position that's either visionary or treasonous depending on who you ask. The clan maintains the Long Watch, a network of signal towers stretching into the wastes. When the fires go dark in sequence, something is coming.

The Frost Gate.description: The entrance to Clanhold Icerune, where carved stone meets living ice. Guards in white furs watch from positions that blend into the glacier. The gate itself is iron-bound oak, practical and sturdy. Beyond, a passage transitions from ice-carved tunnel to proper dwarven stonework.

The Hearth-hall.description: The clan's great hall, carved from dark stone veined with quartz. Multiple hearths blaze along the walls - warmth is sacred here, never taken for granted. Long tables seat the clan for meals of preserved meat, root vegetables, and ale that could strip paint. Furs and tapestries cover every surface.

Stonehome.name: Stonehome
Stonehome.basicInfo: A stone giant settlement in the highest peaks of the Highlands, above the clouds.
Stonehome.hiddenInfo: The stone giants here descend from those who sided against chaos in the War of the Giants. They won, they withdrew, and they've spent millennia living as they please—feasting, fighting, boasting. Proud, vital, and larger than life in every sense. Feast for days, fight for glory, and measure worth by power, passion, and deed. They care little for smallfolk—too small in spirit as well as stature. But they respect those who prove themselves exceptional. They test smallfolk they don't know by throwing boulders at them as they climb the mountain the first time. Those who don't make it weren't worth knowing. The stone giants reject the chaos of their primal ancestors, but they're divided on most else, and some grow restless with a world ruled by smallfolk.

The Gauntlet.description: The lower slopes of the mountain, where switchback trails cross exposed cliff faces beneath towering ledges. Scattered boulders and loose scree cover the paths. Here and there, skeletal remains lie half-buried in the rubble.

The Shoulder.description: A broad plateau halfway up the mountain, windswept and open. Massive flat stones lie scattered across the space.

The Great Gates.description: The formal entrance to Stonehome, flanked by pillars carved with the deeds of giants who fought in the War. The gates themselves are sized for beings three times a man's height. A giant guardian stands watch.

The Greathall.description: A vast stone building at the heart of Stonehome, its ceiling lost in shadow high above. Fire pits large enough to roast whole elk line the central aisle. Barrels of mead stand taller than a human. Giants feast at tables made from whole trees, arm-wrestling and bellowing songs and stories of ancient victories.

The Stone Moot.description: A circular arena carved into the mountainside where giants gather to fight and debate—activities that are much the same thing for them most of the time. Stone seats rise in tiers around the ring.

The Watchfire.description: A panoramic viewpoint on a high bluff where the giants keep a fire burning day and night. The valley below spreads out in full view, dominated by Fire Mountain in the distance.

Whitefang Hold.name: Whitefang Hold
Whitefang Hold.basicInfo: An ice giant stronghold in the highest peaks of the Whitefang Range Reached only by the North Wind Pass, one of the most brutal roads in the Highlands.
Whitefang Hold.hiddenInfo: The Whitefang believe the vanishing of the First One from Gods-tomb fulfills an ancient prophecy — when the slumbering ones awaken, giantkind will reclaim the world. Jarl Thrymkar is marshaling his people for war, though giant war-preparation looks more like a permanent festival of feasting, boasting, and brawling to determine who leads. Raiding parties strike lowland settlements at the jarl's whim and return with plunder and stories. The hold is dangerous for smallfolk but not a death sentence. Giants respect courage, endurance, and a good story. Those who impress the jarl may find unexpected opportunities. Those who bore him....

North Wind Pass.description: A mountain road through a high pass where blizzards rage more often than not. The wind hits like a fist. Snow blinds.

The White Gate.description: The entrance to Whitefang Hold. A rough-hewn opening in the mountainside wide enough for three giants abreast. No doors. No portcullis. The Whitefang don't believe in locks. Sentries watch from ledges above, deciding whether approaching visitors are worth announcing or worth pelting with boulders.

The Jarl's Hall.description: A vast cavern where the Whitefang gather. The ceiling vanishes into darkness. Fire pits blaze along the floor. Giants feast at stone slabs, bellowing songs and challenges across the hall.

The War Grounds.description: An open plateau behind the hold where giants train, spar, and settle disputes. The distinction between training and combat is unclear to outside observers. Wrestling matches become brawls. Brawls become competitions. Trophies from raids stand on poles driven into the frozen ground.

The White Wyrmspire.name: The White Wyrmspire
The White Wyrmspire.basicInfo: A solitary peak in the Whitefang Range where ice drakes have nested since before recorded history. Enormous, ancient, and fiercely territorial. The peak belongs to them and they suffer no trespassers.
The White Wyrmspire.hiddenInfo: The ice drakes are cunning, patient predators. The oldest, called the White Eye by the ice giants of nearby Whitefang Hold, has outwitted giant raiding parties for generations. Young giants prove themselves by stealing drake eggs — a tradition that costs lives on both sides and neither side shows interest in stopping. Hunters and adventurers seek drake materials, which fetch extraordinary prices, but no one maintains a camp on the peak for long. The drakes hold grudges and they have long memories.

The Approaches.description: The lower slopes of the Wyrmspire, where the mountain narrows toward its peak. Exposed terrain with little cover. Temporary camps occupy sheltered overhangs — some recent, some abandoned in haste.

The Nesting Grounds.description: A windswept shelf of rock high on the peak. Nests of packed ice and stone hold eggs the size of barrels. Adult drakes circle overhead or watch from ledges.

The White Eye's Lair.description: A deep cave near the summit where the eldest drake makes its home. The passage narrows before opening into a chamber of blue-white ice. The cold here is beyond natural.

The Crevasse.name: The Crevasse
The Crevasse.basicInfo: A massive glacier in the Whitefang Range, split clean in two. In the walls of the chasm, the ruins of an unknown city are visible — towers and walls of white stone, shattered by glacial movement but unmistakably built. The architecture matches nothing in known history.
The Crevasse.hiddenInfo: No one knows who built the city in the ice. The Nanukin's oldest stories mention the white towers that sleep but name no builders. Dwarven records are silent. The glacier could shift again at any time — new sections have been uncovered recently, drawing explorers and treasure-hunters. Artifacts recovered from the ruins behave unpredictably. Inscriptions appear in no known language.

The Rim.description: The edge of the crevasse, where the glacier splits. The gap is hundreds of feet deep and runs for miles. Below, white towers and broken walls jut from the ice at angles, half-encased, half-exposed.

The Crevasse Floor.description: The bottom of the chasm, reachable by rope or a treacherous climb down the ice walls. Fallen masonry and chunks of glacier litter the ground between intact sections of white stone. The light here is blue and strange, filtered through ice overhead.

The White Hall.description: A partially exposed structure accessible through a gap in the ice. Inside, a hall of white stone with a vaulted ceiling, cracked but standing. The walls bear carvings in a script no one has deciphered.

Fort Redclaw.name: Fort Redclaw
Fort Redclaw.basicInfo: A Nanukin ice-fort in the Whitefang Range, carved from glacier and snow-pack by the claws of the Redclaw bloodline, who hold it as vassals of the Nanukin Kingdom of Aurora. Thick walls of packed ice, tunnels wide enough for armored icebears to pass shoulder-to-shoulder.
Fort Redclaw.hiddenInfo: Chief Redclaw commands a garrison of warriors sent south by the Nanukin Kingdom to maintain their claim on this part of the range. The Nanukin are pragmatic and direct. They tolerate visitors who are useful or interesting and remove those who cause problems. They trade, share information, and hire capable smallfolk for tasks that need someone smaller or more nimble. Social dynamics run on dominance and respect — strength of deed matters, not words. The Ice Throne in the great hall was carved by Chief Redclaw from a single block of glacial ice with his own claws. Each chief carves their own when they take command. The old thrones are broken.

The Ice Road.description: A track across exposed glacier toward a white mass rising against white landscape. Fort Redclaw is nearly invisible until you are close. Armored Nanukin sentries watch from the walls.

The Gate.description: A passage through walls of packed ice ten feet thick, scored with claw marks from its construction. Armored icebears stand guard to either side — a head taller than the tallest human, arms thick as tree trunks.

The Great Hall.description: The fort's central chamber, carved from solid ice. The Ice Throne dominates the far end — a rough seat hacked from a single glacial block, still bearing the marks of claws. Warriors eat, drink, argue, and spar here.

The Dens.description: Tunnels branching off the great hall into sleeping chambers carved from ice. The Nanukin sleep in groups for warmth, their armor stacked at arm's reach.

The Yard.description: An open space behind the fort where Nanukin train. Armored icebears grapple, charge, and run formations. The ground is packed ice worn smooth by heavy use.

Lowgate.name: Lowgate
Lowgate.basicInfo: A trade town built where the mountain road from Highvale finally crests into the Highlands. Stone warehouses and timber inns cluster around a courtyard where caravans unload goods too fragile for mountain trails onto hardy highland ponies. The town marks the boundary between lowland commerce and clan territory - beyond here, a trader needs a clan's welcome or a hired guide who knows whose lands they're crossing.
Lowgate.hiddenInfo: Clan Bronzeaxe holds Lowgate and has grown wealthy controlling the southern trade. They're more merchant than warrior now, which other clans mock openly and envy privately. Guildmaster Torvin Bronzeaxe runs Lowgate and takes a cut of everything - fair rates, but nothing moves without his seal. His daughter Eira handles the actual negotiations and everyone knows she's the sharper mind.
