The Syndicate has been pushing for 'open passage' rights that would let their caravans travel deeper into the Highlands without clan escorts - the Bronzeaxes are stalling while quietly selling the idea to other clans as an invasion of lowlander merchants. A smuggling network runs goods through old mine tunnels to avoid Lowgate entirely.

The Customs Yard.description: A broad stone-flagged yard where Highvale caravans end their journey. Warehouses line three sides. Traders haggle over rates while laborers transfer goods from lowland wagons to mountain-bred ponies. The Bronzeaxe guild hall overlooks everything from a raised stone platform, its doors carved with scales and coins.

The Packhorse Inn.description: The largest inn in Lowgate, built to house caravan crews waiting for their goods to clear customs. Lowland wine and highland ale both flow freely. The common room hosts a permanent crowd of traders, guides-for-hire, and locals who've learned that news and coin both pass through here. A job board near the door posts work for able bodies heading either direction.

Dun Solas.name: Dun Solas
Dun Solas.basicInfo: A dwarven city in a vast cavern lit by the Eye, a massive circular opening in the ceiling. At the bottom of the cave lies Lake Two-Waters, fed by underground rivers as well as Skyriver, a waterfall that pours down from the Eye. From the lake's shores the cavern walls curve up and away into tiered rings. Each ring-road is lined by buildings with beautiful stonework facades cut from living rock, their interiors tunneling back into the cavern walls. The city's terrace farms grow rare mushrooms, lichens, and mosses famed for their uses in brewing and alchemy. Known for metalwork, mechanisms so finely made they seem alive, light magic and trade goods from the Deep Roads available nowhere else in Larion. Busy, prosperous, and welcoming to those who respect local ways.
Dun Solas.hiddenInfo: In times of crisis, the city can be sealed by shutting the Iris, a massive mechanism of metal plates that can slide across the Eye. The Trade Guild controls all commerce with the Deep Roads. In fact, the only entrance to the Deep Roads is through their headquarters. They deal with morlocks and other creatures from below who won't trade with surface merchants. Guild members never reveal their sources. But the Guild is always in need of adventurers who can help them deal with the threats from the deeps that disrupt trade.

Edge of the Eye.description: An area near the topside aperture of the Eye, high on the mountainside. A broad stone platform surrounds the opening. The Waterdrop - a system of pulley-operated lifts - descends into the city below. The Long Stair spirals down the inside of the cavern wall, but no one uses it by choice.

Lakebottom.description: The lowest tier, where the Waterdrop lifts land at stone piers extending into Lake Two-Waters. Skyriver Waterfall roars down from the Eye above. From here the city rises in carved rings - Lakebottom, the Market Ring, the Craft Ring, the Farm Ring, and the High Ring. Fishing boats bob at the piers. Dwarves swim in the freezing water year-round. A rope-ferry connects the shore to Lake Isle, staircases wind up to the Market Ring, and the Wetted Whistle inn and tavern dominates the lake shore.

Lake Isle.description: A large island at the center of Lake Two-Waters, accessible by rope-ferry from Lakebottom. Colorful cave mushrooms form a canopy over undergrowth of mosses and lichens. No buildings except for the old shrines that predate the city. Dwarves come here to commune with their ancestors, stroll among the mushrooms, or just get away from the bustle of the city.

Market Ring.description: The second tier, one level up from Lakebottom. Shop fronts carved into the cavern wall sell everything from dwarven mechanisms to exotic materials from the Underroads. The ring is a continuous curved street, busy at all hours, with goods displayed on stone counters and hanging from iron hooks. Lodging houses and money-changers cater to visiting traders.

Craft Ring.description: The third tier, where workshops cut deep into the rock, each one a specialist operation - lens-grinders, lantern-makers, locksmiths, and the famous mechanism-wrights. Apprentices run errands between masters. The craftspeople here are proud, particular, and not interested in rushing for anyone's coin.

Farm Ring.description: The fourth tier. Terraced beds of mushrooms, lichens, and mosses grow in carefully tended rows, thriving in the light that filters down from the Eye. Children from the lower rings are sent up here when they get underfoot.

High Ring.description: The uppermost tier, carved just below the rough cavern ceiling. Old families and wealthy merchants live here, removed from the noise and bustle of the lower rings. The view encompasses the entire city - the descending rings, Lake Two-Waters far below, Skyriver's endless fall. Quieter, cleaner, colder. The residents prefer it that way.

The Wetted Whistle.description: A sprawling tavern built into the cavern wall at Lakebottom, its front terrace half over the water. Perpetually busy, perpetually loud. Owned and operated by the Trade Guild, who use the upper floors as their headquarters, which contain the only passage to the Underroads that surfacers can access, and only with Guild permission. The Guild is always looking for capable adventurers who can handle trouble from below and keep their mouths shut about what they see. Good pay. No questions asked, no answers given.

Trade Guild Headquarters.description: The upper floors of the Wetted Whistle, accessible by a guarded stair behind the bar. Offices where Guild factors negotiate contracts, log shipments, and interview prospective hires. A heavy iron door at the back opens onto the only sanctioned passage to the Deep Roads. The Guild doesn't guard the entrance. They are the entrance.

The Warm Pools.description: An open-air bathhouse fed by lake water heated over vents. Steaming pools at multiple temperatures, from merely warm to scalding. Traditional Dun Solas opinion holds that warm water weakens the constitution, so the clientele skews toward outlanders, the elderly, and young dwarves who pretend they're only here ironically. A good place to hear gossip from across the Highlands.

Dun Beithir.name: Dun Beithir
Dun Beithir.basicInfo: A fishing village on the shore of the Frozen Sea, built around a massive broch tower that serves as lighthouse, watchtower, and the village's beating heart. The folk of Dun Beithir fish through the ice for their living, but the ice hides something — the beithir, a species of great serpent that surfaces through the frozen sheet to hunt. When the broch horn sounds, the village doesn't flee. It fights. Serpent-hunting is an art here, passed down through generations, and the kill is a communal effort.
Dun Beithir.hiddenInfo: The beithir is not one creature but a species — ice serpents that breed in the deep water beneath the frozen surface. Most are manageable. Every few years, a big one surfaces, and the hunt becomes dangerous. The village treats the hunts as a central part of their identity. The serpent's meat, bone, and hide are valuable trade goods. Harpoonmaster Niall runs the hunts with absolute authority — when the horn sounds, he commands and everyone obeys. The broch was built in the first age and no one remembers by whom. It predates the village by centuries.

The Ice Shore.description: A path descending through snow-covered rock to the village below, clustered along the edge of the Frozen Sea. Stone houses with heavy timber roofs, smoke rising from every chimney. Fishing racks stand in rows. The broch tower rises above everything — ancient, round, tapering slightly toward its peak, a fire burning at its crown.

The Village.description: Stone buildings tight together against the cold. The Warm Hearth inn anchors the main street, its windows glowing. A smithy specializes in harpoon-heads and ice-hooks. A trading post buys and sells serpent materials — hide, bone, venom, oil.

The Broch.description: The great round tower at the village's heart, older than anything else here. Stone stairs spiral up the interior to the watch platform, where a fire is kept burning and a massive horn hangs from iron chains. When a watcher spots movement beneath the ice — a shadow, a crack pattern, a sudden upwelling — the horn sounds and Dun Beithir goes to war.

The Ice.description: The Frozen Sea stretches to the horizon, white and featureless. Fishing holes are cut in careful patterns, each one monitored. Harpoon stations are positioned at intervals, equipped and ready.

Cave of the Forsworn.name: Cave of the Forsworn
Cave of the Forsworn.basicInfo: A cave system that opens into the deep places beneath the Highlands. Local clans call the inhabitants the Forsworn — the descendants of oathbreakers, banished below in ancient times and changed by the darkness.
Cave of the Forsworn.hiddenInfo: In truth, the caves are home to morlocks, the degenerate remnant of a dwarven people who retreated underground during the War of Giants and never returned to the light. Territorial, hostile to surface-dwellers. Crude social structures, fight among themselves, and hoard anything metallic. Raid surface settlements for tools and weapons. Their tunnels connect to the Deep Roads, making this cave system a back door into the underworld that bypasses the Trade Guild's controlled access in Dun Solas.

The Cave Mouth.description: A wide opening in a cliff face, partially concealed by a rockfall.

The Upper Tunnels.description: Rough-hewn passages that show traces of ancient dwarven craftsmanship beneath centuries of neglect. Gnawed bones, hoarded scrap metal, crude shelters built in alcoves.

The Hoard Hall.description: A natural cavern the morlocks have claimed as their central gathering place. Piles of scavenged metal — tools, weapons, ore, scrap — are arranged in mounds. Deeper tunnels lead down from here into true darkness.

Ravenvale.name: Ravenvale
Ravenvale.basicInfo: A steep-sided valley of pine forest where a flock of ravenfolk have settled. They have built a town of wooden platforms in the trees and along the cliff walls, and they have made it clear the valley is theirs.
Ravenvale.hiddenInfo: Highland clans tolerate the ravenkin, despite their reputation for aerial larceny, because they are useful: sharp-eyed, well-informed, and willing to trade news for things that glitter, which they collect. Hate being called thieves.

The Valley Mouth.description: A narrow entrance between high cliff walls where pine trees crowd close. The valley opens ahead and the ravenkin settlement becomes visible: wooden platforms in the trees, caves in the cliff faces, the whole thing alive with dark-feathered figures.

The Eyrie.description: The main settlement, spread vertically across both cliff walls and the tall pines between them. Crowkin perch and glide between platforms. A ground-level market caters to visitors who can't fly — stalls of collected oddities, salvaged goods, and information for sale. Parliament convenes in a large nest-structure at the valley's highest point.

The Old Shafts.description: Abandoned mine entrances in the cliff walls, predating the ravenkin settlement. Some have been converted to storage. Others are sealed with timber and rope.

Highlands League Chapterhouse.name: Highlands League Chapterhouse
Highlands League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: A giant's bothy (shack) on a windswept ridge, converted into the League of Adventurers' Highland headquarters. What was a one-room shelter for a giant traveler is a multi-story stone fortress to anyone human-sized. The original walls are ten feet thick and older than any dwarven hold nearby. The League found it abandoned, built floors and stairs into the vertical space, and has been improving it for decades. A switchback trail climbs the ridge to reach it. Locals call it the Big Bothy or the Adventure Shack.
Highlands League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The bothy was built by a hill giant centuries ago as a simple overnight shelter on a ridge route between grazing territories. It was one room to its maker—a place to sleep out of the wind with a fire and a roof. The League claimed it after a Blade company sheltered here during a giant raid and realized the walls could hold against anything in the Highlands.

The conversion is ongoing and charmingly imperfect. Giant-scale architecture means doorways are cavernous, the original hearth could roast a whole elk, and the ceiling disappears into shadows three stories up. Human-scale wooden platforms, stairs, and rope bridges fill the interior. It creaks in high wind.

A portal stone connected to the Tower of Venture in Ardan's Watch sits on the flat roof. The League's Wardens maintain it. A Greater Owl roost occupies the remains of the original chimney vent, adapted into a stone perch with good thermals off the ridge.

The chapterhouse serves as staging ground for expeditions into giant territory, dwarven deep-roads, and Highland wilderness. The contract board posts bounties from Highland clans, merchant caravans, and the dwarven holds. Adventurers heading into dangerous country form up here.

The Master of the House is always a veteran of Highland campaigns. The posting is considered hardship duty by League standards but attracts the type who prefer mountains to cities.

Relations with local dwarves are cool. The clans respect the League's usefulness but don't love outsiders fortifying a position in Highland territory.

The Goat Trail.description: A switchback trail up the ridge of stone steps and iron chains bolted to the rock where the wind gets bad. The bothy squats at the top—a low, massive shape against the sky. A wooden gate and palisade mark the League's addition at the trailhead. On clear days, you can see three clan territories from the first switchback.

The Bothy.description: One room to a giant. A fortress to everyone else. The original stone walls rise sheer and windowless—ten feet thick, fitted without mortar in the giant fashion. Inside, the League has built upward. Timber platforms divide the space into three stories connected by stairs and rope bridges. The ground floor holds the contract board, a quartermaster's stores, and the Master of the House's desk. Above, bunks and private quarters for resident adventurers. The original hearthstone dominates one wall, big enough to walk into, perpetually lit and throwing heat through the whole structure. Giant-scale iron hooks in the ceiling hold lanterns, banners, and the occasional adventurer's hammock.

The Snug.description: Carved into the thickness of one wall where the stone was already hollowed—possibly a giant's storage niche. Now it's a taproom. Low ceiling by the bothy's standards, which means normal ceiling by anyone else's. A plank bar, benches, and a stove that vents through the original stonework. The League's Highland veterans drink here between expeditions. A keg of dwarven stout is contractually maintained by arrangement with the nearest hold.

The Roof.description: A flat expanse of giant-cut stone open to the sky, reached by a staircase the League bolted to the interior wall. Wind hits hard up here. The portal stone stands at the center—an Elder ring maintained by the League's Wardens, connecting to the Tower of Venture and other chapterhouses. The Greater Owl roost occupies the old chimney vent, a natural stone column with good thermals rising off the ridge. Handlers keep the birds here year-round. On the roof's edge, a lookout post with a long brass telescope watches the Highland approaches.

Cairn Bruadar.name: Cairn Bruadar
Cairn Bruadar.basicInfo: A great burial cairn on a wind-scoured moor where dwarves go to sleep among their ancestors and dream. Some clans send their young here as a rite of passage. Others send emissaries to seek the wisdom of the dead before wars, successions, or great undertakings.
Cairn Bruadar.hiddenInfo: You enter alone. You sleep alone. What you dream is between you and your blood. Most return within a day. Some take longer. A few never come back at all. The dreams are real — the ancestors speak, argue, warn, and judge. They do not always agree with each other, and their counsel can be contradictory or cruel. The deeper into the cairn one sleeps, the older and stranger the ancestors one meets. Those who go too deep encounter dead who remember the Age of Heroes, whose perspectives are alien and whose demands can be impossible to fulfill. The Keeper, an old dwarf named Bridei, maintains the site, tracks who enters and when, and decides when to send someone in after a dreamer who's been under too long.

The Moor.description: Open heathland stretching in every direction, treeless and wind-battered. The cairn rises from the moor's center — a mound of stacked stone far larger than it appears from a distance, its entrance a dark opening framed by carved lintels. Bridei's cottage sits nearby.

The Dreaming Halls.description: Stone passages descending into the earth, lined with alcoves where the dead rest. Sleeping niches are carved beside them — narrow stone shelves where the living lie down among their ancestors. The air is still and cold. Deeper passages branch downward into older sections of the cairn, their entrances marked with warnings in Old Dwarvish. A few are sealed with stone and iron.

Folkmoot.name: Folkmoot
Folkmoot.basicInfo: A market town at the crossroads. Built around the Stone of Bonds, an ancient monolith said to bind any oath sworn in its presence. No tariffs, no taxes, no clan authority — Folkmoot exists for trade, and trade alone. Once a gathering place for highland moots, now a permanent market where dwarves, giantborn, Nanukin traders, goblin merchants, and Highvale caravaneers do business side by side.
Folkmoot.hiddenInfo: The Tavish family has run Folkmoot for nine generations, maintaining its independence through sheer usefulness — every power in the region benefits from a neutral market, and none wants to be the one who ruins it. Old Tavish is the current patriarch, a quiet man who settles disputes with a handshake and a pint and has never raised his voice in living memory. The Syndicate recently opened an office here, a development that has shaken up the locals.

The Crossroads.description: Where the roads meet, marked by a carved stone waypost. Folkmoot spreads along both roads, low stone buildings with bright-painted doors. The Stone of Bonds is visible from here — a dark monolith rising from the center of town.

The Moot Market.description: A natural hillside amphitheater where highland clans once gathered to debate and settle disputes. Now filled with market stalls. Goods from four regions change hands here — Black Fells metalwork, Aurora furs, Highvale grain and wine, Highland dwarf-crafts.

The Springs.description: A series of outdoor hot springs at the town's edge where the real business of Folkmoot happens. Stone-lined pools steam in the open air, surrounded by benches and low tables.

The Bonded Coin.description: Folkmoot's inn and tavern, a rambling stone building that has been expanded so many times it has no coherent floor plan. Good food, strong drink, and rooms for every budget.

Free Fortress Samhraidh.name: Free Fortress Samhraidh
Free Fortress Samhraidh.basicInfo: A magnificent dwarven fortress that serves as neutral ground during the highland summer when passes thaw enough for travel. By ancient law, all clans maintain right of passage here for the summer moot, where marriages are negotiated, trade agreements sealed, and disputes arbitrated. Bloodshed within its walls is forbidden on pain of eternal dishonor. The fortress rises in concentric rings of carved stone, its halls decorated with the banners and histories of every highland clan.
Free Fortress Samhraidh.hiddenInfo: A murder during last summer's moot has left the fortress shaken. The killer was never identified. The fortress commander suspects the murder was orchestrated to destroy the moot's sanctity and drive the clans toward open war. Clans still gather here because refusing to attend would be seen as weakness. Bitter rivals conduct their business while their champions issue formal challenges in the dueling circles.

Dun Dorcha.name: Dun Dorcha
Dun Dorcha.basicInfo: A frontier fortress that guards a strategic pass in the borderlands where the Highlands meet the Black Fells, patrolling for threats coming across the border.
Dun Dorcha.hiddenInfo: The garrison is a band of misfits - exiles, renegades, clan castoffs, and dwarves who never fit anywhere else. Commander Torgen with cheerful disregard for military discipline. Warriors brawl in the courtyard, drink until dawn, and call their commanding officer by his first name. He tolerates it because they're ferocious in a fight and fiercely loyal to each other. The border is dangerous work - goblin war parties, kobold tunnelers collapsing roads - and Torgen would rather have dwarves who fight like demons than ones who salute properly. New arrivals get hazed mercilessly, then adopted like family.

The Main Gate.description: A stone gatehouse flying the fortress banner - a black mountain surmounted by a red axe.

The Courtyard.description: The fortress hub, always busy with warriors training, gambling, arguing, or recovering from last night. A sparring circle sees constant use. Battered weapon racks line the walls.

Stoneheart Alehouse.description: The garrison alehouse, named for Commander Brynn Stoneheart, who rallied the garrison to hold out against a goblin horde. Her notched battle-axe still hangs above the bar.

The Barracks.description: A stone longhouse with rows of bunks along the walls and a central firepit that never goes out.

Gods-tomb.name: Gods-tomb
Gods-tomb.basicInfo: A mountaintop burial ground where the bones of ancient giants lie exposed to the sky, their remains far larger than any giant alive today. The greatest skeleton, called the First One, was so vast its remains were a part of the mountain. Now the First One has mysteriously vanished. Where it lay for millennia there is only a rift in the earth, leaving a wound in the land where a mountain should be.
Gods-tomb.hiddenInfo: Godstomb was sacred to all giantkind. Now, sporadic giant warbands raid the site, destroying smallfolk interlopers. Between their visits, tomb-raiders risk the harsh weather and the giants' wrath to pry ancient weapons and ornaments from the hands of the ancient dead in the tunnels beneath the Rift.

The Great-stair.description: A path of giant-sized stone steps leading up to the site, worn smooth and perilous due to the risk of slipping and the winds that buffet the slopes. Any creature smaller than a true giant will need climbing gear to scale each step.

The Field of the Fallen.description: The main burial ground, spread across a high alpine plateau. Enormous skeletons lie here, some intact, others scattered by millennia of weather. Ribcages arch like the frames of ruined cathedrals. Skulls the size of boulders stare at the sky.

The Rift.description: Where the First One used to lay. A chasm in the mountaintop shaped like a body, the impression of a skull, the channel of a spine, depressions where limbs rested. The scale is staggering. At the deepest points, exposed tunnel openings in the mountainside that were sealed beneath the skeleton lead down into the earth.

The Underbones.description: Tunnels beneath where the First One rested, unsealed by its disappearance. Ancient remains of various species, some unknown to the latterday world, have been uncovered here, many already despoiled by raiders.

Greathearth.name: Greathearth
Greathearth.basicInfo: A dwarven mining town carved into a mountainside halfway up the slope, with stone buildings built into the rock face overlooking a sweeping valley. Named for its famous communal hall, a masterwork of dwarven masonry centered on an enormous stone hearth. The town sits where three clan territories meet, making it neutral ground - and occasionally a flashpoint when old grudges follow miners to the tavern.
Greathearth.hiddenInfo: Greathearth survives because no clan can take it without the others allying against them - so everyone pretends the arrangement is tradition rather than necessity. The miners have developed their own quiet pride in this independence. Halla Deepdelve runs the Greathearth hall and serves as unofficial mayor, settling disputes with a wooden spoon and a voice that can silence a brawl. Her neutrality is legendary - she's turned down marriage proposals from three clans' thanes over the decades. The mines produce steady iron and occasional silver, enough to keep everyone interested but not enough to be worth a war. Old Bram the assayer claims he's found traces of mithril in the deeper shafts, but he's been saying that for forty years and nobody believes him anymore.

The Valley Road.description: A switchback road carved into the mountainside, offering views of the valley far below. Stone buildings emerge from the cliff face ahead - workshops, homes, storage cellars, all built into the living rock. A sign marks the boundary: 'Greathearth - All Clans Welcome, All Feuds Forbidden.'

The Greathearth.description: The communal hall that gives the town its name. A long stone building with a roof of fitted slate, its great doors always open during daylight hours. Inside, the famous hearth dominates the far wall - fifteen feet wide, built from stones hauled from all three clan territories, its fire kept burning year-round. Long tables fill the hall where miners eat, drink, argue, and occasionally throw punches before Halla breaks it up.

The Terrace.description: A broad stone platform carved from the mountainside, where locals gather on clear evenings to watch the sunset paint the valley gold. Benches worn smooth by generations of use. A few market stalls sell provisions, mining supplies, and the occasional trinket.

The Mine Head.description: The main entrance to Greathearth's mines - a reinforced tunnel mouth with rail tracks for ore carts, a winch house for the deeper shafts, and a small shrine to the ancestors who first struck iron here.

The Crossroads Inn.name: The Crossroads Inn
The Crossroads Inn.basicInfo: A busy inn on the High Road around a day's journey to the north of Ardan's Watch. The inn complex is surrounded by a wooden palisade fence with front and back gates. In addition to the main inn building, which stands two stories tall, a stable and barn complete the property.

The Yard.description: The front courtyard inside the palisade gates where carriages unload passengers and cargo. Worn ruts in the dirt show the heavy traffic this inn receives from merchant caravans and messengers.

Stables.description: A wooden stable building with stalls for a dozen horses, space for storing carriages, and a hayloft above.

Taproom.description: The inn's main common room with long tables for dining, a large fireplace, and a bar where the innkeeper serves ale and wine. Stairs lead up to the guest rooms while doors connect to the kitchen and front yard.

The Royal Chamber.description: The inn's supposedly finest guest room on the second floor. Despite its grand name, the room suffers from a leaking roof, drafty windows, and thin walls that offer little privacy from the taproom noise below.

Copperhill.name: Copperhill
Copperhill.basicInfo: A mining settlement built around played-out copper mines, now sustained by smelting ore brought from the Highlands. The town's distinctive red-orange dust stains everything, and the great smelting furnaces never sleep. House Copperhill made their fortune here before moving to Ardan's Watch, leaving a castellan to manage their interests. The lower town houses workers, the upper ridge holds the old manor.
Copperhill.hiddenInfo: The mines aren't completely dead - the deepest shafts were sealed after workers reported strange sounds and several disappearances. House Copperhill still sends expeditions down in secret, seeking the source of unusually pure copper samples that occasionally surface. The castellan has been skimming from the smelting operation for years, doctoring records to hide the theft. Some workers whisper about finding pre-kingdom ruins in the sealed sections.

Silverleaf.name: Silverleaf
Silverleaf.basicInfo: An old settlement at the Wolfwood's edge where the forest meets farmland. Named for the distinctive silver birches that grow nowhere else in Highvale, whose bark provides ingredients for healing poultices and expensive inks. The town carefully manages woodcutting rights, and their Forest Wardens patrol the boundary between civilization and wildwood. The Silverleaf Inn claims to be the oldest continuously operated tavern in Highvale, its great room built around a living tree.
Silverleaf.hiddenInfo: The silver birches grow where an elven delegation was massacred - their bodies were buried with full honors and the trees grew from their graves. The Forest Wardens have a secret pact with something in the Wolfwood, offering periodic tributes to keep the dark things from emerging. The Inn's tree is actually a dryad who took root to escape some ancient tragedy, occasionally whispering prophecies to those who sleep beneath her boughs. Several townspeople have reported seeing the same mysterious figure at the forest edge.

The Bleakmire.name: The Bleakmire
The Bleakmire.basicInfo: A fetid swampland where the Silverflow's tributaries slow and spread into trackless marsh. The King's Road skirts its edge on raised causeways, but the depths remain unmapped. Twisted trees grow from water black as pitch, and strange lights dance above the bog at night. The few solid islands hold the ruins of ancient watchtowers, their builders forgotten. Even justiciars won't pursue criminals who flee into the mire - those who enter either emerge changed or don't emerge at all.
The Bleakmire.hiddenInfo: The swamp is a thin place where the world bleeds into somewhere else. The bog waters preserve everything - Legion soldiers from the old empire still patrol their routes, perfectly preserved but no longer human. A coven of hags dwelling at the mire's heart trades in memories and years of life, sending will-o'-wisps to lure the desperate to their door. The ancient towers were built to contain something, not watch for it - their foundations go deep, pinning down sites where worse things nearly broke through. Recently, someone has been leaving fresh corpses arranged in ritual patterns on the islands.

The Broken Hills.name: The Broken Hills
The Broken Hills.basicInfo: Rough hill country where rocky ridges and hidden valleys provide perfect outlaw territory. The hills earned their name from the shattered stone formations that crown each peak. Goblin raids have grown frequent. Ruins dot the landscape - tumbled walls and broken monuments. Patrols occasionally clear them out, but the goblins always return.
The Broken Hills.hiddenInfo: These hills contain abandoned iron mines that once supplied regional smiths. Brigands and deserters use the rough terrain as a base, raiding travelers and settlements.  Some settlements near the hills maintain quiet relationships with the outlaws - trading for protection or information. The outlaw bands change leadership and composition over time through internal conflict and external pressure. A disgraced knight and his band of deserters have allied with the goblins. They hide in a cave complex. These hills contain abandoned iron mines that once supplied regional smiths. Various parties hold mining rights they cannot exercise while outlaws control the area.

Hillfoot Village.description: A small settlement clinging to hills' edge. Stone houses with slate roofs cluster around a central well. The people maintain watch day and night on a crude wooden palisade. Fields lie partially unharvested - too dangerous to work far from the walls.

The Broken Crown Tavern.description: The village's only gathering place, a two-story stone building with thick walls. The common room has a large fireplace and mismatched furniture.

The Upper Hills.description: Rocky slopes and ravines where goat paths wind between standing stones and scraggly trees. The terrain is treacherous with loose scree and hidden drops. Multiple trails branch in all directions, most leading nowhere or into ambush points.

The Broken Crown.description: Ruins of an ancient watchtower perched on the highest peak, its top shattered as if struck by lightning in ages past.

Holdfast Cave.description: A network of habitable, defensible caves on the hills' face. An underground stream provides fresh water.

Graves Hunting Preserve.name: Graves Hunting Preserve
Graves Hunting Preserve.basicInfo: A private hunting forest belonging to the wealthy Graves family, enclosed by a low stone wall and maintained by gamekeepers. Oak and ash trees shade riding trails. Hunting blinds overlook clearings stocked with deer. The Graves family has hunted here for generations.
Graves Hunting Preserve.hiddenInfo: Something is hunting in the preserve. The gamekeepers have heard strange sounds from the deep covert—calls that sound like men shouting for help, but wrong somehow. Two gamekeepers who went to investigate haven't returned. Lord Graves has quietly hired outside help rather than alarm the household. The managed forest near the gate is safe enough, but the wild growth around the ravine has become dangerous. Whatever lives there is smart, patient, and hungry.

The Preserve Gate.description: A stone arch in the boundary wall where the road enters the forest. A gamekeeper's cottage sits beside it, tools and snares on the porch. The main riding trail leads north under the canopy.

The Riding Trails.description: Paths wide enough for horses, winding through managed woodland. Undergrowth cleared, sightlines good. Hunting blinds and feeding stations at intervals.

The Deep Covert.description: Where the managed forest turns wild. Trees crowd together, brush chokes the trails, game paths replace riding tracks. Boar and deer shelter here

The Ravine.description: A rocky gulch cutting through the preserve's heart. Fallen trees span the gap. A stream threads the bottom past cave mouths and stony overhangs. No horse can pass here.

The Wolfwood.name: The Wolfwood
The Wolfwood.basicInfo: Ancient woodland pressing against Ardan's Watch's western walls, where towering oaks cast deep shadows and game trails vanish into thornbush mazes. Locals dispute the name's origin: dire wolves, a gang of bandits who ruled it in olden days, or even werewolves.
The Wolfwood.hiddenInfo: The Wolfwood's outlaw bands are more than mere bandits: they're led by deserters, victims of persecution, and rebels. Smuggling routes move contraband: untaxed goods, weapons, even slaves and darker cargo. Criminal networks with reach far beyond Ardan's Watch use the forest as their base, with local outlaws as muscle. The dire wolves grow bolder each season - rumors claim they walk upright by moonlight. Some claim the stone circles were created by druids to keep something bound here: hostile fey, ancient magic, or something in the forest itself.

The Forest Edge.description: Where farmland meets woodland. Stone walls marking ancient field boundaries disappear into the undergrowth. A few hunting lodges and woodcutter shacks, some maintained and some abandoned, dot the clearings. The King's Road runs along the southern border.

The Winding Tracks.description: A maze of game trails and old logging roads winding through the forest. Trees press closer here, their branches interweaving overhead. Thornbushes grow thick between the trunks, forcing travelers to follow the established paths. Small clearings appear unexpectedly.

The Oak Groves.description: Where the truly ancient trees stand - massive oaks with trunks wider than a man can reach around. Their roots create natural walls and hollows. The canopy blocks most light, leaving the forest floor in perpetual twilight. Moss grows thick on everything.

The Deep Woods.description: The forest's wild heart, where the trees grow so dense that paths disappear. Rocky outcroppings break through the undergrowth. Natural springs form pools among the roots. Caves and hollows open in the hillsides.

The Circle Stones.description: Clearings scattered through the deep woods where ancient standing stones rise from the earth. The trees around these places grow in perfect circles, and the stones themselves bear worn carvings in no known script. Some circles are small, just a handful of stones. Others are vast rings that must have taken generations to raise.

The Outpost Ruins.description: The remains of a ranger outpost, its wooden palisade rotted and collapsed. Stone foundations mark where buildings once stood. A tower of fitted stone still rises three stories, its interior open to the sky. Equipment racks, sleeping quarters, and an armory can still be identified among the ruins.

Halcyon.name: Halcyon
Halcyon.basicInfo: An elegant riverside castle famous for hosting tournaments and festivals at the nearby Halcyon Festival Grounds. House Halcyon sponsors champions and entertainers, funding their training in exchange for glory and prestige. The castle's Master of Games runs combat schools for warriors and the College of Bards teaches bards.
Halcyon.hiddenInfo: House Halcyon's obsession with competition stems from cutthroat rivalry between family members who sponsor competing champions. Fixed matches, poisoned blades, and sabotaged performances are common.

Castle Gates.description: Grand entrance with colorful banners and House Halcyon's crest. Guards in ceremonial armor check visitors.

Great Hall.description: Elegant hall where Duke Halcyon holds court. Walls display weapons and trophies from past tournaments. Musicians and entertainers gather here seeking patronage.

College of Bards.description: Where bards learn their craft under Master Lyrian. Performance halls, music rooms, and a library of songs and stories. Training costs 200 gold but includes room and board.

Champion's Quarter.description: Training yards where the Master of Games runs combat schools. Fighters spar while trainers critique form. Equipment shops sell quality weapons and armor. Training costs 150 gold per week.

Games Guild.description: Officially: gambling den and entertainers' guild. Unofficially: recruiting grounds for spies and rogues to service House Halcyon clandestinely.

Duke's Chambers.description: Private quarters where Duke Halcyon meets with favored champions and sponsors.

Halcyon Festival Grounds.name: Halcyon Festival Grounds
Halcyon Festival Grounds.basicInfo: A sprawling fairground beside Halcyon Castle where tournaments, festivals, and competitions run year-round. The Tournament Arena hosts combat contests daily, the Performance Pavilion stages bardic competitions, and the Games District offers every gambling opportunity imaginable. Champions come here to make their names, gamblers to make their fortunes, and bards to win audiences. Prize money flows freely, as do opportunities for glory—and trouble.
Halcyon Festival Grounds.hiddenInfo: Beneath the colorful spectacle, rival family members sabotage each other's champions, Games Guild enforcers rough up cheaters (and competitors), and someone's been fixing high-stakes matches. Recently, several champions have disappeared after winning too much money. The Duke knows there may be black sheep in the family but can't act without proof.

Festival Gate.description: Main entrance bustling with merchants, performers, and crowds. Sign-up tables for various competitions stand beneath colorful awnings. Criers announce upcoming events.

Tournament Arena.description: Large wooden arena where fighters compete for prize money. Current brackets posted on boards.

Performance Pavilion.description: Open-air stage where bards compete for audiences and silver. Performances judged by crowd reaction. Winners earn money plus noble patronage.

Games District.description: Gambling tents and dice games. Games Guild enforcers keep order—their definition of order, anyway. Rumor has it skilled cheats can earn special 'guild attention' - recruitment to spy for House Halcyon.

Merchant Camps.description: Temporary stalls selling weapons, armor, performance instruments, and festival goods. Some merchants offer 'special services' for those with coin and discretion.

Champion's Rest Inn.description: Large inn catering to competitors. Private rooms available for those who can afford them.

Greyhall Fortress.name: Greyhall Fortress
Greyhall Fortress.basicInfo: A dark stone fortress overlooking the eastern approaches, seat of House Ashford and headquarters for mercenary operations along the frontier. Lord Ashford runs Greyhall like a military camp, hiring fighters, rangers, and specialists. The War College trains soldiers in battlefield tactics, while the Scout's Guild teaches wilderness survival and tracking. The Specialist's Hall trains rogues in infiltration and 'problem solving.'
Greyhall Fortress.hiddenInfo: Lord Ashford faces troubling choices. He purged non-humans from his lands years ago, claiming security concerns, and his contracts sometimes cross moral lines. The eastern border faces goblin, gnoll and ogre raids, but Ashford's forces have been provocatively aggressive.

Fortress Gate.description: Heavily guarded entrance where mercenaries report for duty. A sergeant checks papers and directs recruits. Notice board lists current contract openings and pay rates.

Parade Ground.description: Open yard where soldiers drill in formation. Lord Ashford occasionally inspects troops from the reviewing stand. Strict military discipline—follow orders or face consequences.

War College.description: Where fighters learn battlefield tactics, unit coordination, and military strategy. Master Sergeant runs intensive training.

Scout's Guild.description: Rangers train in tracking, survival, and reconnaissance. Guildmaster teaches wilderness skills and border patrol tactics.

Specialist's Hall.description: Unassuming building where rogues learn infiltration, sabotage, quiet elimination..

Contract Office.description: Where Ashford's adjutant issues contracts and handles payments.  Desertion means bounty on the deserter's head.

Armory.description: Well-stocked with military-grade weapons and armor. Contract soldiers can requisition gear. Civilians can purchase at steep prices.

Mercenary Quarter.description: Barracks and taverns where off-duty mercenaries drink, gamble, and swap war stories. Rough crowd. Good place to hear about upcoming missions or join existing units.

Millbrook.name: Millbrook
Millbrook.basicInfo: A small farming village built around an old mill the Silverflow River just to the west of the Wolfwood. Millbrook serves the surrounding farms and mills grain for the region. The village is home to around two hundred souls, mostly human farmers and craftsmen, with a handful of halfling merchants and a dwarven blacksmith. Wooden houses with thatched roofs cluster around a central common, while the great millwheel turns endlessly beside the brook.
Millbrook.hiddenInfo: The mill sits on the Sweetbrook where it's deep and fast. Bodies and debris from upriver sometimes catch in the sluice gates. The wheel is old and breaks down regularly - when it stops, grain spoils and the village loses money. The millpond is deep and murky. Hunters and travelers use the road through town regularly, and not all of them are what they seem. The Wolfwood starts a few miles east - close enough that predators occasionally come out to take livestock. Ruins in the forest draw treasure seekers and trouble. Village feuds over property, marriage, and old grievances simmer beneath the surface and sometimes boil over. The village handles its own justice for minor crimes but serious matters go to Ardan's Watch.

The Weary Traveler.description: Millbrook's only tavern, a humble two-story building with a common room that serves as the village's informal meeting place. Rough wooden tables and benches fill the space around a large stone hearth. Serves simple fare and local ale to travelers and villagers alike. A narrow staircase leads to a handful of small guest rooms above.

Village Common.description: The heart of Millbrook, a grassy square surrounded by the most important buildings in the village. A stone well sits at the center, with an old oak tree providing shade. The common serves as a marketplace on fair days and a gathering place for village meetings. Wooden stocks stand near the oak tree, rarely used but maintained as a symbol of village justice.

Mill Quarter.description: The area around the great mill that gives the village its name. The massive wooden wheel turns constantly in the Sweetbrook's current, grinding grain from the surrounding farms. Miller's Lane runs alongside the brook, lined with granaries, the blacksmith's forge, and workshops. The sound of rushing water and grinding stones fills the air.

The Alderman's Residence.description: The largest house in Millbrook, a two-story stone and timber structure with glass windows and a slate roof that speaks to the alderman's prosperity. A well-tended garden surrounds the house, and a stable houses the alderman's horse and cart. The building serves as both private residence and informal village hall where official business is conducted.

Village Gates.description: The main entrance to Millbrook, where the High Road passes between two wooden watchtowers connected by a simple gate. Usually left open during daylight hours, the gates now stand barred with armed villagers standing nervous guard.

Keep Corwin.name: Keep Corwin
Keep Corwin.basicInfo: A prosperous limestone castle overlooking Highvale's richest farmlands, seat of House Corwin and defender of the Heartlands. House Corwin maintains the Rangers' Lodge, teaching wilderness skills and beast tracking; the Grove Temple, where druids study nature magic; and the Stone Monastery, where monks train in martial arts. The family offers bounties for eliminating beasts threatening their lands, plus free room and board for those protecting farming communities.
Keep Corwin.hiddenInfo: House Corwin's prosperity is threatened by growing problems. The beast attacks aren't random—something is driving predators toward settlements, making them unusually aggressive. Druids suspect corruption spreading from the Wolfwood, but the family doesn't want panic affecting the harvest. Meanwhile, tensions rise between old farming families and newcomers backed by Corwin money. Some villages mutter that the beasts are nature's judgment against greed.

Castle Approach.description: Tree-lined road leading to the keep's gates. Farmers' wagons come and go, delivering harvests. Guards are friendly but alert—beast attacks have everyone nervous.

Great Hall.description: Warm hall where Lord Corwin meets with village elders and defenders. Currently discussing the beast problem and rising tensions between farming families. Bounty board lists current threats and rewards.

Rangers' Lodge.description: Training grounds and dormitory for wilderness defenders. Master Ranger teaches tracking, survival, and beast lore.

Grove Temple.description: Natural shrine where druids commune with nature and study the land's health. Druid training available for those with nature affinity.

Stone Monastery.description: Simple temple where monks practice martial arts and meditation. Master Monk teaches unarmed combat, prayer, ritual magic and meditation. Monks are welcome to stay free while training, following the monastery's rules of discipline and service.

Lord's Study.description: Private chambers where Lord Corwin coordinates defense efforts.

The Wannmere Foothills.name: The Wannmere Foothills
The Wannmere Foothills.basicInfo: The Wannmere Foothills rise into jagged peaks at the northern edge of Highvale, where mountain passes lead towards the Highlands region. Fort Ravenwatch guards the main pass, a grim stone fortress built to guard Castle Wannmere—a cursed keep that looms on a distant rise across a narrow alpine valley. The foothills are sparsely populated, with only a few hardy villages clinging to the lower slopes.
The Wannmere Foothills.hiddenInfo: Fort Ravenwatch houses a rotating garrison of conscripts—criminals, debtors, and military deserters sentenced to serve. The scattered villages maintain minimal populations of shepherds and miners who avoid the upper valleys. Castle Wannmere was once a proud border fortress until something terrible occurred within its walls a generation ago. Now it stands empty but somehow alive. Fort Ravenwatch was hastily constructed to contain whatever emerges from the castle. The fort has become a dumping ground for those the realm wants forgotten. Conscripts serve six-month rotations if they live that long. The garrison commander maintains discipline through fear while trying to keep morale from complete collapse. Villages below provide minimal supplies in exchange for protection, though they trust the fort's criminals little. Strange incidents increase during winter months when fog fills the valley.

Fort Ravenwatch - South Gate.description: The main entrance to Fort Ravenwatch, heavily fortified with iron-bound doors and a portcullis. Guards check all who enter, though visitors are rare.

Fort Ravenwatch - Courtyard.description: A muddy square surrounded by barracks, armory, and mess hall. A gibbet stands as warning to deserters.

Fort Ravenwatch - Ramparts.description: The fort's walls overlook the foggy valley toward Castle Wannmere. Braziers provide warmth for the watch.

Fort Ravenwatch - Barracks.description: Cramped quarters where conscripts sleep in shifts. The smell of unwashed bodies and despair hangs heavy.

Fort Ravenwatch - North Tower.description: The watchtower facing Castle Wannmere directly. A looking glass is kept here for observing the cursed keep.

Fort Ravenwatch - Commander's Tower.description: Where the garrison commander maintains quarters and office. Few conscripts are summoned here for good reasons.

Fort Ravenwatch - Mess Hall.description: A low building where watery gruel and hard bread are served twice daily. The only warm room besides the commander's.

Mountain Trail.description: A narrow path winding down toward the villages below. Heavily watched to prevent desertion.

Shepherd's Crossing.description: A small village of stone houses huddled against the mountainside.

Castle Wannmere.name: Castle Wannmere
Castle Wannmere.basicInfo: Castle Wannmere stands on a rocky promontory overlooking a high mountain pass, its towers and walls still intact despite decades of abandonment. A perpetual mist clings to its base while shadows pool even at noon.
Castle Wannmere.hiddenInfo: Lord Wannmere achieved lichdom through corrupted Old Empire knowledge, but the ritual killed everyone in the castle, raising them as undead bound to his will. His wife wanders the halls as a banshee, the servants shamble as zombies, and his knights patrol as death knights. The lich lord still continues his research, sending undead to raid tombs and libraries for more forbidden knowledge. He's discovered that consuming the life force of the living can temporarily restore his human appearance and mind - for a few hours after draining someone.

The Approach.description: Mist-shrouded moorland stretching before the castle walls. The ground is boggy with treacherous pits hidden by fog. Scattered bones mark the road. Skeletal warriors patrol in groups of three. Will-o'-wisps float above the moors at night.

The Gatehouse.description: Twin towers flanking iron gates, with murder holes above and a portcullis behind. Two death knights stand guard at the gates. Six skeleton archers man the murder holes. The gate mechanism is in the left tower.

The Courtyard.description: A walled courtyard with stables on the left, barracks on the right, and the keep straight ahead. Zombie servants shamble between buildings. Four skeletal guards patrol the walls. Lady Wannmere's banshee appears in the tower window above.

The Great Hall.description: A long hall with two rows of pillars, collapsed tables, and a raised dais at the far end. Zombie servants stand along the walls. Three death knights guard the doors to the throne room. Wraiths drift through the shadows between pillars.

The Throne Room.description: A chamber with the throne on a stepped dais at the back. Lord Wannmere the lich sits on his throne. Two death knights flank him. Shelves of tomes and scrolls line the walls. A mirror behind the throne shows disturbing visions.

Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.name: Spice Isles League Chapterhouse
Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: A Gold Empire brass warship grounded on a reef in the Bay of Veils, converted into the League of Adventurers' Spice Isles headquarters. The Copperclad, as everyone calls it, is enormous—a vessel built for a civilization that thought in grander proportions than the present age. Its hull still gleams where the League polishes it. The rest has gone green with centuries of salt air. Docks and gangways connect it to the shore. The League operates from the upper decks; the lower holds are sealed, flooded, and full of things no one has catalogued.
Spice Isles League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The Copperclad ran aground centuries ago under circumstances no one agrees on. Gold Empire ships were built from alchemical brass that resists corrosion, which is why the hull is still structurally sound when any normal vessel would be reef-dust. It is the largest intact Gold Empire artifact in the Spice Isles and multiple factions would very much like to own it. The League's claim holds because they maintain a garrison and because removing them would mean war with the League across Larion.

The Master of the House runs the chapterhouse and is always a veteran of island operations—someone who knows the currents, the politics, and which merchant princes to trust, which is none of them. The posting is considered glamorous by League standards. Competition for it is fierce.

The upper decks are fully converted: quarters, storerooms, a forge, a contract board fed by bounties from every island and merchant house in the region. The main deck is large enough to stage a small expedition. Adventurers form up here before heading to ruins, pirate holdouts, or Gold Empire sites across the isles.

The lower holds are the Copperclad's open secret. Three decks down, seawater seeps through hull breaches and the brass corridors go dark. The League has mapped some of it. Gold Empire mechanisms still function in places—locked doors, active wards, things that hum behind walls. Official League policy forbids unauthorized exploration below the third deck. Unofficial League culture treats sneaking down there as a rite of passage.

A Greater Owl roost sits atop the main mast. The birds handle the sea winds well. A portal stone was hauled up and installed on a platform built around the crow's nest—high enough for stable passage, dramatic enough to suit the location.

Local pirates give the Copperclad a wide berth. The merchant princes of the isles treat the League as a useful neutral party and a source of deniable problem-solving.

The Gangway.description: A floating dock complex lashed to the Copperclad's hull, where small boats tie up and a broad gangway climbs to the main deck. The dock has grown over the years—planks, rope bridges, a harbormaster's shack, vendors selling provisions to departing adventurers. The hull rises above like a brass cliff, green below the waterline and gleaming above where the League keeps it polished. Barnacles and sea-growth crust the lower plates. Gulls roost on every horizontal surface the owls haven't claimed.

The Main Deck.description: The Copperclad's upper deck, open to the sky and large enough to muster a company. The League has built a wooden operations hall amidships where the contract board, quartermaster's stores, and the Master of the House hold court. The original brass deck is still underfoot—warm in the sun, etched with Gold Empire patterns. Cargo cranes adapted from the ship's original rigging haul supplies from the docks below. Fore and aft, stairs and hatches lead to the converted interior decks. A few hatches are chained shut and marked with League warnings.

The Quarterdeck.description: The stern section, once the officers' quarters of whatever Gold Empire captain sailed this thing. High ceilings by any standard—the Gold Empire built tall. Brass-framed windows look out across the bay. The League converted it into a taproom and common area. A long bar runs beneath the windows. Hammocks and bunks fill the adjacent cabins for adventurers between jobs. The walls are brass plate that someone decorated decades ago with carved wooden panels, trophy mounts, and flags taken from defeated pirates. A mounted sea-serpent skull hangs above the bar. The drink of the house is whatever came off the last prize ship.

The Crow's Nest.description: The top of the Copperclad's main mast, reached by a stair that spirals up the mast's interior—original Gold Empire construction, wide enough for two abreast. The platform at the top has been expanded with a reinforced wooden deck. The portal stone stands here, lashed down against the sea wind, connecting to the Tower of Venture and other chapterhouses. The Greater Owl roost wraps around the mast just below—a purpose-built wooden structure where the handlers keep the birds. Views stretch to the horizon in every direction. On clear days you can count islands.

Gavarra.name: Gavarra
Gavarra.basicInfo: The City of Fortune. A terraced island rising steeply from the sea, crowned by the Tower of Fortune — an ancient Gold Empire structure repurposed as the most exclusive gambling venue in Larion. The whole city is built around wagering, from dockside dice games on the lowest tier to the private salons of the Heights. Three rules govern Gavarra: no cheating, all debts honored, and you can wager anything you can pay. The city is neutral ground — enemies sit at the same tables, bound by the same rules. Kings, crime lords, merchant princes, and adventurers come here to stake fortunes, settle disputes, and make deals that can't be made anywhere else.
Gavarra.hiddenInfo: The council: Masters of the five High Houses plus the Master of the Tower as chair. Elite guards called Surety are embedded citywide — often in disguise, indistinguishable from visitors. Mages and informants among them. The Watch handles ordinary crime. The Surety handles the rest. Gavarra is neutral ground. Enemies sit at the same tables, bound by the same rules. Open violence outside sanctioned duels and bouts in the arena and other contests is dealt with harshly, though street crime against unimportant visitors raises no eyebrows, as long as they're not connected. Unrivaled for deals, intelligence, and negotiations that can't happen anywhere else. The Houses and the Tower are exclusive. Money and status determine access — there are tiers and levels throughout the city, and you can't just walk in anywhere, lest you face the legendary wrath of the Surety. Each House maintains its own guards, staff, and agents. Houses have changed hands dozens of times. Alliances form and dissolve over card games. Those who cannot pay debts may enter bonded service to a House. Old Gold Empire tunnels run beneath the island.

The Landings.description: Sea-level docks and the lower slopes. Piers crowded with merchant vessels and island traders. Taverns, street gambling, food stalls, chandlers, moneychangers. Loud, crowded, jostling, in-your-face. Sailors losing their pay at dice next to adventurers fresh off the boat. The houses on this level take all comers and ask no questions past the buy-in.

The Promenade.description: Middle terraces. Broad stone walkways connecting the great Houses of Fortune, entertainment halls, the Dueling Ground, the Arena. Gavarra's heart — where serious money moves and spectacle draws crowds. The better Houses require membership, patronage, or enough coin to matter. Bookmakers work the terraces openly. Street performers, food vendors, and pleasure houses fill the gaps between venues.

The All Inn.description: A well-kept inn on the Promenade, popular with visitors who want comfort without House prices. Dark wood, copper lamps, a warm taproom, decent kitchen, clean rooms, rooftop terrace with a view of the Tower. The crowd is the draw — Players cooling off between sessions drink alongside ship captains, sell-swords, and merchant factors. Work finds people here. A drink bought for a stranger is usually an overture.

The Heights.description: Upper terraces. Quieter. Private salons, invitation-only Houses, walled estates where the wealthy keep residences for the season. Nobles wager land holdings over card games. Trade magnates bet shipping routes over wine. Conversations here shape Spice Isles politics. The views are extraordinary — the whole city below, the sea beyond, the archipelago on the horizon.

The Atrium.description: Ground floor of the Tower of Fortune. Gold Empire hall, vaulted and columned, polished to a mirror sheen. Silk banners, soft light, princely furnishings. A small army of black-and-gold personnel — attendants, porters, and enough well-built staffers standing at ease to make the point. The bursar's desk at the center, where aspiring entrants present letters of credit, valuables, or specie. Gracious, welcoming, completely inflexible. The minimum stake is the minimum stake, and there are ways out besides the front door if you can't take a hint.

The Mezzanine.description: First floor of the Tower. High ceilings, Gold Empire columns, long tables and private alcoves. Where new Players are tested — not formally, but by exposure. The games are real, the stakes significant but not ruinous. Card games, dice, tiles, wagers on contests elsewhere in the city. Regulars, House agents, and Tower staff circulate and watch. How you play matters as much as whether you win.

The Dueling Ground.description: Open terrace on the Promenade, overlooking the sea. Broad stone floor marked with a circle. Weapon racks along the walls — dulled steel for honor duels, live blades for blood duels. A judge's platform and tiered seating, always at least half full. Anyone can challenge, anyone can refuse. The crowd wagers. Skilled fighters attract attention from the Houses fast.

The Arena.description: Small stone amphitheater carved into the Promenade terrace, seating perhaps a thousand. Intimate enough to see sweat and hear steel. Armed combat, unarmed bouts, obstacle courses, beast fights, and spectacles that defy easy naming. The schedule changes daily. Contestants enter voluntarily and compete for purses, but the real money is in the stands. Open to all comers willing to sign the terms.

Falmastra.name: Falmastra
Falmastra.basicInfo: A small island city-state home to the thrallmages. White stone and terracotta climbing steeply from a busy harbor, gardens on every terrace, clean streets, honest markets. Falmastra is beautiful, prosperous, and extraordinarily well-ordered. The thrallmages rule openly as patrons and protectors, and the citizens regard them with genuine pride. Visitors are welcome. Trade is encouraged. The island is a functioning port with excellent facilities and fine hospitality. It is also the seat of the most feared psionicists in Larion, and everyone who sets foot here knows it.
Falmastra.hiddenInfo:
The thrallmages govern through a council of senior members. Day-to-day administration is handled by a civil authority that answers to the council. The citizens are prosperous, well-treated, and largely content — they see the thrallmages as benefactors. Crime is rare but not nonexistent. The thrallmages concern themselves with threats, contracts, and power, not the small details of city life.

Visitors are watched. Not overtly, not aggressively, but the thrallmages are aware of who arrives and why. Clients seeking contracts are received at the Consul's House. Those who come with hostile intent tend to find their business known before they state it.

Falmastra trades normally with the Spice Isles and beyond. Many merchant captains consider it one of the safer ports — no piracy, no protection rackets, no crooked harbor officials.

The White Harbor.description: A deep natural harbor ringed by white stone quays. Well-maintained, busy with merchant traffic. Warehouses, chandlers, customs offices staffed by polite and efficient officials. Visiting crews speak well of the port — fair tariffs, clean berths, no trouble. Taverns and boardinghouses line the harborfront for sailors and short-stay visitors.

The Pomella.description: The main city, climbing the island's slopes in terraces of white stone and terracotta. Markets, workshops, residences, gardens heavy with citrus and bougainvillea. A handsome central square with a fountain. The people are friendly, the food is good, the streets are clean. Visitors move freely here. It feels like a prosperous Spice Isles town that simply works better than most.

The Consul's House.description: An elegant villa on the upper edge of the Pomella, where Falmastra receives official visitors and clients. Airy rooms, shaded courtyards, attentive staff. Business is conducted here — trade negotiations, diplomatic matters, and private consultations for those seeking the thrallmages' services. The consul is a civil appointment, not a thrallmage, and handles most affairs with practiced charm.

The Masters' Quarter.description: The upper slopes of the island, where the thrallmages keep their residences. Fine villas set among gardens and old trees, quiet and unhurried. Not walled or guarded in any visible way. Visitors are not forbidden, but few come here without invitation. The views over the harbor and the open sea are extraordinary.

Allhaven.name: Allhaven
Allhaven.basicInfo: A fortified harbor city serving as haven for pirates, privateers, and mercenary companies. Ships crowd the docks while treasure hunters outfit for expeditions to sunken Gold Empire ruins. The Water Arena draws crowds betting on gladiatorial combat, and the Floating Market sells everything needed for underwater exploration. Storm mages in the lighthouse guild guide ships safely to port while captains display their wealth in hillside villas.
Allhaven.hiddenInfo: The city maintains profitable relationships with seafolk and deeper things, trading salvage rights and water-breathing magic for information about Gold Empire sites. Several captains are actually licensed privateers serving various Spice Isles city-states, making Allhaven a neutral ground for shadow wars. The Stormcallers' Guild sells weather magic to anyone who can pay, sometimes to both sides of a conflict. Orcs, half-orcs, half-elves, catkin and demonborn make up nearly half the population, with humans, halflings, and various other races comprising the rest.

The Docks.description: A sprawling harbor where ships crowd against stone piers. A monumental bridge lands at the northern edge of the harbor. Crews unload cargo while merchants hawk supplies from waterfront stalls.

Captains' Row.description: Hillside villas overlooking the harbor, each a fortified display of plundered wealth. High walls surround estates decorated with marble columns from looted temples, gilded ship figureheads, and gardens full of exotic plants. Each successful captain competes to have the grandest mansion. Guards patrol the gates.

The Water Arena.description: A massive floating platform anchored in the bay where gladiators fight for prize money and glory, whether with each other or trained sea creatures - sharks, giant octopi, and stranger things brought up from the deep. Betting booths line the edges while spectators crowd the docks around the bay.

The Floating Market.description: A market held on dozens of barges lashed together in the harbor. Famous for its water-breathing potions and magical diving equipment. Con men hawk forged Gold Empire treasure maps to the gullible. The market shifts and reorganizes constantly as barges come and go.

The Yards.description: Massive shipbuilding docks where vessels are constructed and repaired. Workers hammer planks, tar hulls, and raise masts while foremen shout orders.

The Stormcallers' Guild.description: A tall lighthouse tower converted into an academy for storm mages. The light at the top guides ships safely to harbor while students practice weather magic on the balconies below. Inside, instructors teach wind-calling, fog-summoning, and storm-reading to paying students.

The Wreck.description: A massive galleon that ran aground and now serves as Allhaven's most famous tavern. The ship is beautiful despite being unseaworthy - ornate carvings cover the hull and the captain's quarters have been converted into private meeting rooms. The gun deck serves as the main drinking hall where crews gather, the hold stores barrels of rum and wine, and the crow's nest offers views of the entire harbor.

Crescent Bay Isle.name: Crescent Bay Isle
Crescent Bay Isle.basicInfo: A sheltered island built around a warm, shallow bay where merfolk and surfacers live side by side. The merfolk herd fish in the bay's waters while the surfacers tend the coral reefs and shoreline. A village of whitewashed stone lines the crescent shore, its docks and platforms built low to the water so merfolk can haul themselves up and join the life of the town.
Crescent Bay Isle.hiddenInfo: The arrangement is old and practical — merfolk drive fish into the bay during spawning runs, surfacers maintain the reef health with druidic tending that keeps the waters rich. The village has adapted over generations: the tavern has a saltwater pool, the market stalls are at wading depth, and children from both communities grow up swimming together. Some surfacer families have intermarried with merfolk — their half-mer children are equally at home on land or in the water. The merfolk settlement in the bay is visible from shore on calm days: shell-roofed structures anchored to the reef, gardens of cultivated kelp, lit at night by lanterns that glow beneath the surface. Mersene, frogkin, and a handful of half-sea-elves round out the aquatic population. The mood is unhurried and generous. Visitors are welcomed with food, drink, and the assumption that anyone who made it here probably needs a rest. The biggest ongoing dispute is whose fish stew recipe is better — the surfacers' or the merfolk's.

The Low Docks.description: Stone docks built barely above the waterline, where fishing boats tie up alongside merfolk who rest on the warm rocks. Steps descend into the shallows so that arriving visitors wade the last few feet to shore. Merfolk lounge at the water's edge, trading gossip with surfacers mending nets on the quay.

The Anchorage.description: The village tavern, built half on shore and half over the water on sturdy pilings. The main room has a long bar and a broad saltwater pool fed by the bay, where merfolk float with drinks balanced on their chests while surfacers sit at tables along the pool's edge. On warm evenings the shutters open wide and the whole place becomes one conversation — merfolk singing from the water, surfacers joining in from the rail, frogkin croaking percussion from the rafters.

The Reefmarket.description: An open-air market where the shore meets the shallows. Surfacer stalls on the sand sell bread, fruit, and cooked food. Merfolk vendors float in waist-deep water behind stone counters, offering fresh catch, pearl jewelry, coral carvings, and kelp-woven goods. Haggling happens in a cheerful mix of Common and the merfolk trade-pidgin that locals learn as children. At midday the whole market breaks for a shared meal — long tables set at the waterline where both communities eat together.

Wildflower Island.name: Wildflower Island
Wildflower Island.basicInfo: An island of terraced hillsides where rare flowers and aromatic plants grow in profusion, cultivated by perfumer families who have worked the land for generations. Stone cottages with clay-tile roofs climb the slopes between gardens of lavender, jasmine, sea-rose, and dozens of rarer blooms. The perfumes produced here are prized across the Spice Isles and beyond.
Wildflower Island.hiddenInfo: Five or six old perfumer families tend the island's gardens, each guarding blends and growing secrets passed down through generations. These rival houses is compete at the annual Bloom Festival where the year's finest perfumes are judged, and the winning house earns bragging rights and the most lucrative contracts with Tevantian and Water-Lily merchants. The families are proud, hospitable, and will talk your ear off about perfumes if you let them. Halflings and gnomes are well-represented among the perfumers — their sharp noses make them natural talents. A few of the rarer blooms on the upper terraces have mild magical properties. Nobody makes a fuss about this. It's just what the island grows. Workers come from neighboring islands during harvest season, and the population swells with seasonal labor, traveling merchants, and buyers from the city-states.

The Landing.description: A small stone harbor where flower garlands hang from the dock posts. A cart track climbs through garden terraces toward the village above. Porters with handcarts wait to haul goods and luggage uphill for a coin.

The Village Square.description: A sun-warmed plaza shaded by old fig trees, where townsfolk gather in the evenings. Stone benches ring a central fountain. A communal oven produces bread all day. The tavern on the square's east side — The Pestle — is small, loud, and serves wine infused with local herbs.

The Stillhouse.description: The island's largest distillery, a long stone building open on one side to let the breeze through. Copper stills line the walls. The master perfumers work here during the day, surrounded by racks of drying flowers and shelves of glass bottles in every color. A shop at the front sells the island's products, from common lavender water to rare blends that cost more than a sword.

Parrot Island.name: Parrot Island
Parrot Island.basicInfo: The loudest island in the Spice Isles, home to a community of parrotfolk whose bright plumage and brighter personalities fill the air with color and noise from dawn to well past midnight. Three great flocks — the Greens, the Reds, and the Blues — roost in cliff-face dwellings and treetop platforms connected by rope bridges and ziplines. The parrotfolk are famous across the archipelago for their music, their opinions, and their absolute refusal to stop sharing both.
Parrot Island.hiddenInfo: The three flocks are extended family groups distinguished by plumage color. The rivalry between them is passionate, constant, and mostly harmless — they compete over everything from who has the best singers to whose cliff-roost has the finest view, and disputes are settled through musical contests called Squawks where each flock performs and the crowd (meaning everyone on the island, since nobody would miss it) judges by volume of applause. Greens consider themselves the wittiest. Reds claim the strongest voices. Blues insist they have the best ear for harmony. All three are wrong and right depending on who you ask, which is everyone, because they will all tell you without being asked. Parrotfolk are natural polyglots who pick up languages effortlessly and mimic accents perfectly, which makes them sought-after as translators, messengers, and entertainers across the Spice Isles. Their instruments are as colorful as their feathers — drums made from hollow driftwood, shell-harps, reed flutes, and a local invention called the screech-pipe that sounds exactly like its name suggests. Visitors are welcomed enthusiastically and talked at without pause. Silence is considered rude. If a parrotfolk stops talking to you, you've caused genuine offense. The island exports music, messenger services, and handmade instruments. It imports patience.

The Docks.description: A rickety harbor at the base of sea-cliffs. Parrotfolk in every color shout greetings, unsolicited advice, and commentary. Rope ladders and a creaking lift provide access to the cliffs.

The Stage.description: A natural amphitheater formed by a ring of tall palms on the clifftop, their crowns lashed together with rope bridges where parrotfolk perch during performances. The stage is a broad wooden platform at the center. Somebody is always performing — soloists practicing, flock choirs rehearsing for the next Squawk, young parrotfolk busking for attention. On contest nights the whole island crams into the surrounding trees and the noise carries for miles across the water.

The Market.description: A sprawling bazaar across multiple cliff platforms where the three flocks sell their crafts — hand-painted instruments, feather jewelry, dyed textiles in vivid greens, reds, and blues. Each flock's stalls cluster together and compete for customers by being louder than the neighboring section. A groundling tavern called The Earplug occupies a sheltered nook at the market's edge, run by a half-deaf dwarf who claims it's the quietest spot on the island. It is not.

Earthsky Island.name: Earthsky Island
Earthsky Island.basicInfo: A volcanic island famous for its glassworkers, who turn the mineral-rich black sand into everything from common bottles to enchanted mirrors in the forges that line its terraced shore. At dusk the islanders light thousands of handmade glass lanterns in every color, and the island becomes a second sky — a constellation of earthbound stars visible for miles across the water. Earthsky glass is traded across Larion.
Earthsky Island.hiddenInfo: The black sand beaches contain volcanic minerals that produce glass of unusual clarity and strength, and the island's glassworkers have refined techniques over centuries that no one else has matched. Common glass — bottles, windows, tableware, lanterns — is the island's bread and butter, but the real money is in the magical work. Enchanted mirrors that show true reflections (useful for detecting shapeshifters and illusions), spirit-bottles that can trap or contain ethereal beings, glass weapons that hold enchantments better than steel, scrying orbs, and stranger commissions for wealthy clients. The magical glass workshops are run by master artisans who are equal parts craftsman and mage — you can't blow a spirit-bottle if you don't understand both the glass and the binding. Frogkin make up a significant part of the workforce. Their tolerance for heat lets them work closer to the forges and for longer shifts, and some of the best masters are frogkin whose families have been glassblowers for generations. The island also has a community of fire elemental folk drawn by the volcanic forges. The Lantern Lighting at dusk is a daily ritual the whole island participates in — each household lights its own lanterns, and the tradition is that a dark house is an empty house. Visitors are given a lantern to hang outside wherever they're staying. You leave Earthsky with a lantern. Everyone does.

Black Sand Beach.description: A harbor of dark volcanic stone where the beach glitters with mineral-flecked black sand. Glass lanterns in colored clusters hang from every post and railing, unlit during the day but promising something at nightfall. The air is warm from the forges further up the shore. A glassblower's shop at the dock sells lanterns to new arrivals — tradition says you need one before you go any further.

Forge Row.description: A long stone terrace cut into the hillside where the island's forges and workshops operate in the open air.
