The Rut.description: The churned-mud approach to the wallow, where boar-tracks and boot-prints overlap in a wide swath of destroyed undergrowth. Tusker sentries watch from platforms in the trees. The palisade gate is a rough affair of whole logs lashed together.

The Mud Ring.description: The wallow's center, a trampled clearing where Tuskers gather, argue, eat, and settle disputes by fighting. A forge cobbled together from looted parts bangs away constantly — the camp's most prized possession. The chief holds court from a log throne, surrounded by their best fighters.

The Loot Pit.description: A roofed dugout where raided goods are stored and divided. Metal tools, weapons, bolts of cloth, barrels of ale — everything the Tuskers take and can't make.

Mount Dragonseal.name: Mount Dragonseal
Mount Dragonseal.basicInfo: A cave network in the side of a mountain in the deep Everwood where the bones of ancient dragons are said to have been sealed behind wards placed in ancient times by the legendary Suleyvan the Sealer. But now the great seal-stone at the entrance has been shattered — split clean through by something powerful enough to break magic that held for millennia.
Mount Dragonseal.hiddenInfo: The caves were a nesting ground for dragons in an age before elves. When Suleyvan sealed the extraplanar breaches, he also sealed places where draconic power had soaked into the world itself. The Scaled Covenant found this place and broke the seal — one of the few acts that could draw a Harbinger's personal attention. They've established a hidden camp deeper in the caves where recruits undergo transformation by bathing in pools of liquid suffused with ancient draconic power. The caves themselves are dangerous independent of the Covenant — the residual magic has warped the wildlife near the entrance, and deeper in, things that fed on draconic power for millennia are waking up. The broken seal is also a political crisis: Suleyvan's wards are supposed to be unbreakable. If this one fell, others could too.

The Shatterseal.description: The cave mouth, where the two halves of Suleyvan's seal-stone lie cracked apart like a split skull. The carved wards are dark.

The Bone Gallery.description: A long cavern where dragon skeletons are embedded in the walls and ceiling — enormous ribcages arching overhead, skulls the size of carriages jutting from the stone.

The Nests.description: The innermost caves, where the draconic residue is strongest. Pools of dark liquid gather in hollows worn smooth by creatures that slept here for ages.

Whitehill Kobold Warren.name: Whitehill Kobold Warren
Whitehill Kobold Warren.basicInfo: A network of tunnels and crude fortifications dug into a rocky hillside. Kobolds have infested this place for generations, but recently they've become organized — patrols, watch-posts, even a gate.
Whitehill Kobold Warren.hiddenInfo: A goblin expeditionary squad from the Greater Goblinate has taken command of the warren, 'recruiting' the local kobolds into the empire's service. The goblin captain sees the warren as a forward outpost — a foothold in the Everwood far from Goblinate territory. The kobolds are split: some welcome the weapons, training, and sense of purpose; others resent taking orders from outsiders who treat them as expendable labor. The goblins are expanding the tunnels aggressively. One branch is heading toward Sanctuary's Underhalls, though neither the dwarves nor the goblins have broken through yet. The captain has also been sending kobold scouts to map the Everwood's trade roads — preparation for something bigger. The whole operation is small enough to go unnoticed for now, which is exactly how the Goblinate prefers to work.

The Scrabble.description: The hillside approach, where the ground is pocked with small holes and loose rock. Crude snare traps and pit traps line the obvious paths. A reinforced gate — new, and better-built than anything kobolds usually manage — blocks the main tunnel entrance.

The Workings.description: The warren's main hub — a tangle of low tunnels opening into a central cavern where kobolds eat, sleep, and squabble. Goblin-issue crates are stacked against one wall. A crude banner of the Greater Goblinate hangs over the captain's alcove, which is the only space with a proper door.

The Deep Dig.description: The newest tunnels, where kobolds dig under goblin direction. Shored with timber — another new addition. The air is bad and the tunnels branch in several directions.

Sanctuary.name: Sanctuary
Sanctuary.basicInfo: The only major non-elven city in the Everwood. It exists by high elven permission within ancient walls and wards that hold the forest back. Every species and culture in Larion has found a foothold here — dwarves below, Tevantines on the canals, halflings in the eastern hills, elves in the Heartwood where the original forest still stands. Packed tight, built upward and downward for lack of room to sprawl.
Sanctuary.hiddenInfo: Sanctuary exists because the high elves allow it, and not all of them agree that they should. Radicals among the high elves want the Outsiders expelled and the forest reclaimed. The Char - a city block gutted by magefire - may well have been their warning shot, though the Wardens have never proven it. The city is a major hub of the illegal life-stone trade. Dark elves control the supply from below and their agents move it through Sanctuary's underworld to buyers across Larion.

Eastgate Square.description: A vast plaza just inside the main gate, where the road from Highvale enters the city. Press of bodies, calls of hawkers and criers, chaos of cultures colliding.

Canal Square.description: The heart of the Tevantine quarter, where the main canals converge around a broad stone plaza. Merchants in bright silks haggle from gondolas while warehouses and trading offices line the water's edge.

The Floating World.description: A grand tavern-barge drifting through the canals, its musicians and callers inviting the city aboard. Lanterns sway from silk canopies. Below deck are private cabins.

Forge Row.description: A narrow street of smithies and armories, the clang of hammers constant from dawn to dusk. Dwarven craftsmen and gnome tinkers work side by side. The street slopes toward heavy stone doors carved with clan symbols — the entrance to the Underhalls.

The Underhalls.description: The dwarven quarter beneath the city — stone halls, clan holdings, taverns serving mountain ale. The clans handle their own disputes down here. Deeper passages wind into darkness.

Triumph Square.description: The government heart of Sanctuary, where a Kindred War memorial stands before Council Hall. The Watch Tower looms nearby. Banks and guild halls ring the square.

Goldmount.description: A low hill lined with the tower compounds of Sanctuary's great merchant Houses. Behind iron gates and private guards, these estates contain everything their owners need to avoid mixing with common folk.

The League Chapterhouse.description: Where adventurers register with the League for passage through the Everwood. The great board lists bounties, expeditions seeking escort, and warnings about what's been spotted this halfmoon.

The Heartwood.description: Ancient oaks that were never felled, where the original forest still stands within the walls. This is high elven ground. The Wardens keep their headquarters here beneath the great trees. Outsiders enter only with permission.

The Greenwarren.description: A winding maze of halfling terrace-mounds, each level connected by paths and shortcuts only locals know. Behind the round doors lie trading offices, cozy taverns, and produce markets.

Grave Street.description: Where all quarters meet and laws get thin. A maze of tenements, unlicensed taverns, and back-alley dealings. If something needs to move quietly through Sanctuary, it passes through here.

The Char.description: A whole block gutted by fire. The Watch keeps the streets barricaded. Whatever's living in the ruins, it isn't friendly, and the sounds at night keep the neighboring streets empty after sundown.

The Last Stand Tavern.description: Built into Westgate Tower where Sanctuary's defenders held the line in the Kindred War. Trophies from legendary kills hang beneath the Great Bell, which still rings when something tests the wards. The best tables face windows overlooking the Everwood.

Hillhome.name: Hillhome
Hillhome.basicInfo: A halfling village built into the gentle hills of the Everwood Valley, where round doors and grass-roofed hill-homes peek from every slope and plots of vegetables, herbs and fruit trees fill the dells between them. Kids and dogs run loose and someone is always calling someone else across the lanes or stopping by unannounced. Doors are unlocked or, better yet, open, and not knowing someone's name is downright rude.
Hillhome.hiddenInfo: Hillhome is a halfling-majority village in the Everwood Valley, with a steady minority of humans and elves who've settled in over the years. Halflings here run to large families and longstanding rivalries between them: the Mossbottoms and the Sweetmeads, the Honeycutts and the Tickbottoms, the Greenbottles and everyone else. Most of these rivalries are friendly — bragging at the Fair, friendly insults at the Saucy Chicken, the occasional pie thrown in jest. The exception is the Balewoods and the Goldspoons: the Goldspoons own most of the village mill-rights and lend coin at hard terms, and have been trying to acquire The Saucy Chicken for thirty years. Swapping gossip is an act as natural as breathing; silence for high elves and the dearly departed. The Newmoon Fair is the village's main event, contested in pies, ale, archery, sheep-shearing and singing, with the Balewoods holding the pie-crown for as long as anyone alive can remember. Beyond the village, the Truffle-Woods supply the moonbloom truffles that anchor the local cuisine.

Hill Street.description: The main lane of Hillhome, winding between the hills that the village is built into. Round doors and round windows on every slope, gardens spilling between them.

Hearthhome.description: A cozy hill-home on Hill Street with a bright blue door and window boxes full of flowers. Inside, wooden beams cross the ceiling of the warm living room, while a study nook offers views across the village. The cellar stays cool enough for storing food, and the small garden outside provides fresh herbs.

The Saucy Chicken Tavern.description: The Balewood family's tavern and the heart of the village, where halflings come together in the evening to drink, eat, talk, and sing. A long bar of polished oak, low rafters hung with dried herbs and copper pots, mismatched chairs around mismatched tables, a great hearth that's always lit. Pernilla Balewood's truffle-pies have made the place famous across the Vale.

Manu's Blacksmith Shop.description: A stout stone-and-timber forge built against a hillside, with the living quarters dug into the hill behind. Manu Morganstone — the village's human blacksmith — works the anvil under a wide-eaved roof; tools, harness, mended kettles and the occasional sword hang from pegs along the walls. The forge runs hot most days, and the door is usually open.

Bark and Dandy's Home.description: A modest hill-home on a side lane off Hill Street, with a slightly crooked round door and a garden that has clearly been planted by enthusiastic siblings rather than a careful adult. Bark Balewood and his older sibling Dandy live here; the inside is warm, lived-in, and rarely entirely tidy.

Goldspoon Manor.description: Two hills, joined. Three generations ago, the Goldspoons decided one hill wasn't enough and paid a small army of halfling diggers to bridge the gap, producing the only double-hill home in the Vale. The doors are painted gold, the windows stained glass, and a little pond with imported koi has replaced the garden. A lackered wrought-iron gate is guarded at all hours by the family's servants. The family crest, a spoon rampant on a field of wheat, is carved above the door.

Greenbottle's Goods.description: The village general store, run by Tobin Greenbottle out of a hill-home with the front parlor knocked through and rebuilt as a shopfront. Inside, the shelves are stacked floor to low halfling ceiling with the daily necessities of Vale life: flour and salt, candles and lamp-oil, twine and tallow, jars of preserves with handwritten labels, dried herbs in bundles, sturdy boots in halfling sizes (and a single shelf of bigger boots for other visitors), wool blankets, fishing line, sewing notions, a glass case of small luxuries — sugar lumps, smoked salt, candied ginger from the Spice Isles.

The Fairground.description: A wide grassy meadow at the south edge of the village, ringed by oaks. Most of the month it's a quiet village common — kids practicing archery, dogs chasing each other, the occasional courting couple. Wooden booth-frames sit stacked at one edge. A great oak at the meadow's heart, the Festival Oak, has names and dates carved into its bark. From the Festival Oak you can see the Sleeping Bear's smoke, the lower slopes where Knothole begins, and the willows down by the brook. Come the monthly Newmoon Fair, the field transforms — pavilions, ribbons, contest rings, pie-tables.

The Sleeping Bear Inn.description: A welcoming inn built half into a hillside at the south edge of the village proper, where the lane down from Hill Street meets the open ground of the Fairground. The common room features a massive stone fireplace and comfortable chairs; the innkeeper, Rosie Butterworth, serves excellent meals and keeps a well-stocked cellar. Travelers passing through the Vale lodge here, which makes the Sleeping Bear the part of Hillhome most likely to know what's happening in the wider world.

Mossfoot Brook.description: A clear shallow stream that runs along the south edge of the village, fed by springs in the hills above. Flat stones along the banks have been worn smooth by generations of laundry; a deeper pool at the bend below the willows is where the village kids learn to swim and the village grandfathers fish for stickleback. A simple footbridge crosses where the path comes down from Hill Street. In summer, half the village ends up here at some point in the afternoon. In winter, ice fishing, and the kids slide on the frozen pool.

Knothole.description: The wrong side of the village, if Hillhome has one. Tucked below the Fairground on the lower slopes, where the village proper gives way to weedy gardens and crooked doors, Knothole is a cluster of smaller hill-homes where nobody asks where anyone got their goods from. Halflings of dubious reputation drink on stoops, kids of dubious parentage run errands of dubious legality, and the mead being sold without a license is — most agree — actually quite good. Respectable Hillhome clucks its tongue about Knothole and does not look directly at it.

Honeycutt's Apiary.description: A modest hill-home set above the village on a path winding up through wildflowers, with a row of painted wooden hives along a sun-warmed slope. The bees move in slow purposeful clouds between hives and meadow; an old halfling chair sits in the shade of an apple tree, with a battered walking staff usually leaning beside it.

Withergleam's Cottage.description: A small thatched cottage set apart from the village on the lane that climbs toward the truffle-woods, half-buried in a wildling garden of foxglove, comfrey, mint, and a hundred other plants. Bunches of drying herbs hang from the porch eaves. A worn flagstone path leads to a green door that's left ajar most days.

The Truffle-Woods.name: The Truffle-Woods
The Truffle-Woods.basicInfo: The wooded slopes above Hillhome where the village's truffle-hogs forage. Old oaks and chestnuts, mossy roots, brambles and bracken. Halfling foragers have walked the same paths here for generations, and the woods feel safe enough — at least the parts close to the village.
The Truffle-Woods.hiddenInfo: The Truffle-Woods are the cleared belt of the Everwood that Hillhome treats as its own — bordered uphill by the Wardens' line, beyond which the deep woods begin and outsiders do not go. Tinytrolls have nested in the bracken and steal from foragers.

The Forager's Path.description: A well-worn footpath leading into the woods, where halfling truffle-hunters have walked for generations. The trees thicken as you climb. Old oaks with mossy roots, bracken to the knee

The Truffle Patches.description: Where the truffles grow, if you know how to look — under the old oaks, in the soft earth between root and root. Halfling truffle-hogs have been working this ground for so long the soil itself smells faintly of moonbloom in the right season.

The Bramble Hollow.description: A low dell choked with thorn-brambles, where the path forks and most halflings turn back. The brambles are tall and thick enough to scratch through cloth. Beyond the hollow, the woods grow older and quieter.

The Felled Giant.name: The Felled Giant
The Felled Giant.basicInfo: A Greatheart lies here - one of the sacred trees where druids once communed with the forest's deepest spirits. Something felled it long ago. The trunk stretches longer than a city block, bark like weathered stone, hollow chambers within where the heartwood rotted out. The road passes close enough to see it through the trees. Travelers who stray from the path sometimes don't come back.
The Felled Giant.hiddenInfo: The tree isn't dead. It's been dying for centuries, and its death is hungry. Sap pools have thickened into something toxic - animals that drink become twisted, aggressive, drawn to drag prey back toward the roots. The hollow interior is worse. People who enter feel welcomed, warm, sleepy. They don't leave. The druids could end it but won't destroy a Greatheart, even a ruined one. The League posts warnings and pays hunters to cull the corrupted wildlife. No one's solved the deeper problem.

The Scar.description: Where the road passes closest to the fallen tree. A worn path branches off toward the massive trunk - pilgrims still come despite the warnings, and hunters come despite the pilgrims. League markers warn travelers to stay on the road. From here, the scale becomes real: the trunk rises higher than a house, stretching away into green gloom.

The Hollow.description: A wound in the trunk where the heartwood rotted out, opening into chambers within. It smells sweet inside - too sweet, like overripe fruit. Soft light filters through cracks in the bark. The walls are warm to the touch. Deeper in, the passages branch and descend.

The Roots.description: Where the root ball tore from the earth when the tree fell, leaving a cavern of tangled roots descending into darkness. Pools of thickened sap glow faintly amber. The corrupted creatures lair here - things that were once deer, boar, wolves, now something else. Bones accumulate in the deeper hollows. Some say the roots reach all the way down to the tunnels below.

Serpent's Coil.name: Serpent's Coil
Serpent's Coil.basicInfo: A river bend where the water runs deep and the current coils back on itself. Boats are often attacked here - hulls staved in, crew dragged under, cargo scattered downstream. The river's the fastest route for moving goods out of the Everwood, and the attacks are a constant threat to trade.
Serpent's Coil.hiddenInfo: A river wyrm lairs in the deep pool at the bend - old, territorial, large enough to capsize a barge. It's been here longer than Sanctuary. Ophidians have settled the riverbank, worshipping the wyrm as a god and feeding it offerings - including captives from the boats they help wreck. The attacks aren't random; they're directed. The cult is small but growing, drawing desperate folk who seek transformation. Their leader is far along the change, more serpent than human now, and claims to speak the wyrm's will.

The Wrack.description: A gravel shore where the current deposits debris and wreckage from attacked boats. Splintered hull planks, torn canvas, scattered cargo.

The Deeps.description: The pool at the river's bend, where the water turns black and the current stills. From above, you can see the darkness where the bottom drops away.

The Traitor's Rest.name: The Traitor's Rest
The Traitor's Rest.basicInfo: A burial ground in a hollow where the trees grow thin and wrong. During the Kindred War, elves who broke faith were executed and interred here - oathbreakers from both sides, high elf and dark elf together in unmarked graves. The victors warded the place and forbade anyone to speak of it. The forest has been trying to swallow it ever since. Lately the wards have weakened. The dead are restless.
The Traitor's Rest.hiddenInfo: This is the one place in the Everwood where high elf and dark elf dead share ground - traitors, deserters, those who switched sides or refused to fight. Both factions contributed bodies; neither acknowledges the place exists. The wards were meant to keep the dead contained, not honored. Now they're failing. The spirits that stir aren't mindless - they remember the war, the betrayals, the executions. Some want revenge. Some want to be remembered. Some want the names that were taken from them. The Wardens know the wards are weakening but investigating means admitting the place exists.

The Withered Ring.description: A clearing where the undergrowth dies back and the trees lean away, as if repelled. Ward-stones stand at intervals, carved with faded binding marks. Several have cracked or toppled. The ground is uneven - dozens of graves, unmarked, overgrown. Nothing grows directly over them. At the clearing's center, a stone slab lies broken in two.

The Sunken Crypt.description: Beneath the broken slab, stairs descend into a chamber carved from living rock. This is where they buried the worst of them - or the most important, depending on who's telling the story. Niches in the walls hold remains wrapped in rotting shrouds. Names were chiseled off the stones; you can see where they were.

Canopy.name: Canopy
Canopy.basicInfo: The high elven capital, built among colossal silverleaf trees with the ancient First Tree at its heart. The city rises in tiers connected by winding staircases carved into the trunks. Outsiders are welcome here, though the upper reaches are elven domain.
Canopy.hiddenInfo: The Council of Elf-Lords governs the Everwood. They are divided on the dark elf question: hawks want the Sundering enforced more harshly, doves seek reconciliation, and the majority holds to containment and vigilance. The Wardens who enforce high elven law throughout the Everwood answer directly to the Council. Elves at the lower levels are accustomed to outsiders and many find them amusing - particularly their fear of heights, which elves consider endearing. The staircases are safe but exposed, and the drops are real. Elves move through their city with effortless grace and would never be so clumsy as to fall. Outsiders who show courage, usefulness, or genuine respect for elven ways are welcome. Those who cause trouble are simply escorted down and out.

Greenshade.description: The ground level of Canopy, where the silverleaf trunks rise like pillars and the light filters down green-gold through the city above. A market fills the spaces between the great trees - elven craftwork, forest goods, food stalls serving honeycakes and smoked game. Lodging houses cater to visitors. The staircases begin here, winding up the trunks into the branches.

The Silver Bough.description: A tavern and inn built where three silverleaf trunks grow close together, their branches forming a natural platform a hundred feet up. The common room wraps around all three trunks, open-sided. The drinks are excellent. The company is better - off-duty Wardens, traders from the Understory, elven artisans, and the occasional outsider who's earned a regular seat. The best source of news and rumor in the city.

The Warden Hall.description: Headquarters of the Wardens, built around a massive branch of the First Tree. Armories, planning chambers, quarters for Wardens between assignments. A board posts current concerns throughout the Everwood - beast sightings, border incidents, disturbances that need investigating.

The Academy.description: Where elven mages study the old arts in chambers shaped from living wood. Libraries of pressed-leaf books, workshops for enchantment, gardens for meditation. Students spend decades here before they're considered merely competent. Outsider mages with genuine talent can petition for a guest residency - the elves are selective but curious about magical traditions they haven't encountered.

The High Boughs.description: Three hundred feet up, where the noble houses keep their estates among the upper branches. The architecture is less built than persuaded - walls of living bark, floors of woven branch, windows the trees agreed to leave open. The Council of Elf-Lords meets in a chamber grown from the First Tree itself.

The Crown.description: The top of the canopy, where you step out of the leaves onto a platform above the world. An endless sea of green stretches in every direction, unbroken to the horizon. Wind moves through the treetops in slow waves. Druids tend a small sacred space here.

Web.name: Web
Web.basicInfo: A colony of giant spiders in the high canopy where a vast web stretches between ancient trees like a second forest above the forest floor. The Spinners — intelligent, horse-sized spiders — are known for trading their legendarily strong silk threads to those able to navigate their often alien ways.
Web.hiddenInfo: Spinners cannot speak; their communication is telepathic impression: images, emotions, senses. They understand speech but respond in projections. They're deeply curious about other minds, but have often been wrongly accused of kidnapping and murder of forest folk, even though their ways forbid preying on sentient species. Their silk is the finest textile in the Everwood — light, strong, and fire-resistant. They trade it for things they find beautiful or interesting, which is unpredictable and often makes little sense to non-Spinners. Spinner territory is clearly marked with boundary-webs. Those who cross uninvited get captured and brought to the Span to be inspected by the Weavespeaker, the colony's eldest, and the only Spinner who can project something close to language — full sentences rather than impressionistic flashes.

The Edge.description: Where the boundary-webs begin — shimmering lines of silk strung between trees at every height, catching light like dew. The webs aren't sticky but they vibrate at a touch, announcing visitors.

The Spokes.description: The main habitable area — a network of silk platforms stretched between tree trunks a hundred feet up, strong enough to hold a dozen people, swaying gently. The silk is translucent in daylight and faintly luminous at night. Spinners move freely across vertical and horizontal surfaces.

The Hub.description: An enclosed silk chamber where the Weavespeaker receives visitors.

Horn Rock.name: Horn Rock
Horn Rock.basicInfo: A ramhorn stronghold on the cliff heights above Adamant's gorge. The ibex-folk who hold it call it Krag. They've carved holds into the vertical cliff face and connected them with paths no one without hooves could walk.
Horn Rock.hiddenInfo: The ramhorns are mountain beastfolk: stocky, sure-footed, with curving horns they use as weapons in devastating charging strikes. They scale surfaces that should be unclimbable. The current hold-chief has been fighting the elves for forty years. He respects the Legion's courage and despises their claim to his canyon. His children lead the raiding parties and are less patient than their father. The stalemate with the elves has lasted decades, without any end in sight - but ramhorns never back down.

The High Track.description: A narrow path up the cliff face that a ramhorn walks casually and anyone else walks with their back pressed to the rock. Handholds are carved at ramhorn height — too low for most humans, too high for halflings. The wind is constant. The view is extraordinary if you don't look down.

Krag Hall.description: A natural cave enlarged into a great hall where the ramhorns gather. The ceiling is high enough for a troll and the hearth-fire is built for warmth against mountain wind. The chief holds court here on a stone seat worn smooth by generations of hold-chiefs. Trophies from defeated rivals line the walls — the ramhorns' only record of their history.

The Warren.description: Living quarters carved into the cliff — dozens of small chambers connected by passages sized for ramhorn shoulders. Families, stores, a forge that makes simple but effective weapons. The smell of mountain herbs and cook-fires.

The Perch.description: A high ledge overlooking the valley below. Ramhorn scouts maintain constant watch.

Adamant.name: Adamant
Adamant.basicInfo: A high elven fortress-city built into a gorge of silver-white adamant stone. Waterfalls pour through the chasm in sheets of mist and thunder. But step through the Gorge Gate and the fortress reveals a green valley along the river, warm and sheltered, where gardens and orchards grow beneath the high walls. The Legion of the Vigil garrisons the fortress and guards the Everwood.
Adamant.hiddenInfo: Adamantines believe Canopy has grown soft, too focused on trade and politics while forgetting why the Sundering happened, and also too tolerant of the beastfolk aggression from the Wild Grass Sea. They see themselves as the true defenders of elvenkind and the Everwood. The Legion recruits outsiders and treats them as equals once they've proven themselves — a radical stance by high elven standards. New recruits drill hard but are welcomed into a brotherhood. Veterans who settle in the valley raise families, tend gardens, and answer the horn when it sounds. Secretly, the Legion conducts raids into the dark elf tunnel network. The Sundering's terms forbid both sides from crossing the surface boundary — surface elves stay above, dark elves stay below. Adamant has stopped respecting this bargain. They hit dark elf outposts, destroy tunnels. The dark elves retaliate. This quiet war has been escalating for decades, and it's why dark elf incursions near Adamant are worse than elsewhere in the Everwood.

The Gorge Gate.description: The main entrance where the river flows out through a narrow gap in the adamant walls. Twin watchtowers carved from living stone flank the passage. Beyond, the valley opens in a rush of green and the sound of falling water.

The Greenway.description: The valley floor along the riverside - the heart of Adamant and the reason people stay. Cottage gardens and small orchards line the water. A tavern called The Seventh Bell serves everyone from mud-spattered recruits to off-duty commanders. Market stalls sell valley produce and Legion surplus. Children fish in the shallows while veterans mend gear on their porches.

The Legion Grounds.description: Where the Legion of the Vigil trains and musters, at the valley's far end where the gorge narrows again. Barracks, armories, the sparring yard where recruits learn that elven swordsmanship is faster than they thought. The mess hall is the social center - long tables, loud arguments about technique, and a tradition that new recruits buy the first round. A board posts current assignments and open patrols.

The Veil Stair.description: A switchback stair carved into the gorge wall, climbing behind the largest waterfall to the cave-temples above. The spray soaks everyone who climbs. At the top, the Skymirror Temple opens onto a ledge where priests tend sacred pools and read the dragon's dreams.

Skymirror Temple.description: A temple complex in the caves behind the great waterfall, where the roar of water drowns all but shouts. Sacred pools reflect the sky through gaps in the stone. Here Adamantines renew their oaths and priests interpret the river dragon's prophetic dreams.

World-vault.name: World-vault
World-vault.basicInfo: Maanvalta. The great cavern-city of the dark elves and capital of their underground realm. It lies deep beneath the Everwood at the center of a vast tunnel network that extends throughout the forest and beyond. Under the ancient terms of the Sundering, the high elves brook no trespass by the dark elves in the World Above, but they also do not interfere with surfacers who choose to visit the World Below. Many make the journey, for dark elf craft, magic, and resources are found nowhere else.
World-vault.hiddenInfo: The cavern is vast. The Root-sky — a constellation of roots glowing amber to violet — casts the a dim twilight glow across the enormous space. In the cener, world-root, a great column of interwoven roots, descends from the ceiling into the World-spring, an enormous underground lake. The city is built into stalactites, stalagmites, the cavern walls, and World-root itself. Dark elves are the finest illusionists in Larion and legendary thieves and assassins. The city is also a hub of the life-stone trade, its tunnel networks providing it perfect access to smuggle the illegal life-extending substance to the surface. The city's culture prizes wit, craft, style and ostentatious wealth. Being dull is worse than being wrong. Competing noble houses dominate city life — they recruit surfacers as agents and proxies in trade and diplomacy. Surfacers can make a fortune here - or find their way to an early grave. Dark elves are notoriously charming and decadent, but panache and manipulation go hand-in-hand here.

The Mouth.description: A surface clearing deep within the Everwood where a dark opening descends into the earth. Above the opening is carved the root-arch symbol of the dark-elves, marking this as a gateway to their underground domain.

The Ledgeways.description: A series of carved stone ledges along the cavern's upper wall, where tunnels open from every direction and paths wind down toward the lake. From here the city reveals itself — the huge World-root piercing the cavern ceiling and extending down into the underground lake, the stalactites hanging from the ceiling and stalagmites rising from the dark water crowded with structures and connected by bridges.

The Lakeshore.description: The waterfront that rings the great underground lake known as the World-spring, where the business of the city happens. Markets spread along the shore — dark elf craftwork in carved stone and spider-silk, fungi and deep-earth spices. Boats load and unload at stone quays. Dark elves and foreigners throng the shore, trading, arguing, eating at stalls.

The Still Isle.description: An island in the underground lake reached by boat. On it rises a monastery of dark stone where a dark elf monastic order known as the Still Ones study shadow magic, illusion, and the arts of concealment.

The Needle.description: A thin stalactite high above the lake, reached by a single bridge. Home to the Unseen — an cross-House federation of spies and assassins dedicated to protecting dark elf interests by whatever means necessary. Their apothecaries are famous for poisons that leave no trace.

The Kettle.description: A deep hollow in the cavern floor where hot springs bubble up from below, filling the air with mineral steam. Serpentfolk, deep gnomes, and other underground peoples have settled here. A neighborhood that runs by its own customs. The steam makes it hard to see far, which suits the residents fine.

The Hang-Over Inn.description: An inn and tavern in a hollow stalactite over the Lakeshore, opened up with arched windows that look out across the underground lake. The best tavern in the city, or at least the one where surfacers are most likely to end up. House agents drink here alongside merchants, off-duty soldiers, and ordinary dark elves.

World-root.description: The great root-column at the city's heart, wide as a castle. The dark elf court meets in chambers grown within the living wood. Temples occupy the deepest hollows where the roots meet the water. Bridges and stairways spiral up through the tangle, crowded with petitioners, courtiers, and priests.

The Stoneways.description: Where most dark elves live. Stalactites and stalagmites carved into dense warrens of apartments, workshops, and local markets, connected by stone bridges at every level. Some columns are wealthy house compounds behind carved gates. Others are packed neighborhoods where laundry hangs from bridge-rails and arguments carry across the gaps.

The Stonetome.description: A large stalactite, hollowed into a walkable history of the dark elf people. Visitors enter at the top where dioramas carved from living stone begin with the united elven kingdom. The path spirals downward through the Kindred War, the Betrayal, the Sundering. At the bottom, the final scene: the Exodus, dark elves descending into the earth. At the end, the passage opens onto the Lakeshore and the city they built.

The Dims.description: Where the stalactites and stalagmites grow so dense the root-light barely reaches. A maze of narrow bridges, tight passages, and carved-out chambers stacked on top of each other. Dark elves who've lost house patronage end up here. So do the trades the city prefers not to acknowledge — fences, forgers, brokers of dark magic.

The Farwall.description: The cavern's far reaches where dark elf soldiers garrison the tunnels leading deeper underground. They watch for threats from below — things older and stranger than elves live in the deep earth.

The Wildmarket.name: The Wildmarket
The Wildmarket.basicInfo: A sprawling trading ground outside Sanctuary's walls, beyond the reach of the wardstones. What can't be sold in the city gets sold here: dark elf goods through intermediaries, forest products that don't survive the wards, fey-touched items, work that can't be posted on the League board. The market spreads through forest clearings and beneath ancient trees, stalls and blankets and shouting merchants, dawn to dusk only - traders who linger past sunset take their chances with what comes out at night. No guards, no law, no protection. The traders police themselves, badly. Everyone needs this place to exist, so everyone tolerates its chaos.
The Wildmarket.hiddenInfo: This is the only place where surface and underground trade meets. Dark elves can't come themselves, but their intermediaries operate openly in the Grove Market - humans, halflings, wood elves who carry goods up from below and take surface products back down. High elf Wardens watch from the crowds, everyone knows they're there, but the market is too useful and too chaotic to shut down. They're looking for dark elves foolish enough to come in person, not the trade itself. Fey creatures who won't cross Sanctuary's wards do business here - satyrs, dryads, stranger things. If you need a guide into the deep forest, a message carried underground, or work that won't appear on any official board, this is where you come.

Wardside.description: The market's edge nearest Sanctuary, where the city walls are visible through the trees. Established merchants set up here - sturdy stalls, familiar goods, quick retreat to the wardstones if trouble starts. The safest part of the market, which isn't saying much.

The Trading Ground.description: The market's chaotic heart. Blankets spread with goods, shouting merchants, buyers picking through piles of forest herbs and fungi and craft-work. Dwarves haggling over deep metals. Halflings selling provisions to expedition parties. A dozen deals happening at once, every one of them someone's livelihood.

The Satyr Stands.description: Wine stalls and tavern-tents where satyrs pour drinks that range from merely strong to genuinely dangerous. Music, dice games, gossip. If you want to know what's happening in the forest - or find someone who knows someone who can help with a problem - buy a round and start talking.

The Grove Market.description: Deeper into the trees. Dark elf goods spread on blankets - fungi, cave-spider silk, maps of passages below. The rule here is don't ask where things come from or where they're going.

Backshadow.description: The market's far edge, where the forest closes in and the light dims even at noon.

Velantami.name: Velantami
Velantami.basicInfo: A massive hollow oak that serves as the sacred headquarters of the Everwood's main druid circle, the Friends of the Forest. Known as the Oathoak in Common. The tree's trunk could hold a castle, with chambers and halls carved by centuries of habitation. Druids from all Pact races study here, maintaining forest balance, recording lore, and teaching the old ways. The tree where the original Pact of Sanctuary was signed has become a living city of perhaps two hundred druids and their initiates.
Velantami.hiddenInfo: The druids are divided on how to handle increasing disruptions in the forest's balance - some believe in a hands-off approach, others think more aggressive action is needed. They've been tracking the movements of something huge in the Deep Woods that hasn't been seen but leaves valleys stripped of life. The eldest druids know that the forest was once much darker and more hostile. Several of them have gone missing while investigating disturbances at the edge of the Deep Woods.

The Glade.description: A cleared circle around the massive oak's base where visitors first arrive. The tree towers above, its trunk wider than a village square. Petitioners wait here on carved benches, initiates practice forms on the soft grass, and druids meet with rangers bringing news from the forest. A carved archway marks the entrance to the hollow trunk.

The Great Hall.description: The main chamber within the trunk where the full circle gathers for important decisions. The Pact's original text spirals up the walls in multiple languages. A massive fire pit in the center never goes out. Initiates serve meals here and all are welcome at the long tables.

The Speaking Floor.description: A wide platform built around the oak's exterior where druids teach initiates and debate philosophy. Carved benches face a central speaking stone. Hawks and ravens perch in the branches above, some of them more than just birds.

The Rookery.description: Upper chambers where druids maintain messenger birds and flying creatures. Also serves as the circle's library, with centuries of observations carved into the living wood. Young druids compete to be assigned here to learn from the lore-keepers.

The Root Halls.description: Living quarters carved among the massive roots. Senior druids have private chambers while initiates sleep in common halls. A natural spring here provides water. The social heart of the community, where off-duty druids drink, game, and argue.

The Grove of Stones.description: A ritual space beneath the tree where ward-stones are created and repaired. Also contains the circle's seedbank - thousands of seeds from every plant in the Everwood, preserved against catastrophe. Only full druids may enter without invitation.

Vilitara Grove.name: Vilitara Grove
Vilitara Grove.basicInfo: A satyr village built among ancient grape arbors in the Everwood, where wild vines climb towering trees and the air is sweet with fermenting fruit. The satyrs here are master brewers of wines and potions, known throughout the region for their powerful and intoxicating concoctions. Their seasonal festivals draw visitors from across Larion, though many leave with only hazy memories of what transpired.
Vilitara Grove.hiddenInfo: The village's location shifts slightly with each full moon, following the migration patterns of certain rare berries essential to their most potent brews. The satyrs' famous wines contain trace amounts of fey magic that makes drinkers more susceptible to enchantment. Several noble houses in Ardan's Watch pay handsomely for their vintages, unaware that drinking them has bound them into ancient fairy contracts. The village elder, Master Bromius, maintains a network of informants who trade gossip for wine, making the grove a surprising nexus of regional intelligence hidden behind its reputation for endless revelry.

Grovegate.description: The village entrance where massive grape arbors form natural archways. Visitors are immediately offered cups of wine and drawn into whatever celebration is currently ongoing. Strings of glowing berries illuminate the space, and the ground is scattered with flower petals.

The Dell.description: A natural depression filled with enormous wooden casks and clay amphoras where the satyrs age their wines and potions. The air is thick with intoxicating fumes, and even the mushrooms growing here can cause mild euphoria if touched.

The Garden.description: A chaotic but beautiful garden where satyrs cultivate rare berries, herbs, and flowers for their concoctions. Small stills and experimental batches bubble everywhere, tended by apprentice brewers who sample their work perhaps too frequently.

The Nook.description: A relatively quiet corner where the more business-minded satyrs conduct trade with merchants brave enough to visit. Samples are mandatory, and negotiations often devolve into drinking contests.

The Circle.description: A wide clearing where the nightly revels center, surrounded by naturally curved trees. Musicians play from tree platforms while revelers whirl below. The ground has been worn smooth by countless hooves.

Tale Corner.description: A cozy nook where cushions and low tables surround a bonfire. Here, visitors trade gossip and stories for drinks, while the satyrs sing songs and tell jokes.

The Elder's Arbor.description: The home of Master Bromius, built into an ancient grape arbor. Despite the chaos elsewhere, this space maintains a certain dignity. Master Bromius conducts 'serious business' here when needed, though his definition of 'serious' usually involves sampling the wares.

Almaki.name: Almaki
Almaki.basicInfo: A pixie settlement built around and within an ancient flowering hill, where the worlds of big folk and little folk intersect. The Overhill contains buildings sized for normal races - a tavern, trading post, and the famous Cordial Shop where visitors purchase shrinking elixir. Those who drink it can enter the Underhill, a vast pixie city of colorful chambers and flower-clad halls hidden within the hill itself.
Almaki.hiddenInfo: The pixies' constant 'borrowing' follows ancient fey laws of reciprocal exchange - they always return something of equal value, just never what was actually taken. The shrinking cordial requires morning dew from flowers that bloom only during the full moon, mixed with pixie dust freely given. The Pixie Queen maintains a network of spies who trade gossip for favors. Several items in the Queen's collection are actually powerful artifacts that their original owners don't realize are missing.

The Overhill Commons.description: A meadow before the hill where normal-sized buildings cluster. Merchant wagons park beside a massive message board covered in tiny scrolls. Pixies flit between the tavern and trading post, offering guidance and mischief in equal measure. The famous Honeydew Tavern serves both big and little folk from different-sized bars.

The Trading Circle.description: An open market where pixies exchange their crafts and goods with larger folk. The Speaker mediates disputes and negotiates deals.

The Cordial Shop.description: A peculiar building riddled with tiny doors through which pixies constantly emerge. Here visitors purchase the shrinking cordial (reversible at will) needed to enter the Underhill. The shopkeeper, an ancient pixie named Grandmother Thimble, judges who is worthy to enter based on criteria known only to her.

The Bloom Gate.description: A flower-wreathed entrance only visible to those who've drunk the cordial. Pixie guards in acorn helmets ceremoniously 'inspect' all who enter, though their inspections mainly involve jokes and riddles. Beyond lies a tunnel of earth covered in flowers.

The Underhill Plaza.description: The heart of the pixie city, a vast chamber within the hill where hundreds of pixies live in flower-covered homes carved from the living rock. A fountain of liquid moonlight provides illumination while market stalls, workshops, and gathering places fill every level. Stairs and slides connect the various tiers.

The Queen's Court.description: The throne room where the Pixie Queen holds court, its walls lined with 'borrowed' treasures from across the realm. Her throne is carved from a single solid dewdrop. Only those with specific business or invitation may approach.

Korpimire.name: Korpimire
Korpimire.basicInfo: A boggy glen deep in the Everwood said to be where three hag sisters are magically bound to share territory despite their hatred for each other. They offer dark services to those brave or desperate enough to seek them out: true visions, flesh-shaping, and curses to order. Every deal has a price, and every price has a catch.
Korpimire.hiddenInfo: The sisters were trapped here by an elven archmage centuries ago. The binding prevents them from leaving or directly harming each other, so they scheme through mortal proxies. The Seer trades true visions but enjoys revealing futures that drive people to despair. The Shaper's transformations always include a hidden flaw. The Howler sells curses that twist to harm the buyer as much as the target. The bog's paths shift with the sisters' moods - when they fight, safe routes become deadly. They occasionally cooperate against a common enemy, which is worse.

Bog Landing.description: Where solid ground gives way to mire. Three boggarts squat on rotting stumps, one loyal to each sister, each spouting promises about their mistress's superior magic while spreading lies about the other two. They'll guide you to whichever hag they serve - for a price.

The Truce Stone.description: An ancient menhir at the bog's heart where the binding spell is carved in Old Elvish. The three territories meet here. Each sister has a dwelling visible from this spot - the Seer's mirror-hut in pale birches, the Shaper's cocoon on a peat mound, the Howler's bone-and-spite den in a hollow.

The White Tower.name: The White Tower
The White Tower.basicInfo: A peculiar, immaculuately maintained white stone tower standing alone in the forest.
The White Tower.hiddenInfo: The tower used to belong to a wizard whose death was caused by a backfiring experimental ritual meant to give her spells more autonomy. As a result of this ritual gone awry, her former spells have taken become spellhaunts: sentient spells with humanoid physical form. Each spellhaunt manifestation attempting to fulfill its original purpose in increasingly confused ways. The tower remains in pristine condition due to an obsessive Mend spell that constantly repairs any damage. Recently, some have begun attempting to leave the tower, though crossing the threshold seems to pain them. The wizard's body still sits in her study chair, perfectly preserved by Mend's constant repairs.

Tower Entry.description: The ground floor entrance from the forest where Shield manifests as a translucent knight.

The Living Room.description: A cozy chamber where Fire Bolt lives in the hearth as a salamander of pure flame.

Kitchen Level.description: A well-equipped kitchen where Prestidigitation manifests as a fussy little sprite that compulsively cooks food and cleans up.

Library Level.description: Bookshelves line the walls while Detect Thoughts floats about as a weeping eye, discovering and crying over the secrets in ancient tomes..

The Workshop.description: The wizard's laboratory where Mend scurries about as a ghostly handyman, fixing everything.

Observatory.description: The tower's top level where Clairvoyance constantly recounts visions from nearby locations. Feather Fall drifts around as a gentle breeze.

Rajameza Ranger Station.name: Rajameza Ranger Station
Rajameza Ranger Station.basicInfo: A fortified ranger post where the Everwood meets the Sunless Depths, built from massive logs and stone to withstand attacks from the dark forest.'
Rajameza Ranger Station.hiddenInfo: Rangers of all races maintain constant watch here, tracking and eliminating threats. Despite being understaffed, they take pride in holding the line. Every ranger here has sworn the Border Oath: 'Nothing passes unmarked, nothing dark goes unchallenged.' More creatures pass each month, stranger things that shouldn't exist. They've stopped reporting the worst incidents to avoid causing panic. Three rangers have gone missing this season, but their bodies were never found.

The Main Lodge.description: A sturdy two-story building of thick logs and stone where rangers eat, plan, and rest between patrols. The main hall has a massive fireplace that never goes cold, walls covered with maps, and a board listing patrol schedules. Rangers gather here at dawn and dusk for briefings.

The Watchtower.description: A tower providing clear views into the Sunless Depths. Rangers maintain constant watch, using horns to signal different types of threats. The top platform holds a ballista and signal fire.

The Training Yard.description: An open ground where rangers drill with weapons and practice archery. A corner holds practice dummies wrapped in armor for sword work.

The Stockade.description: A reinforced building serving as armory, storage, and emergency shelter. Weapons are well tended: silver-tipped arrows, cold iron blades, and torches that burn even in rain. The cellar holds preserved food for three months and a well for water. Heavy shutters can seal every opening. Rangers joke it's where they'll make their last stand.

Eanleana.name: Eanleana
Eanleana.basicInfo: Eanleana. In the Common Tongue it means 'Eternal City' or 'Final City'. The last refuge of the Undying Elves, who achieved immortality centuries ago but lost the ability to bear children. Built among ancient silverwood trees, the city rises in graceful tiers of grown wood and shaped stone, with delicate bridges spanning between platforms and towers that spiral up massive trunks.
Eanleana.hiddenInfo: The city is beautiful, vast, and largely empty. Fountains burbles, gardens bloom in riotous beauty, and halls built for thousands echo with the footsteps of the few who remain. The Undying welcome visitors with ancient courtesy, eager for news of the changing world beyond their borders. The immortality is bound to the city itself - Undying who venture beyond its boundaries age rapidly and die within days. They call this 'The Final Walk' and it's considered an honorable end when one grows too weary of eternity. The city's magic sometimes captures wanderers who stumble into its borders, offering them the same immortality if they choose to stay. Several humans and halflings from different centuries now dwell here as adopted Undying, though they too can never leave. The remaining elves are vastly powerful mages and warriors with centuries to perfect their arts, but they use their powers mainly to maintain the city and create small wonders to break the monotony of endless time.There are no children, only adults.

The Arrival Garden.description: A magnificent garden where paths from the outside world enter the city. Flowers from every season bloom simultaneously here, and fountains create rainbow mists in the eternal sunshine. A Sentinel is always waiting to receive visitors.

The Plaza of Eternity.description: A vast circular plaza paved with white stone, surrounded by graceful colonnades. At its center stands an enormous tree: the Tree of Eternity. The remaining elves gather here for their seasonal celebrations. Benches of living wood offer places to sit.

The Lower Halls.description: Residential quarters built for thousands, now mostly empty. Doors stand open to furnished homes where no one has lived for centuries. The few occupied dwellings are scattered far apart, their windows glowing with soft light. Gardens between the buildings grow wild but beautiful, and occasionally an Undying can be found tending them, taking decades to perfect a single arrangement.

The Hall of Chronicles.description: A library containing the accumulated knowledge of millennia, its shelves reaching up three stories. The Undying librarians know every book's location but sometimes take years to retrieve a requested volume. Reading alcoves provide comfortable spaces where scholars might spend decades on a single subject.

The Workshop Quarter.description: Studios and workshops where the Undying pursue their crafts with infinite patience. A sculptor might spend a century on one statue, a musician decades composing a single symphony. The tools here are ancient but perfectly maintained.

The Sunward Towers.description: The highest spires where the eldest Undying dwell, connected by bridges so delicate they seem made of spider silk. From here, one can see to the city's boundaries - the invisible line between eternal life and certain death. The towers' balconies are where Undying stand when contemplating the Final Walk, looking out at the mortal world they can never rejoin.

Stonewatch Keep.name: Stonewatch Keep
Stonewatch Keep.basicInfo: An ancient fortress defended by the Stone Wardens, golem soldiers who requested eternal service as their reward after the Fey War. The keep guards a strategic pass into the Everwood from the Sunless Depths region to the west.
Stonewatch Keep.hiddenInfo: Twenty-four Stone Wardens remain from the original hundred, their bodies of animate granite worn smooth by centuries of patrol. They challenge all who approach with formal military courtesy, requesting identification and stated business. Those who answer honestly are generally permitted to pass; those who lie or refuse are turned away; those who attack are killed without hesitation. The Stone Wardens chose eternal service because they were soldiers who found meaning only in duty - the war's end left them purposeless until this option was offered. Over centuries they've developed distinct personalities. They 'rest' by standing perfectly still in the courtyard, sometimes for years, but always wake when the keep is threatened. They've buried hundreds of would-be invaders in neat graves outside the walls, each marked with a simple stone listing date and manner of death.

The Gate Court.description: The entrance courtyard where visitors are challenged by whichever Wardens are on gate duty this decade. Two always stand at attention while a third questions arrivals from the gatehouse above. The protocol never varies: state your name, your business, and whether you mean harm to the people of the Everwood.

The Drill Yard.description: An open training ground where the Wardens practice combat forms they perfected centuries ago.

The Hall of Service.description: The keep's main hall where the Wardens gather to discuss current affairs in the forest or share memories of their 'lives' during the war.

The Battlements.description: The walls where Wardens maintain eternal watch. Each knows every tree, rock, and shadow in their sector after centuries of observation. The view extends for miles across the strategic pass they guard.

Bright Kingdoms League Chapterhouse.name: Bright Kingdoms League Chapterhouse
Bright Kingdoms League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: A fallen section of an ancient sky citadel, half-buried in the desert where it crashed centuries ago. The League of Adventurers claimed the ruin and converted it into their Bright Kingdoms headquarters. The architecture is unmistakable—horseshoe arches, geometric tilework, walls carved into lattice screens that throw sunlight in patterns across every surface. It was built for the sky and it still looks like it belongs there. One end buries into a dune; the other juts upward at an angle, crowned by a minaret that was once the tallest point of a quarter that flew. Locals call it the Fallen Star.
Bright Kingdoms League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The Fallen Star is a sheared-off section of the Sky Citadel of Alar, one of the the ancient flying fortresses that allowed the Sun Kings to rule all the Bright Kingdoms from above for three centuries. When the Citadel fell—how and why is still debated by every kingdom differently—it broke apart. This section came down in open desert and buried itself at an angle. The impact should have destroyed it, but Sky Citadel construction was made to endure forces beyond the ordinary. The walls held.

The ruin sat contested for generations. The desert tribes called it god-cursed. The League arrived two decades ago, cleared a manticore pride from the interior chambers, and established a garrison. Their neutrality is the only reason the arrangement holds—no kingdom will tolerate another possessing it, but all tolerate the League.

The Master of the House manages the most politically delicate posting in the League. Every kingdom sends representatives, spies, and petitioners. The contract board reflects this—bounties from rival courts, expeditions to other Citadel crash sites across the desert, escort jobs through hostile territory.

The buried lower chambers remain partially unexplored. Sand has filled some.

A Greater Owl roost occupies the minaret's upper tier. The birds handle the desert thermals well. The portal stone was installed on the minaret's observation platform—the highest point of the ruin, where the original builders would have been closest to the sun they revered.

Caravan routes pass near enough that the Fallen Star serves as a waypoint. A small market has grown up at its base, serving adventurers and travelers alike.

The Sand Gate.description: Where the Fallen Star meets the desert floor. The buried end of the ruin rises from the dune like a broken temple, its arched entrance half-swallowed by sand that League workers clear every season. Geometric tilework in blue and gold frames the doorway—chipped, sand-scoured, still beautiful. A small market clusters in the shade of the outer wall, selling provisions, water, and dubious maps to other crash sites. Camels and sand-horses are picketed nearby. The structure rises at an angle above, its latticed walls catching the light.

The Court of Light.description: The heart of the chapterhouse. An interior courtyard open to the sky, tilted slightly from the impact but still functional. The original geometric screens survive in the surrounding walls, casting precise patterns of light across the mosaic floor as the sun moves—the builders designed this to track the hours, and it still does. A stone fountain at the center has run without interruption since the crash, fed by channels no one fully understands. The League's contract board stands against the eastern wall. The Master of the House works from a raised gallery on the upper tier. Around the courtyard, arched doorways lead to storerooms, quarters, and the sealed passages deeper into the buried section.

The Shade.description: The lower chambers where the structure buries into the dune. Cooler than anywhere else in the desert. The League converted a series of vaulted rooms into a taproom and common area—the original tilework on the walls and ceiling makes it the most beautiful bar in the Bright Kingdoms, though sand sifts through cracks when the wind blows hard. Lanterns hang from brass hooks meant for something else. The regulars are expedition types—sun-dark, sand-scoured adventurers between jobs. At the back, a heavy door marked with League seals leads to the deeper buried sections. It's locked. The lock gets picked about once a month.

The Minaret.description: The highest surviving point of the Fallen Star, reached by a spiral stair inside the tower that climbs at the structure's tilted angle. The observation platform at the top is open to the sky and the wind. The portal stone stands here, anchored to the original stone floor, connecting to the Tower of Venture and other chapterhouses. The Greater Owl roost occupies the tier below—a latticed enclosure where the birds shelter from the midday sun and launch into the thermals at dawn and dusk. From the top, the desert stretches in every direction. Other crash sites are visible on clear days as dark shapes on the horizon.

Serendipity.name: Serendipity
Serendipity.basicInfo: Serendipity, the City of Strange Bargains, is a sprawling mercantile city-state controlling the vital river crossing between the Bright Kingdoms and the Great Lagoon. Maze-like souks and teeming bazaars spread across both banks of the Goldenflow River, connected by great fortified bridges crowded with merchant traffic. Here, desert caravans meet swamp traders and Solarian merchants in a chaotic marketplace where anything can be bought, sold, or arranged — for the right price.
Serendipity.hiddenInfo: Serendipity is ruled by the proud and ruthless Qamarid Princes, who govern through blood-bonds and water magic. Princes of deed rather than word, they duel with magic at the slightest hint of challenge to their honor and treat oaths as sacred pledges. Their motto declares 'We bow only to the sun' — and they have never forgotten a broken promise, as the city's ancient feuds attest. The population includes gnomes, centaurs, dragonkin, and clans of lizardfolk who fled the Great Lagoon when the Solarian Dominion began draining their ancestral marshes. These lizardfolk dominate the smuggling trade with their knowledge of swamp routes the legions have never mapped. The Exarcate maintains a trade mission in the Foreign Quarter — officially for commerce, unofficially to watch the river crossing and recruit informants. The Qamarid Princes tolerate the Solarian presence because Dominion coin spends as well as anyone's, but they have made it clear that Serendipity bows only to the sun.

North Gate.description: The desert-facing entrance where caravans from the Bright Kingdoms arrive dusty and road-weary. Customs inspectors weigh goods while bodyguards and guides hawk their services.

South Gate.description: The heavily fortified entrance facing the river crossing to the region of Great Lagoon. Guards check for contraband while marsh-fever physicians examine new arrivals from the swamps.

South Gate Bridge.description: The massive bridge spanning the Goldenflow River. Toll collectors and bridge guards manage the constant flow of traffic.

Spice Bazaar.description: The most prosperous market district where legitimate merchants trade in exotic goods. Colorful awnings shade stalls selling everything from desert silks to rare gems.

Perfumed Souk.description: An ironically named malodorous maze of narrow alleys and hidden stalls where questionable goods change hands. What's sold here officially doesn't exist, and questions aren't welcome.

Qamarid Palace.description: The Qamarid Palace rises from the heart of Serendipity like a mirage made stone, its soaring towers and water gardens defying the desert heat. Within, the structure follows ancient design - sanctums within sanctums, each inner chamber more private and sacred than the last, where only those bound by the deepest oaths may enter the presence of the Princes.

River Quarter.description: Docks and warehouses line both banks of the Goldenflow. Barges and river boats load cargo while dock workers and bargemen conduct their own informal markets.

Foreign Quarter.description: Embassies, trading posts, and cultural enclaves for visitors from distant lands. Each building flies different flags and follows different customs.

Caravan Quarter.description: Stables, supply shops, and lodging houses serve the desert traders. Guides offer their services for dangerous routes while beast-handlers tend to camels and pack animals.

Mudbank District.description: The poorest quarter built on stilts over the marshy ground near the swamp border. Refugees, failed merchants, and those hiding from their past cluster in ramshackle buildings.

The Golden Opportunity Inn.description: The finest establishment in the city, catering to wealthy merchants and foreign dignitaries. Deals worth kingdoms are negotiated in its private dining rooms.

The Blind Eye Tavern.description: A dimly lit establishment where smugglers and information brokers conduct business. The drinks are strong and the proprietor sees nothing, hears nothing.

The Worldly Wanderer Inn.description: An eclectic establishment decorated with curiosities from across the known world. Travelers share tales and contacts from distant lands over exotic cuisine.

Oasis.name: Oasis
Oasis.basicInfo: Oasis is the greatest city of the Bright Kingdoms, built around the world's largest oasis where springs never run dry. White stone buildings rise among palm groves and fountains, their districts protected behind high walls. This jewel of the desert produces the finest magical components, alchemy ingredients, and enchanted goods in the world. Its markets throng with traders and tomb-raiders trafficking in artifacts and treasures salvaged from the many nearby ruins.
Oasis.hiddenInfo: Oasis is ruled by House Serayaf, the Sky Lords, whose sorcerous bloodline grants them mastery over flying carpets and great power over wind and flame. Some claim their abilities stem from ancient pacts with demons or djinn - a bloodline too pure and powerful for mortal heritage alone. The city faces constant threats: tomb robberies from the Sealed Crypts have escalated, with ancient seals being broken and dangerous artifacts stolen. The Order of the Desert Rose hunts a demon cult operating in the city - worshippers who leave burned offerings and charred bodies. Desert ghouls grow bolder, attacking caravans closer to the city walls each month. Rival noble houses plot against the Serayaf, believing the Sky Lords' demonic heritage makes them unfit to rule. The population includes humans, desert elves, djinnborn, ifrit, jackalkin, pygmies, and various desert races.

The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads.description: A massive walled compound where desert caravans rest between journeys and expeditions form. Covered stables house camels and pack animals while merchants sleep in communal halls around central courtyards. Notice boards post calls for tomb raiders, beast hunters, and caravan guards. Water troughs and feeding stations serve both beasts and travelers. Equipment vendors sell climbing gear, desert survival supplies, and maps of dubious accuracy.

Great Serpent Market.description: A sprawling souk that winds through narrow streets like a serpent, dedicated to the desert deity revered for wisdom, patience, strength, and beauty. Colorful awnings shade stalls selling everything from common goods to rare alchemical components. Snake charmers and fortune tellers work the crowds. Merchants peddle sunstones, desert glass, enchanted silks, and stranger things. Information brokers trade rumors for coin in shadowed alcoves. A bronze serpent statue coils at the market's heart, where offerings are left for good fortune.
paths: The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads

The Eternal Springs.description: The sacred heart of Oasis where clear water bubbles up from deep underground sources that have never run dry. Priests in white robes guard the pools day and night. Stone channels direct the flow throughout the city while pilgrims leave offerings at shrines surrounding the main spring. The water is said to grant visions to those pure of heart. Entrance to the springs' source - caves descending into darkness below - is strictly forbidden.

Citadel of Seven Winds.description: House Serayaf's magnificent palace of white stone and golden domes, its spired towers reaching toward the desert sky. Gardens of lush beauty surround the structure, watered by spring-fed channels. Guards in flowing robes patrol the grounds, allowing entry only to those with business before the Sky Lords. The tallest tower's peak holds the Sky Platform - a restricted aerial dock where the family's legendary flying carpets land, visible from across the city but accessible to none but House Serayaf.

The Red Sands.description: A large arena where sand-covered fighting pits host gladiator matches and warrior competitions. Stone benches rise in tiers around the blood-stained sands while spectators cheer and place bets. Training halls beneath the arena offer instruction in desert combat styles - scimitar work, spear fighting, and sand-dancer techniques. Masters observe from covered walkways, occasionally recruiting promising fighters. Prize money flows freely for victors, as do offers of sponsorship from wealthy patrons.

The Sunfire Sanctum.description: A temple-school where priests and mages study the magic of fire, air and light, teaching those with the talent and coin to pay. The building's dome glows at night with the light of braziers burning with flames that never consume their fuel. Initiates practice controlling fire in warded chambers while senior mages work on enchantments and alchemical formulae. The head priest offers training in sun magic, air channelling, fire binding, and light-based healing arts. A library contains scrolls on elemental theory and divine fire.

The Alchemists' Quarter.description: A district of workshops and laboratories where skilled craftsmen work with exotic materials and rare components. The air shimmers with vapors while workers tend complex apparatus and bubbling vessels. Alchemists sell potions, magical components, and enchanted items to those who can afford their prices. Master crafters offer instruction in potion-making and reagent identification for substantial fees. Some workshops deal in darker goods behind closed doors - poisons, curse components, and substances best not named.
paths: The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads

The Sealed Crypts.description: Ancient stone passages beneath the city where Oasis's honored dead rest in elaborate tombs filled with grave goods. Entrance is forbidden on pain of being burned alive - only the blindfolded Priests of the Dead may walk these sacred halls. Despite the prohibition, tomb raiders strike regularly, seeking the treasures and magical artifacts buried with ancient nobles. The deeper passages are said to connect to older ruins from before the city's founding.

Terrace Town.description: A cramped slum district where rickety wooden homes are stacked precariously on stone foundations, each level built atop the last. Flat roofs sit close enough that the agile can leap between them, creating a second network of passages above the maze-like alleys below. Washing lines stretch between buildings while children play on precarious ledges. Smugglers use hidden tunnels beneath the district to move contraband. The Thieves' Guild maintains quiet influence here, and the city guard rarely patrols these twisting passages.
paths: The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads

The Scorch.description: An abandoned district of blackened stone and melted structures, destroyed decades ago when an alchemical experiment involving magical fire went catastrophically wrong. The ground still radiates heat and nothing grows here. Scavengers occasionally brave the ruins seeking lost treasures or studying the lingering fire magic. Some claim the flames weren't entirely natural, and that something was summoned that day which was never properly banished. The area smells of sulfur and ash.

The Old Sewers.description: A network of ancient tunnels and channels running beneath the city, originally built to manage the spring's overflow but now serving darker purposes. Smugglers use these passages to move goods unseen while criminals hide in alcoves and forgotten chambers. Some passages are flooded, others dry and filled with refuse. The deepest sections connect to natural caves and possibly to the Sealed Crypts above, though such routes are closely guarded secrets. Strange things live in the darkness where the city's waste flows.

The Tombs of the Sun Kings.name: The Tombs of the Sun Kings
The Tombs of the Sun Kings.basicInfo: A vast necropolis in the badlands where ancient rulers of the Sun Dynasty were buried with their treasures and servants. Seven great pyramids rise from the sand, their stone facades carved with warnings in glyphs and runes whose meaning has long been lost. Tomb raiders come seeking fortune, scholars come seeking knowledge, and most never leave. The tombs are known to be well protected by ancient traps, magical wards, and guardians both living and undead.
The Tombs of the Sun Kings.hiddenInfo: The Sun Kings were priest-monarchs who ruled before the current Bright Kingdoms, wielding power over life and death through solar magic. They were buried with artifacts of immense power, their tombs sealed with curses meant to protect their rest. The Third Pyramid recently collapsed partially, revealing new passages. Something is stirring in the depths - the undead guardians grow more actives. A cult of sun worshippers believes the Sun Kings can be awakened to rule again. Meanwhile, a competing group of tomb raiders led by a ruthless jackalkin named Khenret has claimed the First Pyramid as their base, killing any who interfere with their operations.

Raider's Camp.description: A semi-permanent camp where tomb raiders, scholars, and fortune seekers gather before venturing into the pyramids. Tents cluster around fire pits while merchants sell supplies at inflated prices. Information brokers trade maps and rumors. Khenret's gang maintains a fortified section, marked by jackal skulls on poles. The camp observes an uneasy truce - no violence here, or face collective justice.

The Plaza of Kings.description: A vast courtyard paved with stone slabs, where the seven pyramids are arrayed in a pattern matching the constellation of the Eternal King. Each pyramid's entrance faces the plaza's center, where a massive stone altar stands. Statues of the Sun Kings, weathered by time, watch from pedestals. The stone here is hot even at night.

The First Pyramid - Entrance.description: The smallest pyramid, its entrance a dark portal carved with solar rays. A gang of jackalkin raiders has claimed this pyramid, posting guards at the entrance. Inside, they've disabled the upper traps and looted the accessible chambers, using it as their base of operations. The deeper levels remain unexplored - even the raiders fear what lies below.

The First Pyramid - Raider's Den.description: The pyramid's upper chambers have been converted into a fortified base. Stolen treasures are sorted and stored, guards patrol the passages, and Khenret holds court in what was once a burial chamber. The walls still show murals of the sun's journey through the underworld. Stairs descend into darkness - the sealed lower levels that Khenret's people won't enter.

The First Pyramid - The Descent.description: Stone stairs descending into complete darkness, the air growing colder with each step. Ancient wards still function here, creating a sense of crushing weight. The passages branch in multiple directions, walls carved with warnings. This is where the real tomb begins - trapped, cursed, and deadly.

The Third Pyramid - Breach.description: The pyramid's northern face collapsed recently, creating a gaping wound in the structure. Rubble spills down the side, and the breach opens directly into previously sealed chambers.

The Third Pyramid - New Chambers.description: Chambers that haven't been entered in millennia, their air still and stale. These rooms are intact - grave goods still arranged, murals still bright. But something is wrong. The stone sarcophagus in the central chamber is open from the inside, its lid cracked and pushed aside. Whatever was buried here is gone.
paths: The Third Pyramid - The Deep Hall

The Third Pyramid - The Deep Hall.description: A long corridor descending into the pyramid's heart, lined with alcoves containing mummified servants.
