Their positions have changed since the last expedition - they're facing the passage now, not the walls. At the hall's end, massive doors carved with solar symbols stand slightly ajar.

The Fifth Pyramid - Entrance.description: The largest pyramid, its entrance sealed by a massive stone disk carved with intricate solar patterns. The disk rotates slowly, aligning different symbols with markers on the doorframe. Scholars believe it's a puzzle-lock, but none have solved it. Attempts to force entry have failed - the stone resists all damage.

The Seventh Pyramid - Sealed.description: The easternmost pyramid, its entrance completely sealed with black stone that absorbs light. No markings, no decorations, just smooth black stone. Nothing has ever successfully damaged or opened it. At night, some swear they hear singing from within.

The Lodge of the Wind Dancers.name: The Lodge of the Wind Dancers
The Lodge of the Wind Dancers.basicInfo: The Lodge of the Wind Dancers sits among towering natural rock pillars where desert winds create powerful, swirling currents. Simple stone buildings shelter against the rocks while the spaces between pillars form natural training grounds where initiates learn the spinning blade techniques of the Whirling Path. The Lodge trains dervishes in paired scimitars, trance meditation, and the art of fighting in perfect flow with motion and wind.
The Lodge of the Wind Dancers.hiddenInfo: The masters guard a secret: in the deepest trance state, the most skilled dervishes don't just sense attacks coming - they can see a few heartbeats into the future. This isn't magic but heightened awareness achieved through motion, meditation, and the wind's teaching. The Lodge accepts any with coin and dedication, but only those with true commitment reach the deeper mysteries. Recently, several students went on vision quests into the deep desert and haven't returned.

The Outer Pillars.description: Where the rock formations begin and visitors first encounter the wind. Natural stone pillars rise from the sand, their surfaces carved smooth by centuries of wind.

The Wind Circle.description: Open space between the tallest pillars where constant vortices spin and dance. This is the main training ground where students practice their blade-work, learning to move with the wind rather than against it.

The Lodgehouses.description: Simple stone dwellings built against the rock faces, providing shelter from the harsh desert sun and sandstorms. Sleeping quarters are sparse - just mats and storage for personal belongings. A communal area serves meals and provides space for rest between training sessions. The forge sits in an open courtyard here, where smiths craft and balance paired scimitars for each dervish.

The Masters' Chambers.description: Private quarters carved into the largest rock pillar, accessible only by a steep climb. Here the senior dervishes keep their chambers and guard the Lodge's deeper secrets. A small library contains scrolls documenting the Whirling Path's techniques and philosophy. Only advanced students are permitted entry.

Ghoultown.name: Ghoultown
Ghoultown.basicInfo: Ancient ruins where a plague killed thousands centuries ago. The dead rose as ghouls that nest in crypts and tunnels, growing bolder each year. Once a prosperous town, now only crumbling walls and bone-filled chambers remain.
Ghoultown.hiddenInfo: The plague wasn't natural - it was summoned by a necromancer during the War of Three Princes. The necromancer died, but his curse persists, animating any corpses left in the ruins. The ghouls have recently begun coordinating their attacks under the leadership of a ghoul king - a particularly ancient and intelligent undead who wears the crown of the last prince. He's directing raids on caravans to gather food and bodies to expand his pack. Beneath the ruins lie sealed chambers containing the necromancer's laboratory and the source of the curse.

The Outer Ruins.description: Crumbling walls and collapsed buildings mark where the town's edge once stood. Scattered bones lie half-buried in sand. During daylight the ruins seem empty, but shapes move in the shadows of broken doorways. At night, this place belongs to the dead.

The Bone Fields.description: Open ground where bodies were piled and burned during the plague's height. The pyres didn't burn hot enough to destroy everything - charred bones protrude from the sand in massive heaps. Ghouls nest in burrows dug among the remains, emerging at dusk to hunt. The strongest ghouls claim the best territory here, driving off weaker pack members.

The Market Square.description: The town's central plaza, now a maze of collapsed stalls and overturned carts half-buried in sand. Stone buildings around the square have mostly kept their walls, though roofs have caved in. The ghouls use these structures as dens, storing bodies and sleeping during the hottest parts of the day. At the square's center stands a dry fountain where the ghoul king holds court.

The Mayor's Hall.description: The town's grandest building, a two-story structure whose walls still stand. The ghoul king has claimed this as his lair, decorating it with bones arranged in disturbing patterns. He sits on the mayor's throne wearing a tarnished crown, surrounded by his strongest followers. Bodies are dragged here for him to feast upon first. The hall's basement contains the mayor's vault, sealed but not opened since the plague.

The Sealed Vault.description: A stone vault beneath the mayor's hall, its iron door still locked after all these years. The families of Oasis claim this vault contains records, heirlooms, and treasures locked away when the town was abandoned. The ghoul king has tried to open it but failed - something about the seal resists undead touch. Fresh scratch marks on the door show repeated attempts.

The Crypts.description: Underground burial chambers that honeycomb beneath the town, connected by narrow passages. Bodies were interred here before the plague, and many more were thrown in after. Now it's a warren where ghouls breed and multiply in the darkness. The air is thick with rot. Some passages have collapsed, others lead deeper than anyone has explored. Sounds echo strangely down here - sometimes screams, sometimes laughter.

The Necromancer's Laboratory.description: A sealed chamber in the crypts' deepest section, protected by ancient wards that have kept even the ghouls out. The door is carved with necromantic symbols and warnings. Inside - if the seals could be broken - lies the source of the curse: ritual components, forbidden texts, and the apparatus used to create the plague.

Citadel of the Desert Rose.name: Citadel of the Desert Rose
Citadel of the Desert Rose.basicInfo: A fortified monastery headquarters and training ground for the Order of the Desert Rose: demon-hunters who defend the Bright Kingdoms from infernal threats, teaching those with righteous hearts to recognize, resist, and destroy demonic influence. The temple rises from the desert in white stone, its walls carved with protective wards and its courtyards filled with warriors training for holy war.
Citadel of the Desert Rose.hiddenInfo: The Order is stretched thin. Demon cult activity has increased dramatically - cults operate openly in some regions. The Order's militant approach creates enemies among nobles who profit from demon-pact magic or who view the hunters as dangerous zealots. Internal divisions weaken the Order: focus solely on demons or combat the necromancy and dark magic that makes populations vulnerable to demonic corruption. The Grand Master walks a careful line between them.

The Gates of Vigilance.description: Fortified entrance where guards in white tabards bearing the red rose emblem check all who approach. Pilgrims seek blessing, warriors seek training. A notice board posts bounties for confirmed demon sightings, cult activity, and infernal artifacts. Petitioners wait here for audience with the Order's officers.

The Pilgrim's Rest.description: Simple quarters where visitors stay while conducting business with the Order. Clean, spare rooms provide shelter, and a communal dining hall serves basic meals. Priests offer counsel. This is also where the Order evaluates potential recruits, watching how they conduct themselves and listening to their stories.

The Courtyard of Trials.description: A large training ground where initiates and knights practice combat against demon-forms - magical constructs that mimic infernal abilities without the danger. Training dummies bear demonic sigils for recognition practice. Masters observe from covered walkways. At the courtyard's center stands the Rose Stone - a pillar carved with the Order's oaths that each knight must swear before.

The Hall of Blades.description: An armory and workshop where weapons are blessed and maintained. Smiths work silver into blades, enchanters inscribe wards onto armor, and priests perform rituals over finished pieces. Knights come here to be fitted for demon-slaying equipment.

The Chapel.description: A temple where the Order's priests lead prayers at dawn and dusk, asking for strength in the fight against darkness. The chapel's altar bears the symbol of a desert rose blooming from scorched earth. Here priests teach divine magic to those with the calling - healing, protective wards, and holy fire.

The Library.description: A secured library containing the Order's accumulated knowledge of demons, cults, and infernal magic. Books and scrolls describe demon types, their weaknesses, their habits. Case files document every cult the Order has destroyed. Access requires permission - this knowledge is dangerous even to read. A section sealed behind iron gates holds the most forbidden texts: actual demon summoning grimoires and infernal contracts, kept to study the enemy.

The Grand Master's Tower.description: The tallest structure, where the Order's leader maintains chambers and holds council with senior knights. Strategy is planned here, reports are compiled, and difficult decisions are made. The Grand Master's private chambers occupy the top floor, while meeting rooms below host debates between the traditional and reform factions. Only knights in good standing may enter without summons.

The Descent.description: Stone stairs descending from the tower's lowest level into darkness. An iron gate bars the way, inscribed with protective wards. Beyond lie ancient ruins that predate the Order, predating perhaps even the Bright Kingdoms themselves. The Grand Master keeps the only key. What lies below is the Order's most closely guarded secret - ruins that the founders discovered when they built the temple, ruins they chose to seal rather than explore.

Hillwatch.name: Hillwatch
Hillwatch.basicInfo: A fortified village built on a rocky hill overlooking the main caravan route. White-washed buildings cluster behind adobe walls, their flat roofs connected by wooden walkways. The village serves as a waystation where caravans rest and resupply. A watchtower at the hill's peak provides warning of approaching sandstorms or raiders.
Hillwatch.hiddenInfo: Hillwatch survives by charging reasonable fees for safety and supplies - a service caravans gladly pay for given the dangers of the desert. The village elders maintain careful neutrality between Oasis's noble houses, accepting all trade regardless of politics. Recently, caravans have been arriving with tales of a new threat. The village militia is skilled at defending walls but outmatched against magic. The elders have been quietly seeking adventurers to deal with the raider threat before it destroys Hillwatch's reputation as a safe haven.

The Caravan Square.description: An open plaza just inside the main gates where caravans park and unload. Water troughs and hitching posts line the edges. Merchants hawk goods from temporary stalls while caravan masters negotiate with guides and guards for the next leg of their journey. At the square's center, a deep well provides the village's water - drawn from an underground spring.

The Trading Post.description: A large warehouse and shop run by a halfling merchant named Daro Quicktongue. He buys and sells everything - desert survival gear, weapons, maps, trade goods, and information. Daro knows every caravan route, every water source, and every danger between here and the horizon. His prices are fair and his word is good, which is why caravans trust him.

The Waystation Inn.description: A three-story building with thick walls and small windows. The common room is cool and dim, offering respite from the desert heat. Rooms upstairs are simple but clean. The innkeeper, a retired caravan guard, maintains strict rules: no violence, no thievery. Break them and the militia escorts you into the desert without water.

The Village Walls.description: Adobe walls eight feet high surround the village, thick enough to walk along the top. The militia patrols here, watching for threats. Stairs at intervals provide access to the wall-walk. The watchtower rises at the hill's peak, manned day and night. From here, you can see the desert stretching in all directions - and spot danger approaching from miles away.

Council House.description: A modest building near the watchtower where the village elders meet and conduct business. They make decision for the village and negotiates with caravan masters, nobles, and anyone else who needs dealing with.

Whitestone.name: Whitestone
Whitestone.basicInfo: A mining village built into the foothills where desert meets mountains. The village produces quality marble, sandstone, gypsum, and occasionally precious gems from its quarries and mines. Dwarf foremen oversee human and pygmy workers, their combined expertise making Whitestone a major source of building materials for Oasis. The village is practical and rough - more work camp than home, but with its own stubborn pride.
Whitestone.hiddenInfo: The miners recently broke into a sealed chamber deep in the mines - a worked passage that predates their digging by centuries. Inside they found carved walls and artifacts of a style no one recognizes. The foreman ordered it sealed and said nothing, but miners have been sneaking back to explore and pilfer artifacts to sell. Strange things have happened since.

The Quarry.description: An open pit where sandstone is cut from the hillside in massive blocks. Workers lever sections free and lower them on ropes while masons mark the best stone for cutting. Dust hangs in the air and the sound of hammers never stops during working hours. A simple camp of tents houses workers who prefer the open air to the cramped village below.

The Village.description: Simple stone houses built from rejected quarry blocks, arranged in terraces on the hillside. A communal well provides water hauled from a spring deeper in the hills. The Mason's Hall serves as tavern, meeting place, and social center where workers drink after their shifts. The foreman's house sits at the highest terrace, overlooking the quarry and mines.

The Mason's Hall.description: A long, low building with a communal eating area, a bar, and space for workers to gather. The walls display examples of fine stonework - intricate carvings and perfectly fitted blocks that showcase what Stonefoot's craftsmen can do. After shifts, this place fills with tired workers seeking drink and companionship. Talk turns to work, always work - how much was cut, how hard the stone, what they found in the deeper tunnels.

Foreman's House.description: A sturdy dwelling where the dwarf foreman, Thorek Ironhand, lives and manages operations. Maps of the quarries and mines cover the walls, showing veins of stone and current excavations. Thorek keeps his own counsel but worry has been etched deeper into his face lately. He knows something is wrong in the mines but doesn't know what to do about it.

The Mines.description: Tunnels driven into the hillside seeking gypsum and gemstones. Support timbers hold back tons of rock while miners work by lamplight in the darkness. Recently they broke through into older workings - passages too regular to be natural, carved by hands unknown. The foreman sealed it, but the seal has been broken multiple times by curious miners.

Wadi of the Beasts.name: Wadi of the Beasts
Wadi of the Beasts.basicInfo: A deep canyon system where year-round water sources draw desert creatures to drink and shelter. Desert druids maintain a school here, teaching those who wish to understand, track, and bond with desert beasts - from giant scorpions to sand drakes.
Wadi of the Beasts.hiddenInfo: The canyon has been used for beast-work for millennia - ancient carvings in the upper caves show this tradition stretches back to before the current kingdoms. The masters teach that forming bonds with beasts requires patience, respect, and accepting that some creatures cannot or should not be tamed. Recently, creatures arriving at the canyon have been more aggressive and unpredictable - the masters suspect something in the deep desert is disturbing them, but haven't identified the source.

The Descent.description: A winding path carved into the canyon wall, descending from the desert above. Cairns and warning stones mark the way. The walls rise higher with each switchback until they tower overhead, creating shade and blocking the worst of the desert wind.

The Canyon Floor.description: Where water pools and flows between rocks, creating patches of green in the otherwise harsh environment. Desert creatures gather here throughout the day - scorpions, lizards, drakes, great cats, and others. The constant presence of dangerous beasts makes the canyon floor treacherous for the unprepared.

The Training Grounds.description: Rock enclosures and reinforced pens where captured creatures are kept for observation and training work. Different areas suit different beasts - sand pits for scorpions, high-walled cages for drakes, open runs with shade for cats. Students work here under supervision, learning to read behavior and earn trust.

The Cliff Path.description: Carved handholds, rope assists, and natural ledges providing access up the canyon wall to the settlement above. The climb is deliberate - reaching the school requires effort.

The School.description: Natural caves carved wider and connected by ledges halfway up the canyon wall, where the masters live and teach. Communal sleeping areas, a cooking fire, storage for equipment. A central cave serves for instruction - walls marked with diagrams of creatures, their tracks, their behaviors. The masters expect students to be self-sufficient and respectful of the space.

The Ancient Caves.description: Higher caves reached by a difficult climb, their walls covered in carvings showing humans working alongside beasts in hunts, rituals, and daily life. The images date back millennia, showing this canyon has been used for beast-work since before the current kingdoms. Some carvings show creatures that no longer exist or haven't been seen in living memory.

Palmgrove.name: Palmgrove
Palmgrove.basicInfo: A prosperous farming village built around a reliable spring-fed oasis. Date palms and citrus groves surround the settlement, while fields of vegetables grow in irrigated plots. The village supplies fresh food to passing caravans. Adobe houses with shaded courtyards cluster behind low walls, their gardens fragrant with herbs and flowers.
Palmgrove.hiddenInfo: Palmgrove's peace is threatened from multiple directions. Tomb-raiders break into the town at night to search for a legendary artifact said to be hidden in ruins beneath the oasis. A band of desert nomads claim the land was stolen from their ancestors. The spring flows from extensive ruins below.

The Oasis Pool.description: Clear water bubbles up into a stone-lined pool surrounded by date palms and flowering bushes. Children play in the shallows while women wash clothes. Stone channels direct water to the fields. This is the village's heart - without it, Palmgrove would be just another ruin in the sand.

The Grove.description: Date palms and citrus trees grow thick here, creating welcome shade. Farmers tend the trees and harvest fruit for sale. Irrigation channels wind between the rows. At the grove's edge, where cultivation gives way to wild growth, an old shrine to the water spirits stands half-hidden among the palms.

The Fields.description: Vegetable plots and grain fields stretch across irrigated land. Farmers work from dawn until the heat grows too intense, then retreat to shade. A simple barn stores tools and harvested crops.

The Village Square.description: A shaded plaza where villagers gather in the evening to share meals and stories. A simple market operates here - farmers sell fresh produce, craftsmen offer their work, traders from passing caravans display goods. The village council meets in an adobe building at the square's edge, where decisions are made by consensus of the elder families.

The Healer's House.description: Home of the village healer and priestess. She treats injuries, delivers babies, and performs blessings.

The Council House.description: A sturdy adobe building where the village elders meet to decide matters of importance.

Temple of the Balance.name: Temple of the Balance
Temple of the Balance.basicInfo: A monastery built into a sheer cliff face in a honeycomb of caves, home to the Order of the Balance: assassins who maintain equilibrium by taking lives. They kill tyrants, demon cultists, and those who upset the natural order, acting on intelligence from across the Bright Kingdoms. The climb to reach the temple is treacherous, killing the unprepared before they can even knock on the door.
Temple of the Balance.hiddenInfo: The Order of the Balance follows an ancient code: they kill only to restore equilibrium, never for personal gain or at another's request. When word reaches them of a tyrant's oppression, a cult's corruption, or a noble's overreach, they debate in council whether death serves balance. If consensus agrees, an assassin is dispatched. They refuse all contracts and turn away those seeking vengeance or personal advancement. The order is currently split between traditionalists who believe balance requires patience and careful judgment, and activists who think the world tilts toward chaos and decisive action is needed now. The Grand Viper walks a careful line between factions. Messenger birds arrive daily with intelligence from informants across the kingdoms - priests, merchants, servants, and others who recognize when power corrupts beyond redemption.

The Cliff Base.description: A rocky shelf at the cliff's bottom. The climb is 800 feet of sun-blasted stone with few handholds. Bodies of those who fell lie scattered in the rocks below - the order leaves them as warnings. A simple shrine holds offerings left by those seeking the order's attention. A stone marker carved with scales indicates where the climbing route begins.

The Ascent.description: The climb itself. Handholds are sparse and deliberately difficult to find. The rock burns hot enough to blister skin. The route crosses vertical sections where climbers must leap between holds. Wind constantly buffets the cliff face, threatening to tear climbers loose. At about 400 feet up, the route reaches a narrow ledge.

The Threshold.description: A simple cave mouth with a door of dark wood carved with scales and serpents. The door has no handle on the outside - it opens only from within, when those inside decide to grant entry. Petitioners must somehow convince the order they're worthy of admission. The door is magically warded against forcing - those who try to break through find their strength turns against them.

The Hall of Scales.description: The entrance hall, a natural cave expanded and smoothed by generations of inhabitants. The floor is level stone, the walls carved with the order's history in images: tyrants falling, demons banished, balance restored. Alcoves hold statues of past Grand Vipers who served with distinction. Two passages branch from this hall - one leading to the training areas, one to the council chambers.

The Training Caves.description: A network of cave rooms arranged in honeycomb fashion, each carved for specific training purposes. Natural light filters through openings facing the desert, illuminating different rooms at different times of day. The Poison Garden grows deadly plants in carefully controlled conditions using reflected sunlight. The Shadow Chamber teaches movement without sound in complete darkness. The Blade Cave holds practice weapons and sparring space. The Meditation Cells are small rooms where initiates sit for days learning to still their minds.

The Lookout.description: A large cave with openings offering spectacular views across the desert. This is where the order receives intelligence. Dozens of messenger birds roost on carved perches - ravens, hawks, desert falcons trained to return here from across the kingdoms. Wall maps track current situations of concern.

The Council Chamber.description: A circular cave with a domed ceiling, lit by oil lamps and shafts of sunlight from carefully positioned openings. Cushions arrange in a circle where the council of twelve masters sits in deliberation. The Grand Viper sits at the circle's north point, marked by a serpent carved into the wall behind. Here the order debates whether death serves balance. Intelligence is presented, arguments are made, counter-arguments heard. When consensus is reached, an assassin is chosen for the task.

The Grand Viper's Chamber.description: A modest cave room serving as both office and living quarters for the order's leader. A simple bed, a writing desk covered in reports, shelves holding the order's most secret records. A window faces east, allowing the Grand Viper to watch the sunrise over the desert.

Tower of the Pact.name: Tower of the Pact
Tower of the Pact.basicInfo: A dark stone tower ringed with brass, headquarters of the Pactkeepers - warlocks who hunt wayward djinn, ifrit, and shaitan that threaten the Bright Kingdoms. The order teaches summoning, binding, and banishment to those with the talent, training hunters who patrol the deep desert seeking entities that violate ancient boundaries.
Tower of the Pact.hiddenInfo: The tower stands at the center of something older than the current kingdoms - wards carved into the bedrock, symbols in the foundations that no one living can read, persistent magic that predates the order itself. The Pactkeepers maintain these without fully understanding them, knowing only that the tower's presence keeps certain things at bay. Recently, entities have been appearing more frequently in the deep desert - djinn spotted near trade routes, ifrit burning villages, shaitan collapsing wells. The masters don't know if the boundaries are weakening or if something is deliberately testing them.

The Brass Gate.description: The tower's entrance, marked by brass pillars and protective wards. Petitioners wait here - villages reporting entity sightings, nobles seeking protection, merchants wanting bound familiars as guards.

The Courtyard.description: An open space where initiates practice basic summoning and banishment under supervision. Chalk circles mark the ground, some still glowing with residual magic.

The Stables.description: Where the order keeps desert mounts and bound elementals that serve as transportation for hunting parties.

The Tower Hall.description: The ground floor where masters teach the theory and practice of pact magic. Heavy doors lead to upper chambers and the deep foundations.

Cloudtop.name: Cloudtop
Cloudtop.basicInfo: Cloudtop is a mountain city built on the highest peaks of the Sky Pillar range, where the air grows thin and clouds drift below the terraced workshops. Here master craftsmen work metals in forges that burn pure in the rarefied air, infusing magical properties impossible to achieve at lower altitudes. The city's spired towers pierce the sky above a sea of clouds, offering breathtaking views of the desert kingdoms far below. Cloudtop's legendary smiths create the magical weapons and jewelry that make the Bright Kingdoms famous across the known world.
Cloudtop.hiddenInfo: Cloudtop is ruled by House Darawat, the Jeweled Princes, whose ancestors discovered the richest veins of precious metals high in the Sky Pillar peaks. The population includes humans, mountain dwarves, eaglekin who serve as aerial scouts, underground gnomes who know the tunnel networks, and various desert races adapted to high altitude living. The city maintains the Deepguard - specialized tunnel-fighters who battle goblin sappers in the mountain's depths - while the Tower of the Eight Winds teaches air magic and magical flight using mountain updrafts. The Wyvernrider Guild tames the great desert drakes that nest in the high peaks, providing both transportation and defense against aerial threats from the Greater Goblinate.

Sky Gate.description: The city's main entrance carved from living rock, where prayer flags flutter in the thin wind and newcomers pause to catch their breath in the rarefied air.

High Forges.description: Workshop levels built into the mountain's heart where master smiths work metals in the pure flames that burn only in rarefied air. The forges glow with intense heat, infusing magical properties into every piece.

Cloud Terrace.description: An open plaza perched on the mountain's edge where citizens gather to watch clouds drift through the desert valleys far below. Tea houses serve the bitter highland brews that help visitors adjust to the altitude.

Darawat Palace.description: The Jeweled Princes' gem-adorned palace, its faceted walls catching and fracturing sunlight into rainbow patterns. The throne room's windows look out over an endless sea of clouds.

Deepguard Barracks.description: Training halls and armories for the elite tunnel-fighters who battle goblin sappers in the mountain's depths. Maps of underground passages cover the walls alongside specialized cave-fighting equipment.

Deep Mines.description: Mining tunnels that delve into the Sky Pillars' metallic heart, where rare ores await harvest. Deepguard patrols constantly watch for goblin incursions from hidden passages.

Tower of the Eight Winds.description: A soaring spire where air mages study the mountain updrafts and teach magical flight. Students practice leaping from graduated platforms while wind-spirits whisper secrets of the sky.

Wyvernrider Aerie.description: Massive carved perches where desert drakes and their riders roost between flights. The smell of sulfur and mountain wind mingles with the sound of beating wings.

Guild Halls.description: Ornate meeting chambers where the craft guilds govern their ancient mysteries. Masterwork displays showcase techniques perfected in the rarefied air of the peaks.

Gem Vaults.description: Heavily guarded chambers where the city's greatest treasures rest. The thin air preserves even delicate works for centuries while magical wards protect against both thieves and goblin raids.

Artisan Quarter.description: Residential terraces where craftsmen and their families live in houses built for the mountain climate. Thick walls and clever ventilation systems help residents of all races adapt to the altitude.

Eyrie Quarter.description: Residential area built with eaglekin in mind, featuring high perches and wind-carved balconies. Mixed with traditional dwellings for the various mountain-adapted races.

Windcatcher Plaza.description: A market square where cloth banners and wind chimes create music in the constant mountain breeze. Traders from lower altitudes rest here before attempting the final ascent.

Aerie of the Sunscale.name: Aerie of the Sunscale
Aerie of the Sunscale.basicInfo: A dragonkin fortress-monastery built into mountain peaks near Cloudtop, constructed atop and within the vast empty lair of Heliaxes, a legendary sun dragon who departed centuries ago. The Order of the Dragon maintains this sacred site, training dragonkin warriors and guarding dragon lore from those who would corrupt it. The original lair's massive chambers now serve as halls, libraries, and meditation caves.
Aerie of the Sunscale.hiddenInfo: The Order exists to protect dragon lore and relics from those who would abuse them. The Sunscale track dragon site discoveries, fight the black market in dragon materials, protect dragonkin from cult abductions, and launch strike teams to raid cult cells. The Sanctum holds dragon bones and relics properly interred after being recovered from cultists or newly discovered sites.

The Gate of Scales.description: The fortress entrance carved into the mountain face, flanked by massive pillars of sun-bleached stone that once framed Heliaxes's lair. Dragonkin sentries in ceremonial armor bearing the Order's sunburst emblem stand watch. A mosaic floor depicts the legendary sun dragon in flight, its tiles still brilliant despite centuries of wear.

The Chamber of Truth.description: A vast library built within what was once the dragon's meditation cave, light by day by natural skylights. One wall displays a massive map marked with known dragon sites, suspected cult locations, and recent black market smuggling routes.

The Sanctum.description: A sealed chamber deep within the mountain where dragon bones, scales, and other relics recovered from cultists or secured from newly discovered sites are properly interred with reverence. Stone sarcophagi line the walls, each marked with the name and lineage of the dragon whose remains rest within. Only senior Order members may enter.

The Roost.description: The massive ledge where Auraxes once slept, now serving as the Order's council chamber and leadership quarters. The platform juts from the cliff face with commanding views across the mountain range toward Cloudtop. Here the Order's commanders meet to debate strategy. The Roost also houses the Mission Hall where strike teams receive briefings before deploying to raid cult cells, escort vulnerable dragonkin, or secure threatened dragon sites.

The Training Grounds.description: A series of interconnected caves and external ledges where dragonkin practice combat.

Red Rock Badlands.name: Red Rock Badlands
Red Rock Badlands.basicInfo: Sprawling maze of dramatic crimson stone formations and wind-carved canyons where broken ruins emerge from the red rock. Gnoll packs control different districts of the ruins, each led by self-styled 'sultans' and 'princes' who live in decadent luxury while their slaves maintain the essentials. Travelers who know the right clan leader can buy safe passage, but the price is steep and the guarantee questionable.
Red Rock Badlands.hiddenInfo: The Badlands contain the shattered remains of Naran-Kadish, City of the Flame Princes, once ruled by djinnborn nobles. In an age long past, a ritual to banish an otherworldly invasion backfired catastrophically, destroying Naran-Kadish and leaving residual magic that still occasionally manifests itself. Rival gnoll clans have divided the ruins into territories. They raid caravans and nearby settlements, taking slaves whom they sell or keep for themselves. The Bright Kingdoms consider the gnolls a dire menace but regard the Badlands as cursed and avoid venturing into them.

Redwall Canyon.description: Narrow passages between towering red stone walls.

The Shattered Gates.description: Massive ruined gates that once marked the entrance to Naran-Kadish. Gnoll sentries lounging in the shade of crumbled statues of djinn. Crude banners mark this as territory of the 'Sultan of the Gnolls.'

Palace Quarter.description: A vast ruined palace complex of toppled towers and debris-strewn courtyards. Gnolls have converted the spaces into fighting pits and slave pens.

The Flesh Markets.description: A courtyard where captured travelers and workers are bought, sold, and traded between gnoll clans.

Copperstone.name: Copperstone
Copperstone.basicInfo: A pygmy village built at the base of a rocky outcrop rich in copper deposits. Low stone buildings with copper-tiled roofs cluster around open workshops. Known for skilled metalwork, precise craftsmanship, and a fierce independence despite their small stature.
Copperstone.hiddenInfo: Copperstone's prosperity comes from two sources: the copper they mine and refine, and the steady stream of talented young pygmies who leave for the Academy of Brass and send money home. The copper veins are also running thinner, forcing miners to dig deeper where the rock is less stable and the heat becomes dangerous.

The Village Square.description: An open plaza paved with copper-flecked stone, surrounded by low buildings scaled for pygmy height - doorways four feet tall, windows at waist level for humans. Awnings provide shade while workshops open directly onto the square.

The Workshop Quarter.description: A district of open-fronted workshops where pygmy craftsmen work at benches and forges scaled to their size. Workshops specialize - copperwork, fine mechanisms, jewelry. Apprentices learn beside masters, continuing traditions passed down through generations.

The Crafters' Guild.description: A low stone building with a copper-tiled roof where the village's master craftsmen meet. Inside, examples of exceptional work line the walls - proof of the village's skill and tradition. This is where masters judge apprentice work, where elders discuss the village's direction.

The Market Street.description: A row of shops and stalls where villagers sell finished goods to traders and visitors. Copper cookware, jewelry, small mechanisms, decorative pieces - all worked with the precision pygmies are known for.

The Waystation.description: A building with higher ceilings and larger furniture where non-pygmy visitors can stay and conduct business more comfortably. The pygmies built it as a practical necessity - traders need somewhere to sleep, and it's easier to have one properly-sized building than retrofit the entire village.

The Mines.description: Tunnels driven into the copper-rich outcrop. Support timbers shore up passages and warning signs mark dangerous sections.

Sacrate of the Seraphot.name: Sacrate of the Seraphot
Sacrate of the Seraphot.basicInfo: The Sacrate of the Seraphot is a prosperous, militant desert empire ruled by the Seraphot, a dynasty of serpentfolk princes who are worshipped as living gods by their subjects. Built around the fertile Eternal Flood valley, the capital rises in terraced pyramids crowned with platforms where the serpentfolk godlings emerge to be venerated at dawn. Wide avenues lined with black marble serpent statues connect great bathhouses, gymnasiums, and granaries that demonstrate the Seraphot's benevolent rule. The empire welcomes traders and pilgrims while maintaining formidable legions, offering incredible harvests from their magically-controlled seasonal floods.
Sacrate of the Seraphot.hiddenInfo: The Sacrate is a picture of orderly prosperity and contentment - subjects are happy, well-fed, and devoted to their serpentfolk rulers. Yet no one criticizes the Seraphot. Ever. The Seraphot believe in their divine right to rule all the Bright Kingdoms, but are patient in pursuing this goal. A secret police of snake-masked inquisitors emerge from the forbidden tunnels under the city at night and remove any trouble-makers, then use mesmeric magic to cause any witnesses to forget what they have seen: these are ophidians, hybrids between serpentfolk and other races magically crafted to be the perfect servitors of the Seraphot.

Gates of Eternal Prosperity.description: Grand entrance where the Eternal Flood River flows through massive stone arches carved with serpent reliefs.

Way of the Seraphot.description: Wide boulevard lined with towering black marble serpent statues. Stalls cluster in the shade of the great monuments. Alive with music, traffic and crowds at all hours of day and night, except for the dawn prayer. Subjects prostrate when litter processions approach, clearing the way for their divine rulers.

Great Dais Plaza.description: Vast plaza built around an enormous raised platform where the Seraphot emerge at dawn, bedecked in blinding splendor, to greet the sun and receive the veneration of their subjects through the haunting dawnsong, sung by the Children of the Sun, young and beautiful virgin priestesses. At other times of day, the plaza is a sprawling, chaotic marketplace crowded with stalls and packed with buskers and pickpockets.

Harbor District.description: Bustling river port where grain from the Eternal Flood Valley is brought downriver to the feed the city's teeming masses.

Great Bathhouses.description: Marble-columned halls where subjects compete for honors in athletic contests and theological debates of the Seraphot's greatness.

Underground Passages.description: Massive stone doors leading to the serpentfolk's true domain below the city. Though these entrances are in plain view, it is blasphemy to speak of them, and entering them is a crime punishable by public immolation.

Deep Sanctum.description: The serpentfolk's forbidden underground realm beneath the city. Here the Seraphot conduct their true business. Only serpentfolk royalty and ophidian servitors are admitted.

The Golden Serpent Inn.description: Well-appointed inn popular with merchants and travelers. Clean rooms overlook the harbor while the common room serves excellent meals and local wine. Staff are quick and discreet.

The Harbor Lantern.description: Busy neighborhood tavern where dock workers, craftsmen, and locals gather after long days. Known for strong beer, dice games, and heated arguments about chariot racing. Gets loud after sunset. Forcibly emptied for the dawn prayers.

The Great Arena.description: Massive amphitheater where crowds gather for chariot races, gladiatorial contests, and spectacular naval battles when the arena is flooded by diverting the Eternal Flood River. Sand-covered fighting pits can be reconfigured for different events. Vendors hawk food and wine while spectators cheer their favorites.

The Academy of Brass.name: The Academy of Brass
The Academy of Brass.basicInfo: A sprawling workshop-academy where artificers study the art of creating clockwork companions, animated constructs, and mechanical servants. Master craftsmen of multiple races - pygmies, gnomes, humans, and others - teach students to combine engineering with enchantment, producing creations powered by machine and magic.
The Academy of Brass.hiddenInfo: The Academy was founded centuries ago by a consortium of pygmy and gnome inventors seeking to advance their crafts. It thrives on commissions from nobles and merchants. Competition between students is fierce - everyone wants to create the masterwork that makes their reputation. Recently, several advanced constructs have malfunctioned, and no one can determine why.

The Brass Gates.description: Massive doors of polished brass depicting famous constructs from Academy history - a mechanical guardian that held a fortress alone, a clockwork physician that saved a prince, an automaton that solved an ancient puzzle. The gates are themselves a masterwork - they open at dawn and close at dusk without any visible mechanism, responding to spoken commands in multiple languages.

The Grand Workshop.description: An enormous hall filled with workbenches, tools, materials, and the constant sound of creation. Students and masters work side by side on projects ranging from simple clockwork toys to sophisticated constructs. Different sections specialize - fine mechanisms in one corner, heavy construction in another, enchantment integration in a third.

The Testing Grounds.description: An open courtyard where constructs are tested. Obstacles test mobility, targets test combat functions, puzzles test intelligence (for the most advanced creations). Crashes and malfunctions are common - students learn as much from failures as successes

The Proving Hall.description: Where students present their masterworks for judgment. The hall contains examples of legendary creations from Academy history under glass - the first walking automaton, the clockwork scribe that never errs, the brass falcon that can truly fly. To graduate, students must create something worthy of adding to this collection. Most fail their first attempt.

The Masters' Sanctum.description: Private workshops where master artificers work on advanced projects - military contracts, commissions for wealthy patrons, experimental designs. Entry requires invitation or urgent business. The masters also investigate construct malfunctions here.

The Vault of Failures.description: A sealed chamber where dangerously malfunctioning constructs are stored rather than destroyed. The Academy keeps them for study.

Gesenthame.name: Gesenthame
Gesenthame.basicInfo: Gesenthame is a cursed ruin of a razed, burned ancient city at the heart of the Bright Kingdoms, destroyed and pulled down in ages past by its sister kingdoms after it attempted to conquer them. Toppled stone blocks mark where mighty walls and great monuments once stood. At the ruins' center rises the rocky acropolis of the Black Fortress, a burned yet inviolate citadel whose magical defenses still function even after  generations of desolation. Squatters, slavers, tomb-raiders, and cultists infest the ruins seeking to reclaim the terrible lost glory of Gesenthame.
Gesenthame.hiddenInfo: The ageless Jeweled Princes who ruled Gesenthame reportedly immolated themselves rather than surrender, but their bodies and the legendary Desert Jewels were never found. Some believe they escaped and survive to this day, secretly pursuing their goals of restoration, conquest and recovery of the long-lost Sky Fortress. The other kingdoms periodically raid the ruins and kill those who dare settle here, but no permanent garrison has ever succeeded in holding the ruins of Gesenthame for long. All efforts to penetrate the Black Fortress have resulted in annihilation. This is a lawless den of madness, iniquity and cruelty where vicious bands, sects and cults vie for a futile supremacy.

Rubble Fields.description: Scattered stone blocks and collapsed walls stretch for miles. Most avoid the cursed stones, but mad and desperate folk have built crude shelters in the shadows of fallen towers.

The Breach.description: A massive gap in the outer walls where the conquering armies entered. Crude fortifications built by squatters mark territorial boundaries between rival camps.

Squatter's Market.description: Ramshackle stalls and tents where scavengers trade salvaged goods, stolen artifacts, and dubious information. Merchants hawk supplies to treasure hunters and desperate provisions to the desperate.

The Undercroft Tavern.description: A drinking establishment built in the basement of a ruined palace. Popular with treasure hunters, smugglers, and anyone seeking to forget their circumstances. Strong drink and stronger rumors.

Pilgrims' Row.description: A street of small shrines and makeshift temples where various cults gather. Some seek forgiveness for the city's sins, others whisper of darker purposes and promised restoration.

Blackstone Square.description: A vast courtyard leading to the Black Fortress, its stones permanently blackened by ancient fires. Few dare cross it, but those who do report strange whispers from the citadel above.

The Black Fortress.description: The burned but intact citadel crowning the ruins. Its magical defenses still function, summoning stone golems, dust devils, sand elementals, sandspiders, and other magically bound creatures to defend the cursed fortification.

Bazaar.name: Bazaar
Bazaar.basicInfo: Bazaar, the anarchic market-city in the desert. A sprawl of tents, ramshackle constructions and permanent structures where caravans from across the desert converge. Here you can buy everyday goods, exotic beasts, alchemical reagents, and things the other kingdoms officially condemn.

The Crossroads.description: A huge open main square where all roads through the desert are said to meet. Caravans unload under striped awnings. Merchants shout in many languages while camels bellow and crowds surge. Street performers, storytellers, pickpockets, and traders of every kind fill the space. Bright Folk nobles haggle alongside desert nomads.

Market.description: The largest market in the Bright Kingdoms, maybe all Larion. Endless stalls sell cloth, metalwork, spices, brass lamps, jewelry, carpets, and trinkets. Dense, loud, impossible to navigate without getting lost. Permanent shops along covered walkways house serious merchants with real inventory.

Alchemy Quarter.description: Where mysteries of the desert are bought and sold. Dried herbs hang in bundles, jars of powders line high shelves. Merchants sell phoenix ash, ground sunstone, serpent venom, and stranger things. The real treasures hide among fakes - canopic artifacts, preserved organs from tombs, reagents for forbidden magic. Everything has a price if you know what to ask for.

Tea Stalls.description: Open-air gathering places under vast tent canopies. Low tables on worn rugs, brass hookahs, mint tea and honey cakes. Merchants close deals, musicians play, servers carry trays through crowds. Curtained alcoves provide privacy for sensitive business. Half social club, half neutral ground.

Pens.description: Where they trade desert mounts and stranger beasts. Camels for caravans, swift horses, pack animals. Also caged creatures, serpents, scarabs, creatures of all sizes and descriptions. Handlers train riders in desert survival. Beast-tamers demonstrate their skills. Constant noise - bellowing, hissing, shouted instructions.

Thousand Gods.description: A chaotic shrine district where centuries of devotion pile atop each other. Statues of gods, ancestors, heroes, and beloved dead crowd together with no order or plan. Elaborate marble shrines stand beside painted stone cairns. Locals leave offerings before dangerous journeys. Pilgrims pray for transformation. The mad leave tributes to dreams. Touch the wrong shrine and answer to someone's family or a fanatical cult.

Wayfarers' District.description: Where outsiders stay while finding their footing. Walled caravanserais with rooms around courtyards, stables for animals, common areas for trading information. Different lodging houses cater to different clientele. The Pilgrim's Rest sits at the district's heart - more expensive but guards keep out beggars, scammers, and the dream-touched.

Bazaar.hiddenInfo: Bazaar grew from a caravanserai at a crossroads. No single authority rules it. Rules come from custom: honor debts, no blood feuds inside the walls, don't touch another's shrine. Beyond that, anything goes. Inhabitants: merchants and nobles, desert nomads, northern traders, tomb-robbers, refugees from Gesenthame, moon elf agents (magically disguised), alchemists, blood mages, beast-tamers, con artists, the desperate, the ambitious, those driven mad by the desert's dreams. The canopic trade, condemned by the Kingdoms, secretly flourishes here - but it gets people killed. Moon elf infiltrators, disguised, purchase materials for demon-binding and recruit the dispossessed. The canny profit from lawlessness, while the reasonable fear anything that might garner Bazaar dangerous attention from powers-that-be that might shut it down.

The City of Ancestors.name: The City of Ancestors
The City of Ancestors.basicInfo: A vast necropolis east of Ardan's Watch where centuries of burials have created a second city of tombs and monuments. Great houses maintain their family crypts in the High Tombs through hired guards and tomb-keepers. Beyond the maintained quarters, the older sections have fallen into disrepair. Squatters shelter in abandoned mausoleums, scavengers work the forgotten vaults, and the deepest passages descend into darkness.
The City of Ancestors.hiddenInfo: Tomb-keeper families serve the great houses in exchange for housing and stipends. Professional mourners work funerals in the safe quarters. Squatters occupy abandoned mausoleums in the Commons because they have nowhere else. Scavengers rob unguarded tombs at night. The Royal Reburial Society tracks down and re-inter the restless dead - their grey cloaks are recognized throughout the city. The necropolis accumulated without planning over centuries. Older sections were buried when new construction built over them. These subsurface levels contain tombs from before the kingdom. Within the last generation, the dead began rising with increasing frequency. The Royal Reburial Society has not determined the cause. Great houses pay premium rates for protection because a family member rising causes scandal. The poor cannot afford tomb-keepers and rely on the Society's patrols. Houses compete for burial plots near the maintained quarters. Further into the Commons, tomb robbery is accepted risk. The Descent contains the oldest crypts - few enter and fewer return.

The Keepers' Gate.description: Main entrance where guards and grey-cloaked Wardens from the Royal Reburial Society inspect those who enter. The gate closes at sunset. Tomb-keepers and mourners work the safe side, selling flowers and grave goods. Past the gate, you're on your own.

The High Tombs.description: Maintained quarter where great houses keep their dead behind locked gates and hired guards. Clean paths, fresh flowers, tomb-keepers in house livery. The wealthy can still visit their ancestors safely. At night, even here, the guards stay alert.

The Commons.description: Middle ground where lesser families try to maintain their vaults. Some tombs still see regular visits. Others stand neglected, gates broken, inscriptions fading. The edge between maintained and wild. Tomb robbers work here after dark. Squatters shelter in abandoned mausoleums.

The Founder's Circle.description: Ancient amphitheater ringed by the oldest monuments, where Tournament was once held among the ancestors. Now abandoned. The stone faces still watch, but no Champions have fought here in years. Too dangerous. Something about this place draws attention from the depths.

The Wilds.description: Everything beyond the maintained quarters. Vast sprawl of overgrown tombs, collapsed vaults, forgotten monuments. Paths disappear into weeds. Inscriptions worn illegible. The dispossessed live here because they have nowhere else. Scavengers and worse move through the ruins. Don't go alone. Don't go at night. Don't go at all if you can help it.

The Descent.description: Where the necropolis goes underground. Some passages are sealed with iron gates and warning markers. Others just drop into darkness. Catacombs branch beneath the surface. The oldest crypts are down here - tombs from before anyone can remember, inscriptions in dead languages. Things that should stay buried don't always. The truly desperate venture into the Descent. Most don't return.

The Shattered Crypts.description: Ancient tombs broken open, their stone lids cracked and pushed aside.

The Ossuary of Kings.description: A vast chamber where the bones of ancient rulers have been gathered and arranged along the walls - thousands of skulls watching with empty sockets. Something has disturbed this place recently; the patterns are broken, bones scattered across the floor.

The Ritual Chamber.description: The deepest sanctum, where seven stone sarcophagi form a circle around a raised dais.

The Field of Valor.name: The Field of Valor
The Field of Valor.basicInfo: Open ground west of Ardan's Watch where Tournament convenes for its public sessions. Colorful pavilions rise when representatives gather - silk tents for each house, merchant stalls, competition grounds. Champions demonstrate excellence through contests of arms, tests of knowledge, displays of skill. The field can accommodate thousands of spectators. Between sessions, it serves as common ground for fairs, celebrations, and military musters.
The Field of Valor.hiddenInfo: Tournament sessions transform the field into a temporary city where house retinues, merchants, performers, and spectators gather. Champions demonstrate excellence through contests that range from mounted combat to archery and athletics. The outcomes affect house standing and Council votes, making every contest politically significant. Some houses invest heavily in their Champions' success through training, equipment, and support. Others rely on natural talent. Accusations of cheating, bribery, or unfair judging create ongoing disputes. Tournament Masters maintain the grounds and arbitrate conflicts, but they cannot prevent all friction between ambitious houses.

The Grand Approach.description: Wide road leading from the capital to the field. During Tournament, it fills with processions - house banners, armored retinues, merchant wagons. Stone markers line the route commemorating past Champions.

The Tournament Grounds.description: Central field where contests take place. Lists for mounted combat, archery ranges, dueling circles marked in white stone. Wooden stands for spectators can be assembled in days. The ground itself is sacred - no violence except in sanctioned contest.

The Pavilion Circle.description: Where great houses erect their silk pavilions during sessions. Each tent displays house colors and heraldry. Inside: weapons racks, armor stands, private chambers for Champions and their advisors. The arrangement shifts each session based on current standings.

The Champions' Ring.description: Raised circular platform of white stone where formal duels resolve parliamentary deadlock. No roof, no shade. When Champions fight here, thousands watch from the surrounding field. The stone remembers blood - old stains darken the center.

The Masters' Manor.description: Fortified stone house where Tournament Masters live year-round. Contains archives of past contests, records of Champions, storage for standards and equipment. The Masters remain neutral in all house conflicts.

The Festival Grounds.description: Open area where merchants set up temporary markets during Tournament. Food stalls, weapon vendors, fortune tellers, performers. Between official sessions, traveling fairs and military musters use this ground.

The Commons.description: Rough camping ground for spectators, lesser retinues, and those who can't afford pavilion space. Tents crowd together without order. Pickpockets work the crowds. Gamblers run dice games. At night, cooking fires spread across the field like stars.

Smoketown.name: Smoketown
Smoketown.basicInfo: Capital of the Greater Goblinate, built in and around ancient ruins rising from the volcanic highlands. Massive carved stone structures of unknown origin now serve as foundations for a sprawling goblin metropolis—forges hammered into ancient chambers, smelters beneath crumbling archways, ramshackle construction climbing the old walls. The city never sleeps. Smoke rises from a hundred vents, visible for miles.
Smoketown.hiddenInfo: Inhabitants: Goblins—workers, smiths, traders, soldiers on leave. Hobgoblin officers. Bugbear enforcers. Growing numbers of humans, dwarves, demonborn, moon elves, and others drawn by opportunity or recruited into the Foreign Legion. Government: The Governor rules absolutely, answering only to the Dragon Queen. Below the Governor, ministers control different aspects of the city—the Minister of Works oversees industry, the Minister of Revels manages entertainment and morale, others handle trade, military supply, and internal security. Ministers wield enormous power but serve at the Governor's pleasure; failure means execution. They scheme against each other constantly while maintaining public loyalty. Church of the Pyre: The Pyre Cathedral is the Church's grandest temple. Archpriests oversee dragonfire production at the Crucible and lead public burnings—captured noble banners, foreign books, symbols of Larion's 'corruption.' They preach that the old world must burn. Tensions: The Goblinate champions goblinhood, but the war economy requires non-goblin skills. True believers resent outsider influence. Opportunists don't care as long as coin flows. Informants watch for spies and dissent. The celebration is real, but so is the danger underneath. Smoketown welcomes outsiders who prove useful: mercenaries, craftsmen, traders, entertainers. Coin flows freely from war plunder and mining wealth. The taverns riot nightly. The forges run day and night. It is a city drunk on its own success, and the party shows no signs of stopping. Goblins consider it a banner announcing their nation's rise. Every evening, firelancers discharge their weapons skyward in celebration—the booms that give the city its nickname ('Boomtown').

The Approach.description: The road from the lava fields climbs toward Smoketown through hardite checkpoints. Guards question travelers: purpose, skills, allegiances. Prove useful or move on. The ancient walls rise ahead, smoke billowing above them, the boom of  celebrations echoing off stone.

The Maw.description: A vast open-pit forge built into a volcanic fissure, where rivers of magma provide endless heat for industrial smelting. Crane-gantries span the pit. Chains lower crucibles into the glow. Hundreds of workers tend the operation night and day.

The Forge Quarter.description: The industrial heart. Massive forges built into ancient chambers, their original architecture repurposed as crucibles and smelters. Hobgoblin smiths oversee production. The heat is crushing, the noise constant. Fire-lances, armor, siege weapons—the Goblinate war machine is built here.

The Crucible.description: A fortress within the city, built on ancient fortification ruins. Maximum security. Church of the Pyre archpriests oversee dragonfire production here—the alchemical fuel that powers Goblinate weapons. Guards at every entrance. No unauthorized access.

The Governor's Bastion.description: Seat of Goblinate power, built into the largest of the ancient structures. The Governor rules from here, attended by ministers who control the city's functions and fear for their lives. Petitioners wait in crowded halls. Soldiers stand watch. Executions happen in the courtyard when the Governor's displeasure falls.

The Pyre Cathedral.description: The Church of the Pyre's grandest temple, built around a burning pit that never goes out. Archpriests preach the coming purification. The faithful gather to watch public burnings—captured noble banners, foreign books, symbols of Larion's corruption fed to the flames.

The Crater.description: Central marketplace where an ancient dome collapsed centuries ago. Now filled with stalls, traders, and crowds at all hours. Plundered luxuries, raw ore, weapons, exotic goods from across Larion. Hobgoblin enforcers keep the peace. Deals are struck, fortunes made and lost, and the wealth of conquest flows freely.

The Sprawl.description: Entertainment district spreading from the ruins in a riot of construction. Taverns, gambling dens, fighting pits, music halls, anything goblins want and can pay for. Noise and revelry around the clock.

The Arena.description: A coliseum built into a natural bowl, where blood sport entertains the masses. Beast fights, gladiatorial combat, prisoners earning redemption through entertaining violence. The crowds are enthusiastic. The betting is fierce. Champions become celebrities. Losers become object lessons.

The Villas.description: Where new goblin wealth lives. Mansions built up from ancient ruins, decorated with plundered art and imported luxuries. Underground chambers converted to mushroom gardens and moss grottos—the subterranean beauty goblins once only dreamed of, now cultivated by the newly rich. Palanquins and fancy carriages crowd the streets.

The Old Ruins.description: Ancient structures the goblins haven't claimed or converted. Massive halls with ceilings lost in shadow. Carved passages leading deeper. Sealed chambers marked with warnings in dead languages. Treasure hunters venture in despite the risks.

The Lava Tubes.description: Passages beneath Smoketown—some carved by ancient builders, others natural volcanic formations, others dug by goblins seeking ore. The networks intersect in unmapped ways. Things live down here that predate goblin arrival. Useful for those who need to move unseen, if they survive.

Briarton.name: Briarton
Briarton.basicInfo: A crossroads market village in Highvale where farmers, traders, and travelers converge. Not large enough for walls or a proper council — the innkeeper and a few senior families settle disputes. Weekly market days bring buyers from surrounding settlements, making Briarton busier than its size suggests.
Briarton.hiddenInfo: Briarton's prosperity depends entirely on the crossroads trade. If merchants stop coming, the village dries up within a season. The senior families know this and will go to considerable lengths to resolve anything that threatens the market's reputation. There is no local militia — trouble is handled by whoever is willing and able.

The Market Square.description: An open cobbled space at the crossroads, ringed by the village's few permanent shops and the inn. A stone well at the center, a notice board, hitching posts for horses and carts. On market days the square fills with stalls, livestock pens, and merchants.

The Crossed Keys Inn.description: Briarton's only inn. Low-ceilinged common room, a large hearth, rooms upstairs for travelers. The innkeeper knows everyone's business. Simple food and local cider.

The Briars.description: Dense thicket and overgrown woodland north of the village. Narrow paths wind through tangled undergrowth. Easy to get lost in. Locals use them for foraging and shortcuts.

The Old Mill.description: An abandoned stone mill on a sluggish creek at the edge of the Briars. Roof partially collapsed, wheel long still, interior open to weather. Local children dare each other to explore it.

Whitecliff Temple.name: Whitecliff Temple
Whitecliff Temple.basicInfo: A fortress-temple carved from pale limestone cliffs, headquarters of the Order of the Dawnblade. The Order trains paladins and clerics in the fight against darkness, teaching holy magic, healing arts, and martial combat. The Temple of Light offers divine training to those with righteous hearts, while the Knight's Hall turns warriors into holy champions. Pilgrims come here seeking healing, blessing, and purpose. The Order pays well for monster hunting and undead purges.
Whitecliff Temple.hiddenInfo: Not everyone in the Order is righteous. Some knights use their authority to settle personal vendettas, and the Templars sometimes target political enemies. The High Priest is genuinely good but aging, and zealots agitate for expansion, while the merciful prefer a softer approach. Meanwhile, something powerful stirs in the haunted Blackbarrow site to the north, and the Order hasn't been able to contain it. All willing hands are encouraged to train and report to Blackbarrow Camp once they're ready to face the threat.

Pilgrim's Gate.description: Fortified entrance where pilgrims and warriors arrive. Guards in white cloaks check weapons while priests offer blessings. A memorial wall lists knights who died fighting darkness.

Temple of Light.description: Soaring cathedral where clerics train in healing and divine magic. Head Priestess teaches heirwho can prove t faith. Healing services available for donation.

Knight's Hall.description: Training halls where paladins and holy warriors learn to fight evil. Master-at-Arms runs brutal drills. Paladin training requires completion of an ordeal, but includes weapons, armor, and sacred oaths.

Armory.description: Well-stocked with blessed weapons and holy symbols. Knights can requisition gear for missions. Civilians can purchase blessed weapons.

Mission Board.description: Where the Order posts bounties and quests: confirmed undead kills, clearing haunted locations, demon/lich elimination. Proof always required for payment.

High Priest's Sanctum.description: Private chambers of High Priest. Only knights in good standing or those with urgent holy business gain audience.

Pilgrim's Rest.description: Simple inn where pilgrims stay for free (donations encouraged). Common room shares tales of miracles and darkness overcome. Good place to hear rumors about undead sightings.

Blackbarrow Camp.name: Blackbarrow Camp
Blackbarrow Camp.basicInfo: A camp set up outside the Blackbarrow, a massive, ancient burial site recently unearthed by a flood. The wards holding the dead within weaken, and undead and other cursed creatures are breaking free. The Order of the Dawnblade has set up the camp outside to contain the menance as it scouts and studies the site. Tent camps house knights, mercenaries, and volunteers preparing for missions into cursed depths.
Blackbarrow Camp.hiddenInfo: The barrows contain more than undead—something ancient and powerful sleeps beneath them, and the recent surge in undead activity suggests it's waking. It may be a demon, or it may be something stranger. The Order suspects cultists are performing rituals in the deep barrows but hasn't found proof. The zealots want to storm the barrows en masse, but the first time it was attempted losses were severe. Unholy relics and ancient treasures lie buried within, protected by the restless dead.

Camp Gates.description: Fortified entrance where knights stand guard. A board lists current threats and bounties. Volunteers can sign up for missions here—no experience required.

Command Tent.description: Where the Knight-Commander assigns missions and pays bounties. Current priority clear the Outer Barrows, investigate the Deep Barrows, rescue missing knights.

War Camp.description: Rows of tents where warriors prepare for battle. Smiths repair armor, priests bless weapons, veterans share advice. Good place to hire companions or join existing groups heading into the barrows.

Chapel Tent.description: Field temple where priests offer healing, blessing, and last rites. Free healing for those on Order missions. Priests will bless weapons and armor for 50 gold (advantage against undead for one mission).

Supply Depot.description: Quartermaster sells holy water, blessed oil, silver weapons, and anti-undead supplies at fair prices. Those on Order missions get 20% discount.

The Academy of the Arcane.name: The Academy of the Arcane
The Academy of the Arcane.basicInfo: A fortress-academy of white stone rising from a forested island in a lake, where mages from across Larion train in the arcane arts. Tall towers connected by enclosed bridges overlook courtyards where students practice spells under watchful eyes. The Grand Library's dome dominates the skyline, while dormitories, laboratories, and lecture halls fill the grounds. The Academy fields mages across the realm, responding to magical threats and violations of arcane law.
The Academy of the Arcane.hiddenInfo:
Students advance through nine ranks known as Circles based on  completing Practica—missions beyond the Academy's walls. New arrivals are First Circle and unassigned to any College. After completing initial Practica, students join one of five Colleges: Sentinels (protection, combat magic, wards), Seekers (investigation, analysis, lore), Menders (healing, curse-breaking, purification), Binders (summoning, planar negotiation, pacts), and Shapers (artifice, enchantment, transformation).

First Circle students are always paired with a higher-Circle partner for their initial Practica.

Practica are posted on boards in the Great Hall: Investigation (something magical is wrong, find out what), Containment (secure a creature or effect), Recovery (retrieve something from somewhere dangerous), Assistance (help a settlement with a magical problem), and Monitoring (check on restricted sites or known practitioners).

The Academy is currently stretched thin. Reports of Pact violations are increasing, fire cult activity is escalating, and strange magical phenomena are being reported across multiple regions. Senior mages are deployed constantly, and there's constant debate among the Masters. First Circle students are being sent on Practica earlier than tradition would dictate.

A permanent portal in the Gatehouse connects to a hidden alcove in Ardan's Watch's Old Town, allowing quick travel between the Academy and the capital. Similar portal connections link to the Tower of Spells in Ardan's Watch and the Tower of the Pact in the Bright Kingdoms.

The Gatehouse.description: Fortified entrance at the end of the only bridge across the lake. Guarded by a pair of golems that finish each other's sentences and whichever mage is on duty. Visitors are admitted on approval of the Masters.

The Grounds.description: Manicured lawns and gravel paths between buildings. Students hurry to classes, study under trees, practice spells in designated areas. Notice boards post lecture schedules, tournament brackets, and—most watched—Practica assignments.

The Sphinx's Chamber.description: A domed chamber of ancient stone beneath the Academy's central tower, older than the buildings above. The Sphinx has dwelt here since before the Academy's founding, bound by pacts no one fully understands. Students may visit if they choose. What happens inside is private—she might speak, might show visions, might say nothing at all.

The Portal Chamber.description: A secure room housing portal connections to Ardan's Watch and other Academy-affiliated sites. The main portal to the capital sees constant use. Others require authorization and logged purpose. Students departing for distant Practica often leave from here.

The Great Hall.description: Soaring ceiling. Long tables where students cluster by College, though First Circle students sit together in their own section until they're assigned. Masters dine at the high table. The Practica board dominates one wall.

The Training Yards.description: Open courtyards here students train under supervision. Scorch marks, frost patches, and residual enchantments mark where things went wrong. Training constructs repair themselves between sessions. Masters and senior students observe from covered walkways.

The Dueling Platform.description: Raised stone circle with protective wards where students settle disputes or compete in sanctioned matches. Formal duels require witness and registration. Informal ones happen anyway.

The Grand Library.description: Five floors of books, scrolls, and catalogued artifacts. Enchanted finding systems help locate materials. Study carrels and reading rooms fill with students preparing for Practica or researching problems they've encountered in the field.

The Dormitories.description: Several towers housing students. First Circle share rooms; senior students earn private quarters. Common rooms by College serve as gathering spaces where students decompress after Practica, share stories of what they've seen, and speculate about assignments to come.

The Laboratories.description: Specialized workshops for alchemy, artifice, and experimental work. Each lab has a Master in charge and a waiting list for time. Students preparing for Practica often need specific preparations—potions, enchanted tools, protective items.

The Masters' Wing.description: Private chambers and offices where faculty live and work. Students are summoned here for assessment reviews, disciplinary matters, or special assignments.

Forge-town.name: Forge-town
Forge-town.basicInfo: A prosperous artisan town founded by dwarf, elf and gnome crafters. Originally known for smithing and metallurgy, it has become a haven for artisans and builders of all trades and arts and draws students, travelers and merchants from all Larion. It is home to the Guild of All Arts, whose golden hammer-and-chisel emblem is famous throughout the kingdoms.
Forge-town.hiddenInfo: The Guild of All Arts governs through its Council of Masters. Masters compete for commissions and prestige while training the next generation. Masters take apprentices who become like family, creating loyalty networks that sometimes conflict with Guild unity. The quarterly Craft Fair draws buyers from across the realm and determines annual rankings. Everyone knows everyone's work - reputation is everything. Dwarven smiths, gnomish artificers, elven woodcarvers, and halfling jewelcrafters are common sights. The Guild actively recruits talented craftspeople regardless of race. Recent monster and bandit attacks from the nearby Wolfwood have everyone nervous - the Guard is stretched thin and the Guild is hiring adventurers for protection. Competition for commissions and prestige drives Guild politics. The quarterly Craft Fair determines annual rankings and attracts buyers from across the region.

Town Square.description: Paved plaza surrounded by workshops. A bronze statue of the Founder (a legendary dwarven smith) stands at center. Benches where masters gather to discuss techniques and gossip. Market stalls sell materials, tools, and finished works. The quarterly Craft Fair transforms this into a massive showcase.

The Guild of All Arts.description: Grand building where the Guild Council meets, contracts are negotiated, and apprentices register. Inside: trophy cases display past masterworks. The main hall hosts gatherings, competitions, and celebrations. Notice boards post available commissions and calls for help.

The Forge Quarter.description: A street of smithies. Forges burn hot, apprentices work bellows, masters hammer steel into shape. Specializations vary - armor, weapons, tools, decorative work.

The Proving Yard.description: Open area where finished weapons and armor are tested. Straw dummies, striking posts, and worn practice equipment. Masters demonstrate their work's quality here. Combat training offered to those who want to understand what they're making.

Woodworkers' Row.description: Carpenters, carvers, furniture makers, bowyers - if it's made of wood, someone here crafts it.

The Leather District.description: Tanners, leatherworkers, and cobblers. The smell takes getting used to. Hides hang in various stages of treatment. Skilled hands cut and stitch leather into armor, boots, belts, bags.

The Market Stalls.description: Permanent stalls selling raw materials - ingots, leather, wood, gemstones, alchemical supplies. Merchants who understand quality and can spot flaws.

The Steady Hand Inn.description: Where craftspeople drink after long days. Built by carpenters showing off their skills - carved and inlaid woodwork covers almost every surface. The food is hearty, portions generous. Conversations center on commissions, techniques, apprentices. Brawls over craft quality are common but rarely serious.

The Masters' Chamber.description: Private rooms where master craftspeople meet to discuss Guild business and judge disputes. Apprentices aren't allowed. The walls display the masterworks that earned each Master their title.

The Archive.description: Library of techniques, patterns, and designs accumulated over generations. Detailed diagrams, material properties, master-crafted examples. Accessible to Guild members working on serious projects.

The Timber Store.description: Massive warehouse storing seasoned wood - oak, ash, maple, exotic imports. The smell is incredible. Knowing which wood for which purpose takes years to learn.

The Sinkwood.name: The Sinkwood
The Sinkwood.basicInfo: A forest where the ground is collapsing into caverns below. Trees grow at strange angles, roots exposed, new sinkholes appearing weekly. The village of Wortwell harvests rare reagents from the edges - moonpetals, witchcap mushrooms, silvervein moss - but safe gathering grounds shrink every season..
The Sinkwood.hiddenInfo: A myconid colony is excavating toward a buried grove - a petrified forest from an earlier age, saturated with old magic the myconids want to cultivate.
