They don't understand or care that they're destroying the surface. The myconids have workers, guards, and a sovereign who thinks in centuries.

The Tilting Pines.description: Where the healthy forest starts leaning. Witch-signs painted on bark mark safe routes and gathering grounds - some crossed out as the danger has spread. The ground feels hollow underfoot. Through the tilted trunks, you can see where trees have fallen entirely, root-balls torn up, dark holes leading down.

Witches' Hollow.description: A mossy depression where Wortwell's gatherers harvest the safest of the rare reagents. Moonpetals glow faintly in the shade. Silvervein moss coats the northern rocks. The hollow is still stable - for now - but the ground has dropped a foot since spring. Gatherers work quickly and leave before dark.

The New Sinkhole.description: A ragged hole thirty feet across, tree roots dangling over the edge, darkness below. The earth still crumbles at the edges. Something carved these tunnels - the walls are too smooth for natural collapse.

The Upper Caverns.description: Rough tunnels descending into the earth, walls studded with pale fungal growths. Passages branch ahead, some barely person-sized, others wide enough for something much larger. Spores drift in the still air.

Greenmeadow.name: Greenmeadow
Greenmeadow.basicInfo: A beautiful farming village in Keep Corwin's fertile heartlands, where golden wheat fields and flowering orchards spread across gentle hills. Stone cottages with flower boxes line cobbled streets, and an ancient oak shades the village green.
Greenmeadow.hiddenInfo: The harvest is always bountiful here - something about this land makes things grow with unnatural vigor. Old-timers speak of fey bargains made generations ago, but the specifics are lost. The villagers are kind, hospitable people who've never had to be warriors. Recent beast attacks have left them nervous. They need defenders but can't afford mercenaries. The population is mostly human farmers with a few halfling families who manage the orchards. A handful of gnomes run the mill.

The Village Green.description: Ancient oak tree shading a grassy common. Children play here during the day, adults gather in evenings. A stone well at center provides sweet water. Notice board posts news and calls for help. This is where the community meets.

The Golden Grain.description: Cozy tavern and inn with a thatched roof and flower boxes. The common room has a large hearth and mismatched comfortable furniture. The food is simple but excellent - everything grown locally. Rooms upstairs for travelers. The innkeeper knows everyone's business.

Market Square.description: Small marketplace where farmers sell produce, bakers offer fresh bread, and the occasional traveling merchant sets up. The abundance here is obvious - cabbages the size of shields, apples perfect and gleaming, grain that seems to shimmer gold in sunlight.

The Elder's House.description: Largest cottage, home to the village elder. A combination residence and meeting place where important decisions are made. The elder is a wise woman who's lived here seventy years and remembers when things were different.

The Mill.description: Water mill run by gnomish family who also maintain the village's clever irrigation system. The mechanism is more sophisticated than a village this size should have - enchanted, some say, or at least built by someone who knew more than just mechanics.

The Fields.description: Golden wheat, green orchards, vegetable gardens. Everything thrives here with minimal effort. Walking these fields feels peaceful, restorative. Farmers work the land, singing old songs. The attacks always come from the forest side.

The Forest Edge.description: Where the fields meet the dark forest. A line of old stones marks the boundary - not quite a wall, more like territorial markers. Rangers have built a small watchtower here.

Riverside Manor.name: Riverside Manor
Riverside Manor.basicInfo: A modest manor house overlooking the Silverflow, built of gray stone with a slate roof and tall windows. The grounds include gardens, stables, and a small vineyard.
Riverside Manor.hiddenInfo: Seat of a minor noble line with roots three centuries deep. The estate's wealth comes from the vineyard, tenant farms in Willowford village, and river trade. Ashbury Hall on the eastern hill is the nearest noble neighbor—relations have varied over the generations. The old Fallen Chapel near the grounds predates the manor itself, built over something older still.

The Courtyard.description: Gravel drive circles a stone fountain carved with leaping salmon—the family crest. Outbuildings frame the space: stables, carriage house, servants' quarters. An old wishing well near the gate, coins still visible at the bottom. The road continues past the gate toward Willowford village.

The Entry Hall.description: Flagstone floor, vaulted ceiling, the main staircase rising to the upper chambers. The family portraits begin here—oldest by the door, most recent near the stairs. A rack for cloaks and muddy boots, a stand for walking sticks and umbrellas.

The Great Hall.description: High ceilings, a fireplace large enough to stand in, old hunting trophies on the walls—including a basilisk head that may or may not be genuine. Banners of the family line hang above the mantel. The flagstones near the hearth are worn smooth from generations of feet.

The Study.description: Bookshelves line three walls—history, estate records, and an unexpectedly deep collection on local folklore. A window seat overlooks the courtyard. The desk is positioned to catch morning light.

The Dining Room.description: Long oak table that could seat twenty, built from a single tree felled on the estate. Silver candlesticks, old and heavy, bear the salmon crest. Windows overlook the gardens, designed to catch the sunset.

The Kitchens.description: Twin hearths, walk-in pantry, copper pots on ceiling hooks. Built for a full staff and large gatherings. A dumbwaiter connects to the dining room above. Herb garden visible through the window, planted in the old Valdric style.

The Stables.description: Stone building with stalls for a dozen horses, built to the same standard as the manor itself. Tack room with pegs for a full hunt's worth of gear. A carriage house stands beyond.

The Gardens.description: Walled garden of roses—varieties collected over generations, some quite rare. Gravel paths wind between mossy stone benches and copper sundial. A gate in the far wall opens onto a path descending toward the Fallen Chapel.

The Vineyard.description: Terraced hillside planted with old grape stock—Silverflow whites, prized in the region. Stone retaining walls built by the estate's founders. View down to the river and the Old Mill on its banks.

Willowford.name: Willowford
Willowford.basicInfo: A village of two dozen households where the Silverbend Brook meets the Silverflow River. Stone cottages with thatched roofs cluster around a common green. The ford that gives the village its name is still crossable on foot at low water.
Willowford.hiddenInfo: Tenant village of Riverside Manor—the families here work the estate's farmland and owe rent and loyalty to whoever holds the manor. The Willow Ford Inn serves as the village's social hub and informal council hall. Old rivalries between farming families simmer beneath the surface. The village has its own rhythm and politics regardless of what happens at the manor above.

The Village Green.description: Common grazing land at the village center, ringed by cottages and the old wellhouse. A standing stone of unknown origin marks one corner—locals leave small offerings there at planting and harvest. The road forks here: north to Riverside Manor, east toward Ashbury lands.

The Willow Ford Inn.description: Two-story building of whitewashed stone, the largest structure in the village. A common room with a fire always burning, four rooms to let upstairs. The innkeeper knows everyone's business and most of their secrets.

The Smithy.description: Forge and workshop at the edge of the green, serving the village and surrounding farms. The smith makes tools, repairs plows, shoes horses. A small shrine to the Crafter sits in the corner, kept hot with coals from the fire.

Ashbury Hall.name: Ashbury Hall
Ashbury Hall.basicInfo: A grand manor house on the eastern hill overlooking the Silverflow valley. Red brick with white stone trim, tall chimneys, manicured grounds. Larger and more prosperous than neighboring Riverside Manor.
Ashbury Hall.hiddenInfo: Seat of the Ashbury family, minor nobility with ambitions toward greater influence. The estate's wealth comes from sheep, wool trade, and strategic marriages. Lady Ashbury is the current head of household—young, widowed, sharp, patient. The family has long had designs on absorbing Riverside Manor into their holdings. Relations between the houses have swung between rivalry, alliance, and intermarriage over the generations.

The Front Drive.description: Sweeping gravel approach lined with ancient oaks, each trunk broader than a cart. The family crest—a ram's head in gold—marks the iron gates. Visitors can see both Riverside Manor and Willowford in the valley below.

The Main Hall.description: High-ceilinged entrance hall of polished wood and red carpet. Portraits of Ashbury ancestors in gilded frames, displayed more prominently than taste might dictate. A footman always present to receive guests or turn away the unwelcome.

The Drawing Room.description: Where Lady Ashbury receives visitors—velvet chairs, a marble fireplace, windows facing toward Riverside Manor. Tea service always ready. The room is designed to make guests feel either welcomed or outmatched, depending on Lady Ashbury's preference.

The Sculpture Garden.description: Formal garden behind the hall, gravel paths between marble statues and trimmed hedges. A private space for confidential conversations. The centerpiece is a fountain featuring the hero Halidan slaying a wyrm—an Ashbury ancestor, supposedly.

The Fallen Chapel.name: The Fallen Chapel
The Fallen Chapel.basicInfo: An old stone chapel near the grounds of Riverside Manor, partially collapsed into a sinkhole. Moss-covered walls, broken roof beams open to the sky. A path descends from the manor gardens above.
The Fallen Chapel.hiddenInfo: The chapel predates the manor by centuries—built over an older site whose purpose is unclear. The collapse revealed tunnels beneath, carved stone passages that are not natural and not part of any known construction. The chapel was once dedicated to the Lady of Mercy, but the iconography in the tunnels below is older and stranger.

The Chapel Ruins.description: Broken stone walls, pews rotted and scattered, the altar cracked down the middle. Half the floor has collapsed into darkness below. Faded frescoes of the Lady of Mercy still visible on the standing walls. Birds nest in the exposed rafters.

The Descent.description: A treacherous climb down through the collapsed floor into the tunnels beneath. Broken flagstones, roots pushing through the walls. The worked stone of the tunnels is visible below—too regular to be natural, too old to be the chapel's foundation.

The Ancient Passage.description: Carved stone corridor predating the chapel above by unknown centuries. The walls bear symbols in a script no longer used—spirals, eyes, shapes that might be letters or might be warnings. The passage branches into darkness. Air moves from somewhere deeper.

The Old Mill.name: The Old Mill
The Old Mill.basicInfo: A stone watermill on the banks of the Silverflow, downstream from Riverside Manor. The great wheel turns with the current. Visible from the manor's vineyard above.
The Old Mill.hiddenInfo: The mill serves Willowford and the surrounding farms, grinding grain for the region. Ownership has historically been tied to Riverside Manor. The millpond attracts wildlife—and occasionally things from upriver that wash down during floods. The miller's family has worked here for four generations and knows the river's moods better than anyone.

The Mill House.description: Stone building straddling the millrace, the great wheel groaning as it turns. The grinding floor above, storage below, the miller's quarters in the back.

The Millpond.description: Dammed section of river behind the mill, still and deep. Willows trail their branches in the water. Good fishing here—the miller sells rights to locals. A wooden dock for small boats, used for clearing debris from the sluice gates.

Clearwater Springs.name: Clearwater Springs
Clearwater Springs.basicInfo: A tranquil sanctuary built around natural hot springs whose waters possess healing properties. Stone pools of varying temperatures cascade down a hillside beneath ancient willows. The Springs are maintained by the Order of the Flowing Waters, healers who welcome all who come seeking restoration of body or spirit. Simple lodges provide accommodation while bath houses offer privacy and comfort.
Clearwater Springs.hiddenInfo: The springs have been sacred since before the kingdom's founding - the waters truly do heal, speeding recovery from injury and curing many diseases. The healing is real but not magical; something in the water and the peaceful atmosphere creates genuine restoration. People come here to recover from battles, cure lingering illnesses, or simply find peace after trauma. The Order charges enough to maintain the facilities but turns no one away for inability to pay. The Springs attract all races and social classes - nobles share pools with farmers, knights with scholars. The healing waters don't care about birth or wealth. This creates an unusual social space where normal hierarchies don't apply. The Order maintains strict rules: no weapons, no violence, no loud voices. Break the rules and you're expelled permanently. Most visitors stay a week or two; some stay months. Romance and unlikely friendships blossom here.

The Welcome House.description: Entry building where newcomers register and are shown to lodging. The Order's healers assess each visitor's needs and prescribe soaking regimens. A notice board lists rules and activities.

The Main Pools.description: Series of stone-lined pools at varying temperatures, open to the sky and surrounded by willows. People soak in comfortable silence or quiet conversation. Steam rises gently. The water is clear and slightly sulfurous. Attendants ensure rules are followed.

The Private Baths.description: Individual soaking chambers for those seeking solitude or privacy. Small pools in stone rooms, each with a wooden door and simple furnishings. Peaceful and quiet.

The Hot Springs.description: The natural source where water bubbles from deep underground. The hottest pools, surrounded by smooth rocks. This is the most potent area for healing. Only those with serious ailments are directed here.

The Deep Pool.description: The source spring, a deep pool where water wells up from far below. Ropes mark the edge - it's dangerous to swim here, the current from below is strong. The Order's senior healers sometimes descend here for meditation, but they don't encourage others to try.

The Meditation Garden.description: Landscaped grounds with walking paths, stone benches, and carefully tended flowers. A place to sit and think, or walk and talk quietly with fellow visitors. Many friendships begin here.

The Gardens.description: Herb gardens where the Order grows medicinal plants, vegetable plots that supply the kitchens, and flower beds for beauty. Visitors can help with gardening as part of their recovery - gentle labor that occupies the hands while freeing the mind.

The Lodges.description: Simple wooden buildings with shared rooms. Clean, comfortable, sparse. All are welcome regardless of ability to pay. Meals are served communally in a dining hall - simple, healthy food. Conversation stays quiet and civil.

Blackbarrow.name: Blackbarrow
Blackbarrow.basicInfo: A vast field of ancient burial mounds recently uncovered by floods. Templars from Whitecliff keep watch over the threats emerging from the site. The barrows stretch across windswept moorland. At the field's center rises a great earthwork mound larger than all others. The Order of the Dawnblade permits forays but forbids anyone from approaching the central mound.
Blackbarrow.hiddenInfo: The barrows are awakening because the dead were buried alive in a ritual, transformed into undying guardians for what lies sealed beneath the central mound - and that threat is stirring. Each quadrant represents a different stratum of that ancient civilization—warriors, nobility, commoners, priests—all sacrificed to create eternal wardens. They guard the undead form of their own sorceror-emperor, a ruthless tyrant who will stop at nothing to dominate Larion. The guardians' vows forbid them from allowing anyone into the site, regardless of their intentions. They fight with a fanatical fury born of unshakable conviction. NOTE: The site is dangerous during the day, but extremely hazardous at night, when supernatural threats are most active.

Templar's Watch.description: A field fortification at the field's southern edge garrisoned by templars of the Barrow Guard. The knights' main mandate is to keep any undead from escaping the site, but they also warn visitors that they will hunt down and kill any who attempt entry into the The Heart.

North Barrows.description: The northern quarter contains low, broad mounds reinforced with fitted stone. Their entrances are narrow gaps between massive capstones, many sealed with rusted iron bars bent outward from within. Stone markers bear weathered symbols: crossed swords, shield bosses, helm crests.

East Barrows.description: The eastern quarter holds tall, elaborate passage tombs built from dressed limestone blocks. Their facades feature carved columns and lintels inscribed with flowing script in no known language. Many tombs have collapsed porticos revealing glimpses of interior chambers—stone benches, carved niches, the suggestion of deeper halls. Precious metals once decorated these entrances; now only empty settings remain where gems were pried out. The grass here grows lush and green even in winter, fed by something beneath.

South Barrows.description: The southern quarter is a chaotic sprawl of simple earthen mounds, barely distinguishable from natural hills. Their entrances are crude stone-lined pits or rough-cut passages, many collapsed or overgrown with thornbushes. These barrows cluster around larger central mounds. Crude stone markers lean at angles, their inscriptions worn to illegibility. Wildflowers bloom thick here—poppies, nightshade, foxglove—in colors too vivid.

West Barrows.description: The western quarter features chambered cairns built from black stone unlike anything found in Highvale. Their entrances are marked by carved pillars showing spirals, crescents, and symbols that seem to shift when viewed peripherally. Some passages descend at unnatural angles. Altars of smooth dark stone stand before several entrances, their surfaces stained. Bones hang from the lintel stones: small animal skulls strung on rotted cord, forming curtains across certain doorways.

The Heart.description: At the field's center rises the Great Mound, a massive earthwork three times the height of any other barrow, its slopes too steep and regular to be natural. A ring of standing stones surrounds it—weathered megaliths carved with glyphs. The templars have placed blessed chains between the stones and forbidden all approach on pain of execution. The entrance, if there is one, isn't visible from outside the ring. The grass around the Great Mound grows in a perfect circle of absolute dead brown, and no bird crosses above it. Whatever sleeps here, the dead buried in their thousands were meant to keep it sleeping.

Wortwell Village.name: Wortwell Village
Wortwell Village.basicInfo: A village of hedge witches, herbalists, and gatherers at the edge of the Sinkwood. No formal governance - practitioners settled here to be near rare reagents and stayed to share knowledge. Most residents work the drying sheds, process alchemical ingredients, or guide buyers through safe forest paths. Wortwell supplies tinctures, poultices, and rarer things to apothecaries across Highvale.
Wortwell Village.hiddenInfo: The village runs on practical knowledge passed between practitioners, not Academy training. Residents are territorial about expertise and dismissive of book-learned wizards. Tension is building over the Sinkwood crisis - the forest that sustains them is collapsing, shipments are delayed, and no one agrees on what to do about it. Outside alchemists are getting impatient. The village needs capable help but won't trust just anyone.

The Wort Road.description: A cart track winding through meadows thick with cultivated herbs - feverfew, woundwort, ghostmallow. Drying racks and small cottages dot the roadside. Wortwell has no walls or proper center, just a gradual thickening of buildings until you're among them. Larger structures cluster near the forest edge - workshops and drying sheds.

The Common Ground.description: An open space where the village conducts its business - a well, a market post for visiting buyers, and Tetha's cottage with its perpetually open door. Witches and gatherers pass through at all hours. No tavern, but Grandmother Yew's place serves drinks and food to anyone who asks nicely. The Sinkwood looms at the northern edge, trees already tilting at wrong angles.

The Drying Sheds.description: Long wooden buildings where harvested reagents are processed - herbs hanging from rafters, mushrooms laid on racks, roots packed in sand. Ryel's operation dominates: three sheds and a proper alchemical workshop with a brick chimney that never stops smoking. Workers sort, dry, grind, and pack. Carts wait to carry finished goods to buyers across the region.

The Oneirary.name: The Oneirary
The Oneirary.basicInfo: A crooked stone tower on a hill outside of Ardan's Watch, three stories tall and listing slightly. Ivy and flowering vines cover the lower half. The upper windows glow at all hours. A winding path connects it to the city. No fence, no gate, no lock. A wooden sign by the path reads THE ONEIRARY, with a smaller addendum: Visitors Welcome.
The Oneirary.hiddenInfo:
Home of Mikol, gnomish chronicler of the History of Undiscovered Realms. The tower is cluttered, warm, and welcoming—less a place of study than of habitation by a person who happens to study constantly. Volucats (small winged cats) come and go through open windows at every level. The manuscripts of the History fill most of the second floor and have begun colonizing the first. They are not organized in any system a visitor could follow; Mikol finds passages by memory and by asking the quills, who maintain their own idiosyncratic index.

The Oneirary is a good place to hear rumors, oblique warnings, or fragmentary descriptions of distant places—filtered through Mikol's visionary idiom. Nothing he says should be taken as reliable intelligence, but players who listen carefully may find seeds of real adventures in his ramblings. He is also simply a pleasant person to visit.

The volucats are friendly to visitors, will sit in laps, and occasionally steal small shiny objects, which Mikol apologetically returns.

The Path.description: A winding dirt path climbing the hill east of Ardan's Watch. Wildflowers grow thick on either side. Partway up, a wooden sign painted in wobbly letters. Small winged shapes circle the tower above—volucats on their rounds. The tower door at the top stands open.

The Hearth.description: The tower's ground floor. A kitchen and sitting room in one, cluttered but comfortable—mismatched chairs, a fire that's always going, tea things perpetually in use. Stacks of manuscript have colonized every horizontal surface. Volucats doze on shelves and chair-backs. A narrow stair spirals up.

The Scriptorium.description: The second floor. A round room dominated by three writing desks where enchanted quills scratch away without pause, recording in three languages simultaneously. Tottering stacks of manuscript surround them—the accumulated decades of the History of Undiscovered Realms. Mikol's dictation chair sits at the center, a battered armchair with a volucat-sized dent in each cushion. The quills pause when someone enters, nibs lifted, as though listening.

North Canal.name: North Canal
North Canal.basicInfo: A major shipping lane threading between the outer islands of the Spice Isles. Merchant galleys and fishing boats share these waters with naval patrols and, increasingly, pirates. Redfin Waystation sits on a rocky islet mid-channel, offering shelter, supplies, and news for passing vessels.

Redfin Waystation.description: A weathered trading post built on a rocky islet. Stone quays can accommodate a dozen vessels at once. A tavern called the Saltspray, a chandlery, and the harbormaster's office cluster around a small plaza where sailors swap news and captains haggle for cargo space.

The Canal Waters.description: Open water between green-clad islands. Merchant ships under colorful sail pass fishing boats and the occasional patrol galley. The channel runs deep and well-marked, but rocky shoals lurk near the islands.

North Canal.hiddenInfo: The Feathered Fleet—a crew of seabird beastfolk pirates—has preyed on merchant ships in the North Canal for over a year. They strike from hidden coves and vanish before patrols arrive. Their captain, a vicious pelicanfolk named Scraw, took command after mutinying against the previous captain six months ago. Redfin's harbormaster Gull Makris is Scraw's uncle—he doesn't approve of the boy's choices but won't turn him in either. Ships damaged by pirate attacks often limp into Redfin with wounded sailors and angry merchants demanding someone do something. The Tevantia Admiralty has posted a bounty on Scraw, but the Fleet knows these waters better than any patrol captain.

Nocturne.name: Nocturne
Nocturne.basicInfo: The greatest city in the Moon Kingdoms sprawls across an alpine valley so steep and narrow that the sun never touches its western slope: the Shadowside. Black stone and silver spires climb the mountainside in foggy terraces that have never seen daylight. Across the valley floor, the eastern slopes catch a few hours of sun each day—the Sunside, where serfs work terraced farms under the constant gaze of the city above. Nocturne is beautiful, wealthy, and operates by its own rules.
Nocturne.hiddenInfo:
Nocturne is neutral ground where the great houses maintain estates. All prince-magisters are vampire lords, blessed by the Lady of Blood during the Elven Schism or sired by those who were. Four houses dominate:

House Vyrel: The oldest. Orthodox followers of the old rituals, believing proper reverence keeps the Lady's blessing flowing.

House Setharis: Ambitious and politically active. They honor the Lady as source of their power but focus on using that power in the world.

House Malren: Reclusive scholars who study the nature of vampirism and blood magic.

House Kaeris: The youngest house, only three centuries old. They built their power through trade and operate the Crescent House.

The Crescent House is where anything can be purchased: blood magic services, bindings, hexes, creatures, introductions to those who provide darker services. Nothing is displayed openly. You inquire, and arrangements are made.

The Lady of Three Faces is the moon goddess worshipped by common moon elves. The Temple of the Moon serves the populace; the prince-magisters honor the Lady of Blood above all.

The Silver Company is a mercenary force of exiles from Highvale, implicated in a failed coup a generation ago. They serve the prince-magisters but take outside contracts.

Demonborn are common. The Blackflame Quarter is theirs—taverns, gambling houses.

Crowkin roost in the city's high places—cliff-caves, tower-tops, ledges.

Mountain gnomes run shops throughout the Shadowside, supplied from their warren-workshops.

Giant bats used as flying mounts roost in the high caves.

The serfs on the Sunside live in relative prosperity.  But they have a lottery, and sometimes one of their number wins and is sent to the palace to live in luxury.

The Syndicate has a heavy presence in the city at its Factorage in Riverside and a major base of operations in the nearby Citadel. Foreigners are welcome in Nocturne, but watched.

High Pass Gate.description: The mountain passes converge here at a fortress carved into the living rock. Beyond the gate, the valley opens—Nocturne visible in the perpetual shadow of the western slope, the Sunside farms catching thin light across the river. Moon elf guards in black scale armor process arrivals. The air is cold and thin. Mist curls through the gate even at midday. Past the customs houses, a switchback road descends to the valley floor.

Riverside.description: The valley floor where the river runs cold and fast from the peaks. Warehouses, stables, markets for ordinary goods—grain, cloth, livestock, metalwork. The Syndicate's Factorage is a hub of trade in goods of all kinds. Merchants from the Bright Kingdoms and occasionally Highvale trade here under the watchful eye of moon elf factors. The real city rises above on the shadowed slope.

The Blackflame Quarter.description: The demonborn district climbs a terrace between the Foreigners' Quarter and the Shadowside proper. Black stone buildings are decorated with motifs of dark flame—carved, inlaid in copper and brass, burning in braziers that give off no heat. Taverns and gambling houses do good business here, run by demonborn families who've been in Nocturne for generations. They're charming hosts, and the entertainment is excellent. At the quarter's heart stands the Temple of the Black Flame, honoring the Bearer—the culture-hero who led the demonborn to sanctuary here. Inside, the eternal blackflame burns: dark fire that casts shadows instead of light.

Foreigners' Quarter.description: Where those who are not moon elves make their homes in Nocturne. The buildings here are newer, built in a mix of styles—Bright Kingdom arches beside Highvale timber-frames beside structures of no identifiable origin. The Guest House dominates a small square, its lanterns always lit. Nearby, the Silver Company maintains their headquarters, their banner a silver sword on black. Temples to foreign gods cluster on one street. The residents here are exiles, merchants, scholars, mercenaries.

The Guest House.description: A sprawling stone building around a central courtyard, part hostel and part caravanserai. Brenn keeps it running—a former mercenary healer from the Bright Kingdoms who came here years ago and never left. The lodging is free or nearly so. The courtyard serves meals at long tables where everyone sits together. The rooms are simple but clean. New arrivals are encouraged to register—for their own safety, of course. The other guests are a cross-section of everyone who washes up in Nocturne: merchants, scholars, exiles, adventurers, people who don't explain themselves, and a few who've been here for years.

Shadowside.description: The city proper, climbing the western slope in terraces connected by steep stairs and winding streets. No sunlight reaches here—ever. Mage-lights in silver cages provide cold illumination. The architecture is black stone inlaid with silver and obsidian, elegant and imposing. Moon elf businesses, residences, and social spaces fill these streets. Gnomish shops sell fine craftwork—jewelry, glass, silver, stonework of remarkable delicacy. The Temple of the Moon occupies a plaza of silver-veined marble. The Crescent House sits on a quiet street. Outsiders can walk here freely, but the city watches, and lingering too long in the wrong place draws attention.

The Estates.description: The great houses rise where Shadowside meets the peaks—baroque palaces of black stone and silver, their spires vanishing into perpetual mist. Each prince-magister's estate is a compound: palace, gardens of night-blooming flowers, servant quarters, and whatever else lies behind their walls. Private lifts and guarded stair-streets connect the Estates to the city below. You don't walk here uninvited. The air is colder, the shadows deeper, and the fog never lifts. From the Estates, the entire valley is visible: the city below, the Sunside farms, the river, the passes. The prince-magisters see everything.

Velmere.name: Velmere
Velmere.basicInfo: A crescent-shaped alpine valley famous throughout Larion for its wines and alchemical tinctures. The sunside slopes are covered in terraced vineyards. At the valley's heart stands a cold alpine lake. The village clusters along the lakeshore—stone houses with slate roofs, a market square, taverns serving the valley's vintages. Above on the shadowside, Chateau Velmere rises in elegant black stone, home to the lady who has ruled here for six centuries. Velmere is prosperous, picturesque, and proud of what it produces.
Velmere.hiddenInfo:
Lady Isaveth rules Velmere. She is old even by vampire standards—one of the first generation turned after the Lady of Blood's bargain. She has made winemaking and alchemy her passion across the centuries, and Velmere's reputation is her life's work. She is proud, cultured, and genuinely hospitable. She does not tolerate disruption to her domain's peace or reputation.

The wines of Velmere are exceptional. The alchemical tinctures—produced in the chateau's own laboratories—are harder to acquire and considerably more valuable. Healing draughts, elixirs of clarity, tinctures that grant temporary night-vision or resistance to cold. Lady Isaveth trades these selectively.

The villagers live well. The work is not hard, the valley is beautiful, and the lady's rule is light so long as nothing disturbs the order of things. They know what she is. They accept it. The blood price here is paid through a lottery system like elsewhere in the Moon Kingdoms—someone wins, goes to the chateau, doesn't return. The vintners' families have served for generations; some consider it an honor.

The high caves on the shadowside are forbidden. Locals say wolves den there. What actually lurks are feral vampires—a small pack, descendants of a lord who failed centuries ago. Lady Isaveth contains them rather than destroying them. Some say she feeds them; some say she uses them to dispose of problems; some say she simply cannot bring herself to destroy her own kind. Whatever the truth, travelers who wander too high on the shadowside sometimes don't come back.

The Silver Company occasionally takes contracts to thin the ferals when they grow too bold. Lady Isaveth permits this, barely.

Moonrise Pass.description: The narrow cut through the peaks that opens into Velmere. The road winds down through switchbacks, and the whole valley spreads below—the dark lake, the village lights, the terraced vineyards catching whatever thin light reaches the sunside, and the black shape of the chateau above it all. Moon elf guards keep a small post here.

Lakeshore.description: The village of Velmere proper, built along the cold alpine lake. Stone houses with slate roofs, flower boxes in windows. The market square hosts traders in wine, tinctures, and the valley's modest crafts. Taverns serve the local vintages. The lake is clear and very deep. Locals fish it but never swim in it.

The Vineyards.description: Terraced slopes climbing the sunside of the valley. The grapes here are varieties found nowhere else—cultivated over centuries for wines that fetch extraordinary prices in distant markets. Vintner families work the terraces as their ancestors did, knowledge passed down through generations. Stone pressing-houses dot the slopes.

Chateau Velmere.description: The lady's estate rises on the shadowside above the lake, black stone and silver trim, elegant spires disappearing into the mist that always clings to these heights. Gardens of night-blooming flowers surround the walls. Those who receive an invitation—merchants, visiting nobles, travelers who've caught the lady's interest—find a refined household, impeccable hospitality, and conversations that last until dawn. The chateau's laboratories produce the valley's famous tinctures. The wine cellars are legendary. What else lies within is not discussed.

Veildren.name: Veildren
Veildren.basicInfo: A river town built where a fast mountain stream drops through a series of falls into a narrow valley. Mills line the water—silk mills, grinding mills, the workshops of artisans who've practiced their trades for centuries. Veildren is famous for three things: spidersilk woven from creatures kept in the shadow-caves above the town, silvered weapons forged for those who hunt what lurks in the mountains, and glasswork of strange beauty—lenses, prisms, vessels said to hold moonlight itself. Above the town, on a promontory that catches the moon's path across the sky, stands the Seleneum—a temple-observatory of the Lady of Three Faces.
Veildren.hiddenInfo:
Lord Kethryn rules Veildren from a manor built into the cliffs above the falls. He is younger than most prince-magisters—only four centuries—and more interested in craft than courtly intrigue. He considers himself a patron of artisans and takes genuine pride in Veildren's reputation. He is demanding but fair, and the town's craftspeople respect him for understanding their work.

The spidersilk comes from veil-spinners—large, pale spiders kept in the shadow-caves above the town. They are not aggressive if properly handled, but they are unsettling, and the keepers who tend them are a strange and insular group. Recently something has disturbed the spinners.

The silvered weapons are genuine—effective against vampires, ghouls, and other creatures of the night. The smiths who make them know exactly what they're forging and why. Lord Kethryn tolerates this because the weapons are needed; feral vampires and worse things haunt the mountains. Hunters come to Veildren to arm themselves before heading into the high country.

The glasswork serves the Seleneum primarily. The priestesses of the Lady of Three Faces use crystal lenses and silver mirrors to gather and concentrate moonlight for rituals, prophecy, and purposes they don't fully explain. The vessels that 'hold moonlight' are real—pale liquid light that glows softly and has various alchemical and magical applications. The Seleneum sells these carefully and at high prices.

The Seleneum's high priestess, Mother Vaelith, is not a vampire—she is a common moon elf who has served the Lady of Three Faces for two centuries. She and Lord Kethryn have an understanding: he rules temporal matters, she rules spiritual ones.

The Gorge Road.description: The river cuts through a narrow gorge before opening into Veildren's valley. The road follows the water, carved into the cliff face, spray from the falls misting travelers as they pass. The roar of water echoes off the stone. At the gorge's end, the town comes into view—mills lining the river, workshops climbing the slopes, and high above, the glittering structure of the Seleneum catching whatever light reaches it.

The Millrace.description: The heart of Veildren, built along the river where the falls drive dozens of waterwheels. Silk mills process the pale thread from the shadow-caves into fabric that sells for gold-weight in distant markets. Grinding mills powder minerals and glass. The constant rush of water provides the rhythm of daily life. Workshops, merchant houses, and modest homes crowd the riverbanks. Bridges arch over the millrace at intervals, slick with spray.

The Silvering.description: The street of weaponsmiths, built on a terrace above the main millrace where the forges won't set the silk mills ablaze. The ring of hammers on metal sounds at all hours. The smiths here work silver into steel, crafting blades and arrowheads for those who hunt creatures that fear the pure metal. The shops sell openly to anyone with coin—hunters, mercenaries, adventurers, the occasional vampire lord arming retainers against feral kin. A silvered blade from Veildren is a mark of serious intent.

The Seleneum.description: The temple-observatory rises on a promontory above the town, positioned to catch the moon's path across the sky. Crystal lenses the size of cartwheels are set in silver frames. Mirrors angle and pivot on mechanisms. On clear nights, the whole structure glows as it gathers moonlight and focuses it into the inner chambers. Priestesses of the Lady of Three Faces tend the apparatus and conduct rituals during the lunar phases. Pilgrims come seeking prophecy. The curious come seeking wonder. The inner sanctums are closed to all but the initiated.

The Spider Caves.description: Above the town, where the shadowside cliffs rise sheer and dark, cave mouths open into the mountain. Here the veil-spinners are kept—pale spiders the size of dogs, tended by keepers who've learned their ways across generations. The caves are cool, damp, and thick with webbing that catches the faint light in strange patterns. The keepers move through with ease; visitors find it disorienting. The spiders are not aggressive, but they are watchful, and the deeper caves are off-limits to all but the senior keepers.

The Vale Sable.name: The Vale Sable
The Vale Sable.basicInfo: A quarantined valley in the high peaks, its only pass sealed by collapsed stone. Once the domain of House Sable, an ancient vampire bloodline known for blood magic mastery. Now overrun by feral vampires. Keep Sable still stands at the valley's head, black towers against black cliffs.
The Vale Sable.hiddenInfo:
Feral vampires have splintered into territorial packs throughout the vale. Some claim the high caves, some the forest, some the outer keep. They compete and occasionally fight. Lady Sable remains in the inner keep—the apex predator the others avoid. She is ancient and powerful, but her mind is gone.

The village has survivors—villagers who couldn't escape before the pass was sealed. They hide in basements and attics, move by day when the ferals are sluggish, and barricade themselves before dark. They cannot leave; the mercenaries would kill them. They know the vale's patterns: which packs hunt where, when Lady Sable emerges, where things of value might be found.

Keep Sable's library-laboratory is the greatest repository of blood magic research in Larion. This is why the great houses have not simply burned the vale clean. An assault might destroy it.

Lord Alayne Setharis, heir of House Setharis, was in the vale when it fell. He is believed to still be inside. His status is unknown.

The Cordon.description: A Silver Company encampment where the mountain road ends at a wall of collapsed stone. Warning markers declare the valley quarantined. Mercenaries in black and silver maintain the checkpoint. A row of heads on the palisade—some withered, some recent—shows what happens to those who emerge from the vale without permission. But the Company watches new arrivals carefully. Certain visitors, the capable and desperate-looking ones, get approached by a cloaked figure who lingers at the edge of camp. The pitch: there is a way in through the caves, and House Setharis pays very well for results.

The Caves.description: A network of natural caverns winding through the mountain, bypassing the sealed pass. The passages are narrow and dark, descending steeply before climbing again. The caves emerge through a trapdoor in a root cellar beneath a collapsed house on the edge of Sable Village.

Sable Village.description: Stone houses with slate roofs, like any Moon Kingdoms village—but doors hang broken, windows are dark, and dried blood stains the cobblestones. Ferals hunt the streets after dark. The survivors live in hiding: basements, attics, passages knocked through cellar walls. They move by day, take what they can from abandoned stores, and barricade themselves before dusk.

The Shadowvale.description: The wild heart of the valley between village and keep. Dense forest climbs the shadowside slopes. At the valley's center lies the Sable Lake, its waters so dark they appear black, reflecting nothing. Feral packs hunt here, marking territories in ways only they understand. The forest floor is scattered with bones. The path to Keep Sable winds along the lakeshore, exposed and dangerous.

Keep Sable.description: The castle rises where the valley ends, built into the cliff face—black stone towers against black rock. Keep Sable was part fortress, part laboratory, part library. The outer halls and lower levels are claimed by ferals now, led by something that might once have been Lady Sable's seneschal. They do not enter the inner keep. That is her domain. The library-laboratory lies at the castle's heart, and somewhere within, presumably, Alayne Setharis—and Lady Sable herself.

Seravel Springs.name: Seravel Springs
Seravel Springs.basicInfo: A hot springs sanctuary high in the frozen peaks that caters to local nobility and wealthy foreigners seeking the restorative treatments performed by its resident moon priestesses.
Seravel Springs.hiddenInfo:
Seraval Springs is neutral ground. The great houses have an understanding: no political violence here, no assassinations, no overt moves against rivals. This makes it valuable—the one place vampire lords can meet informally without their full retinues and defenses. Alliances are discussed in the steam. Marriages are negotiated. Secrets slip.

The treatments are real. The moon priestesses practice a blend of blood magic and lunar ritual that can heal wounds, slow aging, cure diseases that resist ordinary medicine. The treatments are expensive, sometimes dangerous, and occasionally require prices beyond coin—a memory given up, a future favor owed, a year of life traded for the removal of a curse. Foreigners who come seeking miracles sometimes get them. Sometimes they get something else.

The priestesses answer to a Mother Superior who has tended the springs for four centuries. She is not a vampire but has extended her life through the same arts she practices on guests. She maintains the sanctuary's neutrality with absolute authority. Those who break the peace here are not seen again.

The Grotto of the Veiled is for private consultations and the most delicate treatments—what happens there is not discussed. The Grotto of the Radiant is the main social pool, open to the moon through crystal skylights. The Grotto of the Waning is for those releasing something: illness, grief, curses, the dying seeking a peaceful end.

The Seravel Road.description: The approach to Seravel Springs climbs through snowfields and frozen pine forest, switchbacking up the mountainside. The air thins and sharpens. Ice hangs from branches in sparkling curtains. Near the top, the steam becomes visible first—white plumes rising against the dark peaks. Then the sanctuary appears: black stone buildings tucked into the shadowside cliffs, warm light glowing from windows, gardens somehow green despite the snow. Attendants in white greet arrivals and take their mounts to heated stables.

The Guest Hall.description: The main lodge, built from black stone with silver inlay and heated by channels of spring water running beneath the floors. The hall serves as inn, restaurant, and gathering space. Private chambers branch off the upper levels—each one appointed with dark wood, soft furs, and windows of moonlight glass that glow softly even on clouded nights. The common areas are hushed and refined. Guests take meals at small tables. The staff anticipates needs before they're voiced. A terrace offers views of the frozen peaks. For those seeking sunlight-there is none in the Seravel proper, as the entire complex lies in the vale's shadowside-attendants can guide them to the Sun Terrace above—a climb, but some consider it worth the effort.

The Grottoes.description: Three great chambers carved into the shadowside cliffs, connected by passages lined with shadow-blooming flowers—night orchids, black hellebore, moonpetals that open only in darkness. Each grotto houses a pool fed by the hot springs, steam curling up toward crystal skylights that glow with magically collected moonlight. The Grotto of the Radiant is largest, its pool open to the gathered moon-glow, where guests socialize in the warm water. The Grotto of the Veiled is smaller, private, curtained—for consultations and treatments not meant to be observed. The Grotto of the Waning is quietest, its waters said to ease the release of burdens, illness, and pain. Moon priestesses in pale robes move between the chambers, attending to guests and performing rituals.

The Sun Terrace.description: A climb up carved steps brings guests above the shadowside, to a stone platform at the peak where sunlight reaches for a few hours each day. The terrace is simple—benches, windbreaks, warming braziers for when the sun passes. Some guests come here to feel the light they've been denied below. Others come for the view: the frozen peaks stretching to the horizon, the valleys lost in shadow below, the world feeling very far away. Attendants bring warm drinks and blankets.

Temple of the Black Flame.name: Temple of the Black Flame
Temple of the Black Flame.basicInfo: A fortress-temple carved into a high peak, where black fire burns in braziers that have never been extinguished. The Temple of the Black Flame is the spiritual heart of demonborn culture—a place of pilgrimage, scholarship, and vigilance. The priests here guard against infernal incursion, maintaining wards and watching for signs of demonic activity throughout Larion. The temple is difficult to reach, the final approach a steep climb through ice and wind, but demonborn from across the world make the journey. Others come too: those seeking knowledge of infernal matters, those bearing curses they cannot break, those who need help that only the flame-keepers can provide.
Temple of the Black Flame.hiddenInfo:
The temple was founded by the Bearer of the Black Flame, the demonborn hero who led their people to sanctuary in the Moon Kingdoms. The Bearer's tomb lies in the deepest chamber, and the original blackflame still burns there—the source from which all other black flames are kindled.

The flame-keepers are demonborn priests who spend their lives studying infernal lore. They know the names and natures of demons, the terms of ancient pacts, the signs of possession and corruption. This knowledge exists to be used against the infernal, not to serve it. The temple maintains wards throughout the Moon Kingdoms and watches for breaches in the barriers between worlds.

Visitors seeking help with infernal matters—curses, possessions, contracts gone wrong, haunting—can petition the flame-keepers.

The library contains texts on infernal matters that exist nowhere else.

The moon elf lords tolerate the temple because it serves their interests—the flame-keepers have stopped more than one infernal incursion that would have threatened the Moon Kingdoms. An understanding, not an alliance.

The Pilgrim's Stair.description: The final approach to the temple—a stair carved into the mountain face, switchbacking up through ice and howling wind. No shelter, no railings, nothing but black stone steps worn smooth by centuries of pilgrims. Those who cannot make the climb on their own are not meant to reach the top. At the stair's foot, a waystation offers a last chance to rest and reconsider. At its peak, the temple gates.

The Black Court.description: An open courtyard just inside the temple gates, where braziers of black flame burn against the mountain wind. Pilgrims rest here after the climb. Petitioners wait to be seen. The architecture is severe—black stone, no ornamentation except the flame motifs carved into every surface. Flame-keepers move through, evaluating new arrivals.

The Hall of Names.description: A long gallery where black flames burn in niches along the walls, each flame representing a demon whose name the temple knows. Hundreds of flames. The keepers say that to name a thing is to have power over it, and this hall is a catalog of power. Petitioners with infernal problems are brought here for consultation.

The Library.description: The temple's library, carved deep into the mountain where the cold preserves the texts. Scrolls, books, tablets in languages that predate the Moon Kingdoms. The collected knowledge of infernal matters—demon hierarchies, pact structures, ward designs, case studies of possessions and exorcisms.

The Pilgrim Halls.description: Dormitories and training spaces for those who've come to stay—demonborn learning to master their heritage, petitioners undergoing treatment, the occasional outsider granted sanctuary. The rooms are austere but warm, heated by channels that draw from the black flames.

Crowkin Eyrie.name: Crowkin Eyrie
Crowkin Eyrie.basicInfo: A crowkin settlement built into sheer cliffs high above the treeline, where crowkin nest on wind-scoured ledges. The Eyrie is nearly inaccessible to those without wings—a vertical maze of roosts, halls, and bridges connecting cave-mouths across a cliff face. The crowkin here serve as messengers, guides, and information brokers throughout the Moon Kingdoms and beyond. They know things. They collect things. They trade in both. Those who make the difficult journey to the Eyrie—by crowkin escort, by magic, by climbing routes that kill more than they don't—find a market for secrets, lost items, and services available nowhere else.
Crowkin Eyrie.hiddenInfo:
The crowkin are acquisitive and inquisitive by nature—traits that made them unwelcome in their original homelands. They collect information obsessively. Their messenger network spans the Moon Kingdoms and reaches into Highvale, the Bright Kingdoms, even the Spice Isles. Every message they carry, they remember. Every rumor they hear, they file away. The Eyrie is the central node of this network.

The Crow Council governs the Eyrie—five elder crowkin who've accumulated the most valuable collections. They trade in secrets the way merchants trade in coin. Information has a price here, but so does discretion. The crowkin will sell what they know, but they won't sell who asked.

The giant ravens are not pets or mounts—they're partners. Crowkin and ravens share a bond that outsiders don't fully understand. The ravens carry messages, transport passengers, and scout the peaks. In return, the crowkin tend their nests and share their food. The eldest ravens are said to remember things from centuries ago.

Crowkin guides know paths through the mountains that appear on no maps—routes through the high passes, around the dangerous territories, into places sealed off from the world. They'll guide travelers for a price, which isn't always coin. Sometimes they want a secret. Sometimes they want an item. Sometimes they want a favor to be named later.

The Hoard is the Eyrie's market, but also its museum—a chaotic collection of objects the crowkin have acquired over centuries. Most is junk. Some is treasure. The crowkin don't always know which is which, but they're shrewd negotiators regardless.

Crowkin from Nocturne return to the Eyrie regularly, bringing news from the city and taking news back. The Crow Council knows more about what happens in the prince-magisters' courts than most moon elves do.

The Ascent.description: The only approach for those without wings—a series of climbing routes, rope bridges, and narrow ledges that wind up the cliff face. Crowkin guides wait at the bottom to escort paying customers, carried by giant ravens or led along the safest paths. Without a guide, the climb is possible but dangerous. At intervals, small platforms offer rest. The wind never stops.

The Perch.description: The main landing for the Eyrie—a broad ledge where giant ravens come and go, where visitors arrive wind-battered and grateful. Crowkin attendants sort arrivals: merchants to the Hoard, petitioners to the Council Roost, travelers seeking guides to the Wayfinder's Ledge. The view is vertiginous.

The Hoard.description: A cavern stuffed with centuries of crowkin acquisitions. Shelves, bins, hanging baskets, piles—organized by a system only the crowkin understand. Weapons, jewelry, books, tools, clothing, art, bones, keys to unknown locks, maps to unknown places. The crowkin who run the Hoard are shrewd traders who enjoy negotiation as sport. They'll buy almost anything. They'll sell almost anything. The price is never simple.

The Council Roost.description: The highest point of the Eyrie, where the five elders of the Crow Council receive petitioners. The chamber is open to the sky, giant ravens perched on the edges like living gargoyles. The elders are old, sharp-eyed, and speak in a formal dialect that other crowkin find pretentious. They trade in information—secrets bought and sold, questions answered for a price. They know things about the Moon Kingdoms, about the great houses, about events in distant lands. Getting an audience requires either significant payment or significant interest.

The Wayfinder's Ledge.description: Where crowkin guides gather between jobs, swapping stories and negotiating contracts. Travelers seeking passage through the mountains come here to hire. The guides know every route—the safe paths, the fast paths, the secret paths. They know which territories to avoid and which ferals are hunting where. Their prices depend on the destination, the danger, and how interesting they find the client. Some routes they won't guide at all. Some they'll guide only if the story is good enough.

Forge Mountain.name: Forge Mountain
Forge Mountain.basicInfo: A gnomish workshop-town built into a mountain, its surface presence modest—a few stone buildings around a market square—while the true settlement extends deep underground.
Forge Mountain.hiddenInfo:
The gnomes produce the finest craftwork in the Moon Kingdoms: black stone carvings, silver inlay, moonlight glass, mechanisms. They came generations ago, drawn by the rare materials and the freedom to pursue projects that got them exiled from other lands. They supply the shops of Nocturne and take commissions from those willing to pay their prices. The gnomes are obsessive craftspeople who left their homelands because their curiosity led them into forbidden territory—not forbidden by law, but by gnomish tradition. They wanted to work with materials others considered dangerous, pursue techniques others considered unwise, create things others considered better left unmade. The Moon Kingdoms offered them freedom and resources. In return, they provide exceptional work.

The surface market sells standard goods—fine, but not extraordinary. The real work happens below, in workshops carved into the mountain. Each master maintains their own domain, specializing in particular crafts. Commissions for exceptional items require descending to find the right master and convincing them to take the job. They don't need the money. They need to be interested.

Moonlight glass is a Forge Mountain specialty—crystal that captures and holds moonlight, glowing softly even in darkness. The process is secret, jealously guarded, and apparently involves moon-phase timing and materials the gnomes won't discuss.

The Forges are where experimental work happens—projects too dangerous, too strange, or too valuable to risk near the surface. What the masters create down there is not discussed. Occasionally something goes wrong and a tunnel is sealed forever. The gnomes consider this an acceptable cost.

Forge Mountain supplies the gnomish shops throughout the Moong Kingdoms Caravans travel between them regularly, carrying finished goods to the cities and raw materials back.

The Guildmaster changes yearly, rotating among the masters. The position is considered a burden rather than an honor—time spent administering is time not spent crafting.

Topside.description: The surface settlement—a modest square surrounded by stone buildings where Forge Mountain's more ordinary goods are sold. Black stone carvings, silver jewelry, glass ornaments, clockwork toys. Quality work, but nothing that would alarm the uninitiated. A few inns serve travelers. The gnomes who work the surface stalls are friendly but evasive about what lies below. The main lift descends from a building at the square's edge, operated by gnomish attendants who decide who gets to go down.

The Upper Warrens.description: The first level below ground—living quarters, common spaces, and the workshops of journeymen still learning their craft. The tunnels are gnome-scaled but passable for larger folk, lit by moonlight glass set into the walls. The air is warm from the forges below. Gnomish families live here in comfortable apartments carved from the rock. The atmosphere is industrious but welcoming. Visitors with legitimate business can move freely here.

The Masters' Halls.description: The second level, where the true craftspeople work. Each master maintains a workshop-domain, some modest, some sprawling across multiple chambers. The specialties vary: one master works only in moonlight glass, another in silver mechanisms, another in black stone so finely carved it seems like frozen cloth. Taking a commission here requires finding the right master and making a case. They're not interested in money—they're interested in challenge, in novelty, in creating something they haven't created before.

The Forges.description: The lowest level, where the experimental work happens. Access is restricted to masters and their chosen assistants. The forges burn hotter. The materials are stranger. The projects are not explained to outsiders. Those who commission work from the Forges pay extraordinary prices and sign contracts with unusual clauses.

The Black Gate.name: The Black Gate
The Black Gate.basicInfo: A mountain fortress sacred to the vampire lords of the Moon Kingdoms. The Black Gate is the only place where new vampire lords can be created—a portal to another realm where aspirants must prove themselves worthy of the Lady of Blood's gift. Each great house maintains representatives here as priest-wardens, making the fortress neutral ground where houses can convene, negotiate, and witness transformations.
The Black Gate.hiddenInfo:
The Gate opens rarely; creating a new lord is a matter of political significance and risk. Between such occasions, the fortress serves as neutral territory for inter-house diplomacy and a place of pilgrimage for those who venerate the Lady of Blood. The priest-wardens are vampires of middling status appointed by their houses. The posting is prestigious in name but tedious in practice—decades may pass between transformations. The priests maintain the fortress, conduct the rituals, and represent their houses' interests in the small disputes and negotiations that constitute daily business here. Some grow to enjoy the quiet scholarly life. Others go slowly mad with boredom.

The transformation rite requires sponsorship from an existing lord, approval from the priest-wardens (rarely refused if the sponsor is powerful enough), and then the trial itself. The aspirant enters the Gate and must defeat and drink the blood of a Fiend of the Blood—servants of the Lady who dwell in her realm. Those who succeed return transformed. Those who fail do not return.

Opening the Gate carries risk. Things sometimes try to come through. The wards are strong, but the priests are cautious—they don't open the Gate frivolously, and they maintain defenses against what might emerge.

The fortress sees regular traffic despite the Gate's infrequent use. Envoys conduct diplomacy on neutral ground. Supplicants petition for sponsorship. Merchants supply the garrison. Scholars study vampire lore under careful supervision. Those with grievances between houses seek arbitration. The priest-wardens hear all of it, which makes them surprisingly well-informed despite their remote posting.

The Warden's Pass.description: The mountain approach to the Black Gate—a narrow road climbing through snow and rock to the fortress above. Waymarkers carved with the symbols of the great houses line the path. The fortress becomes visible halfway up: black stone walls against black peaks, almost invisible until you're upon them. Guards in the livery of all four houses watch the road. Visitors state their business and are escorted through. Most are admitted—the fortress welcomes envoys, petitioners, merchants, and scholars. Only those who cannot explain their presence are turned away.

The Hall of Witnesses.description: The great hall where the houses convene. Four galleries rise along the walls, one for each house, with the priest-wardens' seats at the cardinal points. The floor is black marble veined with red. Here sponsorships are announced, disputes are arbitrated, and—on rare occasions—the houses gather to witness a transformation. Between such events, the hall serves as a forum for negotiation. Envoys meet in the galleries. Petitioners wait on benches along the walls.

The Supplicants' Quarters.description: Lodgings for visitors—austere but comfortable chambers carved into the mountain stone. Envoys, merchants, scholars, and petitioners all stay here, sometimes for extended periods while business is conducted. A refectory serves simple meals. The quarters have an odd social atmosphere: rivals from feuding houses share meals, petitioners hoping for sponsorship size each other up, and everyone watches everyone else.

The Preparation Chambers.description: Where aspirants are made ready for the trial. Access is restricted to those undergoing transformation and their sponsors. The chambers are spare—cells for fasting, ritual baths, meditation rooms. An aspirant may spend days or weeks here, preparing body and mind for what awaits beyond the Gate. The priest-wardens conduct the preliminary rites: blessings, bindings, the formal acceptance of risk. The atmosphere is somber. Not everyone who enters the Gate returns.

The Gate Chamber.description: A cavern deep within the mountain, accessible only through the Preparation Chambers. The Black Gate stands at the chamber's heart—an arch of dark stone that opens onto nothing until the ritual activates it. When opened, the Gate reveals the realm of the Lady of Blood: a red darkness where the Fiends wait. The chamber is heavily warded. Priest-wardens stand ready with bindings and weapons when the Gate opens, watching for anything that might try to come through. The aspirant enters alone. What happens beyond is between them and the Lady.

The Citadel.name: The Citadel
The Citadel.basicInfo: A large and heavily guarded Syndicate fortress in the mountains that serves as the organization's regional headquarters and a secure depot for high-value goods moving through the Moon Kingdoms.
The Citadel.hiddenInfo:
The Syndicate conducts business here—storage, security, brokerage—but the scale of the operation raises questions. No one outside the Syndicate's senior inner circle knows what the Citadel is really for.

The Grey Guards know they're protecting something important.

The Citadel hires capable people for various jobs: caravan escorts, investigative work, problem-solving that requires discretion. They also make enemies disappear, broker deals between parties who can't be seen dealing, and maintain files on everyone of consequence in the region. Working for them is lucrative. Working against them is dangerous.

The vaults extend deep into the mountain—deeper than the visible architecture suggests.

The Gate.description: The main entrance to the Citadel, where caravans arrive and depart under Grey Guard escort. The gate is built for defense—thick walls, murder holes, a killing ground before the inner doors. But during business hours it bustles with commerce: wagons being loaded and unloaded, factors checking manifests, merchants negotiating storage fees. The Syndicate conducts legitimate trade here. Visitors with business are processed through a customs house. Those seeking employment are directed to the Hall.

The Hall.description: Where the Syndicate recruits capable people. The Hall is comfortable—good chairs, decent wine, a fire in cold weather. Syndicate factors interview applicants, match talent to tasks. The jobs vary: debt collection, investigation, retrieval, 'problem-solving'. Successful contractors get offered more work. Exceptional ones get offered something more permanent.

The Factor's Quarters.description: Offices and residences for the Syndicate staff who run the Citadel's daily operations. Factors, clerks, vault-masters, and senior Grey Guards live and work here. A common room serves as informal gathering space where business is discussed over meals..

The Vaults.description: Secure storage carved into the mountain—chamber after chamber of goods held for clients, goods in transit, and goods that belong to the Syndicate itself. Grey Guards patrol.

Harpy Mountain.name: Harpy Mountain
Harpy Mountain.basicInfo: A jagged peak where harpies nest in great numbers. Aaloof and territorial, they are notorious for abducting travelers who stray too close to their domain. The harpies are known to have some arrangement with the vampire lords—the details are unclear, but the prince-magisters leave them alone and they leave Nocturne alone.
Harpy Mountain.hiddenInfo:
The harpies watch the high passes and report to the vampire lords—movements, strangers, anything unusual. In exchange, they're left to their hunting and hoarding. The harpies have amassed considerable treasure over the centuries, taken from victims or traded for services rendered. The matriarch who leads them is old and cunning, willing to negotiate with those who approach correctly, though her price is steep and her temper short.

The treasure is real. Centuries of victims have yielded coins, jewelry, weapons, artifacts the harpies don't understand but recognize as valuable. The nests are scattered across the cliff faces, each harpy guarding her own collection. The matriarch's nest holds the finest pieces.

The harpies don't kill everyone they catch. Some are kept, for a time.

The Windbreak.description: The last shelter before Harpy Mountain proper—a narrow canyon where the wind dies down and climbers can rest. Old camps here suggest others have come this way. Some gear remains, abandoned. The cliffs above are dotted with dark openings: harpy nests. From here, the path climbs steeply into their territory.

The Nesting Cliffs.description: Sheer rock faces honeycombed with caves and ledges where the harpies make their homes. The wind howls. Wings beat overhead. The harpies circle, assessing, deciding whether visitors are prey or something more interesting. The matriarch's nest is highest, marked by bones and bright cloth. Reaching it means climbing through the colony—or being carried.

Blackscale Warren.name: Blackscale Warren
Blackscale Warren.basicInfo: A kobold warren built in the ruins of an ancient black dragon's lair. The kobolds call themselves the Blackscale Tribe and claim descent from the dragon's servants.
Blackscale Warren.hiddenInfo:
The Blackscale Tribe paints their armor black and insists it's dragonscale, though it's clearly scavenged junk. The warren is hostile to outsiders—traps fill the tunnels, and the kobolds defend their territory with fanatical devotion. Whatever the dragon left behind, the kobolds guard it jealously. They do not welcome visitors. The dragon has been dead for centuries. There is no treasure hoard—whatever gold existed was spent or stolen long ago. But the kobolds have two secrets worth killing for: dragon bones in the deepest chamber, and a single dragon egg, black-shelled and cold, that they've tended for generations. They believe it will hatch when a worthy master comes.

Emissaries from the Scaled Covenant have found the warren. They speak of the Dragon Queen, of a new age of dragonkind, of purpose for faithful servants. The tribe is divided. Some elders want to pledge to the Dragon Queen and present her with the egg. Others insist she's no true dragon and the egg must wait for a real one. The debate has turned violent more than once.

The traps are genuine dangers—the kobolds have had centuries to refine them. The tunnels are sized for kobolds, cramped and disorienting for larger folk.

The Dragon's Mouth.description: The entrance to the warren—a cave mouth shaped like a dragon's maw, carved or natural, it's hard to tell. The kobolds have added teeth of sharpened stone. Warnings in Draconic mark the threshold. Beyond, the tunnels descend into darkness. Traps begin immediately. The kobolds are watching from murder-holes, deciding whether to attack, parley, or let the tunnels do their work.

The Bone Shrine.description: The heart of the warren, where the dragon's skeleton is displayed like a monument. The bones are enormous, black, and ancient. The kobolds worship here, burning incense and chanting in broken Draconic. The egg is not kept here—its location is known only to the elders. But the shrine is where the tribe gathers, where decisions are made, and where intruders who survive the tunnels are brought to face judgment.

Blackquarry.name: Blackquarry
Blackquarry.basicInfo: An abandoned quarry in the mountains where the black stone used throughout the Moon Kingdoms was once cut. The quarry was evacuated generations ago when gargoyles appeared—constructs of living black stone, hostile and cruel. They remain there still, and the vampire lords have never reclaimed the site.
Blackquarry.hiddenInfo:
The gargoyles are intelligent, malicious, and patient. They don't need to eat, but they enjoy suffering. What they do with captives is unclear—the screaming can sometimes be heard from the mountain roads, but no one has investigated and lived to report. Some say they're building something. Some say they're worshipping something in the deep cuts of the quarry. Some say they simply enjoy their work.

The vampire lords could destroy them but haven't. Perhaps the cost is too high. Perhaps the gargoyles serve some purpose—watching an approach, guarding something buried in the quarry, or simply reminding everyone that some places belong to monsters. The gargoyles don't attack Nocturne or the major settlements. They take only those foolish enough to travel the nearby roads alone.

The Quarry Road.description: The old supply road that once carried stone down from Blackquarry. Overgrown now, the paving cracked and buried. Warning stones mark where the gargoyles' territory begins—travelers are advised to go no further. The quarry is visible above: terraced cuts in the mountainside, dark shapes perched motionless on the ledges.

The Cutting Floor.description: The main quarry pit, stepped terraces descending into the mountain. Abandoned equipment rusts where it was left. The black stone gleams where it's been cut. The gargoyles nest in the cliff faces, in the old worker shelters, on every ledge and outcrop. They don't attack immediately—they watch, they circle, they let fear build. The deepest part of the quarry is dark even at midday. Something is down there. The gargoyles don't let anyone get close enough to see what.

Charnel Mountain.name: Charnel Mountain
Charnel Mountain.basicInfo: Abandoned silver mines in the eastern mountains, now infested with feral ghouls. The ghouls raid outlying farms and ambush travelers on the mountain roads, dragging victims back to the warrens.
Charnel Mountain.hiddenInfo:
The ghoul population has grown larger than the vampire lords realize. The warrens extend through most of the old mine complex now, hundreds of ghouls in the darkness. They're feral but not mindless—they have a cunning pack intelligence, and something like leaders have emerged. The largest and oldest ghouls command the others, organizing raids, hoarding the best meat.

The silver in the mines was never fully exhausted. Veins remain in the deeper tunnels, untouched because the ghouls don't care about metal. Someone with enough force to clear the warrens could claim a fortune—and the gratitude of every settlement that's lost people to the raids.

Recently, the ghouls have been bolder. Raids are more frequent, striking further from the mountain. Something has changed. Perhaps they've simply grown too numerous for the local prey. Perhaps something deeper is driving them out.

The Old Mine Road.description: The track that once carried silver ore down from the mountain. The processing buildings at the trailhead are ruins now, picked clean and abandoned. Bones litter the ground—animal and otherwise. The mine entrances are visible above, dark mouths in the mountainside. The ghouls don't hunt by day if they can help it, but they're watching from the shadows, and dusk comes early in these valleys.

The Upper Warrens.description: The mine tunnels closest to the surface, where ghouls nest in old shafts and equipment rooms. The smell is indescribable. The walls are scratched with claw marks. Gnawed bones pile in corners. The ghouls here are the youngest and weakest, pushed to the edges by their betters. Deeper in, the tunnels branch and descend. The older ghouls lair below, where the darkness is absolute.

Roosthollow.name: Roosthollow
Roosthollow.basicInfo: A vast cavern network in the high peaks where giant bats roost and breed. Roosthollow supplies bat-mounts to the prince-magisters' scouts and messengers, and occasionally to wealthy private buyers.
Roosthollow.hiddenInfo:
The bats are semi-domesticated—they tolerate humanoid presence in their caves—but only a bonded bat will accept a rider. The bond is telepathic; bat and rider share thoughts and sensations. The trainers at Roosthollow can facilitate bonding for those deemed worthy, but the bats choose their own partners. Not everyone who seeks a mount leaves with one. The trainers are moon elf families who've worked with the bats for centuries. They know the animals' moods, lineages, and preferences. They can guide a prospective rider toward a compatible bat, but they can't force a bond—the bat's choice is final.

The telepathic bond is genuine and permanent. A bonded bat knows its rider's mind: emotions, intentions, sometimes surface thoughts. Riders report the connection works both ways—they sense the bat's hunger, its pleasure in flight, its wariness of threats. The bond persists over distance. A rider always knows, roughly, where their bat is and how it feels.

The bats are intelligent enough to have preferences and grudges. They remember how they're treated. A mistreated bat won't bond and may communicate its displeasure to others. The trainers take this seriously—they've refused to work with people the bats dislike.

Deeper in the caverns, wild bats roost—unbonded, untrained, source of new stock. Capturing wild bats for the breeding program is dangerous work the trainers occasionally hire out.

The Cave Mouth.description: The main entrance to Roosthollow—a broad cave mouth on a mountain shelf, large enough for giant bats to fly through in numbers. The shelf serves as a landing platform; bonded bats come and go at all hours. The sounds of bats echo from within—rustling, clicking, the occasional shriek.

The Batmaster's Lodge.description: A stone longhouse built against the cliff face beside the cavern entrance, where the trainers live and conduct business. The common hall has a fire burning in all seasons, long tables for meals, and walls hung with riding gear and bat-related curios like ancient harnesses of famous trainers. The trainer families have quarters in the upper levels. Those waiting for bonding often lodge here for days or weeks, eating with the trainers and learning bat-lore.
