The Training Ledges.description: A series of natural platforms inside the cavern where riders learn their craft. The space is vast, lit by moonlight glass lanterns that don't disturb the bats. Trainers work with bonded pairs here—teaching commands, building trust, practicing takeoffs and landings. Prospective riders spend time among the bats, letting the animals grow accustomed to them, waiting to see if any show interest.

The Roosts.description: The heart of the cavern network, where hundreds of giant bats hang from the ceiling and cluster in alcoves. The darkness is near-total; visitors are given dim lanterns that won't spook the animals. The bats watch with bright eyes, clicking softly, communicating in ways only they understand. The deeper caves, where wild bats live, are accessible from here but not recommended.

Kingstor.name: Kingstor
Kingstor.basicInfo: A semi-permanent, seasonal, mixed-species encampment around a great stone tor that rises from the grass sea. At its base, a permanent trading ring serves travelers year-round. But Kingstor exists for the Moot. When the drums sound, beastfolk converge from every corner of the grass sea to trade, compete, settle disputes — and burn images of tyrants and oppressors, current and former. Moot Law holds here: no killing, no feuds past the boundary stones.
Kingstor.hiddenInfo:
Kingstor is sacred neutral ground — the one place where all beastfolk species mix freely under Moot Law. Any breach is answered by every species present, without exception.

The Moot has no permanent leader. Each species sends speakers for formal disputes.

The Syndicate of the Commonweal wants trade routes through the grass sea. Their agents at Kingstor are polite, patient, and persistent.

Population is mixed beastfolk — lionkin, wolfkin, boarfolk, catfolk, and serpentfolk — with proportions shifting by season and whether a formal Moot has been called. A handful of non-beastfolk traders and agents maintain permanent presence. Non-beastfolk are tolerated as guests and have no voice at Moot.

The Grass Road.description: The tor is visible for a day's walk in every direction — a dark spike against the sky. Boundary stones mark the edge of Moot ground. Past the stones, the camps begin.

The Camps.description: A sprawling, shifting settlement surrounding the tor's base. Hide tents, reed shelters, cook-fires, and pavilions in every style the grass sea produces. Between them — fighting rings, gambling circles, storytellers holding crowds, and arguments about everything.

The Trading Ring.description: A permanent circle of stone-and-timber stalls at the tor's base, roofed with stretched hide. The only structures at Kingstor that stand year-round. Beastfolk traders, a few licensed Syndicate factors, and opportunists of every species haggling over hides, herbs, river-ivory, grassland honey, and stranger goods from deep in the wild.

The Burning Ground.description: A vast, blackened clearing west of the tor where the earth has been scorched and re-scorched across countless Moots. In the grass sea, a 'king' is an effigy built to burn — the word stripped of authority and stuffed with straw. Anyone can build one. The only rule is it has to burn well. During the Burning, the kings go up in walls of flame while the grass sea throws the wildest party in Larion around them.

The Climb.description: A switchback path carved into the tor's face, worn smooth by generations of clawed feet. Steep, exposed, not designed for anyone who isn't sure-footed. Beastfolk take it at a run.

The Summit.description: The top of the tor. Wind and sky and the grass sea in every direction. Standing stones ring the summit in a circle older than any species' memory. This is where the Moot convenes: speakers from each species take their place at a stone, and the debates that shape the grass sea happen under open sky. Between Moots, beastfolk come up alone to look out at the land and remember it's theirs.

The Watering Hole.description: The biggest tent at Kingstor, open-sided when the weather allows. Run by a boarfolk named Gorsha and a halfling named Pell, who between them have fed and watered more beastfolk than anyone alive. The food is communal, the drink is strong, and the arguments are louder than anywhere else at the Moot — which is saying something.

Grass Sea League Chapterhouse.name: Grass Sea League Chapterhouse
Grass Sea League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: The League of Adventurers' outpost in the Wild Grass Sea, built inside the skeleton of a creature so vast and ancient nobody agrees what it once was. The story goes that the beastfolk wouldn't let the League build a proper fortification, and so they jokingly suggested that the outsiders move into the old bones instead. The skeleton has since been carved, dug out, and rearranged to provide considerable security and even a modicum of comfort. Everyone calls it the Carcass.
Grass Sea League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The beastfolk tolerate the League's presence because it's useful — monster hunters, healers, and a steady supply of coin — and because the Carcass is genuinely funny to them. Every new League posting here is a punchline.

The skeleton's origin is unknown. The bones are minerite — harder than stone and faintly warm to the touch. Serpentfolk say it was already bones when their people first walked the plain. Theories range from land-whale to sky-beast to something that crawled out of the earth and died in the sun.

The chapterhouse serves as the League's base of operations for the entire grass sea region. Postings here are considered either a punishment or an adventure depending on temperament. The chapter-master needs people who can work with beastfolk — not just fight alongside them, but earn enough trust to operate on their land.

The Jaw.description: The entrance. You walk in through the mouth — lower jawbone forming the threshold, upper jaw arching overhead, teeth the size of standing stones on either side. The League hung a banner between the fangs. The beastfolk keep stealing it as a joke. The League keeps replacing it. This has been going on for years.

The Skull.description: The main hall, housed inside the cranium. The chapter-master's quarters occupy the upper cavity, reached by a ladder through the brainpan. Below, the mission board covers one wall — bounties, monster sightings, escort requests, and the occasional beastfolk contract. Gear, supplies, and League provisions are stocked along the walls.

The Ribcage.description: The social heart of the Carcass. Ribs arch overhead like cathedral vaults, with hide tarps stretched between them for shade and rain cover. Long tables, a firepit, and a bar built from a shoulder blade. Adventurers, League regulars, and the occasional beastfolk who finds the whole thing too amusing to stay away. Loudest after sundown.

The Spine.description: Quarters run along the backbone — bunks built between the vertebrae, each one a natural alcove. Not luxurious, but solid and surprisingly warm. The bones hold heat during the day and release it at night. Long-term residents have carved shelves into the gaps and hung personal effects from the processes. The tail end is storage.

The Yard.description: Open ground between the forelimbs, where the skeleton's front legs splay wide enough to frame a training area. Sparring rings, target posts, and a trampled oval where mounted combat is practiced. Beastfolk sometimes wander over to watch League recruits train and offer unsolicited, devastatingly accurate criticism.

Mudbank.name: Mudbank
Mudbank.basicInfo: A boarfolk river settlement of mud-brick buildings. The biggest and most permanent community in the grass sea, built where a wide channel cuts through the grassland.
Mudbank.hiddenInfo:
Mudbank is the grass sea's most established settlement and its most controversial. Boarfolk build, farm, and trade — things other species associate with the outside world. Several Syndicate trade agreements run through Mudbank, bringing outside coin and outside influence. Some boarfolk see prosperity. Others see the first step toward kings.

Population is predominantly boarfolk with a growing number of outside traders, Syndicate factors, and opportunists drawn by the river trade. Other beastfolk visit regularly — Mudbank feeds anyone who shows up.

The River Gate.description: The channel narrows here between mud-brick walls, forming a natural harbor where flat-bottomed boats crowd the bank. A wooden gate that's never been closed spans the water. The path into town runs along the top of a levee built by generations of boarfolk shoring up the bank with mud, stone, and stubbornness.

The Wallows.description: A broad, shallow stretch of river where the bank has been shaped into gentle slopes. This is where Mudbank relaxes. Boarfolk half-submerged in cool mud, talking business, settling arguments, and enjoying themselves enormously. Deals get made here that never happen at a table. Non-boarfolk are welcome but should expect to get muddy.

The Long Table.description: A communal eating hall — open-sided, mud-brick columns holding up a thatched roof over a single table that seats a hundred. Boarfolk cooks work a row of fire-pits at one end. You sit, you eat, you talk. Nobody pays. The food comes from the farms and the river and there's always enough. This is where Mudbank meets its guests, hears news, and decides what it thinks about things.

Goldentor.name: Goldentor
Goldentor.basicInfo: A lionkin pride-hold on the tallest tor in the grass sea, flying bright banners visible for miles. Goldentor isn't a capital — lionkin don't have one. It's a stage. The pride that holds it hosts all comers, and other prides visit to posture, negotiate, settle honor disputes, and be seen.
Goldentor.hiddenInfo:
Goldentor is held by a single pride, but its importance comes from being the place other prides come to compete for status. The permanent population is small — one pride and their household. Everyone else is visiting, which means the social dynamics shift constantly as old rivals and allies cycle through.

Lionkin honor culture runs hot here. Disputes are settled by public challenge — combat, contest, or oratory. Hospitality is a display of wealth and generosity. Even casual conversation carries stakes. Visitors are treated as an audience, not guests. Outsiders who understand this and play along earn respect quickly. Those who don't are ignored, which is worse than hostility.

Lionkin carry a collective awareness that their species once produced a tyrant — the king who tried to rule the grass sea and inspired Moot Law. Some prides wear that history as humility. Others think the old king simply lacked the strength to finish what he started.

The Tor Road.description: A processional path carved into the tor face, wide enough for lionkin to walk three abreast. Visitors are met at the base and escorted up. The escort is an honor — and an evaluation. By the time you reach the top, the pride knows what they think of you.

The Circle.description: A broad, flat arena near the tor's summit, open to the sky. This is where disputes are settled, challenges answered, and feats performed. Stone seating rings the edge. When a challenge is called, word spreads fast and the Circle fills. Lionkin treat a good fight the way other cultures treat theater.

The High Seat.description: The pride-lord's hall at the summit — open-walled, canopied in golden hide, with views across the grass sea in every direction. Guests are received here, feasts are held here, and the pride-lord sits in a stone chair that's older than the pride itself. The hospitality is lavish. The scrutiny is constant.

Longrun.name: Longrun
Longrun.basicInfo: Where the great herds pass through during migration, the wolfkin converge. Longrun is a hunting ground, a proving ground, and the closest thing wolfkin have to a homeland. A keeper-pack holds the site year-round, watching the herds and maintaining the runs. When migration season comes, packs from across the grass sea arrive, and the great hunt begins.
Longrun.hiddenInfo:
Wolfkin are pack-nomads who follow herds and patrol territory. Longrun is the exception — the one place they return to. The keeper-pack is a permanent garrison of sorts, volunteers from multiple packs who've chosen to stay and steward the land. Other packs respect them but consider the choice unusual.

The great hunt is the most important event in wolfkin culture. It's where young wolves earn their names, where pack alliances are forged or broken, and where pack-leaders prove their worth. Hunts are coordinated across multiple packs. Waste is forbidden — everything taken is used.

Some wolfkin have started staying at Longrun past their pack's rotation. They like the stability, the connections, the sense of place. Their packs consider this somewhere between eccentric and disloyal.

Population is predominantly wolfkin. Other beastfolk visit but don't linger — wolfkin hospitality is real but spare, and the pack rhythms of daily life can feel exclusionary to outsiders.

The Keeper's Camp.description: A permanent encampment on a low ridge. Hide shelters built for function, not comfort. A central fire-ring where the keeper-pack shares meals and receives  scouts, traders and visitors.

The Runs.description: A wide natural channel between rocky ridges where the great herds funnel through during the season. Outside of migration, it's open grassland marked by old drive-lines of stacked stone. During the hunt, it transforms: wolfkin packs positioned along the ridges, signals passing between them, the ground shaking as the herds move through.

The Muster Ground.description: A flat clearing where the packs gather when the herds are close. During season, it's a sprawl of pack-camps, each with its own banner and fire. The pack-leaders meet at a central ring to plan the hunt — who drives, who flanks, who takes the first kill. Between seasons, it's empty and quiet, just trampled grass and old fire-circles waiting.

Boneyard.name: Boneyard
Boneyard.basicInfo: A cluster of tors the gnolls claimed because nobody else wanted them. Hide bridges strung between the rocks, ramshackle platforms, and a chaotic warren of dens and market stalls. Every pack alpha has claimed a tor and given it a grandiose name. The biggest tor changes hands regularly through violence.
Boneyard.hiddenInfo:
Boneyard exists because gnolls don't build — they occupy. The tors were too small and exposed for anyone else to bother with, which made them perfect for gnoll packs who value defensible high ground and don't care about comfort.

The settlement runs on bluster, extortion, and a surprisingly functional black market. Gnoll alphas carry ridiculous titles — the Fang Paramount, the Lord of Red Dust, the Thrice-Blooded King — earned through violence and maintained through success. Authority lasts only as long as the raids succeed. Failed leaders face challengers before sundown.

The other beastfolk tolerate Boneyard because gnolls serve a purpose: they trade in things nobody else will touch, they take mercenary contracts no one else wants, and they keep the worst banditry concentrated in one place where it can be watched. Gnolls attend the Moot at Kingstor but are not trusted. Their speakers promise everything and honor whatever's convenient.

Visitors come for stolen goods, questionable information, mercenary contracts, and the services of gnoll packs who'll do anything for coin. The only rule is pay up front.

The Rope Walk.description: Hide bridges and rope-lines connecting the tors, swaying over the gaps between. The only way in unless you can climb sheer rock. Gnolls charge a toll at the first bridge. The amount changes depending on who's asking and how much the toll-keeper thinks they can get away with.

The Gut.description: The ravine floor between the tors, roofed by the bridges and platforms above. The black market lives here — stalls, dens, and deal-making in the permanent shadow of the overhanging rock. Stolen goods, mercenary postings, information of dubious reliability, and substances the Syndicate won't touch. Gnoll merchants are aggressive, dishonest, and surprisingly good at reading what you actually want.

The Alpha's Tor.description: The biggest tor, crowned with whatever the current alpha has dragged up there as a throne. Changes hands often enough that permanent decoration is pointless. The alpha holds court in the open air, surrounded by loyalists, issuing proclamations nobody outside the tor takes seriously. Audiences are granted to anyone who brings a sufficiently interesting gift or problem. The current alpha's title is always magnificent. The current alpha's reign is always fragile.

Kingsgrave Kurgan.name: Kingsgrave Kurgan
Kingsgrave Kurgan.basicInfo: A massive earthen mound rising from the flat grass sea, ringed by standing stones and watched by a permanent guard drawn from every beastfolk species. The sorcerer-emperor who once enslaved the grass sea is sealed inside. The wards are ancient. The guardians are vigilant. Things still get out. People still try to get in.
Kingsgrave Kurgan.hiddenInfo:
The sorcerer-emperor was a lionkin of immense magical power who bound beastfolk to his will through domination magic, turning species against each other and building an empire on enslaved minds. When the beastfolk finally broke free and overthrew him, they couldn't be certain death was enough. The kurgan is a prison, not just a tomb — sealed by the combined effort of every species, each contributing wards in their own tradition.

The guard is drawn from all four species on rotation and is the one duty the grass sea agrees on without debate. It is the highest honor and the worst posting. Years of silence punctuated by incidents that remind everyone why the guard exists.

The seals are degrading. The knowledge of how they were constructed has eroded across generations. Echoes leak — undead servitors, fragments of compulsion magic, bound spirits that drift outward and affect wildlife or unlucky travelers near the mound. The guardians contain what they can and report what they can't.

Treasure seekers are a constant problem. The emperor was buried with the wealth of a conquered grass sea. Gnolls at Boneyard trade in rumored maps to side entrances. Outside interests — rogue adventurers, independent fortune hunters — probe the perimeter. Some get in. Fewer come back out, and those who do are not always themselves.

Whispers persist of beastfolk who hear the kurgan's call — not a cult, not yet, but a pull. Power, unity, order imposed by strength. The guardians watch for this too.

The Standing Stones.description: A ring of carved stones circling the mound's base, each one marked with ward-signs from a different beastfolk tradition. The guardian camp sits among them — practical, permanent, grim. Four species sharing watch-fires and patrol routes with the quiet discipline of people guarding something that must not wake.

The Processional.description: A stone-lined passage descending into the mound. The walls are carved with warnings in scripts so old even the serpentfolk struggle with them. The guardians maintain the outer passage and patrol to the first sealed door. Beyond that, you're on your own and probably making a mistake.

The Sealed Halls.description: The tomb complex proper. Corridors of packed earth reinforced with bone and stone, branching into chambers that haven't been fully mapped by anyone still alive and coherent. Wards flicker on the walls — some still holding, others dark. The emperor's burial chamber is somewhere at the heart, behind doors no one has opened. The things that wander these halls are remnants — bound dead, spell fragments given form, echoes of a will that refuses to be forgotten.

The Breach.description: A collapsed section on the mound's eastern slope where something dug out from inside. The guardians sealed it, then it opened again. They sealed it again. It opened again. Now they're sealing it again.

The Black Tower.name: The Black Tower
The Black Tower.basicInfo: A colossal spire of black stone rising from the grass sea, so tall its summit has never been seen. It has many names. The Tower of Trials for those who test themselves against it. The Tower of Treasure for the artifacts that come out of it. The Tower of Fools for those who think the first two names are anything but bait. The Infinite Tower. Heavensgate. The Black Tower. No one knows who built it, what it's for, or what's at the top. Expeditions to answer these questions return as small groups of survivors, if at all. The only way up is through — storms and flying creatures make outside ascent impossible.
The Black Tower.hiddenInfo:
No one knows how many levels the Tower has. No one is known to have reached the summit. The interior is far larger than the exterior suggests — not through any obvious magical distortion.

Level One: A chaotic shantytown filling the tower's ground floor. Con artists, self-proclaimed guides, thieves, grifters, cultists who believe the summit leads to a divine realm, and evangelists of a dozen contradictory theories about the tower's origin. Gear sellers who've never been above the first floor. Veterans who have and charge accordingly. A few honest outfitters amid a sea of fraud. It is loud, lawless, and the first test of any climber's judgment.

Level Two: A labyrinth. Bandits, thugs, and worse have colonized the corridors and prey on ascending climbers. A protection racket operates here — pay up or fight through. Occasionally groups from the first floor mount clearance expeditions, which temporarily open safe passage before new predators move in. The layout shifts subtly over time, making old maps unreliable.

Level Three: A militaristic encampment maintained by a self-appointed order of gatekeepers who call themselves the Threshold. They organize ascending parties, reject climbers they consider unprepared, and maintain the most reliable maps of levels four and five. Their authority is self-granted and hotly disputed, but their survival record speaks for itself. Admission requires passing their muster — combat, knowledge, or demonstrated usefulness to a climbing party.

Level Four: An impossibly vast wilderness contained within the tower's walls — open sky, distant horizons, a landscape that should not fit inside any structure. Forests, ravines, predators, and ruins of unknown origin. Climbing parties can spend days crossing it. The Threshold's maps cover portions of this level but are incomplete. The stair to level five is somewhere in the interior and not always in the same place.

Level Five and above: Unverified. Rumors include:
- A frozen lake with something visible beneath the ice
- A library staffed by constructs that trade knowledge for knowledge
- A level that is a single enormous creature's living interior
- An inverted city hanging from the ceiling over a void
- A garden tended by a blind immortal who answers one question per visitor
- A level where gravity reverses at irregular intervals
- A hall of mirrors that shows other versions of whoever enters
- An armory of weapons that choose their wielders and reject the unworthy
- A completely empty level with no visible features where climbers simply vanish
- A storm that never ends, navigable only by sound
- The summit, variously described as a throne room, a portal, a weapon, a prison, or an empty platform open to the sky.

The Gate.description: A single entrance at the tower's base, tall and rectangular, with no door. A queue forms here most days — enforced by a rotating crew of thugs who sell 'climbing permits' that mean nothing and everyone pays anyway. Veterans walk past without paying. The thugs let them. The system works because nobody important enough to stop it cares enough to bother.

The Ground Floor.description: A shantytown crammed into the tower's cavernous base. Cook-fires, bedrolls, gear stalls, shouting con artists, wild-eyed preachers, and climbers in every state from fresh-faced to hollowed-out. Guides offer their services at outrageous rates. Thieves offer theirs at reasonable ones. A notice board is buried under climbing reports, party recruitment posts, missing-person notices, and warnings about the second floor. The air smells like ambition and bad decisions.

The Labyrinth.description: The second level. Corridors that branch and double back, colonized by bandits who prey on ascending climbers. Pay the toll or fight through — those are the options. The walls shift over time, enough to make old maps dangerous. Occasionally a clearance party from the ground floor sweeps through. The relief is temporary.

The Threshold.description: The third level. An armed camp run by a self-appointed order of gatekeepers who decide who's ready to climb higher and who gets sent back down. Discipline, organization, and the best maps of the upper levels that exist anywhere. They don't let everyone through. Admission requires passing their tests — combat, knowledge, or proving you're useful enough to be worth keeping alive.

The Wild.description: The fourth level. Step through the archway and the tower disappears. Open sky, distant treelines, a landscape that cannot possibly exist inside a building but does. Forests, ravines, predators, and ruins. Climbing parties spend days crossing it. The stair to level five is somewhere in the interior. It is not always in the same place.

Hag's Rock.name: Hag's Rock
Hag's Rock.basicInfo: A tor on the grass sea where a community of witches has made their home. The beastfolk call it the Hag's Tor, and even though the reputation of the witches isn't fully compatible with this name, it seems to have stuck. The witches provide healing, potions, and magical services to the beastfolk, who in turn leave them in peace — an arrangement both sides consider ideal. What else the witches get up to on their tor is a matter of lively speculation across the grass sea.
Hag's Rock.hiddenInfo:
Despite the name, one thing is immediately obvious upon arriving here: none of the witches remotely resembles a hag either in age, beauty or demeanor. In fact, they are all conspicuously young, beautiful and quite friendly to strangers, although they do enjoy pranking visitors by appearing as conventional hags from time to time. The witches settled the grass sea because the beastfolk leave them alone. The understanding is practical — the witches provide healing, potions, midwifery, and magical services to anyone who visits. The beastfolk provide food and a guarantee that nobody comes looking for the witches with torches. Both sides consider this arrangement ideal.

The community is mostly human and half-elf women, with a few exceptions in every category. They are refugees of a sort — women whose magical talents made them unwelcome in more 'civilized' places. The grass sea doesn't care what you are as long as you don't try to rule anyone.

The tor's caves are far grander than their entrances suggest — the witches have shaped the interiors with magic into airy, elegant spaces that wouldn't be out of place in a palace. Silk hangings, enchanted lighting, impossibly comfortable furnishings. The contrast between the modest exterior and the lavish interior is deliberate and amusing to the witches. Familiars — cats, ravens, foxes, and stranger things — roam freely. Brooms and implements drift through the air on errands.

The Tor Path.description: A winding path up the tor's gentle slope, lined with herb gardens and wildflowers that have no business growing on bare rock. Cats watch from the ledges. A broom drifts past carrying a bundle of herbs. The smell of cooking drifts down from above.

The Kitchen.description: The social heart of the tor — an open-air cooking and gathering space under a canvas awning, with a long table, mismatched chairs, and a fire that's always going. Familiars weave between the chair legs. The witches eat here, receive visitors here, and conduct most of their business over food and drink.

The Apothecary.description: A cave carved into the tor's upper face, shelved floor to ceiling with jars, bottles, dried herbs, and things in suspension that are best not examined closely. The witches' workshop and storefront. Potions, salves, remedies, and more exotic preparations are available for trade. The prices are fair. The advice that comes with them is free and unsolicited.

The Caves.description: The witches' private quarters, carved into the tor and shaped by magic into something that has no right to exist inside a rock. Vaulted ceilings, silk hangings, enchanted lights that follow you from room to room. Brooms and bottles float through corridors on errands. Every cave is different — one witch's chambers are a candlelit library, another's a moonlit garden that can't possibly be underground. Visitors are welcome by invitation. The invitation usually comes with wine.

New Anthara.name: New Anthara
New Anthara.basicInfo: A great canal city built against the Whitemount, a ridge of pale stone where the River Saranadi meets the jungle. New Anthara is the gateway to the jungles of Ntar Vanna and the richest port in the south. The jungle's wealth passes through here — timber, reagents, medicines, exotic creatures, and goods best not asked about. Two rulers share power: a Noble Duarch elected by the great houses and a Common Duarch elected by the people. The city is layered — new construction over old, old over ancient demonborn ruins that surface in cellars and canal walls. A dozen species crowd its markets and waterways, and coin spends the same in every hand.
New Anthara.hiddenInfo:
New Anthara is governed by the Duarchy. The Noble Duarch is elected by the heads of the great houses — old merchant dynasties, landed families, and serpentfolk temple estates that function as aristocratic holdings. The Common Duarch is elected by the general populace. Both must agree for the city to act officially. When they align, New Anthara is formidable. When they don't, the city drifts and the underworld fills the gap.

The Whitemount's terraces hold the wealthy — noble estates, temples, the Duarchal Palace. The canal districts below are the commercial heart. The lowest ground spreads into the Lows — a vast, crowded quarter where most of the population lives. Not desperate, but poor. The great houses and temples fund public kitchens and flood relief, partly from charity, partly because hungry crowds threaten commerce.

The old demonborn ruins beneath the city flood and drain unpredictably. Cellars open into ancient chambers. When the water drops, new sections surface. When it rises, the Lows flood first. Things come up — artifacts, bones, and worse.

The grave goods trade is the city's worst-kept secret. Artifacts looted from Ntar Vanna's ruins flow through the port in enormous volume. Officially condemned, practically tolerated, enormously profitable.

No species dominates. Serpentfolk lean aristocratic. Frogkin dominate the river trades. Lizardfolk come and go from the deep jungle. Humans cluster around overseas commerce. Demonborn are found at every level of society.

The River Docks.description: Where the Saranadi meets the city. Ocean-going ships from the Spice Isles berth alongside river barges from the interior and flat-bottomed jungle boats. The Whitemount rises above the wharves, pale stone over dark water. Customs houses, warehouses, chandlers, and the offices of every major trading house in the south line the waterfront. Loud, multilingual, busy from dawn to well after dark. Everything entering or leaving Ntar Vanna passes through here.

The Canal Markets.description: New Anthara's commercial heart, built along and over the canal network. Stalls and shopfronts line every waterway. Customers arrive by boat as often as by foot. Timber, spices, reagents, jungle medicines, exotic pelts, live creatures in cages, enchanted goods, and artifacts of uncertain provenance. Frogkin canal-polers ferry buyers between market sections. The noise carries across the water and doesn't stop.

The Whitemount Terraces.description: The upper city, carved into and built onto the pale stone ridge. Noble estates with terraced gardens, the Duarchal Palace, serpentfolk temple holdings, and institutions old enough to predate the current city. The stone is white and weathers slowly. The air is cooler at height. Views from the upper terraces reach across the jungle canopy to the horizon. The floods never reach here.

The Lows.description: The vast popular quarter, spread across the lowest ground between the canals and the jungle's edge. Dense housing along narrow waterways, street vendors on every corner, public kitchens funded by the great houses ladling out rice and stew. Most of New Anthara's population lives here. When the river rises, the Lows flood first.

The Witchmarket.description: Where the magical trade concentrates. Reagent shops, alchemical suppliers, enchanters for hire, and workshops. Wizards and hedge-witches from across Larion maintain permanent premises here.

The Serpentine.description: The serpentfolk temple quarter. Monumental stone architecture predating the current city — carved facades, sacred pools, ritual halls open to the sky. The serpentfolk priestly houses maintain these grounds as both places of worship and aristocratic holdings. Visitors are welcome but expected to observe customs that no one explains in advance.

The Frogwater.description: The frogkin canal district, where the waterways widen and the streets give way to water entirely. Boat-houses, floating platforms, canal-side stalls selling river catch and jungle fruit. Frogkin families have worked these channels for generations and know every waterway in the city and beyond. Best place to hire a river guide, book passage into the interior, or buy fresh food cheap.

The Burned Garden.description: Once the estate of a great noble house that tried to overthrow the Common Duarch and rule alone. The commoners stormed it, burned it, and the Duarchy claimed the ruins as public land. The jungle did the rest. Now it is the finest public garden in the city — lush growth over old foundations, terraced pools where fountains once played for nobles alone, cook-fires and lantern light every evening. Common ground in every sense. The people of New Anthara gather here to eat, drink, argue, and celebrate.

The Waterside.description: A long stretch of open-air platforms along the canal junctions where the Lows meet the docks. Roofed against the rain, open on every side. Cook-fires, communal tables, drinks poured from large vessels. Each platform has its own character — one rowdy, one quiet, one where the frogkin gather, one where you don't ask what people do for a living. You arrive by foot or by boat, sit where there's room, eat what's cooking. New Anthara's common social life happens here, in the warm rain, over dark water.

Old Anthara.description: The ruins beneath the city. Ancient demonborn construction — black stone, vast chambers, passages that stretch beyond torchlight. The river floods and drains them on its own schedule. Some sections are accessible for months before the water returns. Others open once and never again. Salvagers work the edges, pulling artifacts and building materials from the accessible sections. Deeper in, the ruins are uncharted and dangerous. Things live down here that don't come up. Entry points are found throughout the Lows — through cellars, canal breaches, and stairways that were old before the current city was built.

Saravadi Village.name: Saravadi Village
Saravadi Village.basicInfo: A farming village on the lower slopes where the Whitemount trails into lowland, surrounded by rice terraces and jungle orchards. Saravadi feeds a significant portion of New Anthara on three rice harvests a year and ships fruit, spices, and hardwood downriver to the city markets. Local families have worked these terraces and groves for generations. Life here is governed by rain, harvest, and the constant work of keeping the jungle from taking it all back.
Saravadi Village.hiddenInfo:
Saravadi is proud, self-sufficient, and quietly stubborn. The village knows the city needs its rice and resents being taken for granted.

The rice terraces are the subsistence backbone — they feed the village and the city both. The orchards and spice groves are the cash economy — jackfruit, breadfruit, pepper, cinnamon, and hardwood timber shipped downriver and on to the Spice Isles. Frogkin manage the irrigation channels that keep the terraces productive.

The jungle is a constant adversary. Leave a terrace or orchard untended for a season and the growth swallows it. Wildlife raids crops and livestock. Maintaining the boundary between cultivated land and jungle is daily, physical work.

Governance is by weekly consensus meetings under a great tree in the village center. No formal leader — decisions are argued out until agreement is reached or exhaustion wins.

Young people leave for New Anthara. Old people worry about it. The village is one generation of departures away from losing the labor it needs to hold the jungle back.

The River Landing.description: A simple wooden dock on the tributary where flat-bottomed boats load rice, fruit, spices, and timber for the run downriver to New Anthara. A few storage sheds, a weighing station, and a tea stall run by an old frogkin woman who knows the river schedule better than anyone. This is where the village meets the wider world — traders, city buyers, occasional visitors, and news from downstream.

The Terraces.description: Rice paddies climbing the lower Whitemount slopes in broad, flooded steps. Lizardfolk and demonborn families work them by hand. The irrigation channels are frogkin-built and frogkin-maintained — a network of sluices and diversions that moves water from the upper slopes down through every level. When the light is right the whole hillside is a mirror, broken into green and silver steps. At the base of the terraces, the orchards begin.

The Groves.description: Jungle orchards and spice groves along a tributary of the Saranadi. Jackfruit, breadfruit, pepper vines climbing hardwood trunks, cinnamon, and managed stands of timber. The canopy is thick enough to keep the rain off but open enough to work in. Harvest crews move through the groves year-round — something is always fruiting. The jungle presses close at the edges. Crews carry machetes for the undergrowth and keep watch for predators.

The Drying Barn.description: The village's communal heart. A large open-sided structure on the flat ground between the terraces and the river. Rice dries on racks in the upper level. Below, the packed-earth floor serves as gathering hall, dining room, and meeting place. Lanterns hang from every beam. In the evenings the village congregates here — food shared from common pots, children underfoot, the week's gossip and grievances aired over rice wine. The great tree where consensus meetings are held stands just outside.

Kalanth Landing.name: Kalanth Landing
Kalanth Landing.basicInfo: A wealthy merchant-noble estate where the Saranadi bends, commanding a natural river landing. House Kalanth made its fortune in the spice and timber trade, and the estate is part working plantation, part trading post, part lavish country retreat. Spice groves, managed timber stands, and terraced gardens surround a manor house that has hosted both of New Anthara's duarchs. The Kalanths know everyone worth knowing in the south, and their hospitality is as calculated as it is generous.
Kalanth Landing.hiddenInfo:
House Kalanth is politically active in the Duarchy, backing whichever side serves their commercial interests. They host the Noble Duarch for the dry-season hunt and the Common Duarch for the harvest festival. Their loyalty is to profit, and they are transparent enough about this that both sides tolerate it.

The estate's wealth comes from cultivated spice and timber — pepper, cinnamon, cloves, and prized hardwoods grown in managed jungle holdings and shipped downriver. They compete with upriver communities who wild-harvest the same goods, and the tension is ongoing.

Labor is tenant farmers and seasonal jungle hands. Conditions are decent — the Kalanths can't afford turnover this far from the city. The timber camps deeper in the jungle are rougher and complaints surface regularly.

The family sees everything that moves on the river from their landing and trades in information as readily as in pepper. They maintain a standing offer of work for capable adventurers — predators, bandits, rival sabotage, and the jungle itself all threaten the plantation's operations.

Persistent rumors connect House Kalanth to the grave goods trade. Artifacts have a way of moving through legitimate timber shipments. The family denies it.

The estate is beautiful and slightly excessive. Visitors are welcomed lavishly because every guest is a potential partner, ally, or useful fool.

The River Bend.description: The estate's private landing where the Saranadi curves. A stone dock long enough for several trading boats, with warehouses and a customs shed flying the Kalanth banner. Timber rafts and spice barges load here for the run to New Anthara. The family's own boats are moored at the far end — fast, well-maintained, and occasionally seen at odd hours. The bend gives a commanding view upriver and down.

The Kalanth Manor.description: A sprawling house of pale stone and dark timber on the high ground above the landing. Terraced gardens descend toward the river. An open-air great hall faces the water, built for entertaining — long tables, hanging lanterns, and a covered veranda where the family conducts business over drinks. Guest quarters occupy a separate wing and are conspicuously comfortable. The family quarters above are private and well-guarded. Servants move quietly and know when not to listen.

The Garden Terrace.description: The manor's showcase. Terraced gardens stepping down the slope in cultivated abundance — ornamental plantings alongside productive ones, fruiting trees shading walkways, flowering vines on every trellis. A long stone table under a canopy of woven branches serves as the family's outdoor dining and meeting place. This is where deals are made, alliances proposed, and guests assessed over food and wine. The view downriver toward New Anthara is spectacular.

The Spice Groves.description: The plantation's working heart. Managed rows of pepper, cinnamon, and clove interspersed with hardwood timber on long rotations. Neat, productive, and extensive — the groves stretch back from the river into the jungle for miles. Tenant farmers and seasonal hands work the rows. Overseers on horseback move between sections. Deeper in, where the managed groves give way to active timber cutting, the work gets harder and the jungle gets closer. Crews go armed.

Sangamadi.name: Sangamadi
Sangamadi.basicInfo: A frogkin river village at the confluence of two great tributaries joining the Saranadi. Every boat going upriver or down stops at Sangamadi — to hire pilots, resupply, trade, or eat the best fresh-cooked fish on the river. The village is built half on the banks and half in the water: stilted houses, floating platforms, and boats moored so long they've become buildings. The frogkin families who control the confluence have been here longer than New Anthara has existed, and they negotiate from that position.
Sangamadi.hiddenInfo:
Sangamadi controls access to the upper Saranadi. Frogkin pilots who know the channels are essential for navigating the narrowing river beyond the confluence, and the village holds a near-monopoly on their services. This gives Sangamadi outsized political leverage with New Anthara — the city needs the river open, the frogkin know it, and negotiations over tariffs and pilot fees are perpetual and occasionally bitter.

The permanent population is frogkin families who run the piloting trade, the fish market, and the docks. The transient population — traders, jungle hands, adventurers, merchants — swells and shrinks with the season. In high trading season the village doubles in size.

Rival pilot families compete fiercely for upriver contracts. The best pilots are known by name in New Anthara. Smugglers use the confluence to transfer goods between boats and bypass city customs. Information flows through Sangamadi before it reaches the city — river pilots hear everything and sell what they know.

The frogkin are hospitable and proud. Visitors are welcome, their coin is welcomed more, and anyone who disrespects the river or the families who work it finds Sangamadi's warmth cooling fast.

The Docks.description: Where the tributaries meet the Saranadi, a natural anchorage of calm water sheltered by the joining currents. Boats of every size crowd the mooring — trading barges, dugout canoes, pilot skiffs, and flat-bottomed cargo boats. Warehouses on stilts line the bank. Porters move goods between vessels. Pilots stand at the water's edge advertising their services to upriver-bound traders. The customs shed is a leaky platform where a frogkin elder collects tariffs and settles disputes with unhurried authority.

The Float.description: Sangamadi's social heart. A sprawl of interconnected floating platforms at the center of the village where the water is calmest. Cook-fires on iron braziers, low tables, woven mats to sit on. Food comes from the fish market and the riverbank gardens — grilled fish, rice wrapped in broad leaves, river-fruit, fermented drinks served from clay jugs. In the evening the lanterns come out, the platforms fill, and the whole village gathers on the water. Visitors, traders, and pilots crowd in alongside families. Space is made for anyone willing to share a table.

The Fish Market.description: A covered platform at the water's edge where the morning catch is sold, smoked, salted, and cooked on the spot. Frogkin fishers bring boats in at dawn and the market runs until the fish is gone. Smoked and salted fish packed for transport to New Anthara is a major trade. The cook-stalls at the market's edge compete fiercely — regulars take sides and arguments about whose preparation is superior can last years.

The Stilts.description: The residential village. Houses on wooden stilts connected by raised walkways above the waterline. Frogkin families live close together, their homes open-sided in the warm air, decorated with river shells and painted wood. Below the walkways, the water is shallow enough to wade and children splash between the pilings. Visiting traders and travelers who stay more than a night are offered hammock space in guest platforms at the village edge — simple, dry, and breezy.

The Still Pool.description: A warm, clear pool fed by a thermal spring in a hollow behind the village, shaded by ancient trees whose roots frame the water's edge. Sacred to the frogkin — a place of reflection, ceremony, and quiet. Outsiders are welcome to sit by the pool but not to enter the water uninvited. Frogkin come here for important occasions and private moments alike. The village's oldest stories are carved into the tree roots in a script that the frogkin elders read aloud during festivals. It is the quietest place in Sangamadi.

Mounagiri.name: Mounagiri
Mounagiri.basicInfo: A Mounari settlement built into the sheer walls of a river gorge. Houses cling to the cliff at every angle. Vine bridges span the gap. The river runs far below, deep and clear. For the chameleon-kin this is home and school both — the gorge is where their young learn to climb, to hide, and to move unseen. Students from other species make the journey here seeking training from the Mounari masters, though few find the village without a guide and fewer still complete the discipline.
Mounagiri.hiddenInfo:
Mounagiri is hard to find. This is intentional — the journey is the first test.

The village and the school are not separate. Every cliff face is a training surface. Every route between houses is a lesson in movement. Mounari children grow up climbing the gorge walls before they can walk flat ground confidently. The school for outsiders is simply a formalization of what every Mounari learns from birth.

Non-Mounari students are housed in the Ledge compound at the gorge's base. Training is structured but demanding. Masters teach climbing, stillness, camouflage technique, and movement in three dimensions. The magical mimesis — partial camouflage taught to non-chameleon-kin — requires months of breath and focus training before it manifests. Many students wash out. Those who graduate are among the most capable scouts and infiltrators in Larion.

Graduates are in demand across the continent — as scouts, spies, bodyguards, and thieves. The Mounari take no responsibility for how their students use the training. This neutrality is a source of tension with New Anthara, whose underworld actively recruits from Mounagiri's washouts and graduates alike.

The Mounari trade in jungle medicines, rare dyes derived from their own biological pigment processes, and textiles woven in shifting color patterns. Trade is small-scale and the Mounari prefer it that way.

The Descent.description: A single trail switchbacking down from the jungle rim to the gorge floor. Narrow, steep, carved into the rock by generations of use. The jungle closes overhead and the sound of the river rises from below. Visitors are met at the top by a Mounari guide — or not met at all, if the village doesn't expect them. The descent takes an hour and offers glimpses of the cliff village above and across the gorge: houses on the rock face, vine bridges swaying, figures moving on vertical surfaces as casually as walking.

The Cliff Village.description: Houses clinging to the gorge walls at every angle — horizontal ledges, vertical faces, tucked under overhangs. Connected by vine bridges, carved steps, and routes that only make sense if you can climb anything. Workshops, kitchens, and family homes are built into natural alcoves and widened fissures. The Mounari move through their village in three dimensions, shifting color against the rock as they go. Visitors on the bridges see chameleon-kin appear and disappear on the cliff face around them, going about their lives on surfaces that would kill anyone else.

The Gathering Ledge.description: A broad natural ledge halfway up the gorge where the village comes together. Flat enough to sit comfortably, wide enough for the whole community. Cook-fires burn in stone pits. Food is shared from common pots — river fish, jungle fruit, flatbreads cooked on hot stone. In the evenings the gorge fills with mist from below and the lanterns come out.

The Ledge.description: A compound at the gorge's base where non-Mounari students live and train. Dormitories, a kitchen, a training yard with climbable surfaces at varying difficulties. Simple but functional — the Mounari built it for people who need flat ground to sleep on, and the practicality is affectionate rather than contemptuous. A small market has grown around the compound: vendors serving students, equipment sellers, a tea stall run by a retired master who offers unsolicited advice.

The Wall.description: The sheer face at the gorge's narrowest point, carved with holds and routes of increasing difficulty. This is the school's heart — where students are tested and where masters demonstrate what mastery looks like.

The Gorge River.description: The river at the gorge's bottom, deep and clear and cold. The Mounari swim here daily — they are amphibious and the river is as much a part of the village as the cliff. Underwater grottoes and carved chambers line the riverbed, accessible only by diving. Students train in the water as well as on the rock.

Shilaghar.name: Shilaghar
Shilaghar.basicInfo: A serpentkin city carved into a stone mesa that rises above the jungle canopy into open sun. The serpentkin are among Ntar Vanna's most ancient peoples, and Shilaghar is their seat — part temple complex, part alchemical center, part neutral ground where the region's powers come to talk. Outsiders are welcome, provided they learn the customs. The serpentkin are patient teachers and unforgiving examiners.
Shilaghar.hiddenInfo:
Shilaghar operates on serpentkin terms. Etiquette is layered, and social missteps are remembered. Visitors who take the time to learn are rewarded. Those who don't are frozen out.

The temple complex is the spiritual heart — priestly houses that maintain rituals, records, and oral histories stretching back further than any other people in the region.

The alchemical quarter is the economic engine. Serpentkin alchemists produce potions, medicines, antidotes, and poisons of exceptional quality. The trade is regulated by the priestly houses, which take a share of every sale.

The shadow trade exists beneath the respectable surface. Certain families broker services — assassination, espionage. None of this is acknowledged publicly. The priestly houses take their share.

Shilaghar serves as neutral ground for Ntar Vanna's political disputes. Both of New Anthara's duarchs have sought counsel here. The serpentkin charge in favors as well as coin.

The Sun Stair.description: The only path up the mesa — a wide stone stairway carved into the rock face, switching back and forth through the canopy until it emerges into open sky. Dense green gives way to bare stone, warm air, and sudden brightness. Serpentkin guards stand at the top, still as statues, watching arrivals with unblinking eyes.

The Sunward Court.description: The mesa's broad summit, open to the sky. Sun-warmed stone plazas connect the temple buildings, ritual pools, and priestly residences. The architecture is monumental and ancient — carved facades, colonnaded halls, basking terraces oriented to catch light from dawn to dusk. The views from the mesa's edge reach across the jungle canopy to the horizon in every direction.

The Alchemical Quarter.description: A district of workshops, laboratories, and shopfronts on the mesa's lower terrace. Serpentkin alchemists work in open-fronted shops, their wares displayed in glass and clay vessels — potions, tinctures, antidotes, poisons, and medicines of extraordinary quality. Buyers from across Larion browse alongside local serpentkin.

The Sun Terrace.description: A broad stone ledge on the mesa's south face, carved with shallow heated pools and smooth reclining surfaces. Where the serpentkin gather to rest, socialize, and soak up the sun that their cold blood craves. Food is brought from the kitchens below: small dishes, alchemical teas, and fruit dried on the hot stone.

Citadel Uchaghar.name: Citadel Uchaghar
Citadel Uchaghar.basicInfo: The seat of the High Vanarin — a river citadel built into and onto a great stone island where the Saranadi narrows between high jungle banks. The monkeyfolk have held this rock for as long as anyone can remember and have never lost it. Rope-slides radiate from the island's heights to the canopy on both banks — the Vanarin can launch into the jungle at tremendous speed and scatter through the trees in moments. The citadel commands the river crossing, and the Vanarin collect tolls from every vessel that passes. Uchaghar is loud, colorful, well-fortified, and the undisputed center of Vanarin civilization in Ntar Vanna.
Citadel Uchaghar.hiddenInfo:
Uchaghar is the political and cultural heart of the High Vanarin. A High King rules from the summit hall. Succession is determined by acclaim, challenge, and elaborate contest. The High King's authority is genuine within Vanarin society and largely symbolic beyond it.

The rope-slide network is both transportation and defense. The ropes slope away from the citadel — fast exits, nearly impossible to assault. The Vanarin can cut every line and seal the island in minutes. No force in Ntar Vanna has ever taken Uchaghar.

The river crossing gives the Vanarin real economic leverage. Every passing vessel pays a toll. The tolls are not ruinous but non-negotiable, and the Vanarin spend freely on feasts, construction, and ambitious projects.

The Vanarin are genuinely formidable. Their warriors are fast, fearless, and fight in three dimensions. Their elders are wise and their knowledge of the jungle canopy is unmatched.

The River Dock.description: A stone dock at the island's base where the current slackens in a natural eddy. Trading boats, passenger craft, and the Vanarin's own painted river-skiffs crowd the mooring. Vanarin dock-masters direct traffic and collect tolls with great ceremony. Above, the citadel rises steeply, carved stone and timber construction climbing the rock face to the summit. Steps cut into the stone lead upward through a fortified gate.

The Stone Market.description: A broad platform at the island's base, partly shaded by canopy reaching from the banks. Permanent shops and stalls carved into the rock face sell jungle fruit, canopy herbs, woven goods, carved work, river fish, and items of varied provenance. Frogkin and lizardfolk traders pull alongside in boats. Demonborn and human merchants keep premises here. The Vanarin dominate the market by volume and enthusiasm. Haggling is expected and spirited.

The Great Stair.description: The main path through the citadel — a wide stone stairway carved into the island's core, rising from the docks through the residential levels to the summit. Landings open onto side passages, doorways, and terraces at every level. The walls are carved with murals depicting Vanarin history and heroes. The stair is busy at all hours — Vanarin bounding up and down at speed, visitors climbing at their own pace, children chasing each other between the legs of both.

The Howler's Rest.description: A tavern and inn carved into a wide natural cave halfway up the island, its mouth open to the river with a stone terrace overlooking the water. Hammocks and sleeping platforms fill the back chambers. The front is the drinking hall — low tables, cushions, a long stone bar where fruit wines and fermented jungle drinks are poured generously. The proprietor is loud, opinionated, and keeps order through force of personality. Visitors sleep here, eat here, and hear more about the river and the jungle than anywhere else in Uchaghar. Vanarin, frogkin, and river traders drink side by side.

The High Court.description: The island's summit, open to the sky. A great hall of stone and timber where the High King holds court, receives visitors, and presides over feasts. The hall is decorated with banners, trophies, and collected treasures catalogued with surprising precision. The High King's throne is carved from the island's own rock, the highest seat in Uchaghar. Court is conducted with formality — petitions heard, disputes settled, alliances negotiated. The Vanarin take governance seriously here, whatever outsiders assume.

The Rope Launch.description: A platform near the summit where the great ropes anchor. Thick braided lines stretch outward and downward to the canopy on both banks, some spanning hundreds of feet. The Vanarin launch from here — grabbing a rope, swinging out over the river, and releasing into the treetops at speed. Warriors drill formation launches. Scouts depart for the deep jungle. The view from the platform reaches across the canopy to the horizon in every direction.

Ntar Vanna League Chapterhouse.name: Ntar Vanna League Chapterhouse
Ntar Vanna League Chapterhouse.basicInfo: The League of Adventurers' Ntar Vanna chapterhouse, built into an ancient step-well sunk deep into the jungle floor. A great cenote — a hundred feet across and far deeper — descends in terraced stone levels, with a channeled waterfall pouring down one side.
Ntar Vanna League Chapterhouse.hiddenInfo:
The Deepfort is the League's primary base of operations in Ntar Vanna. It serves as expedition staging ground, monster-hunting coordination post, and the permanent home of a small garrison of League members.

The structure predates the League by centuries at minimum. Who built it and why is unknown. The engineering is precise and unlike either demonborn or serpentkin construction. The League occupies it but does not claim to understand it fully.

The League monitors conditions across Ntar Vanna from here — creature movements, regional disturbances, and threats to settlements and trade routes. Expeditions into the deep interior are planned and launched from the Deepfort. Bounties on dangerous creatures are posted and paid here. Visiting adventurers can find contracts, supplies, and experienced guides.

The single entrance at the rim makes the Deepfort exceptionally defensible. Each level can be sealed independently. The underground rivers at the base provide a secondary supply route and emergency exit.

Relations with New Anthara are cooperative but independent. The League answers to its own command structure, not the Duarchy. This is occasionally a source of friction when League operations and city interests conflict.

The Rim.description: The jungle clears and the ground drops away. The cenote opens like a vast stone mouth — a hundred feet across, terraced walls descending into green-tinged shadow, a waterfall channeled over the far edge falling the full depth of the shaft. Sunlight reaches the upper levels. The lower ones glow with lantern light. The League's banner flies from a stone post at the entrance, and a guardhouse controls the single stairway down. From the rim, looking in, you can see the full scope of the place — level after level carved into the walls, the waterfall's mist catching the light, and somewhere far below, the glint of water at the bottom.

The Cascade Stair.description: The main descent — a broad stone stairway carved into the cenote wall, switching back level by level with the waterfall thundering alongside. The spray keeps the stone damp and the air cool. Landings open at every level onto passages and chambers. The stair is wide enough for equipment, supplies, and stretchers to move freely. League members pass up and down at all hours — expeditions departing, wounded returning, scholars heading to the bottom, supplies coming up from the underground river docks.

The League Halls.description: The chapterhouse proper, occupying several levels of chambers carved into the cenote walls. Quarters for the permanent garrison and visiting members. A great common hall with a long table, a fire pit, and maps pinned to every wall. An armory. A provisions store. A small library of field reports, expedition logs, and studies of the apparatus. The halls are functional and lived-in — gear drying on racks, weapons being maintained, members eating and arguing over reports. A posting board near the common hall lists active contracts, expedition calls, and bounties on creatures causing problems in the region.

The Watchers' Tavern.description: A common room carved into an alcove off the League Halls, where the cenote wall curves to form a natural vault. The name is older than the League's tenancy — carved in stone above the entrance in a script nobody can read. Someone hung a sign beneath it. A stone bar, mismatched furniture accumulated over decades, and a terrace overlooking the open shaft where the waterfall's mist drifts past. League members, visiting adventurers, and the occasional scholar drink here between expeditions.

The Training Terraces.description: Open platforms on the cenote's sunlit side, broad enough for sparring, drills, and weapons practice. The terraces step down several levels, each suited to different training — close combat on the narrow ones, ranged practice on the wide ones, climbing and rappelling on the cenote wall itself.

The Apparatus Chamber.description: The bottom of the cenote. The waterfall ends here in a deep pool that feeds channels cut into the stone floor. The floor is a carved relief map of Ntar Vanna. Stone basins sit at specific points on the map, connected by channels that carry water between them in patterns the League has spent decades learning to read. Most days the water flows calmly and the duty watcher notes the readings in a logbook. Some days a basin ripples when it shouldn't, or a channel reverses, and word goes upstairs. In the center of the map, one large basin sits dry. The channels feeding it are intact. It has been empty since the League first came here. No one talks about it much.

The Underground River.description: Where the cenote's water drains into the rock. A low passage at the base of the pool opens into an underground river that connects to the wider water table beneath Ntar Vanna. The League keeps boats here — small, flat-bottomed, suited to the narrow passages. The river provides a secondary supply route and an emergency exit that surfaces miles away in the jungle.

Durg Sthira.name: Durg Sthira
Durg Sthira.basicInfo: A great fortress and town raised by the elephantfolk on a bend of the Saranadi. Durg Sthira is the finest piece of built architecture in Ntar Vanna — every stone set by gajanari hands and trunks, every wall true, every arch precise. The citadel commands the river from a bluff of natural stone that the gajanari have shaped into the foundation of their stronghold. Below it, the town is known across southern Larion for its smiths, artificers, and enchanters. The gajanari matriarchs govern from the citadel above, and their memory is the law.
Durg Sthira.hiddenInfo:
The ruling matriarch governs with the counsel of the family matriarchs. The matriarchs remember every agreement, every precedent, every debt owed to and by the gajanari.

The town is the finest center of arms, armor, jewelry, and enchanted equipment in Ntar Vanna.

The gajanari practice arcane memory — the ability to witness a spell cast once and reconstruct it through perfect recall. This tradition is taught in the citadel and students from other species seek training here.

Gajanari war-forgers are warriors who fight in masterwork armor of their own making, wielding equipment that is an extension of their craft and their body. A war-forger is a walking fortress.

The gajanari and the monkeyfolk of the High Vanarin have feuded for as long as either people can remember — which in the gajanari's case is a very long time. The feud is not a war — both peoples need the river and occasionally cooperate against real threats — but it is permanent, frequently petty, and runs through every interaction between the two.

The River Approach.description: The Saranadi bends around a stone bluff and Durg Sthira appears above — pale grey walls rising from shaped natural rock, towers and buttresses that seem grown rather than built. A river dock at the bluff's base spreads across broad stone quays with berths wide enough for the largest barges. The fortress looms overhead. The town spreads along the riverbank below.

The Forge Quarter.description: The heart of the town — streets of smithies, armories, jewelers, and artificers' workshops. Buyers come from across Larion for gajanari-forged arms, armor, jewelry, and enchanted equipment.

The Proving Yard.description: A broad stone courtyard where finished work is tested and war-forgers train. Gajanari warriors spar in full armor of their own making. Smiths test blades and enchanters verify their work holds under stress. Disputes between rival forge-families are sometimes settled here, smith against smith, in their own best equipment.

The Thunderfoot.description: The town's largest tavern, built around a courtyard open to the evening air with a stone stage where gajanari musicians play most nights — deep drums, reed pipes, and bass vocals that resonate in the chest. The food is slow-roasted meat, river fish in banana leaf, spiced rice, and jungle fruit. Gajanari drink alongside foreign smiths, traders, mercenaries, and adventurers. A posting wall near the entrance lists work the gajanari need done but would rather hire out.'

The Matriarch's Court.description: An open courtyard at the citadel's heart, paved in fitted stone with a fountain at its center. The ruling matriarch holds court here. The other family matriarchs attend on carved stone seats arranged in a half-circle.

The Grey College.description: The gajanari school of magic, occupying a wing of the citadel overlooking the river. The college teaches arcane memory — learning spells through observation and perfect recall — alongside enchantment, warding, and artifice. Students come from across Larion.

The Long Gallery.description: A corridor running the length of the citadel's river-facing wall, open on one side to the Saranadi and the jungle beyond. The inner wall is carved in continuous relief — the history of the gajanari rendered in stone by their own hands.

Caranthoria.name: Caranthoria
Caranthoria.basicInfo: The capital of the Holy Solarian Exarcate, sole overseas possession of the Solarian Dominion, built on the bones of a Gold Empire city that the lagoon swallowed centuries ago. The western half has been drained, reclaimed, and rebuilt into a showcase of Solarian engineering — colonnades, forums, aqueducts, and the Exarch's palace rising from ancient foundations. The eastern half remains flooded ruins behind a fortified boundary wall.
Caranthoria.hiddenInfo: The Solarians call it the City Reborn. The lizardfolk call it Sseth-Kalaa, the Drowned City. The western city is orderly, well-maintained, impressive — the Dominion's proof that its way of doing things works. The population is cosmopolitan in the Solarian fashion: all species are welcome as citizens and many have taken the offer. Turtlefolk masons and engineers built half the new city and are respected for it. Frogkin run much of the canal trade. Lizardfolk serve in the auxiliary legions or keep to their own quarter. Ratfolk have colonized the drainage tunnels and refuse to leave. The Exarch governs from the palace with a standing legion, a fleet, and the patience the Dominion is famous for.

The Sungate.description: The main approach to Caranthoria — a fortified causeway across the lagoon terminating in a monumental gate carved with the Solarian sunburst. Legionaries in gold-and-white armor check papers and offer the greeting: 'In His Light.' A noticeboard posts Imperial edicts, work levies, and bounties on swamp creatures threatening the outer causeways.

The Forum.description: A grand public square paved in reclaimed Gold Empire marble, ringed by colonnades where Solarian officials conduct the business of the Exarcate. The Exarch holds public audience on the first day of each week — citizens petition, disputes are arbitrated, justice is dispensed. A bronze statue of First Exarch Caranthor stands at the square's center.

The Exarch's Palace.description: Built on Gold Empire foundations, the palace is part administrative seat, part fortress, part temple to the god-emperor. Sunburst banners hang from every tower. The Exarch receives foreign dignitaries, military commanders, and intelligence reports in chambers that grow more private and more heavily guarded the deeper you go. A balcony on the upper floor overlooks the Boundary Wall and the flooded ruins beyond.

The Solarian Arena.description: A stone amphitheater where the legion holds games, exhibitions, and public trials by combat. Auxiliaries prove themselves here before promotion. Condemned criminals are offered a sword and a chance. Foreign fighters are welcomed — the Dominion respects martial skill regardless of origin, and a strong showing in the arena is the fastest path to citizenship for an outsider.

The Canal Market.description: A network of reclaimed Gold Empire canals lined with merchant stalls, workshops, and floating barges. This is where the species of the lagoon mix most freely — frogkin fishmongers sell the morning catch, turtlefolk masons hawk carved stonework, lizardfolk traders offer swamp herbs and cured hides, and ratfolk deal in everything else from rickety boats that never seem to be moored in the same place twice.

The Legion Quarter.description: Barracks, training yards, and armories housing the Exarcate's standing legion. The facilities are immaculate — the Dominion maintains its soldiers the way it maintains its roads. The Auxiliary Office recruits from every species in the lagoon: lizardfolk scouts, turtlefolk siege engineers, frogkin skirmishers. Pay is good, citizenship is guaranteed after ten years' service, and the recruiters are always hiring. A chapel to the god-emperor occupies the quarter's center, its golden dome second only to the palace's.

The Causeway's End.description: A busy tavern at the terminus of the main causeway, where the road meets the canal. Built from Gold Empire stone with a Solarian timber upper floor, it caters to everyone — legionaries off duty, merchants waiting on cargo, auxiliaries spending their pay, travelers drying off from the crossing. The landlord is a retired turtlefolk siege engineer who pours heavy drinks, settles arguments by arm-wrestling, and has never once asked anyone their business. The rooftop terrace has the best view of the lagoon at sunset.

The Warrens.description: The district the Solarians didn't plan. Ratfolk colonized the drainage tunnels beneath the western city and built upward until their ramshackle structures broke the surface between legitimate buildings. The legions maintain an uneasy truce: the ratfolk pay a nominal tax, provide information when pressed, and keep the tunnels from flooding. In return they are tolerated. Anything can be bought here that can't be bought above.

The Boundary Wall.description: A fortified wall bisecting the city where the reclaimed west meets the flooded east. Guard towers every hundred paces, torches burning through the night, legionaries who rotate off this posting quieter than when they rotated on.

The Flooded Ruins.description: The eastern half of the city, still drowned. Gold Empire towers rise from black water, their upper floors above the waterline, their foundations lost in the deep. Collapsed causeways form treacherous paths between structures.

Gorren Crossing.name: Gorren Crossing
Gorren Crossing.basicInfo: A sprawling frontier town at the ford of the River Gorren, where the orc steppe meets the settled south. No clan claims it. Every clan trades here. The old stone center was built by merchants generations ago; the rest grew up around it in timber and hide and optimism. Gorren Crossing runs on iron, horses, cattle, and the fact that everyone needs a place to do business on neutral ground. Duels in the street are legal. Theft gets you hanged. Everything in between is negotiable.
Gorren Crossing.hiddenInfo:
Gorren Crossing exists in a political gap. No orc clan claims sovereignty, but Clan Drokash considers the town within their sphere of influence—a position Clan Vorren increasingly contests. The town's continued independence depends on being useful to everyone and owned by no one.

Governance is layered and informal. The Watch keeps rough order—they'll break up a riot and hang a thief, but an honest fight between willing parties isn't their concern. They're funded by the mercantile houses of the Stone Quarter, which means they serve mercantile interests first. The bounty-hunters of the Crossing Court handles disputes for those rich enough to bring them.

Beyond the Stone Quarter, real authority belongs to the ward bosses—local figures with no legal standing who collect protection fees and settle disputes. In Downwind Ward, the boss rules through intimidation and consistency. In Sunset Ward, the boss rules through popularity and loyalty. The two don't get along.

Mercantile houses hire mercenaries to protect their interests, which creates its own problems—armed enforcers loyal only to money.

The population is mixed: humans, halflings, orcs who've settled permanently (viewed with anything from tolerance to contempt by clan riders), Highland dwarves running trading posts, and every kind of frontier drifter. Orc clan riders pass through constantly, and rivalries between Vorren and Drokash riders spill into taverns and streets regularly.

Despite the disorder, enormous wealth moves through Gorren Crossing—iron from the Mogra mines, horses and livestock from the steppe, trade goods from Highvale and the Highlands. It's rough, rich, and alive.

Ferry Landing.description: The barge landing on the River Gorren, where flat-bottomed ferries work the crossing day and night. Drovers, traders, orc riders, and caravans from Highvale all funnel through here. Rope-lines span the current. Ferrymen shout for fares. On the bank, a customs shack and a row of warehouses mark where the road becomes the town.

The Stone Quarter.description: The old town. A single paved road climbs from the river through two blocks of proper stone buildings—the only ones in Gorren Crossing. Mercantile house offices with iron-barred windows. The Crossing Court and the Watch House are located here.

The River Market.description: A packed commercial strip along the south bank, running from the Landing west toward the wards. Stalls, trestle tables, blankets spread on mud. Iron tools, weapons, leather, fur, Highland wool, Highvale luxuries, charms and remedies of dubious merit. No livestock—that's the Stockyards. Everything else is here, and so is everyone. Loudest place in town.

The Stockyards.description: Pens, corrals, and auction blocks spread across trampled ground south of the market. Cattle, horses, mules, and the occasional exotic beast. This is where the Ironwolds' real wealth changes hands in bulk. Drovers and clan riders do business with Highland buyers and Highvale agents.

Downwind Ward.description: East of the market, downhill, where the wind carries everything nobody wants to smell. Tanneries, dye-works, rendering yards, alchemical processing. The buildings are stained dark by years of output and the streets run strange colors after rain.

Sunset Ward.description: The western sprawl. Warehouses, cheap lodging houses, tent camps at the fringe. Where caravan goods get stored, where the people who haul them sleep, and where anyone who needs to disappear can find a room with no questions. Livelier and looser than Downwind—more taverns, more trouble, more opportunity. The kind of place where a copper buys a favor and a silver buys silence.

The Crowned Ox.description: The finest establishment in Gorren Crossing, which its owner will remind you of. A stone-built tavern and inn on the Quarter's main road with actual glass in the windows, a wine list, and chairs that match. Mercantile agents, visiting dignitaries, and anyone who wants to feel civilized for an evening. The owner enforces a dress code that amounts to no visible bloodstains. Good food, clean rooms, and the best place in town to overhear a scheme worth a fortune.

The Pit.description: Not a tavern with a fighting pit. A fighting pit with a tavern. A huge sunken ring, timber-walled, with tiered seating around the rim and drinking establishments built into the structure on every side. Honor disputes, grudge matches, exhibition bouts, and bare-knuckle entertainment run from sundown until someone dies or the crowd gets bored. Orc riders, human brawlers, anyone with something to prove. The Watch tolerates it because it keeps the killing organized. Reputations are made here—and ended.

The Hearthstone.description: A halfling-run inn on the waterfront near the Landing. Whitewashed walls, a copper-roofed porch over the river, window boxes with herbs. Inside: checked tablecloths, a hearth the size of a cart, and food that has no business being this good in a town like this. The proprietor keeps standards that would shame establishments in cities ten times the size. Clean rooms, fine ale, and a rule against fighting indoors that is somehow universally respected.
