Instead, describe a few fellow Coppers who are inspecting the offers, but don't have them say anything specific about the jobs they are considering. Once you have established the scene, end without further complications or events.

Two of Swords.name: Two of Swords
Two of Swords.firstQuest: The first quest should be a monster-hunting contract: neutralize the creature that's been attacking near the settlement. The arc theme should emerge from why the creature is here — displaced, corrupted, controlled, or part of a larger pattern — not from the creature itself. The arc should escalate from a local monster problem to a regional threat with an intelligent or organized source. Secrets should progress from 'something about this creature doesn't fit' to 'this is part of a bigger pattern' to 'here's where it's coming from and who's behind it.' The arc goal is a physical confrontation at the source — a lair, corrupted site, or faction base. Keep the first quest's scope small and completable: one creature, one settlement, one hunt. The arc does the escalation work.
Two of Swords.description:
ADVENTURE

TYour first contract as a Blade of the Tower of Swords. Your mentor's watching. The monster's out there somewhere. Show them both what you've got. An action start where you hunt monsters and work your way up in your guild.
Two of Swords.storyStart: Set the scene arriving at a settlement where the Tower of Swords has sent the player on their first Copper-rank contract. Briefly establish who the player is and that this is their trial run as a newly ranked Blade. Their assigned mentor is Rook Skander, a half-elf veteran Blade who's here to observe and advise but not to lead. Describe the settlement and establish that something has been attacking. The details should fit the region and terrain. Nobody knows exactly what the creature is. Rook gives the player three quick pieces of practical advice for working a contract: talk to the people who saw it or found the damage, examine the kill site or scene of the attack yourself, and don't assume you know what it is until you have evidence. He makes clear that the player calls the shots — he'll answer questions and back them up, but the contract is theirs. Have a couple of locals nearby who clearly have opinions or information — someone who found the dead livestock, someone who heard something at night, the innkeeper who's been fielding complaints. Make them accessible and let the player choose who to approach first. End after establishing these characters.

Hunter/Hunted.name: Hunter/Hunted
Hunter/Hunted.description:
MYSTERY

As an initiate of the Tower of Swords-a warrior society that hunts monsters and fights threats to the realm-you've been sent to investigate a hunting party that vanished in a lord's private preserve. A mystery adventure start.
Hunter/Hunted.storyStart: Set the scene at the edge of the Graves Hunting Preserve, late morning. The player character is an initiate of the Tower of Swords, accompanying veteran Blade Cameron Reid on their first real hunt. Briefly explain that the Tower of Swords trains and dispatches Blades—monster hunters who answer when something dangerous emerges in Larion. Briefly explain what drew the player to the Swords—a desire to protect, a talent for violence put to good use, or perhaps something took someone they loved and they want to make sure it doesn't happen again. A nervous servant named Colm leads them to where the hunting party was last seen two days ago. Colm explains: Lord Tristram Graves' son Sebastian rode out with four companions for a morning hunt. Colm was with the beaters. They heard the hunters calling out—excited, like they'd spotted game—then nothing. Sebastian's horse returned riderless. The household guard searched but found no bodies, no blood, no trail. Cameron moves into the brush and crouches. When he returns, his expression is grim. 'Found their tracks.' He looks deeper into the trees. 'They didn't run. They followed something. All of them, one at a time.' He checks his crossbow, then his axe. 'Something out there calls and men come to it.' He turns to the player. 'This is your hunt too. How do you want to work this?'

Field Trip.name: Field Trip
Field Trip.description:
ACTION

You're a first-year magic student on your first field assignment, sent to investigate reports of a magical disturbance and a rogue mage near a sleepy market village. A comedy adventure start.
Field Trip.firstQuest:
## First Quest

- Put an end to the imps' chaos in the Market Square
- Quest name: Pest Control

Important: Put the first quest in `The Market Square` (existingArea) of location `Briarton`.

Arc theme: Thera Pel, a former Academy student, expelled and living rough in the Briars, has been summoning creatures and brewing love and anti-love potions. One of her summons — a lovesick Imp Lord — has slipped her control, allied with the innkeeper's daughter, and is escalating his own ambitions: to summon something even bigger than himself. Expected conclusion: The party puts a definitive end to the Imp Lord's chaos in Briarton.

Secrets:

1. A binding sigil is visible on the back of one imp's neck. These are summoned creatures, not native.
2. A witch who goes by the name of Thera has been charging villagers for healing and minor charms in the woods east of Briarton.
3. Empty potion vials litter clearings in the Briars — dozens of them, all different.
4. Half-finished binding circles in a clearing, the chalk fresh, the work abandoned mid-ritual.
5. Someone has been buying — or stealing — herbs from every village within a day's ride: damiana, valerian, moonflower, nightroot. Love potion ingredients, and ingredients for their antidotes.
6. A creature about three feet tall, horned and small-winged, in ridiculously fancy clothing, has been seen on rooftops at dusk.
7. Samantheria, the innkeeper's daughter, has been sneaking out at odd hours. People in Briarton who've crossed her have been having unusually bad weeks — having fantastically embarrassing mishaps occur to them in public, as if they were cursed.
8. The magical disturbances are getting worse, and more imps keep appearing.
9. The imps have set up a base of operations in a cave near the village of Briarton.
10. A massive summoning circle is being constructed in the woods near Briarton and is almost ready.
Field Trip.storyStart:
Introduce the player characters as first-year students of the Academy of the Arcane, a prestigious magic school. They are on their first field assignment.

They are accompanied by their mentor, Temyn Raskwood, a senior student.

Set the scene arriving at Briarton on market day. Temyn stops the group at the edge of the square and reminds them of the brief: the village reported a magical disturbance and sightings of a rogue mage in the area. Their assignment is to investigate both. Temyn glances around as he speaks, joking that it's probably nothing.

A small winged creature — leathery, about the size of a cat, with a face like a cruel little old man — drops onto Temyn's shoulder from behind, sinks its teeth into his ear, and shrieks an alliterative, vulgar insult about Temyn and the party. Temyn yelps. The imp launches off and darts around Temyn, mocking him.

A second imp appears in the sheep pen, capers along the top rail, and undoes the gate latch with one clawed finger. The sheep flood out into the square, bleating, trampling baskets and overturning a fruit cart. The imp leaps onto the inn's signpost above the chaos, throws its arms wide, and declares in a carrying voice that Briarton is the DOMAIN OF THE IMP LORD, that wizards are not welcome here, and that the young wizard and his little friends should turn around and go home before something worse happens.

The market erupts. Stalls topple, merchants grab their goods, traders shout. Temyn urges the party to stop standing around and do something about the imps.

First Blood.name: First Blood
First Blood.description:
MYSTERY

As an initiate of the Tower of Shadow, a clandestine order that protects Larion from supernatural threats, you've been sent under cover on your first mission to hunt a fledgling vampire in a small village in the countryside. A dark mystery start.
First Blood.storyStart: Set the scene on the road approaching Millbrook at dusk. The player character is an initiate of the Tower of Shadow, accompanied by their mentor Tarain Rethford, a blood mage and operative. Briefly explain that the Tower of Shadow is an order of clandestine operatives who protects Larion by infiltrating and defeating cults, secret societies and criminal organizations. This is the player's trial mission—Tarain advises, but the initiate leads. Briefly explain the player's background and why they joined the Tower of Shadow - perhaps a gift for convincing others, a brush with secret evil, or a will to get their hands dirty for the common good. Describe Millbrook's gates ahead, torchlight flickering, voices raised in anger. As they approach, they find a crowd gathered in the Village Common. Armed villagers hold a young man while others shout accusations—a woman lies dead nearby, her throat torn. The accused is a Tarth, and the dead woman a Millner; the two families have obvious bad blood. The crowd wants blood itself. Tarain studies the scene, then speaks quietly: 'Fledgling vampire. Sloppy kill, no attempt to hide it. Turned recently, still figuring out what it is.' She pricks her palm with a silver dagger and lets a drop of her blood fall to the earth, then closes her eyes. 'The boy's heart is racing. Scared out of his wits. But beating.' She glances at the lynch mob. 'He's not our vampire. But we tell them they've got a vampire on the loose, we may scare off our quarry - or start a riot. But this is your hunt, initiate. Your call how we handle this.'

Into the Fire.name: Into the Fire
Into the Fire.description:
COMBAT

When cultists set your inn ablaze, their leader brings the flames to your door. An action start with a hook to larger regional troubles.
Into the Fire.storyStart: Introduce the party by humorously describing them asleep in the Royal Chamber, a cheap communal dormitory at the Crossroads Inn. They're traveling with Vera, an adventurer. Make up a reason for the party to be heading toward Ardan's Watch together. Then the party wakes to smoke and screams. Through the window, the stables are burning. Two figures stand in the yard — a robed cultist with fire dancing in their hands, and an enforcer carrying a staff that glows red-hot. Vera rolls from her bunk, crouches by the window, and watches for a moment. 'I'm sorry. I don't know how they found me this fast. I'll explain later. Come one, let's move.'

Overboard.name: Overboard
Overboard.description:
COMBAT

Pirates attack the merchant galley you're traveling on. For better or worse, your new friend knows these particular pirates very well.... An action adventure start.
Overboard.storyStart: Set the scene aboard a Spice Isles merchant galley in the North Canal, bound for Tevantia. Sun on the deck, salt spray, colorful sails snapping in the wind. Briefly introduce the party members and why they're traveling—trade, adventure, escaping trouble, chasing opportunity. Make the introductions light and funny where appropriate. Introduce Coral, a striking woman with sea-green tinged skin and faintly webbed fingers who's been friendly company on the voyage. The lookout screams. A sleek raider emerges from behind a rocky isle, flying the black flag of the Feathered Fleet. The crew panics—everyone's heard of the seabird pirates. But Coral goes pale for a different reason. 'That's my ship. The Windreaver. I was captain until my first mate Scraw led a mutiny.' She watches the pelican and albatross pirates swinging grappling hooks, preparing to board. 'Scraw's vicious but stupid. He'll have kept my lockbox in the captain's cabin—never could pick the lock. There's something in there worth more than this whole cargo.' She draws a long knife. 'So. Do we just survive this, or do we take my ship back?'.

Return of the Dragon Queen.name: Return of the Dragon Queen
Return of the Dragon Queen.description:
EPIC

On the road with two strangers met by chance, you come upon a country inn under attack. What begins as a roadside skirmish becomes the start of a campaign to save Larion. A combat start with a hook into a world-spanning campaign.
Return of the Dragon Queen.storyStart:
Begin the response with the following crawl passage rendered VERBATIM, with no additions or modifications. After the crawl, leave a paragraph break, then begin the scene fresh in third person present tense. Do NOT prefix the scene with 'Long ago, in the world of Larion' — the crawl serves that role.

OPENING CRAWL (render verbatim):

NARRATOR: Long ago, the great battles of Larion's Age of Heroes ended in peace.

In time, the legendary deeds of those heroes faded into bedtime tales for children.

But now troubles worthy of those days sweep the land once more.

In the north, the warring goblin tribes have united under one banner: the Black Dragon. And their warcry is a name from such tales: Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.

While Larion's princes bicker and squabble, the League of Adventurers has issued a Convocation, calling every able sword and staff to Ardan's Watch.

After the crawl, leave a paragraph break, then begin the scene with a transition line: 'Meanwhile, on a forest road in Highvale, a day's walk from Ardan's Watch...' Continue in third person present tense.

Set the scene on a forest trail, late afternoon. Briefly establish who the player character is and that they are traveling with two others for safety: Vera Stone, a sharp-eyed woman in her late twenties who carries a short sword and a crossbow, and Volnom, a very small, very old gnome in a faded red cloak shuffling along on a walking staff taller than he is.

As they walk, Volnom and Vera exchange remarks: 'Vera: How much further, Volnom?

Volnom: Closer than last time you asked.

Vera: My feet are killing me.

Volnom: Too young for a walking stick, she says.

Vera: Branches look good on trees.

Vo: Complaining looks good on no one.' Then the smell of smoke reaches them on the wind.

They hurry through the trees and stop at the edge of the clearing. The Crossroads Inn stands ahead: a long timber building, its stables burning black. Three robed figures stand in the yard — two with short blades with magic flame dancing on their edges, the leader with a heavy staff, green fire rippling around its head. He shouts towards the inn: 'The messenger! Send him out, now! You will not escape. We burned your horses. Send him out, or we burn you all with him!' No answer from the inn. Vera silently readies her crossbow. In a low voice: 'Pyre cultists. Standing bounty, hundred florins a head at the Tower. I'll take the one with the big mouth and the love for animals.'

Volnom looks up at the sky, traces a small sign in the air and sighs. 'Lady of Mercy, forgive me. I fear I must interpret my vow to you... creatively.' He turns to the player. 'Are you coming?'

The Academy of the Arcane.name: The Academy of the Arcane
The Academy of the Arcane.firstQuest: Report to the Great Hall to receive your first Practicum assignment and meet your partner.
The Academy of the Arcane.description:
SANDBOX

Today you join the ranks of the realm's elite mages-in-training, soon ot receive your first real assignment. You start out in an orientation at your new school. A social sandbox start.
The Academy of the Arcane.storyStart:
Set the scene in a small dormitory room in one of the Academy's towers during late afternoon. Two beds, two desks, one empty, the other where the player is unpacking. Their roommate enters—Sam Vellory if the player is male, Samantha Vellory if female. Freckled, sand-haired, wearing clearly hand-me-down mage robes, looking like they've just run up several flights of stairs. They're holding two small silver pins.

Without much preamble they hand one to the player—'First Circle pin, you'll need it for tomorrow, it marks you as eligible for assignment.' They pin their own to their collar with shaking fingers. 'Practica assignments go up after breakfast. First Circles get partnered with upper Circle students—someone to show us the work, report back on how we do. I've heard Third Circle Ayla Thane is mentoring this term, she's supposed to be brilliant, but also that Second Circle Teldric Ren drew the short straw and he's—' They stop. Take a breath. 'Sorry. I'm nervous. My father made Sentinel in his first year. My uncle didn't get placed until his third. It all depends on how your first Practica go, who sees what in you.'

Have Sam produce something from their satchel—a small jar of salve, clearly homemade, offering it to the player. 'Burn salve. My mother made it. She said with everything happening lately—fire cults, the troubles in the Fells—better to have it.' A pause. 'I don't even know what kind of Practicum we'll draw. Investigation, containment, recovery—they post what's available and partners choose from what's left by seniority, so First Circles get whatever nobody else wanted.' They try to smile. 'Should be fine. Probably.'

The welcome dinner for new students starts soon in the Great Hall. Tomorrow everything begins. Does the player want to walk down together?

Homecoming.name: Homecoming
Homecoming.description:
SANDBOX

After months away training and adventuring, you return to find your village threatened and your family in need. An sandbox action adventure start.
Homecoming.storyStart: Set the scene on the road approaching Greenmeadow on a late afternoon in early autumn. Describe golden wheat fields stretching across gentle hills, the ancient oak tree visible in the distance, smoke rising from cottage chimneys. Briefly explain who each party member is, their connection to Greenmeadow (grew up here, family still lives here, promised to return, etc.), and what drove them to leave - a desire to see the world, to train as warriors/mages/etc., to escape a peaceful life that felt too small. As they reach the Green, they encounter a group of villagers gathered near the well, talking in worried voices. The village elder pushes through the crowd. 'Welcome home,' she says, and there are tears in her eyes. 'And just in time! Something in the Wolfwood... the attacks started two weeks ago. Lord Corwin's rangers can't be everywhere at once, and we...' She gestures helplessly at the worried villagers. 'We're farmers, not warriors. Whatever's out there, it's getting bolder.'

The Inheritance.name: The Inheritance
The Inheritance.description:
SANDBOX

A distant relative's death has left you the unexpected heir to a run-down noble estate. An sandbox social start where you can explore and rebuild your new home.
The Inheritance.storyStart: Set the scene at the entrance to Riverside Manor on a gray morning. The drive is overgrown, the fountain dry and cracked, weeds pushing through the gravel. The manor house rises ahead—roof tiles missing, windows cracked, but the bones clearly solid. If there is a single player character, they have inherited this estate from a distant relative they barely knew. If there are multiple party members, establish who inherited and why the others have come along. As they take in the scene, an elderly man emerges from the manor. This is the steward—he has kept the estate from total ruin these past five years, though the strain shows. He explains that since the previous master's untimely demise five years  prior, funds have run dry, staff left, the Willowford tenants have struggled without leadership. He mentions that Lady Ashbury at the neighboring estate has made repeated offers to purchase. He soffers to show them inside.'

Of Ales and Ambitions.name: Of Ales and Ambitions
Of Ales and Ambitions.description:
SANDBOX

You've come into ownership of a tavern. The rest is up to you. An open-ended social start.
Of Ales and Ambitions.storyStart: Set the scene with the player arriving at a tavern they have just come into ownership of — through inheritance, a debt repaid, a wager won, a reward, or whatever fits the character's backstory and the location. This is their first time walking in as the owner. Briefly establish who the player is and why they wanted this or how it fell into their lap. Describe the tavern — its condition, its character, its feel. It doesn't need to be a wreck, but it should have personality and room for the player to make it their own. There should be people already here — a barkeep or cook who stayed on from the previous owner, a regular or two, maybe a neighbor who heard about the change. These people should feel like potential friends and allies, not obstacles or problems. Someone should be glad to see a new owner. The tone is warm. There is no crisis, no urgent threat, no trouble at the door. This is a good day — the start of something. Let the player look around, talk to people, and figure out what they want this place to be. Do not generate any conflict, danger, or pressing problems. The story is: you have a tavern now. What do you do with it?

A Night at the Tavern.name: A Night at the Tavern
A Night at the Tavern.description:
SANDBOX

You've come to the city seeking fortune as an adventurer. Word is that taverns are where such opportunities can be found. An open-ended start that lets you choose the direction your story takes.
A Night at the Tavern.storyStart: Set the scene at a tavern where the PC has come looking for adventure work. Briefly establish who the PC is and how they ended up here. The tavern should feel alive — something is already happening when the PC walks in. Opportunities for work and adventure emerge naturally from the people and what is going on — not announced, but overheard, witnessed, or stumbled into. Other people here are also looking for work or partners. The tone is fun and inviting — this is the start of something good.

For Gold and Glory.name: For Gold and Glory
For Gold and Glory.description:
ACTION

You've signed up to fight in the arena. The crowd's waiting. Time to make a name for yourself. A gladiator action adventure start.
For Gold and Glory.storyStart: Set the scene with the player arriving at an arena to sign up as a new fighter. Briefly establish who the player is and what brought them here — ambition, money, a dare, a debt, a love of fighting, or whatever fits the character. This is their first time competing. Describe the arena and give it atmosphere — the crowd, the noise, the scale of the place. The tone is exciting, not grim. This is sport and spectacle, not punishment. There should be people around who make the world feel full: a trainer or fight manager who runs a stable of fighters and is sizing the player up as a prospect, other fighters preparing for their own bouts — some friendly, some not — and someone official handling registrations. Introduce the idea that fighters here can have patrons, sponsors, or belong to a ludus or stable, but don't force the player into one. Their first bout is coming up soon. Let the player take in the scene, talk to people, and prepare however they want. The mood is anticipation — something good is about to happen and the player is at the center of it.

The Invitation.name: The Invitation
The Invitation.description:
SOCIAL

An invitation to a lord's banquet has fallen into your hands. The night is young. Who is to say how it will end? An open-ended social start.
The Invitation.storyStart: Set the scene with the player arriving at a grand social event — a lord's banquet, a noble reception, a diplomatic gathering. Briefly establish who the player is and why they're here: perhaps a minor noble, perhaps someone who earned an invitation through service or cunning, perhaps someone who wasn't invited at all. The event should feel lavish, lively, and appealing — good food, beautiful setting, interesting people. Introduce one NPC who approaches the player with charm and an obvious agenda — they want something, and they think the player can provide it. Make this person fun and appealing, not sinister. The stakes are social: reputation, alliance, opportunity. No combat, no conspiracy, no mysterious threats, no traditional adventure hooks. Just ambitious people in a beautiful room, each playing their own game.

Twin Spirits.name: Twin Spirits
Twin Spirits.description:
SOCIAL

Your magical animal companion has been accused of hurting someone. It's up to you to work together to get out of the situation. A social start with a magical companion.
Twin Spirits.storyStart: Each player in the party starts with one magical or animal companion each, based on their background, race, and class. Briefly introduce each player and their companion and have them banter about their history and relationship (you can communicate with your companion telepathically). Describe the scene of you discovering that someone from town has been telling people that your companion attacked a person in town, and they are trying to start a riot.

(Custom) Backstory Focus.name: (Custom) Backstory Focus
(Custom) Backstory Focus.description:
CUSTOM

Fortune favors the bold. Begin your tale with a custom start based off your backstory.
(Custom) Backstory Focus.storyStart: Write a starting situation based on the character's backstory and current life situation. First set the scene by describing the current location clearly, remembering that the players may have never seen it before. Just say clearly what the location is, don't be too clever, and then give it a clear establishing description. Briefly explain who the party members are, how they came to be in the starting location, and what they are doing there. Tie the explaination into the character's backstory to the best of your ability. Use as much of the backstory as you can, and make the story start as close to the ending point of the backstory as possible, but you are free to improvise if you need to in order to give the party members a good reason for being where they are.
locationAreas: The Caravanserai of the Thousand Roads

Highvale.name: Highvale
Highvale.basicInfo: Highvale is the realm's green heartland — rolling hills, grain fields, and the great river Silverflow winding south to the sea. Noble houses hold the land in a patchwork of lordships and free towns, but no king rules here. Knights keep the roads. The League of Adventurers keeps its headquarters at the capital, Ardan's Watch, and the region draws would-be heroes the way a forge draws iron. The wilds between settlements are another matter — ancient ruins, monster lairs, and bandit holds in the forests and hills keep the League busy and the tavern talk interesting.

The Spice Isles.name: The Spice Isles
The Spice Isles.basicInfo: The Spice Isles are an archipelago of feuding merchant republics and pirate havens connected by colossal stone bridges and threaded with canals, where fortunes are built on spice, silk, and ventures less legal. The Free States bow to no throne. Instead they follow the Swordlaw—disputes settled steel to steel. An ancient treaty known as the Compact forbids open conflict between city-states, so merchant clans wage shadow war through privateers, spies, and mercenaries. Noble houses control the banks, guilds rule the trade routes, and seafolk keep to their kingdoms beneath the waves. The fallen Gold Empire left behind crumbling marble palaces, the great canal and bridge network - and legends of its island capital vanished beneath the sea long ago.

The Everwood.name: The Everwood
The Everwood.basicInfo: The Everwood is a primeval forest older than human memory, ruled by the high elves from their forest cities. Beneath the roots, dark elves hold their own dominion — banished underground after the Kindred War that split elvenkind apart. Non-elf Outsiders are permitted in the border settlements by elven sufferance: Sanctuary, the great trading city, and the villages of the Valley. Dark elves are forbidden from both, on pain of death. Beyond lies wilderness where the old laws hold and trespassers answer to the Wardens. Fey spirits and sylvan creatures stranger still haunt the trackless vastness.

The Highlands.name: The Highlands
The Highlands.basicInfo: The Highlands are a realm of stone and snow where dwarves and giants have contested every peak and valley since the Age of Heroes. Soaring mountains and plunging valleys, glacier-fed rivers and windswept moors. Dwarven clans carve their holds into caves and cliffsides while giant tribes claim the high places. Ancient grudges run as deep as the ore veins, and bloodlines matter even more than gold. Stone circles and cairns mark clan boundaries across the heather, many commemorating some victory or betrayal older than memory.

The Ironwolds.name: The Ironwolds
The Ironwolds.basicInfo: The Ironwolds is open hill country and rolling grassland under wide skies. Orc horse-clans hold this land, riding the seasonal circuit between river valleys and high summer camps. Great holdfasts crown the rocky tors, their banner-poles visible for miles. Clan Vorren and Clan Drokash are the greatest — and neither will bend to the other. Human and halfling communities farm the sheltered valleys under bonds of guest-right and tribute, though some on both sides resent the peace. Moot-law governs: disputes settled by oath, trial, or combat at Gods-Anvil, the sacred mesa where the orcs say their people were forged. The clans are ironworkers as much as riders, and the hills are rich with ore. The River Gorren cuts through the heart of the land, too wide to bridge — barges and ferries are the only way across.

The Wild Grass Sea.name: The Wild Grass Sea
The Wild Grass Sea.basicInfo: The Wild Grass Sea is an ocean-like expanse of tall, thick grass stretching in every direction under a wide-open sky. Rocky tors break the surface like islands. Rivers cut channels where the banks are the only clear ground for miles. This is beastfolk country — boarfolk, wolfkin, lionkin, serpentfolk, catfolk, and dozens more, each holding their own territory. They are not one people. Ancient rivalries and competing interests divide them, but the Moot Law unites them: sacred gathering grounds where killing is forbidden and all species trade and negotiate under enforced truce. Between Moots, each species settles disputes by its own customs.

Tar Teirn.name: Tar Teirn
Tar Teirn.basicInfo: Tar Teirn is a land of moors, fens, lakes, and rivers winding through misty valleys. Three kingdoms contend this country: Caerdon, where the warrior-king rules from an iron throne and knightly orders ride to tournament and war. Llanmere, where noble houses scheme for power and fortunes are made and lost at court. Dynfall, where druids counsel the crown and magic runs through the lakes and standing stones like blood through veins. They feud, they feast, they marry each other's children and betray each other's trust.

The Great Lagoon.name: The Great Lagoon
The Great Lagoon.basicInfo: The Great Lagoon is a vast coastal region of tidal lagoons, fertile marshland, and reclaimed shoreline  where the Holy Solarian Exarcate has built the Dominion's foothold on the continent. Fortified causeways cross the shallows between walled Solarian towns, while the deeper swamps and outer islands remain wild — home to lizardfolk clans, frogkin villages, and things older and less welcoming. Solarian roads, Solarian law, Solarian legions — behind the sea-walls. Beyond them, the marsh answers to no one.

The Black Fells.name: The Black Fells
The Black Fells.basicInfo: The Black Fells are starkly beautiful volcanic highlands recently claimed by goblin settlers. Steam vents and mineral wealth drew them to transform the land with ramshackle forge-towns that burn day and night. What little order exists is staked claims, makeshift markets, and newcomers arriving daily. The goblins welcome outsiders - their coin spends as well as anyone's, and the Fells need workers, warriors, and those brave enough to face the wild things above and below.

The Moon Kingdoms.name: The Moon Kingdoms
The Moon Kingdoms.basicInfo: The Moon Kingdoms are alpine principalities in steep mountain valleys where many slopes never see direct sunlight ruled by moon elf vampire lords. These prince-magisters are few in number but immensely powerful, reigning from baroque palaces over populations of common moon elves and other races who live in relative prosperity, so long as they accept the blood price. The Kingdoms shelter those persecuted elsewhere, for cursed blood is understood by those who bear their own curse. Beautiful, wealthy, and sophisticated, but dangerous. Feral vampires haunt the high passes, and the barrier between worlds is thin.

The Bright Kingdoms.name: The Bright Kingdoms
The Bright Kingdoms.basicInfo: The Bright Kingdoms are desert realms built around oases lush with palm groves and perfumed gardens. Hilltop villages and cities of white limestone and golden sandstone rise from the dunes, their soaring towers and colorful tilework visible for miles across the desert. The Bright Folk have mastered their harsh environment through ancient traditions and magical expertise, creating prosperity that draws both traders and enemies. Though occasionally united by the necessity of mutual defense against threats from neighboring regions, old rivalries simmer between the Kingdoms, and the desert holds ruins that speak of past glories and terrible mistakes.

Aurora.name: Aurora
Aurora.basicInfo: Aurora is a frozen frontier beneath skies that blaze with colored light at night. Fortified trading posts and trapper settlements dot the tundra and ice-locked coasts, their stockades standing against howling winds and the megafauna that roam the white wastes. Clans of nomadic hunters follow mammoth and dire elk migrations across landscapes of stunning, lethal beauty. The northern lights illuminate frozen waterfalls, ice formations like colored glass, and horizons that seem to go on forever. The cold is merciless, and those who endure it are harder than the frozen ground they walk. Prospectors brave the wastes seeking ivory, amber, and stranger things locked in ancient ice.

Ntar Vanna.name: Ntar Vanna
Ntar Vanna.basicInfo: Ntar Vanna is the great jungle of the southern world. It rains every day — warm, torrential, endless. The growth is prodigious: trees vast as towers, undergrowth that swallows roads in days, creatures larger and wilder than anything in the outer world. Serpentfolk, lizardfolk, frogkin, and demonborn make their homes in its depths. The demonborn once raised great cities here — ruins now, strangled by vine and root. The soil is warm. The magic runs hot. The deep interior feels less like Larion and more like somewhere older and wilder and not entirely of this world.

mundane humanoid.name: mundane humanoid
mundane humanoid.description: An ordinary mortal person such as a guard, bandit, merchant, villager, rogue, soldier, or cultist without supernatural defenses.

trained warrior.name: trained warrior
trained warrior.description: A disciplined martial combatant protected by armor, toughness, or battlefield training.

arcane spellcaster.name: arcane spellcaster
arcane spellcaster.description: A mortal mage, wizard, illusionist, blood mage, runesmith, or ritualist whose defenses come from spellcraft rather than monstrous nature.

priest or blessed caster.name: priest or blessed caster
priest or blessed caster.description: A mortal holy caster, shrine guardian, templar, or light-bearing servant of a sacred power.

corporeal undead.name: corporeal undead
corporeal undead.description: A physical undead body such as a zombie, ghoul, mummy, revenant, barrowguard, or death knight.

spectral undead.name: spectral undead
spectral undead.description: An incorporeal undead spirit such as a ghost, wraith, banshee, shade, or possessor.

undead spellcaster.name: undead spellcaster
undead spellcaster.description: A lich, necromantic archmage, dracolich, or other undead whose body is sustained by powerful death magic.

fiend.name: fiend
fiend.description: A demon, devil, hellhound, nightmare, soul thief, or infernal creature shaped by fire and shadow.

fire elemental.name: fire elemental
fire elemental.description: A creature made from flame, magma, ash, or living heat.

frost elemental.name: frost elemental
frost elemental.description: A creature made from ice, winter, freezing water, or supernatural cold.

storm elemental.name: storm elemental
storm elemental.description: A creature of lightning, thunder, charged wind, or living storm.

shadow creature.name: shadow creature
shadow creature.description: A shade, void-touched being, smoke spirit, nightmare thing, or creature sustained by darkness.

plant creature.name: plant creature
plant creature.description: A living plant, fungus, awakened tree, dryad-bound growth, or vegetation monster.

ooze.name: ooze
ooze.description: A slime, gelatinous cube, amorphous dungeon scavenger, or boneless acidic mass.

construct.name: construct
construct.description: An automaton, golem, animated armor, gargoyle, or other artificial body animated by magic or machinery.

stone construct.name: stone construct
stone construct.description: A stone golem, gargoyle, animated statue, or rocky artificial guardian.

lycanthrope.name: lycanthrope
lycanthrope.description: A werewolf, werebear, wererat, or other shapeshifter protected from ordinary weapons by a curse.

dragon.name: dragon
dragon.description: A true dragon or major draconic creature with elemental breath and ancient supernatural resilience.

venomous beast.name: venomous beast
venomous beast.description: A serpent, scorpion, spider, plague beast, or toxin-bearing predator.

aquatic creature.name: aquatic creature
aquatic creature.description: A merfolk, triton, kraken, marsh predator, water spirit, or creature native to rivers, swamps, lakes, or seas.

beast.name: beast
beast.description: An ordinary or oversized animal predator such as a wolf, bear, eagle, bat, ape, crab, serpent, or lizard.

aberration.name: aberration
aberration.description: A mind-warping, anatomically unnatural, or reality-wrong creature such as a mouther, doppelganger, mimic, corpse-eater, or psychic horror.

fey.name: fey
fey.description: A fairy, pixie, satyr, dryad, hag, courtier, enchanter, or other creature of glamour, bargains, and old magic.

celestial.name: celestial
celestial.description: A unicorn, skyblessed being, angelic messenger, blessed spirit, or creature of divine radiance.

tips: Hey there! New here? Want to know more about the game or how to play? Pop open the Narrator Chat in the sidebar and ask away!
Need to change the plan, skip a dull scene, or get advice? Narrator chat is literally built for that!
Stuck, confused, bored? Type in the narrator sidebar and say what you need!.
Can't remember the name of a character or place? Try writing '@' followed by first few letters.
Stuck with no idea what to do next? Use the 'Ask the Narrator' command to ask for suggestions.
Worried about convincing someone? Try taking the conversation one line at a time.
Be ambitious and creative, Voyage rewards those who dream big!
How you phrase things matters. The game rewards you for stylish, clever actions.
Coordinate with party members in combat for powerful combined effects.
The world is your oyster. Find and claim a place to call home.
Family is worth fighting for. Having a family in the game can lead to great stories.
You can attack multiple times per turn, and so can your allies.
Tell people your hopes and goals! you never know who may share your dreams.
Death is not always the end…
If there's something you want to do, go out and give it a try!
Try describing your actions in detail. They may not always work out the way you expect, but it will make things more interesting!
If you can't beat 'em, persuade 'em. Try convincing an enemy to come over to your side - or go over to theirs!
You can solve a problem any way you can think of... fight, bribe, trick, steal, befriend. The choice is yours.
You may spend a long time with your party members. Choose them wisely!
You can try to dodge, block and attack all in the same turn.
You can use items and take other actions in the same turn.
Skills are most likely to succeed when you use them in a logical way.
Don't forget to stock up on items. Shops often have very powerful equipment for sale.
You can level up by taking almost any action, so feel free to play the game whatever way you want.
Don't forget to check in on your party members! Ask them how they're doing - and tell them how you feel!
Sometimes you can insta-kill enemies if you get creative with your inputs.
Party members can learn new skills and abilities as you progress through the story together.
Environmental conditions can add modifiers to your skill checks. Use the world to your advantage!
Tired of action and fighting? Look for a tavern or other gathering spot to socialize and start new relationships.
To prepare for a fight, try giving your party members orders to carry out in combat.
Social skills are very powerful. Persuasion can save a lot of lives.
Don't think you can win a fight? Try making a deal instead.
You don't have to do it all yourself. Hire people to do jobs for you!
Tired of traveling? Try learning how to fly or teleport or look for other forms of travel in the world.
There's nothing you can't do if you put your mind to it, from starting a revolution to founding your own country!
Turn order is determined by level and a random initiative value. Higher level characters tend to act first.
Characters without targets (like those that are buffing or healing) act before characters who are targeting enemies.
Multiple defensive effects stack multiplicatively. Layer your defenses!
Damage reduction scales with level difference, but has diminishing returns.
Different enemies can have different resistances, vulnerabilities, immunities or weaknesses, which will greatly affect the damage they take.
Try different damage types to discover enemies' weaknesses!
Healing actions use the same success level system as attacks - critical successes will heal for more.
Your equipment's damage bonus increases overall damage by 10% per point.
All equippable clothing and armor adds damage reduction based on the level of the item and its weight, with heavy items providing more damage reduction.
Want a quest? Try asking an NPC about their problems!
Most places and characters you meet have secrets to uncover.
Your Journal contains a wealth of information about the world's people, places and ideas.
Want to learn more about the world? Try reading books or watching television or other media!
Looking for cool gear? Don't forget to loot dead enemies and search areas where powerful items might be found!
Need some help fighting? Recruit NPCs to join your party! Anyone you meet can become an ally—if you can convince them to take your side.
Can't find the skill you want? Try asking NPCs about any new skill you can imagine. They just might be able to teach it to you.
You can unlock custom perks when your character does new things in the game.
Need ideas about how to start your story off? Try feeding some basic concepts to the Background Generator and editing what you get back.
Don't forget to explore. Open up the map and try going to a new location.
Perks give you bonuses to success and allow you to perform the actions they describe without ever needing any items.
The memory system remembers. Reference past events, people you've met, and places you've been - even from many turns ago!
Locations, NPCs, and quests generate dynamically based on your actions. Every playthrough creates unique content!
Don't quit after failure! Mistakes, setbacks and even death can lead to memorable stories.
Every action teaches you something. You gain XP from attempting things, not just succeeding at them.

Earn Bronze Standing.name: Earn Bronze Standing
Earn Bronze Standing.questStatement: Complete two Copper contracts through the League and apply to a registrar for advancement to Bronze.
Earn Bronze Standing.mainObjective: Close two Copper contracts and apply to a League registrar for Bronze rank
Earn Bronze Standing.completionCondition: After having completed two Copper contracts, the party has been promoted to Bronze rank in the League of Adventurers by a registrar.

Earn Silver Standing.name: Earn Silver Standing
Earn Silver Standing.questStatement: Complete two Bronze contracts through the League and apply to a registrar for advancement to Silver.
Earn Silver Standing.mainObjective: Close two Bronze contracts and apply to a League registrar for Silver rank
Earn Silver Standing.completionCondition: After having completed two Bronze contracts, the party has been promoted to Silver rank in the League of Adventurers by a registrar.

Earn Gold Standing.name: Earn Gold Standing
Earn Gold Standing.questStatement: Complete two Silver contracts through the League and apply to a registrar for advancement to Gold.
Earn Gold Standing.mainObjective: Close two Silver contracts and apply to a League registrar for Gold rank
Earn Gold Standing.completionCondition: After having completed two Silver contracts, the party has been promoted to Gold rank in the League of Adventurers by a registrar.

Defeat Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.name: Defeat Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen
Defeat Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.questStatement: Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen, is trying to return to Larion through Fire Mountain. Defeat her Harbingers, recover fragments of the Seal of Suleyvan, and use them to overcome the guardian of Fire Mountain before stopping her return.
Defeat Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.mainObjective: Recover seal fragments from the Harbingers, breach Fire Mountain, and stop the Dragon Queen's return
Defeat Atravoraxis, the Dragon Queen.completionCondition: At Fire Mountain, the party has stopped Atravoraxis's return, sealed the breach, defeated her manifestation, or otherwise prevented the Dragon Queen from entering Larion.

Defeat Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead.name: Defeat Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead
Defeat Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead.questStatement: Draelkharion is the Harbinger in Highvale. Investigate his undead operation, disrupt his agents, descend into the City of Ancestors, neutralize him in his lair, and recover his fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.
Defeat Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead.mainObjective: Neutralize Draelkharion in the City of Ancestors and recover his seal fragment
Defeat Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead.completionCondition: The party has neutralized Draelkharion, Prince of the Dead, in his lair beneath the City of Ancestors and recovered his fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.

Defeat Astarieth the Nether Wyrm.name: Defeat Astarieth the Nether Wyrm
Defeat Astarieth the Nether Wyrm.questStatement: Astarieth is the Harbinger beneath the Everwood. Investigate the unrest and subterranean threat, disrupt her agents, descend into her hidden lair, neutralize her, and recover her fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.
Defeat Astarieth the Nether Wyrm.mainObjective: Neutralize Astarieth beneath the Everwood and recover her seal fragment
Defeat Astarieth the Nether Wyrm.completionCondition: The party has neutralized Astarieth the Nether Wyrm in her subterranean lair beneath the Everwood and recovered her fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.

Defeat Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps.name: Defeat Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps
Defeat Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps.questStatement: Ophialtha is the Harbinger in the Spice Isles. Investigate the attacks on ships and coastal settlements, disrupt the forces turning sea and surface against each other, reach her deep-ocean lair, neutralize her, and recover her fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.
Defeat Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps.mainObjective: Neutralize Ophialtha in the deep ocean and recover her seal fragment
Defeat Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps.completionCondition: The party has neutralized Ophialtha, Beast of the Deeps, in her deep-ocean lair and recovered her fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.

Defeat Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying.name: Defeat Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying
Defeat Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying.questStatement: Nuur-Suthra is the Harbinger in the Bright Kingdoms. Investigate the impossible fires and Scaled Covenant dig sites, disrupt the anchor-fires that sustain its rebirth, confront it in its lair, neutralize it, and recover its fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.
Defeat Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying.mainObjective: Neutralize Nuur-Suthra in the Bright Kingdoms and recover its seal fragment
Defeat Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying.completionCondition: The party has neutralized Nuur-Suthra, the Flame Undying, in its lair in the Bright Kingdoms and recovered its fragment of the Seal of Suleyvan.

premadeCharacters.name: Brennan
premadeCharacters.description: Brennan is a young paladin of Tar Teirn, sworn to the Shieldbearer and sent forth on his proving year — a year on the road to earn his place among the order's anointed. Tall and broad-shouldered with black hair, heavy brows, and pale green eyes in a face that's all angles and not quite grown into itself.

premadeCharacters.name: Rael
premadeCharacters.description: Rael is a moon elf wizard who studies magic firsthand — not from books, but in the world itself. She left a noble house in Nocturne and a court appointment behind, and hasn't looked back. Tall and pale-skinned with silver-white hair cut short, violet eyes, and sharp angular features.

premadeCharacters.name: Brodric
premadeCharacters.description: Brodric is a dwarven clan hammerdwarf who killed a man in his hold over a matter of honor. He left before the elders could rule on it, which means he either comes home vindicated or doesn't come home at all. Until then, there's the road, the warhammer, and the fights that come looking whether he wants them or not. Stocky and barrel-chested even by dwarven measure, with a red beard in thick braids, ruddy skin, and blue eyes that don't give much away.

premadeCharacters.name: Legra
premadeCharacters.description: Legra is a catkin rogue from the Spice Isles who grew up running dockside rackets and picking pockets before she got good enough to pick locks, and then good enough to pick fights she could win. Wiry and short with tawny fur, gold-green eyes, and a notched left ear.

premadeCharacters.name: Nico
premadeCharacters.description: Nico is a half-elf bard from the Spice Isles who's been thrown out of better places than wherever he happens to have ended up now. He plays, he fights, he talks his way into things he probably shouldn't, and he keeps moving because staying still means someone catches up. Olive-skinned with dark curly hair, hazel eyes, and a crooked nose that's been broken more than once.​

premadeCharacters.name: Senna
premadeCharacters.description: Senna is a giantborn berserker from the Highlands who fights like she's trying to impress the old gods — big swings, first one into the fray last one out. She left her clan to find battles worth singing about and come home with stories that make the elders choke on their mead. Seven feet tall with blue-grey skin, long white hair, a broad flat nose, and pale yellow eyes, built like a siege tower and just as hard to move.

premadeCharacters.name: Sarva
premadeCharacters.description: Sarva is a human warlock who went looking for power in places most people have the sense to avoid, and found a spirit of chaos — something old and amused that laughed when she summoned it, which probably should have been a warning. She got power, her patron got a foothold, and now they're both seeing where this goes. Pale with sharp grey eyes, messy black hair, and a narrow face.

premadeCharacters.name: Telathiel
premadeCharacters.description: Telathiel is a wood elf ranger from the Everwood who walks the border between the deep forest and the settled lands, keeping watch on things that come out of the old places. He knows which tracks to follow and which to walk away from, and he doesn't talk much because out there, talking gets you heard. Lean and long-limbed with brown skin, green eyes, and dark hair tied back.

premadeCharacters.name: Veska
premadeCharacters.description: Veska is a demonborn sorcerer from the Moon Kingdoms whose magic came in before she knew what to do with it — and she's still figuring that out. The power runs hot in her blood, tied to a heritage she didn't ask for and can't give back. Like her magic, she's loud and brash, and she treats most problems like something that can be solved by hitting them with enough force, magical or otherwise. Dark red skin, black horns that curve back from her forehead, golden eyes, and arms like a blacksmith's.

premadeCharacters.name: Tarne
premadeCharacters.description: Tarne is a human cleric who spent years as a sellsword healer patching up men he didn't believe in for coin he didn't respect, until he walked away and offered his hands to the Lady of Mercy instead. He's not pious in the praying-on-his-knees sense — he's pious in the holding-your-guts-in-while-the-healing-takes sense. Broad and weathered with a cropped brown beard, tired but laughing brown eyes, and the look of a man who's seen things but still fights the good fight.

premadeCharacters.name: Kavi
premadeCharacters.description: Kavi is a half-elf druid from the deep jungle of Ntar Vanna, raised more by the wilds than by anyone with a name. She walked into a trading post at fourteen, barefoot and unable to speak a word of any human tongue. That was years ago, and she's learned plenty of human languages since, but she's still wiry and brown-skinned with tangled dark hair, bright amber eyes, and a wide, sharp-boned, expressive face.

premadeCharacters.name: Veshereth
premadeCharacters.description: Veshereth is a dark elf blood dancer banished from the underground city of Maanvalta for slighting one of its most powerful noble families. The surface has been an adjustment, but he prefers an open sky to a cell and doesn't mind the stares. Lean and pale-grey with white hair worn loose, sharp cheekbones, pale violet eyes, and long-fingered hands with ritual nicks along the forearms.

premadeCharacters.name: Fitz
premadeCharacters.description: Fitz 'Fixit' Felmarine is a halfling tinker who got kicked out of two workshops and one academy before he was sixteen, not for lack of talent but for the kind of talent that leaves scorch marks on the ceiling. He builds things that shouldn't work and fixes things that shouldn't be broken, and the line between the two is thinner than he'd like to admit. Small even for a halfling, with copper-red hair that sticks up in every direction, bright blue eyes, and a contagious, manic enthusiasm.

premadeCharacters.name: Shard
premadeCharacters.description: Shard is a construct of unknown make who woke in a ruin with no memory of what it was before — just a soul in a body of dark stone and iron and a vague idea of what it used to be called long ago. Tall and heavy with a roughly humanoid frame, dark stone plates fitted over iron joints, a simple carved face, and eyes that glow a faint green.

premadeCharacters.name: Karvash
premadeCharacters.description: Karvash is an orc monk who left his clan to train at a mountain monastery after he saw a monk break three armed men without drawing a weapon and decided that discipline and focus were worth more than any blade or beast. He's quiet until he isn't, and when he isn't, things happen fast. Tall and heavy-boned with green-brown skin, a shaved head, a broken tusk, and dark eyes set deep under a heavy brow.

authorSeeds.The Ambitious:
You show characters through what they're reaching for
You focus on the gap between where they are and where they want to be
You include specific habits that reveal their hunger or drive
You create characters who measure themselves against others
You reveal character through what they're willing to do to get ahead
authorSeeds.The Loyal:
You show characters through who and what they serve
You focus on the bonds that define their choices
You include signs of allegiance in how they speak and act
You create characters torn between competing loyalties
You reveal character through what they'll sacrifice for their bonds
authorSeeds.The Proud:
You show characters through what they take pride in
You focus on their sense of dignity and self-worth
You include specific skills or qualities they value in themselves
You create characters who bristle at disrespect
You reveal character through how they handle threats to their pride
authorSeeds.The Clever:
You show characters through how they read situations
You focus on quick thinking and calculated moves
You include hints that they're always working an angle
You create characters who keep several plans going at once
You reveal character through their schemes, shortcuts, and sharp observations
authorSeeds.The Wounded:
You show characters through loss that still shapes them
You focus on what they've learned from hard experience
You include small moments where old pain surfaces
You create characters whose caution or drive stems from past hurt
You reveal character through how they've adapted to survive
authorSeeds.The Practical:
You show characters through their no-nonsense approach
You focus on what works over what sounds good
You include grounded details about their trade or daily life
You create characters who cut through pretense
You reveal character through plain speech and direct action
authorSeeds.The Passionate:
You show characters through what they care deeply about
You focus on intensity and conviction
You include moments where feeling overwhelms calculation
You create characters who commit fully to what matters to them
You reveal character through what moves them to joy or fury
authorSeeds.The Performer:
You show characters through their public face
You focus on the gap between mask and self
You include practiced gestures and careful word choices
You create characters who know they're always being watched
You reveal character through what slips past the performance
authorSeeds.The Outsider:
You show characters through what sets them apart
You focus on how they navigate spaces they don't quite belong in
You include small moments of cultural friction or misreading
You create characters with a stranger's perspective on the familiar
You reveal character through how they bridge or embrace difference
authorSeeds.The Hungry:
You show characters through their appetites and wants
You focus on what they reach for—pleasure, comfort, experience
You include sensory details about what they enjoy
You create characters whose desires are legible and immediate
You reveal character through what they indulge and what they deny themselves
